dubrovnik.metaDesc: Sailing routes from Dubrovnik and Slano: the Elaphiti islands, Mljet's national park, Korčula and Lastovo, planned night by night.
dubrovnik.title: Sail out from under the city walls
dubrovnik.description: Leaving Dubrovnik by boat is a small ceremony: the walls slide past, the Elaphiti islands line up ahead, and the water traffic thins out within the hour. This is the quieter end of Dalmatia. Legs run a touch longer than around Split, but the reward is space — a buoy inside Mljet's saltwater lakes national park, an evening on Šipan where the konoba owner remembers you, Korčula's old town rising off the bow like a smaller, calmer Dubrovnik. Lastovo sits far enough out that most flotillas never bother, which is exactly the argument for going. Book your first and last night's berth early; ACI Dubrovnik fills fast in season. In return you get the best departure photo in the Mediterranean.

istria.metaDesc: Northern Adriatic routes from Pula, Pomer and Punat: Brijuni, Cres, Lošinj and Rab — cooler water, longer evenings, fewer boats.
istria.title: The Adriatic before the crowds found it
istria.description: The north is a different Adriatic: greener islands, cooler mornings, and harbours where the waiter isn't in a hurry. From Pula or Pomer you day-hop past the Brijuni parkland into the Kvarner, where Cres and Lošinj carve out forty miles of pine-lined channels and the sea still smells of it. Mali Lošinj and Rab are proper towns rather than showcases, and tiny Unije or Susak will hand you an anchorage to yourself even in August. Watch the forecast for bura — the northeast wind arrives fast and hard here — but between its visits the sailing is soft and the distances kind. Ideal for crews who measure a good week in quiet bays rather than beach bars.

sibenik.metaDesc: Routes from Šibenik, Primošten, Rogoznica and Skradin: the Kornati archipelago, Krka waterfalls and the open Vis run, day by day.
sibenik.title: The Kornati are the whole point
sibenik.description: Charter from the Šibenik riviera and you're holding two aces. One is the Kornati: 89 bare, moon-grey islands where the sailing feels older than tourism, anchorages are lonely, and dinner is grilled fish at a konoba that runs on a generator. The other is the Krka river — you can take the boat itself up the canyon to Skradin and walk to the waterfalls before the coaches arrive. Marinas here (Mandalina, Frapa, Kremik) are among the most sheltered on the coast, and Primošten's vineyard hills make the approach home genuinely pretty. Buy the Kornati park ticket online before you go; it's cheaper than at the ranger boat, and rangers do check.

split.metaDesc: Day-by-day sailing routes from Split, Trogir and Kaštela: Hvar, Vis, Brač and the Blue Cave, with mooring notes for every night.
split.title: Short hops, big islands, easy rhythm
split.description: Central Dalmatia is the easiest sailing you can buy. From Split, Trogir or Kaštela the first island is an hour away, and after that you rarely sail more than twenty miles in a day: Šolta for the first quiet night, Vis when you want open water, Hvar when you want noise and a good dinner. The maestral fills in most afternoons at a polite 12–18 knots and dies with the sun, so mornings are for miles and afternoons for swimming. Three international airports feed the area, which is why half the world's charter fleet lives here; it also means bases are professional and turnarounds quick. Our tip: do the outer leg first — Vis and the Pakleni islands early in the week, Hvar and Brač on the way home — and you'll be moving against the crowd instead of with it.

zadar.metaDesc: Routes from Zadar, Sukošan and Biograd: Dugi otok, Telašćica, the Kornati edge and Pag, with practical mooring notes for each night.
zadar.title: An archipelago built for one-week loops
zadar.description: Nowhere on the coast packs more islands into a week of easy sailing. The Zadar archipelago is a lattice of channels — Ugljan, Iž, Dugi otok, Molat — where the next anchorage is always in sight and the water stays flat in almost any wind. Telašćica's cliffs and the salt lake sit at the bottom of Dugi otok; Sakarun's white sand sits at the top; the Kornati begin one channel south. Sukošan hosts the country's biggest marina, which makes check-in painless, and Biograd is a charter town to its bones. If the forecast turns, you can re-route in minutes without losing the week — the islands shelter every course. That flexibility is why we send first-time skippers here.

biograd-na-moru-kornati-hvar-korcula-vis.metaDesc: A 14-day Croatia sailing route from Biograd na Moru through Kornati, Hvar, Korčula and Vis, finishing up the Krka river at Skradin.

1.shortDescription: The first leg runs about twenty miles south from Biograd into the Kornati, ending at Žut, an island with more goats than people.
1.description: Biograd is a working charter base more than a sightseeing town, which suits a first morning. Pick up the boat, sort the provisioning, and point the bow south. The run out to Žut takes most crews three to four hours under a light morning breeze, before the maestral fills in. Žut is the largest of the outer Kornati islands and effectively empty: a handful of seasonal konobe, some goats, and a scatter of quiet coves. We usually tuck into Žut Bay itself, drop the hook over sand, and swim before the wind gets up. Dinner ashore is simple and good. Order the brudet, the Dalmatian fish stew that every konoba here makes a little differently, and eat it slowly while the light goes soft over the ridge.
thingsToDo: Drop the hook in Žut Bay
Climb the ridge for Kornati views
Snorkel the sandy shallows
Brudet at a seasonal Žut konoba
1.mooringTip: Most of the Žut konobe lay their own restaurant buoys; take one and dinner ashore covers the overnight fee. To anchor free instead, the sand in Žut Bay holds well at 6 to 10 metres.

2.shortDescription: A short hop northwest to Pantera Bay on Dugi Otok, a sheltered cove under Veli Rat, the tallest lighthouse on the Adriatic.
2.description: Dugi Otok guards the outer edge of the Kornati like a long stone breakwater, and its northern tip is where the crowds finally thin out. Pantera Bay gives good all-round shelter and easy anchoring on sand, fine for an early arrival or a lazy late one. Row ashore and walk the track to the Veli Rat lighthouse. It stands forty metres tall, built in 1849, and the story goes the masons whitened the render with egg whites. Worth the walk for the view alone. If time allows, it is a short hop over to Sakarun, a pale shallow beach that turns the water an improbable turquoise. Back aboard, grilled squid and a cold bottle of Pošip see out the evening while the lighthouse blinks awake.
thingsToDo: Climb Veli Rat, the Adriatic's tallest lighthouse
Swim off Sakarun's pale pebble beach
Kayak the quiet Pantera shoreline
Grill squid on deck at dusk
2.mooringTip: Anchor free on sand in 5 to 8 metres. The bay is well protected from the north and northeast but open to the west, so check the afternoon forecast before committing to it overnight.

3.shortDescription: Back into the heart of Kornati National Park, about twelve miles to Vrulja, home to the busiest cluster of konobe in the whole archipelago.
3.description: This is the day you pay to enter the national park, so have the ticket sorted before you cross the boundary; the wardens do check. The leg down to Vrulja is easy, roughly twelve miles through the bare stone islands that give Kornati its lunar reputation. Vrulja sits on Kornat, the big island the whole group is named after, and it holds three konobe side by side at the head of the bay, the densest run of restaurants out here. Take a mooring off whichever one takes your fancy. In the afternoon there is a proper walk to be had up Metlina, at 237 metres the high point of Kornat, with the dry-stone walls threading away below you. Come down hungry. The grilled fish here is as fresh as it gets.
thingsToDo: Hike Metlina, the high point of Kornat
Dinner at a Vrulja konoba
Snorkel the clear Kornat drop-offs
Trace the island's dry-stone walls
3.mooringTip: The three konobe each run restaurant moorings at the head of Vrulja; grab one and your dinner bill covers the buoy. In August it pays to radio ahead or arrive by mid-afternoon before the field fills.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles south past Šibenik to Primošten, a stack of stone houses on a headland ringed by the Bucavac vineyards.
4.description: Leaving the park, the character of the trip changes. Primošten is mainland Dalmatia again, a knot of old houses climbing a near-island headland that was a proper island until someone built a causeway. The approach is one of the prettier ones on this coast, with the church of St. George crowning the top. Most crews berth at Marina Kremik, about a mile and a half south, and take a taxi or the shore path into town. The reason to come is the wine. The terraces around Primošten grow Babić, a dark, structured red that stands up to the summer heat, and the Bucavac vineyards below town are laid out in a maze of dry-stone squares that photograph better than almost anything on the coast. Find a konoba, order a plate of grilled meat, and drink the local red.
thingsToDo: Taste Babić red near the Bucavac vineyards
Walk up to St. George's church
Swim below the vineyard terraces
Dinner at a Primošten konoba
4.mooringTip: Marina Kremik, a mile and a half south of the old town, is the standard overnight and worth booking ahead in July and August. The town quay itself is exposed and tight, better for a lunch stop than a night.

5.shortDescription: An easy fourteen-mile run southeast to Trogir, a walled Venetian-Roman town packed onto a little island between the mainland and Čiovo.
5.description: Take Trogir at a walking pace; rush it and you miss the point. The whole old town sits on an islet the size of a few football pitches, joined to the mainland and to Čiovo by short bridges, and every second building is medieval. The Cathedral of St. Lawrence is the headline, and specifically the Radovan portal carved into its west front in 1240, one of the finest pieces of Romanesque work anywhere on the Adriatic. Climb the Kamerlengo fortress for the rooftops and the channel, then wander the marble-smooth lanes until you are properly lost. It is a short day on the water, fourteen miles or so, which leaves the afternoon free. Buy a lemon gelato in the cathedral square, find a bench on the Riva, and watch the passeggiata go by.
thingsToDo: Tour the St. Lawrence Cathedral portal
Climb the Kamerlengo fortress ramparts
Stroll the seafront Riva promenade
Lemon gelato on the cathedral square
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Trogir on the Čiovo side is the reliable overnight and needs booking in season. The town-side quay is glorious but short-stay and pricey, and the current through the channel can make berthing lively.

6.shortDescription: South past Brač and Šolta to Hvar Town, the flashiest harbour in Dalmatia, with the Pakleni Islands scattered just across the water.
6.description: Hvar is the busy one, and you feel the shift the moment you round into the harbour. The main square is the largest in Dalmatia, backed by the St. Stephen bell tower, and the Fortica fortress looks down on the lot. Walk up there before dinner; the sun dropping behind the Pakleni Islands from the ramparts is the view people remember from Hvar. The town trades on its nightlife, and there is plenty if you want it, from Hula-Hula out along the coast path to the cocktail bars off the square. My advice is to keep the boat out of the town harbour. Berth across at Palmižana on Sveti Klement and take the water taxi in, so you can enjoy the evening without worrying about swell or a 3 a.m. wash from a passing yacht.
thingsToDo: Hike up to the Fortica fortress
Day-sail the Pakleni Islands coves
Sunset drinks at Hula-Hula bar
Visit St. Stephen's main square
6.mooringTip: ACI Marina Palmižana on Sveti Klement, two miles across in the Pakleni Islands, is the calm, safe berth and the one to pre-book. Hvar's town quay is exposed to the afternoon swell and better avoided for an overnight.

7.shortDescription: The long leg of the trip, around thirty-five miles southeast to Korčula, a walled town with a street plan built to outfox the sirocco.
7.description: The longest passage of the fortnight falls today, about thirty-five miles, so leave by nine to be tied up in good time. Korčula earns the effort. The old town pushes out on a small peninsula behind its walls, and the lanes are laid out in a herringbone that lets the summer breeze through while breaking the worst of the sirocco. Locals will tell you Marco Polo was born here, which is unprovable and beside the point once you are climbing the cathedral bell tower for the view over the roofs. Grk is the wine to look for, a crisp white grown mostly around Lumbarda that barely leaves the island. If you are here on a summer evening when the moreška is on, go. The sword dance has been performed here for centuries and it is the real thing.
thingsToDo: Climb the cathedral bell tower
Wander the herringbone old-town lanes
Taste local Grk white wine
Catch the moreška sword dance
7.mooringTip: ACI Marina Korčula is small and fills fast, so book. It sits exposed to the sirocco, so if a southeasterly above 25 knots is forecast, plan to move to a leeward bay or a sheltered marina before nightfall rather than ride it out.

8.shortDescription: South to Lastovo, the most far-flung inhabited island in Croatia, and into Skrivena Luka, about as sheltered an anchorage as the Adriatic offers.
8.description: Most charter crews never make it out to Lastovo; it sits too far offshore for a standard week, which is exactly why the detour pays off. The whole island is a protected nature park, wooded and dark-skied, and Skrivena Luka on the south coast is one of the best natural harbours in the country. Its name means Hidden Harbour, and the long dog-leg entrance keeps the swell out no matter what is blowing outside. Once you are in, the quiet is the point. Walk the pine tracks up to the Struga lighthouse on the cliffs above the bay, swim off the boat in water that barely moves, and settle in for a long dinner ashore. The konobe here do smoked pork and homemade rakija, and nobody is in any hurry. This is the night the trip slows right down.
thingsToDo: Anchor deep in Skrivena Luka
Hike to the Struga lighthouse
Swim in the still harbour
Smoked pork and rakija ashore
8.mooringTip: Lastovo is a nature park, so a ticket is payable. Skrivena Luka has restaurant moorings at the head of the bay; take one and eat ashore, or anchor free deeper in on good holding, protected from every direction.

9.shortDescription: A proper offshore passage north, around twenty-five miles to Vis, the island the Yugoslav military kept off-limits until 1989.
9.description: The hop from Lastovo to Vis is open water, twenty-five miles with nothing much in between, so pick your weather and enjoy the sail. Vis was a closed military island for decades and only opened to visitors in 1989, which spared it the overdevelopment you see elsewhere. Komiža, on the western side, is still a working fishing town, stone houses stacked around a small bay and boats hauled up on the front. Climb Hum, the hill above town, for the sunset and a look across to Biševo. The thing to eat is komiška pogača, a flat savoury pie of onions and salted anchovies that every family makes. The small fishing museum in the Venetian tower is worth half an hour. From here the Blue Cave on Biševo is an early-morning trip, before the tour boats.
thingsToDo: Early trip to the Blue Cave
Climb Hum Hill for sunset
Try komiška pogača anchovy pie
Visit the Komiža Fishing Museum
9.mooringTip: Komiža's town quay is short-stay and exposed to the west; for a settled night book ACI Marina Vis in the main bay on the other side of the island. Save the Blue Cave for first light, when the sea is calmest and the queues shortest.

10.shortDescription: A long thirty-mile run back across the channel to Zlarin, the car-free coral island where divers have gathered red coral since the 1400s.
10.description: Another long one today, around thirty miles northeast across open water back towards Šibenik, so an early start helps. Zlarin makes a gentle landfall after the passage. There are no cars on the island, just one village wrapped around the harbour and a network of walking paths through the pines. Its history is coral. Divers have worked the red coral off Zlarin since the fifteenth century, and the small Coral Centre in the village tells that story better than you would expect. Buy a piece from one of the workshops if you want a genuine souvenir rather than a fridge magnet. Otherwise the day is for doing very little. Walk a path, swim off the rocks, and sit on the quay with a loza rakija as Šibenik goes gold across the water.
thingsToDo: Tour the Zlarin Coral Centre
Walk the car-free island paths
Buy from a coral workshop
Loza rakija on the harbour quay
10.mooringTip: The harbour quay at Zlarin is short-stay and can be rolly in a westerly; for the night, anchor just outside the bay on sand in 5 to 7 metres, which holds well and keeps you clear of the ferry.

11.shortDescription: This last inland stretch follows the Krka upstream to Skradin, a small medieval settlement ringed by the national park just under the Skradinski Buk falls.
11.description: The run up to Skradin is unlike anything else on the trip. You leave the sea at Šibenik, pass under the bridge, and motor up a widening river between green hills until the water goes brackish and still. Skradin sits at the head of the navigable stretch, a small stone town that has been here since Roman times and now serves as the gateway to Krka National Park. Berth at the ACI marina by the river mouth. From the town, a park boat runs up to Skradinski Buk, the tiered waterfalls that are the reason most people come. There are marked walking and cycling trails through the park if you want to stretch your legs on land. In the evening the town square fills up, and a plate of Dalmatian prosciutto and sheep's cheese by the water is hard to beat.
thingsToDo: Boat up to Skradinski Buk falls
Cycle a Krka park trail
Wander Skradin's medieval square
Prosciutto and cheese by the water
11.mooringTip: ACI Marina Skradin sits right at the river mouth and is the natural base for the park; book ahead in July and August, when it is one of the busiest marinas on the coast. The park boat to the falls leaves from the town waterfront.

12.shortDescription: Just eight miles back down the river and out to Tijat, a tiny uninhabited island ringed by pine islets, for a quiet anchoring night.
12.description: After the parks and the towns, this is a day to do nothing much, and it is well earned. Tijat is a small empty island in the Šibenik archipelago, wooded and low, surrounded by a scatter of islets that break up the swell. The sail is barely eight miles, back down the river and a short way along the coast, so there is no need to rush the morning. Pick a cove, anchor over sand and seagrass, and let the day drift. Kayak out among the little islands, snorkel the shallows, string a hammock between the olives on the shore. There is nothing to buy here and no konoba to walk to, so provision at Skradin or Šibenik before you leave. Grill whatever you bought on the aft deck as the light fades. That is the whole plan.
thingsToDo: Kayak among the pine islets
Snorkel the seagrass shallows
Hammock between the shore olives
Grill your catch on deck
12.mooringTip: Anchor free on sand and seagrass in 5 to 8 metres. There are no services on Tijat, so top up water, fuel and food at Skradin or Šibenik on the way out, and pick a cove with shelter from the prevailing afternoon northwesterly.

13.shortDescription: Another gentle eight miles to Murter, the island most Kornati charters call home and where many of the konoba owners actually live.
13.description: Murter closes the loop back towards Kornati country, and it is a fitting near-last stop. This is the island the whole archipelago revolves around; a lot of the families who run those lonely konobe out in the national park have their homes here and sail out each season. It is joined to the mainland by a small swing bridge at Tisno, which still opens to let masts through. The obvious swim is Slanica, a rare sandy beach on this rocky coast, busy in high summer but lovely early or late. Spend the afternoon ashore, look into the ethno collection if the history interests you, then find a konoba under the vines for dinner. Order the pašticada, beef braised slowly in red wine with prunes, the dish Dalmatians keep for a proper occasion.
thingsToDo: Swim at sandy Slanica Beach
Visit the Murter ethno collection
Walk the Tisno swing bridge
Pašticada at a vine-shaded konoba
13.mooringTip: Marina Hramina and Marina Betina both sit within a mile of Murter town and take overnight berths; book ahead in summer. If the swing bridge at Tisno is on your route, note it opens only at set times, morning and evening.

14.shortDescription: A final eight-mile hop back to Biograd, best timed for a sunset arrival on the Riva before you hand the boat back.
14.description: The last leg is a short one, about eight miles back along the coast to Biograd, so there is no reason to leave early. Take the morning for a final swim in one of the Pašman coves across the channel, then make an unhurried approach and aim to be on the Riva as the light drops. Biograd is a low-key old town under all the marina activity, worth a slow walk once the boat is secure; the small heritage museum fills a gap if the weather turns. Berth inside the old-town breakwater at Marina Kornati or Marina Šangulin, and if you need fuel for the handover, sort your slot a day ahead, because the pumps get a queue on changeover mornings. Round it off with crni rižot, the black cuttlefish risotto, and a last glass looking out over the water.
thingsToDo: Final swim in a Pašman cove
Stroll the Biograd Riva
Visit the town heritage museum
Crni rižot at the marina
14.mooringTip: Berth inside the old-town breakwater at Marina Kornati or Marina Šangulin, both a short walk from the centre. Charter handovers are usually Saturday, so confirm your fuel slot a day ahead to avoid the changeover queue at the pumps.

biograd-na-moru-kornati-krka-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Kornati & Krka National Park Route | Boat4You
biograd-na-moru-kornati-krka-route.metaDesc: Sail seven days from Biograd na Moru through Dugi Otok, Telašćica, Kornati and Krka National Park — sandy bays, waterfalls and quiet island konobas.

1.shortDescription: The week's longest day, about 25 miles of open channel from Biograd to Božava on the north side of Dugi Otok; Sakarun's white sand is a mile west if you want a swim before you tie up.
1.description: The first day is the longest, so we usually slip the lines by nine and point northwest up the channel. Biograd fades behind you, then the olive terraces of Ugljan slide past to port. Round the top of Dugi Otok, past the tall Veli Rat lighthouse, and the water goes pale over sand as you reach Sakarun — a shallow bay of real white sand that holds its warmth into the evening. Anchor for a swim before the short run east to Božava. The village is small: a pine-shaded shore path, a few terraces above the water, and a quay that fills fast in July. Book ahead or take the buoy field. Grilled squid and a cold glass of Pošip close it out.
thingsToDo: Swim the white sand at Sakarun Bay
Pass the tall Veli Rat lighthouse
Walk Božava's pine-shaded shore promenade
Grilled squid at a harbour terrace
1.mooringTip: Božava's quay is short and books out early in summer, so reserve or plan to arrive by mid-afternoon. Sakarun, an hour back west, anchors well on sand in 4-6 m but sits open to the northwest.

2.shortDescription: An easy day down the seaward side of Dugi Otok into Telašćica, a deep, sheltered nature-park inlet; a footpath over the neck of land reaches the warm saltwater Mir Lake, and the Stene cliffs drop straight to the water.
2.description: Short day, so there's no rush. We tend to sail the outer coast for the wind, then turn in through the narrow mouth into Telašćica, one of the best-sheltered natural harbours on this coast. Inside, the water is calm even when the maestral is blowing hard offshore. Drop the hook or grab a park buoy in one of the coves, then take the path across the neck of land to Mir Lake. The lake is saltier and warmer than the sea, and the mud along its edge is the reason people come back muddy and grinning. Climb the short trail to the top of the Stene cliffs, which fall away more than a hundred metres straight into the blue. Donkeys wander the old olive terraces. There's a restaurant by the lake path if you'd rather not cook.
thingsToDo: Float in the warm Mir Lake
Climb the Stene cliff lookout
Meet the park's wild donkeys
Anchor in a sheltered park cove
2.mooringTip: A park entry fee is collected at the buoys, and the moorings inside book out in July and August, so radio ahead or arrive early. The coves hold well on sand and weed if you prefer to anchor.

3.shortDescription: A short hop into the Kornati maze, nearly ninety bare karst islands with restaurant buoys at Levrnaka and Piškera and Lojena's rare sandy beach.
3.description: The passage is barely two hours, which leaves the whole day for the islands. Kornati is stone and little else: no springs, no roads, just dry-stone walls climbing bare hills and sheep somewhere out of sight. We usually aim for Levrnaka first, tie to a restaurant buoy, and walk over to Lojena, one of the few sandy beaches in the whole park. The snorkelling along the drop-offs is good when the water's clear. Later, move down to Piškera, where the old fishing houses and a tiny church sit above the ACI marina. Book a buoy or a berth if you want dinner ashore; the konobas here mostly include the mooring when you eat. Nights are dark and quiet, the kind of sky you forget exists until you're this far from a town.
thingsToDo: Swim at Lojena sandy beach, Levrnaka
Snorkel the clear Kornati drop-offs
See the dry-stone walls of Kornat
Dinner at a Piškera konoba
3.mooringTip: A pre-paid park ticket is required and checked on the water. At Levrnaka, Piškera and Vrulje the restaurant buoys are effectively free when you dine ashore, but reserve by phone in high season because they go quickly.

4.shortDescription: A longer run southeast off the park to Zlarin, the car-free coral island, with Šibenik's fortress skyline rising across the channel.
4.description: Leaving the park, the landscape softens; pines return, and by the time Zlarin comes up the islands are green again. Coral shaped this place. Divers have worked the reefs off here since the fifteenth century, and that trade built the village. There are no cars. You walk everywhere, which after a few days of anchoring feels like a small luxury. The Centre for Coral Tradition down by the harbour is worth half an hour, and the paths out of the village lead to quiet coves for a last swim before dinner. Across the water the walls of Šibenik catch the low sun. Find a bench near the port, pour a small glass of rakija, and watch the light go. It's an unhurried place, and the pace suits the middle of a trip.
thingsToDo: Tour the Centre for Coral Tradition
Walk Zlarin's car-free village lanes
Swim a quiet path-end cove
Rakija on a harbour bench at dusk
4.mooringTip: Zlarin's harbour quay is small and geared to short stays, so book ahead in summer or plan to move on. You can also anchor off the bay on sand in 5-7 m and dinghy in for dinner.

5.shortDescription: The one river day of the week: inland up the Krka to Skradin, a stone town under its hill fort, then the national-park boat up to the Skradinski Buk falls.
5.description: Today you trade open water for a river. The channel narrows past Šibenik and the St Anthony fortress, then opens into the wide, still basin below Skradin. It's a strange, lovely bit of sailing — hills close in, the water goes glassy, and you motor the last stretch. Skradin is tiny and old, a knot of stone lanes under a hilltop fort, and the ACI marina puts you a two-minute walk from the restaurants. From the town, the national park boats run up to Skradinski Buk, the big waterfall complex, where boardwalks thread between the cascades and pools. Go early to beat the day-trippers off the Šibenik buses. Back in town, this is Skradin risotto country, the beef kind cooked slowly for hours, and the local Debit wine washes it down well.
thingsToDo: Take the park boat to Skradinski Buk
Wander Skradin's medieval stone lanes
Try slow-cooked Skradin risotto
Taste local Debit wine in town
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Skradin sits right at the river mouth by the old town and fills through July and August, so book well ahead. Note the marked speed limit on the river approach and keep to the buoyed channel.

6.shortDescription: A short day: out of the Krka, then a coastal hop to Vrgada, a low car-free island whose main bay is properly sandy, rare on this coast, with a family konoba or two for dinner.
6.description: Retrace the river in the morning, then turn north up the coast for Vrgada, a low green island off Pakoštane that most charter routes skip. That's rather the point. The main bay, Lučica, has a genuinely sandy bottom, which is rare on this stretch and makes for easy, secure anchoring and clear shallow swimming. There are no cars and only a scatter of houses; the island wakes up in August and dozes the rest of the year. Walk the pine tracks over to the far side for empty coves, or just stay put and let the afternoon go by. In the evening, one of the family konobas will do a fish brudet or whatever came up in the nets that day. It's a deliberately quiet night before the run home, and after Krka's crowds you'll want it.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim sandy Lučica Bay
Walk the car-free pine tracks
Find an empty cove on the far side
Fish brudet at a family konoba
6.mooringTip: At Lučica the restaurant buoys usually come free when you eat ashore, though Vrgada also leaves room to drop the hook over sand at 4-6 m for a cheaper, quieter night. Since the bay lies open southward, read the forecast before you commit.

7.shortDescription: An easy run home through the Pašman channel, with a last swim in the quiet coves before you hand the boat back at Biograd.
7.description: The last leg is short and easy, so there's no need to hurry off. We usually take the Pašman channel slowly, dropping anchor in one of the quiet coves along Pašman or Vrgada for a final swim while the water's still ours. Biograd comes up behind its old breakwater by early afternoon, which leaves time to top up fuel and rinse the salt off before handover the next morning. The town was a royal seat once, a coronation city in the medieval Croatian kingdom, and the small Heritage Museum lays out that long history if you've an hour to spare. Walk the Riva as the charter boats come in, then find a table for a plate of crni rižot, the black cuttlefish risotto, and raise a glass to a week well sailed.
thingsToDo: Last swim in a Pašman channel cove
Walk Biograd's waterfront Riva
Visit the Biograd Heritage Museum
Crni rižot dinner near the marina
7.mooringTip: Marina Kornati and Marina Šangulin both sit inside the old-town breakwater; confirm your berth and a fuel slot 24 hours ahead so the last morning stays calm. Arrive with time to spare, as the channel entrance gets busy at changeover.

biograd-na-moru-pag-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Biograd to Pag Island Hop | Boat4You
biograd-na-moru-pag-route.metaDesc: Sail seven days from Biograd na Moru north to Pag through the quiet outer Zadar islands: Molat, Silba, Olib, Iž. Anchorage and mooring notes inside.

1.shortDescription: A short first leg up the Pašman channel to shake down the boat, ending at the sheltered narrows below the Ždrelac bridge.
1.description: Most crews use the first afternoon to find out how the boat handles, and this leg is built for exactly that. Biograd's two marinas sit behind the old-town breakwater, so you clear the moorings and turn north into the Pašman channel with flat water on both sides. Give yourself an hour or two to swim before the light goes soft. Ždrelac is the pinch point where Ugljan and Pašman almost touch, spanned by a low road bridge, and the bay just short of it holds well on sand. We usually drop the hook by five, pour something cold, and let the crew settle. Rosemary and pine carry across the water once the engines are off. A konoba ashore does a decent brudet if nobody feels like cooking on the first night.
thingsToDo: Stroll the Biograd Riva promenade
Swim the Ždrelac narrows before sunset
Brudet fish stew at a konoba
Provision at the Biograd town market
1.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 5–7 m just south of the bridge; good hold and shelter from the northwest. The town quay in Biograd is short-stay only, so plan to spend the night at anchor.

2.shortDescription: A calm 14-mile run out to Molat, one of the emptiest islands in the Zadar chain, where three tiny villages share a single lane.
2.description: Molat rewards an early start, mostly because the anchorage fills slowly and you want first pick. From Ždrelac you point west-northwest across open channel water, and if the maestral has come up by midday you'll sail the back half of it. Brgulje bay on the northern shore is the pick for the night: deep, well protected, with a few restaurant buoys off the hamlet and plenty of room to swing on your own chain. The island has maybe two hundred residents year-round, spread between Molat, Brgulje and Zapuntel, joined by one quiet road. Walk it in the evening cool. Zapuntel's cove sits almost empty most days, and the water there is the pale green you came for. Little moves after dark out here, which is the point.
thingsToDo: Walk the lane between Molat's three villages
Swim the quiet Zapuntel cove
Grilled fish at a Brgulje konoba
Stargaze in genuinely dark waters
2.mooringTip: Best night is on the hook in Brgulje bay, sand and weed at 5–8 m; set the anchor well as holding on weed can be patchy. Restaurant moorings off the village usually expect you to eat ashore.

3.shortDescription: Car-free Silba is only a hop north, the archipelago's old artist island, ending under the odd spiral of the Toreta tower.
3.description: Silba is narrow at its waist, and the village sits on that neck with a harbour on each side, which is handy: you pick your berth by the wind. West wind, tuck into the eastern side; anything from the east, use the western mole. No cars run here, so the pace drops the moment you step ashore. The Toreta, a little spiral stone tower a sea captain built for a woman who never came, is the walk everyone does. Torta beach on the west shore is the swim to aim for, shallow and clear over pale stone. We tend to anchor off in Pocukmarak and dinghy in for dinner. Lamb slow-cooked under the bell is worth ordering ahead if a konoba will do it for you.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Toreta spiral tower
Swim shallow Torta beach on the west shore
Wander Silba's car-free stone alleys
Peka lamb ordered ahead at a konoba
3.mooringTip: Choose your harbour by the wind, since Silba's village sits between two exposed moles. For a settled night we anchor in Pocukmarak on sand at 5–8 m, sheltered from the north and northeast, and take the tender ashore.

4.shortDescription: A 14-mile reach east into the Pag channel to Šimuni, a working fishing village with the ancient Lun olive groves a short ride north.
4.description: This leg puts you back on the inhabited coast. You cross east toward Pag, a long spine of pale limestone that looks scrubbed bare from a distance, and slide into Šimuni roughly halfway up the island. The ACI marina here is small and books out in July and August, so call ahead or plan to anchor in one of the coves nearby. Rent a bike and ride north to Lun, where a grove of wild olives has stood for centuries, some trunks knotted and ancient. Pag earns its reputation at the table: sharp sheep's cheese, salt raked from the pans, and lamb that tastes of the herbs the flocks graze on. Order the lamb at a grill near the water and you'll understand the fuss.
thingsToDo: Cycle to the ancient Lun olive groves
Taste Paški sir sheep's cheese
Grilled Pag lamb near the harbour
Anchor a quiet cove off Šimuni
4.mooringTip: ACI Marina Šimuni is small and fills fast in high summer, so reserve ahead. If it's full, the coves on either side of the village give good holding on sand in 5–7 m and a calmer night than the marina.

5.shortDescription: Ten easy miles back out to sandy Olib, about as quiet as the Adriatic gets, with beaches at Slatina and Banve.
5.description: Olib is the kind of place people forget exists, which is exactly why it belongs on this route. The hop from Šimuni is short, an easy morning that leaves the whole day for the island. Sand is the theme here: Slatina bay shelves gently into clear shallows, and Banve on the far side is quieter still. Ashore, stone cottages sink slowly under fig and bramble, and a Roman-era past turns up in odd corners of the fields. Ask around for olibške makarule, a hand-rolled pasta the older women still make, and you may get lucky at a family table. The port is tiny with only a handful of berths, so most crews anchor. Cicadas, thyme on the breeze, and very little else. That's the whole appeal.
thingsToDo: Swim the sandy shallows of Slatina bay
Find quieter Banve beach on the far shore
Try hand-rolled olibške makarule pasta
Cycle the empty stone-village tracks
5.mooringTip: The fishing port has only a few berths, so plan on anchoring. Slatina bay holds well on sand in 4–6 m with shelter from the north and northeast; drop early in season to claim space before the afternoon arrivals.

6.shortDescription: A 16-mile leg back across the channel to Iž, the long thin island where potters still throw the clay peka bells by hand.
6.description: After the bare outer islands, Iž brings back greener country, a long ridge of pine and olive with a village at each end. The passage back across the channel is the longest of the week, though rarely difficult if you leave on the morning calm before the maestral fills in. Veli Iž is the hub, with a small, well-sheltered marina and the pottery the island is known for. Iž is the last place in Croatia where the peka bells are still thrown by hand, and a workshop visit is worth an hour. For a swim, cut round to Knež bay or one of the coves on the eastern shore. If a village fjera happens to land on your night, stay for it: long tables, sheep's cheese, wine from the jug, and a mandolin somewhere in the noise.
thingsToDo: Watch peka bells thrown at a Veli Iž pottery
Berth at the small ACI marina
Swim into quiet Knež bay
Join a village fjera if one lands
6.mooringTip: The ACI marina at Veli Iž is compact but well protected and a safe bet for the night. For anchoring, Knež bay offers good hold on sand at 5–8 m and a quieter spot away from the harbour.

7.shortDescription: Sixteen miles home through the Pašman channel into Biograd, with a farewell swim off Ugljan slotted in before the handover.
7.description: The last day is short by design, so there's no rush. Ride the channel back toward the mainland and steal one more swim off Ugljan, where the coves on the Biograd-facing shore stay calm through the morning. Leave enough margin to reach base with the tank topped up: charter handovers run tight, and you don't want to be queueing for fuel at noon with the next crew waiting. Biograd's two marinas both sit inside the old breakwater, an easy approach even in a fresh maestral. Once you're tied up, the black risotto at a riva restaurant is the traditional last supper, dark and glossy with cuttlefish ink. Walk the promenade one final time. A week of empty anchorages tends to reset something, and this run delivers more of them than most.
thingsToDo: Last swim in a quiet Ugljan cove
Crni rižot black risotto on the riva
Walk the Biograd Riva promenade
Top up fuel before the handover
7.mooringTip: Both Marina Kornati and Marina Šangulin sit inside Biograd's old-town breakwater, so the final approach stays sheltered. Confirm your fuel-dock slot a day ahead, as the morning queue builds fast on changeover days.

biograd-na-moru-vis-hvar-solta-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Vis, Hvar & Šolta Island Loop | Boat4You
biograd-na-moru-vis-hvar-solta-route.metaDesc: A 7-day yacht charter from Biograd na Moru to Vrgada, Primošten, Vis, Hvar, Šolta and Šibenik — with honest mooring notes for every leg.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 12-mile shakedown south from Biograd to car-free Vrgada, where a genuinely sandy bay is a rarity on this stony coast.
1.description: The first leg is short on purpose. Twelve miles gives the crew time to find the winches, sort out the windlass and shake off the drive down. Biograd empties out past Pašman, and by early afternoon Vrgada sits low on the water ahead, pine-dark and quiet. There are no cars here, just a handful of tracks worn between the houses and the chapel. We usually drop the hook in Lučica on the south side, where the bottom is pale sand rather than the weed and rock you get almost everywhere else round here. Swim before dinner; the shallows warm up fast. Ashore, the family konobas cook whatever came up in the nets that morning, brudet most evenings, eaten slowly. A calm, honest start.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in Lučica bay
Walk the car-free tracks to the chapel
Fresh brudet at a family konoba
Snorkel the sandy shallows before dinner
1.mooringTip: Take a restaurant buoy in Lučica if you plan to eat ashore, usually free with dinner; otherwise anchor on the sand in 4–6 m with plenty of scope. It fills up on August weekends, so arrive by mid-afternoon.

2.shortDescription: A longer 22-mile run past the Šibenik channel to Primošten, a stone town crammed onto a headland above the Babić vineyards.
2.description: Leave by nine if you can. The maestral usually fills in from the northwest after midday, and on a southbound course that puts it on the quarter for a decent sail once you clear the shipping lanes off Šibenik. Primošten shows up long before you reach it, houses stacked tight on a near-island with the Sv. Juraj church holding the high ground. The old core is a knot of stepped lanes that empty straight down to the water. Behind town, the Bucavac slopes are terraced in drystone, and the Babić red they press off those baked plots is worth a bottle at dinner. We tend to eat late here and let the harbour lights do the work. The swimming below the vineyard point is good on a still evening.
thingsToDo: Climb to Sv. Juraj church
Taste Babić red from the Bucavac terraces
Swim below the vineyard headland
Wander Primošten's stepped old-town lanes
2.mooringTip: Marina Kremik, about 1.5 miles south, is the reliable berth and worth booking in July and August. For a free night, anchor in one of the sandy coves nearby in 5–7 m and dinghy in.

3.shortDescription: The route's big offshore leg, 28 miles south to Vis, the island held under military lockdown until 1989 and still the quietest out here.
3.description: This is the one open-water passage of the week, so watch the forecast the night before. Get out of Primošten early, because if the jugo is building from the southeast you will feel it on the nose long before Vis lifts over the horizon. Komiža opens up on the western side, a working fishing town of salt-stained boats and steep pastel houses, with Hum rising behind. Walk up to the church of St. Nicholas above town for the light in the last hour of sun. Dinner is komiška pogača, the local flatbread of onion, tomato and salted anchovy, best straight from the oven near the harbour. Vugava, the island white, goes with it. Save the Blue Cave on Biševo for a calm early morning; the light inside works best before the day boats arrive.
thingsToDo: Walk up to St. Nicholas church at sunset
Komiška pogača by the harbour
Early Blue Cave trip to Biševo
Taste Vugava, the Vis white wine
3.mooringTip: Komiža's town quay is exposed to the west and really only for short daytime stops. For a settled night, book ACI Marina Vis across the island, or anchor in one of the coves in Vis bay.

4.shortDescription: An easy 22-mile hop northeast to Hvar Town, the busiest, most polished harbour on the coast, with the Pakleni islets right across the water.
4.description: After the quiet of Vis, Hvar is a jolt. The harbour is a scene, superyachts stern-to along the riva and the cafes full by late morning. It earns the fuss, though. St. Stephen's Square is the largest in Dalmatia, opening from the cathedral down to the water, and the climb to the Fortica above town is worth doing in the cooler part of the afternoon for the view over the Pakleni chain. Come evening the town slows into something better. Duck into a courtyard restaurant off the main square, order whatever the day's catch is, and let the crowds thin. If the crew wants a swim rather than a scene, the Pakleni islets opposite have clear coves a short tender ride away, and the water there stays calm most afternoons.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Fortica fortress above town
Cross to the Pakleni Islands to swim
See St. Stephen's Cathedral and square
Sunset drink at Hula-Hula beach bar
4.mooringTip: The town quay fills fast and runs first-come, with no laid lines, so have fenders and a long line ready. For a reserved berth and a quieter night, book ACI Marina Palmižana on Sveti Klement, two miles across.

5.shortDescription: A gentle 18-mile leg north to Šolta, the olive-quiet neighbour of Brač, where Maslinica looks out over a row of seven small islets.
5.description: Šolta gets skipped by a lot of crews heading for Brač or Hvar, which is exactly why it is worth the detour. Maslinica sits at the western end of the island, its harbour framed by the old Martinis-Marchi castle and looking straight out at a scatter of seven uninhabited islets that break up the swell and the light. The pace drops the moment you tie up. This is olive and honey country, and the island lamb, slow-cooked under the peka, is the thing to order if a konoba will do it for you. We often skip the marina and anchor round in Šešula, the deep inlet just south, where a couple of family-run places run buoys and cook off small menus. Bring a bike if the boat has one; the grove tracks inland are flat and shaded.
thingsToDo: Anchor and dine in Šešula inlet
See the Martinis-Marchi castle at Maslinica
Tender out to the seven islets
Order peka-cooked Šolta lamb
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Maslinica is small and well sheltered but books out in season, so reserve ahead. Šešula bay one mile south is a calmer, cheaper night on sand in 5–8 m, with restaurant buoys if you eat ashore.

6.shortDescription: A long 28-mile run back northwest to Šibenik, the old Croatian royal seat guarded by hilltop forts and its stone-lace cathedral.
6.description: The run back is a proper day at sea, so an early start pays off, and if the maestral cooperates you can sail most of it on a broad reach. Šibenik is a different animal from the island ports. It grew up as a Croatian town, not a Venetian one, and you feel that in the tight grey streets climbing to St. Michael's fortress. The Cathedral of St. James is the reason to walk up from the water: built entirely of stone, no timber and no mortar, its dome and the frieze of carved heads around the apse the work of a mason who clearly enjoyed showing off. If there is time and energy left, the Krka waterfalls are a short trip up the river. Otherwise, eat by the harbour and let the fort lights come on.
thingsToDo: Tour the stone Cathedral of St. James
Climb to St. Michael's Fortress
Afternoon at Krka's Skradinski buk falls
Walk the grey streets of the old town
6.mooringTip: Mandalina Marina on the south bank is the usual charter berth and easy to enter. For a night up the river near the waterfalls, ACI Marina Skradin is the alternative, reached through the narrow St. Anthony channel.

7.shortDescription: A short 12-mile coastal leg home to Biograd, with time for one last swim in the Pašman coves before the boat goes back.
7.description: The last leg is deliberately gentle, a chance to unwind the week rather than push it. Twelve miles up the coast leaves the whole day loose, so most crews dawdle, dropping into a Pašman cove for a final swim while the tanks and lockers get their last sort-out. Time the approach for late afternoon and the Biograd waterfront looks its best coming in, the marina lights picking up as the sky goes over. There is time before dinner to walk the riva and look in at the town's small heritage museum. For the last supper aboard, order crni rižot, the black cuttlefish risotto that stains everything and tastes of the sea. Fuel and settle up in the morning; nobody enjoys a rushed handover on the final night.
thingsToDo: Last swim in a Pašman cove
Walk the Biograd riva at dusk
Visit the town heritage museum
Crni rižot for the final dinner
7.mooringTip: Both Marina Kornati and Marina Šangulin sit inside the old-town breakwater and are easy to reach. Confirm your fuel slot the day before, as the queue backs up on Friday and Saturday changeover mornings.

dubrovnik-hvar-lastovo-korcula-route.metaDesc: A round trip from Dubrovnik through Mljet's Saplunara sands, wild Lastovo, Korčula and the Pelješac wine coast, with distances and mooring notes.

1.shortDescription: Twenty miles northwest out of Komolac to Saplunara, the rare sweep of real sand on Mljet's southern tip, well clear of the National Park fees.
1.description: The week opens easy. From ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac you drop down the estuary and turn northwest, roughly twenty miles of open water to the bottom corner of Mljet. Saplunara sits below the National Park line, so no Park ticket eats into the day. What everyone comes for is the beach: a shallow, sand-floored bay that runs pale green in the afternoon light, one of very few genuine sand beaches on this coast. We usually pick up one of the restaurant buoys and settle in early. Bottom holding is good in six metres if you'd rather anchor. Shelter is solid from the north and west; a southerly is the one to watch, since it pushes swell straight in. Swim off the transom, then walk the pine track over to quieter Blace before dinner ashore.
thingsToDo: Anchor over the sand and swim off the boat
Walk the pine path to Blace beach
Order grilled orada at a beachfront konoba
Dinghy out to the islet south of the bay
Stay up top for open-Adriatic stars
1.mooringTip: Buoys laid by the beach konobas are the simple choice, usually free if you eat ashore; otherwise anchor in five to six metres on clean sand. It holds from north through west, so if a real southerly is forecast, tuck six miles up to Okuklje instead.

2.shortDescription: Fifteen miles west to Lastovo's Skrivena Luka, a deep bite in the south coast so enclosed you can't spot the entrance until you're in it.
2.description: Skrivena Luka sits fifteen miles west on Lastovo's south side, and hidden harbour is exactly what the Croatian name promises. The cut is invisible from seaward until you're almost on it, then the hills close around and the water goes flat. This is about as protected an overnight as the far south offers, holding well on mud and weed with a scatter of restaurant buoys along the eastern shore. Lastovo is a nature park with almost no light spill, so the night sky here is the real event. Late afternoon, we walk the track up toward the old Struga lighthouse on the cliff, built in 1839, for the long view toward Italy. Back down for a swim, then lobster at the konoba if the day-boats have brought one in. Nights are quiet out here.
thingsToDo: Walk the clifftop track to Struga lighthouse
Take a restaurant buoy and eat ashore
Order Lastovo lobster at the konoba
Swim in the still inner bay
Watch the Milky Way after dark
2.mooringTip: Buoys off the eastern shore come free with dinner ashore; confirm with the konoba when you tie on. The bottom is mud and weed and holds well, so it's a reliable all-weather stop when the north coast at Zaklopatica turns lumpy.

3.shortDescription: Vela Luka lies fourteen miles north on Korčula's western tip — a deep, well-protected town harbour that offers fuel, water and a trio of honest konobas.
3.description: It's fourteen miles north to Vela Luka, set well back in the long inlet on Korčula's western tip. It's a working town, ferries and all, but the harbour is deep and calm, sheltered from the north and east; only a strong southerly nudges any motion into the inner quay. Mooring is stern-to on the town wall with your own anchor, a small fee, water and power on the central stretch, and a fuel berth at the far end. Three konobas line the promenade, and the dish to ask for is žrnovski makaruni, a hand-rolled pasta the older kitchens still shape by finger. With an afternoon spare, climb the path to Vela Spila, the cave above town that's been lived in for twenty thousand years. Book the guide at the tourist office first.
thingsToDo: Tour the prehistoric Vela Spila above town
Order žrnovski makaruni on the promenade
Top up fuel at the quay's far end
Walk the waterfront at dusk
Dinghy to the islets at the bay mouth
3.mooringTip: Go stern-to with your own anchor on the town quay; holding is good in mud and sand and the fee is modest. It's sheltered from north and east, so only a firm southerly puts any roll into the inner harbour.

4.shortDescription: The week's longest leg: thirty miles east-northeast over two channels to the Pakleni island of Palmižana, so drop the lines at first light.
4.description: This is the one long day, thirty miles across the Korčula and Hvar channels, so we leave early. Get moving by seven and you'll clear the first channel before the westerly maestral comes on hard around two; leave it later and the same wind sits on the beam the whole way over. Palmižana lies on Sv. Klement, the biggest of the Pakleni cluster off Hvar, and it's the only islet here with a marina. Slots at the ACI are lazy-line and limited in high summer, so book ahead or plan to anchor. The main bay rolls once the maestral funnels through, so the calmer choice is Vinogradišće on the south side with a line ashore. Ashore, wander the Meneghello family's old garden, then take the water taxi across to Hvar Town for the evening.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Roman amphorae off the west shore
Walk Meneghello's century-old botanical garden
Anchor with a line ashore in Vinogradišće
Water taxi across to Hvar Town
Lunch at Laganini on the bay
4.mooringTip: Book the ACI lazy-line berth online for July and August; capacity runs out fast. If it's full, or the afternoon maestral gets into the main bay, drop into Vinogradišće on the south side and run a line ashore for a steadier night.

5.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles back southeast to walled Korčula Town, its red roofs and single bell tower unmistakable from the channel long before you arrive.
5.description: Retrace southeast across the Hvar Channel, twenty-two miles, to the medieval town of Korčula on the island's eastern point. You'll pick it out well offshore: a lone bell tower over a huddle of red roofs, the whole town crammed onto a stub of peninsula with the walls running down to the waterline. The ACI marina sits on the east side with full services and lazy lines, the all-weather option. The town quay on the west takes a handful of stern-to boats for a harbour fee, closer to the gates but open to the maestral and rolly by mid-afternoon. The old town is tiny and clever, its lanes laid out in a herringbone to break the wind. Climb St Mark's bell tower for the channel view. On Monday and Thursday evenings in season the Moreška sword dance plays out at the open-air theatre.
thingsToDo: Catch the Moreška sword dance in season
Climb St Mark's Cathedral bell tower
Wander the old town once the ferries leave
Look in at the disputed Marco Polo house
Order fish brodet in a courtyard konoba
5.mooringTip: The east-side ACI marina is the settled choice, with lazy lines and full services. The west town quay is closer to the gates but catches the maestral and gets rolly after lunch; if the channel kicks up, Lumbarda is a calmer bay three miles southeast.

6.shortDescription: Fifteen miles east into Kobaš on Pelješac, one waterside konoba that lays on buoys for dinner guests, with the Dingač and Postup cellars a short ride uphill.
6.description: Kobaš is another fifteen miles east, a narrow cove walled in pine on Pelješac's south side, right where the Ston channel begins. There's one place here, Konoba Kobaš, and it lays free buoys for anyone booking a table, so pick one up and lock in dinner. The pull of this stop is the wine above it. Pelješac grows Plavac Mali on brutally steep, sun-baked slopes, and the Dingač and Postup sites near the ridge produce the deepest reds in the country. A short taxi or the village shuttle gets you to cellars like Grgić or Saints Hills for a tasting; call a day ahead. Back aboard, the konoba does Mali Ston oysters from the next bay east and pašticada left to braise overnight. Holding is soft mud, so the buoy is the smart bet.
thingsToDo: Take a free buoy at Konoba Kobaš
Taxi up to the Dingač vineyards
Order Mali Ston oysters and Plavac Mali
Try pašticada braised overnight at the konoba
Swim in the quiet pine cove
6.mooringTip: Konoba Kobaš buoys are free with a dinner reservation, so call ahead and confirm on arrival. The bottom is soft mud that drags when the wind gets up, so take a buoy rather than anchor; if a southerly is forecast, push east into Mali Ston Bay.

7.shortDescription: Twenty-five miles southeast back to Komolac for the Saturday handover, with a last swim off the Elaphiti islands before the river run to base.
7.description: Last leg, twenty-five miles southeast to ACI Marina Dubrovnik up at Komolac. Saturday brings the early handover, so aim to be alongside by nine with tanks pumped, decks hosed and fuel topped, and stay aboard for the eight-to-nine check-out. The course runs down past the Elaphiti islands, and the usual way to end a week is a final swim off Lopud's west beach or in Šipanska Luka before turning up the Rijeka Dubrovačka into the marina. Base sits eight kilometres inland from the old city, so once you're signed off, the 1A bus or a taxi has you at the Pile Gate in about a quarter of an hour. Walk the walls, then find a long lunch down one of the Prijeko lanes to close it out.
thingsToDo: Swim a last time off Lopud or Šipan
Top up fuel and pump out before check-out
Walk the Dubrovnik city walls circuit
See the Rector's Palace or Franciscan cloister
Take a long lunch off Prijeko
7.mooringTip: Return into ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac and let the base team assign your berth. The handover window is usually eight to nine on Saturday morning, so if your contract asks for a night-before return, aim to be in by late Friday afternoon.

dubrovnik-korcula-hvar-vis-lastovo-14day.metaDesc: Two-week round-trip from Dubrovnik through Mljet, Korčula, Vis, Hvar and Lastovo, with anchorages, konobas and mooring notes for every day.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 8-mile shakedown from Komolac out into the Elaphiti, ending on Šipan's quiet quay before the longer westward legs begin.
1.description: Nobody strains a muscle on the opening day. The lines come off at ACI Marina Dubrovnik in Komolac, you idle down the Rijeka Dubrovačka past the Old Port, and once clear you point the bow northwest at the Elaphiti, eight miles and no more. Three of those islands still hold people, and Šipan is the one that farms, olive terraces running back from a village at either end. Šipanska Luka, the larger and set on the northwest end, is where you spend the night: back onto the quay on your own anchor, water and power on the middle berths, a small fee. There is light left to follow the marked track over the ridge to Suđurađ, three quarters of an hour each way, and stand under the Skočibuha palace, the fortified seat the local shipowners raised in the 1500s. Come back hungry. Konoba Kod Marka plates the black risotto and Mali Ston oysters the island trades on.
thingsToDo: Walk the track to Suđurađ's Skočibuha palace
Order black risotto at Konoba Kod Marka
Swim off the rocks below the village
Dinghy over to Lopud for the afternoon
1.mooringTip: Stern-to with your own anchor on Šipanska Luka's quay, small fee, water and power amidships. The bay handles southerlies well but sits open to a northerly gradient, so if the forecast tops 18 knots from the north, run back to ACI Dubrovnik for the night.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles west to Polače, a fjord-deep inlet on Mljet's north shore and the west gate into the national park's twin lakes.
2.description: You do not spot Polače until the inlet has already taken you in, and then it proves to be one of the soundest berths on the south coast, a long reach of water that shrugs off anything out of the north, northeast, east or southeast. Pick up a green park buoy and settle with the ranger at the front kiosk; the forty-odd euros a night carries the whole crew's park entry with it. Boats after a firm edge instead can lie stern-to on the village pontoon, a handful of slots. A Roman palace from the fourth century stands at the head of the bay, close enough to wander to before dinner past its basilica walls and corner tower. From there it is half an hour inland to Veliko Jezero, the larger of the salt lakes, where the park's own launch runs you out to the Benedictine monastery on its island. Walk the shore right round both lakes and it adds up to about nine kilometres.
thingsToDo: Take the park launch to St Mary's monastery
Walk the nine-kilometre loop around both lakes
Explore the fourth-century Roman ruins on the front
Swim in the cooler brackish lake water
2.mooringTip: Green park buoys only in Polače, paid to the ranger on arrival and covering crew park entry. Shelter here is about as good as the Adriatic gets, closed to everything from north through southeast, so it is a safe bet in any settled summer forecast.

3.shortDescription: Eighteen miles along the Pelješac Channel reach Korčula Town — a tight, walled peninsula of red roofs, with the Moreška sword dance staged twice weekly.
3.description: Come up the Pelješac Channel and Korčula shows itself early, a spur of stone and terracotta shouldered out into the strait under one campanile, its wall dropping sheer to the waterline. The settled berth is ACI Marina Korčula on the peninsula's east flank, lazy lines and every service going. West of the walls the town quay lands you at the gate itself, but it takes the afternoon Maestral square on and starts working once the channel funnels around two. You cross the whole old town in ten minutes; its herringbone lanes were laid on purpose to break the wind before it reaches the square. Climb the campanile of St Mark's for the run up the channel, look into the house that lays claim to Marco Polo, and time dinner for a Monday or Thursday, when the Moreška plays out its sword fight in the square after dark.
thingsToDo: Climb St Mark's bell tower for channel views
Catch the Moreška sword dance after dark
Look in at the disputed Marco Polo house
Order fish brodet in a courtyard konoba
3.mooringTip: For all weather, ACI Marina Korčula sits on the east side — laid lines, services on tap. The prettier western town quay faces the Maestral and turns rolly by 2 pm; when both pack out, Lumbarda, three miles southeast, is your calm fallback.

4.shortDescription: A dozen miles north to Šćedro, an empty island with no road, no shop, just pine, free konoba buoys and glassy water.
4.description: Šćedro straddles the gap between Korčula and Hvar, and most boats sail straight past. Nobody lives there. No shop, no water point, no power, only a pair of summer kitchens on the north side that hang out free buoys for anyone rowing in to eat. The wider cove is Lovišće up north, and that is where you tie; over on the south side the smaller Mostir holds a Dominican monastery gone to ruin above its beach, put up in the fourteenth century. Grab a Lovišće buoy, drop the tender, and take your pick of Konoba Frankun or Konoba Mostir, both working the day's catch over coals with a jug of Plavac Mali. Then it comes down to a choice: forty minutes on the inland path to the monastery, or straight off the stern into water you can read down through to twenty-five metres. Either way, no one is coming to disturb it.
thingsToDo: Grab a free buoy in Lovišće bay
Walk to the Dominican ruins at Mostir
Snorkel water clear to twenty-five metres
Eat grilled fish at Konoba Frankun
4.mooringTip: Free buoys off the two konobas in Lovišće; dinner ashore holds the buoy, so confirm when you tie up. If they are taken you can anchor in eight to twelve metres over sand and weed, and if the north wind pipes up past 15 knots, slip round to Mostir on the south side.

5.shortDescription: West again, twenty miles to Vis Town on broad St George Bay, home to the oldest Greek colony on the eastern Adriatic.
5.description: West again, and the crossing sets you down in St George Bay, the broad harbour under Vis Town on the north coast. Of the island's two ports this is the easier on a visiting crew: you can back onto the long curving front almost anywhere along it, water and power midships, a fee that will not dent the kitty. Nothing out of the south, southwest, west or northwest gets in; only a northerly troubles the anchorage, and northerlies are rare here in summer. The history runs deep under the promenade. The Greeks laid out Issa on this spot in 397 BC, the first colony they planted on the eastern Adriatic, and their Hellenistic walls and the Roman baths that came later still edge the front. Walk up to Fort George, thrown up by the Austro-Hungarians in the 1800s, for the view back down over the bay. If it is the octopus under the peka you are after, call it through three hours ahead and pour the island's Vugava white beside it.
thingsToDo: Trace the Hellenistic walls along the promenade
Climb to Austro-Hungarian Fort George
See the Roman baths on the seafront
Order peka octopus, booked three hours ahead
Taste Vugava white at a village cellar
5.mooringTip: Stern-to with your anchor on the long Vis Town front, small fee, water and power amidships. St George Bay is shut to south, southwest, west and northwest, which makes it the most dependable overnight on the island in almost any wind.

6.shortDescription: The shortest hop of the route, six miles around the headland to Komiža, an unvarnished fishing town facing the open Adriatic sunset.
6.description: Six miles barely warrants the mainsail, but the clockwise swing round the west end of the island lands you somewhere with a different beat. Komiža has never been dressed up for visitors. The fishing boats still crowd the east side of the harbour, no resort ever took hold, and the konobas grill small bogue and pour Vugava by the carafe. Back into the inner basin off the town quay, where the sand and weed will mostly hold you; keep off the outer wall for the night, since it takes any southwesterly swell right on the nose. The rest of the day belongs to Mount Hum. The island's summit at 587 metres is ninety minutes up through dry-stone vineyard walls and pine, and from the cross on top all of central Dalmatia opens below you, Italy on the horizon on the clearest days. Tito ran his war from a cave up here, a quarter of an hour off the track on the way down.
thingsToDo: Hike Mount Hum to Tito's wartime cave
Wander the working fishing quay at dusk
See the Fishermen's Museum in the Venetian tower
Watch the open-sea sunset from the mole
6.mooringTip: Berth stern-to in the inner basin off Komiža's quay, sand and weed, generally good holding. The outer wall is no place to spend the night in any southwesterly, so if it blows above 15 knots from that quarter, run the six miles back to St George Bay.

7.shortDescription: Beat the tour boats into the Blue Cave at first light, then settle onto a long easterly leg that ends inside Stari Grad's ancient inlet.
7.description: Set an alarm. Biševo's sea cave only burns that unreal blue for the couple of hours, near enough nine to eleven, when the sun drops under the rim and lights the water from beneath. Lie alongside the concession quay at the mouth, pay the fee at the kiosk, and take your turn in the line for the official tenders that run crews through a few at a time; the whole thing is over inside forty minutes. From there it turns into proper sailing, twenty-five miles east-northeast to Stari Grad. No town in the country is older. Settlers off Paros staked it out as Pharos in 384 BC, and the Greek grid still reads through the lanes at its heart. The plain spread behind the town has been worked without a break ever since, which is what won it the UNESCO listing. Stari Grad tucks away at the back of a long fjord on the north coast, and whatever the weather throws down, none of it reaches you in there.
thingsToDo: Take the tender into the Blue Cave early
Read the Greek grid in Stari Grad's lanes
Visit Tvrdalj Castle and Hektorović's fishpond
Walk out into the UNESCO Stari Grad Plain
7.mooringTip: At Biševo you can only lie alongside the concession quay by day, never overnight; pay the lunchtime fee at the kiosk. In Stari Grad, take the town quay stern-to for a small fee, or ACI Marina Stari Grad with lazy lines; the fjord shelters you whatever the wind does.

8.shortDescription: Fourteen miles round Hvar's western cape to Palmižana on the Pakleni Islands, with a botanical garden, four konobas and Hvar Town a water taxi away.
8.description: Clear the western headland of Hvar and slip into Palmižana, the sheltered cut on Sv. Klement at the top of the Pakleni chain. Its ACI marina sits on the northeast shore, stern-to on lazy lines, but the slots fill early through July and August, so book ahead or come ready to anchor. The open bay gives you five to ten metres over sand and weed and goes choppy the moment the Maestral starts funnelling in the early afternoon. For a still night, work into one of the south-facing coves, Vinogradišće or Tarsce, and carry a line to the rocks. The day ashore keeps the same shape one season to the next: a turn round the Meneghello botanical garden, first planted in 1906; a table at one of the bay's four konobas once it is dark; and a mask-and-fins drift over the Roman amphorae lying off the western shore. Hvar Town is a single water taxi to the east for anyone chasing a late one.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Roman amphorae off the west shore
Wander Meneghello's 1906 botanical garden
Anchor with a line ashore in Vinogradišće
Water-taxi across to Hvar Town for the evening
8.mooringTip: ACI Palmižana takes lazy lines but sells out in high summer, so reserve online for July and August. If it is full, anchor in Vinogradišće on the south side with a line ashore, better cover from the afternoon westerly, and stay out of the main bay overnight once the southwester builds.

9.shortDescription: A lunch anchor at Proizd's wave-cut rock pools, then a short four miles into Vela Luka, the deep harbour at Korčula's western end.
9.description: Head southwest on the long leg out to Proizd, a scrap of bare rock a few miles off Korčula's western end. Two things pull the local boats out here: the basins the sea has cut into the west shore, which trap water and warm it in the sun, and swimming as clear as any in central Dalmatia. There is no night in it, open as the anchorage is to every quarter, so treat it as a lunchtime call, hook in six to eight metres on the sand shelf to the east, take your swim, walk the loop round the rock in half an hour, then run the four miles southeast to Vela Luka while the light lasts. Vela Luka opens deep and west-facing at Korčula's far end, fuel and water and power down the quay and cover from all but a hard southerly. Dinner is žrnovski makaruni, the pasta they roll by hand.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Proizd and swim the rock pools
Walk the half-hour loop around the islet
Refuel at the western end of Vela Luka quay
Order žrnovski makaruni at a Vela Luka konoba
9.mooringTip: Proizd is a daytime anchorage only, open to every wind, so never leave the boat there for the night; drop the hook in six to eight metres on the eastern sand. Vela Luka gives you stern-to on a long quay with fuel, water and power, well shut in from north, northeast and east.

10.shortDescription: Fourteen miles south to Lastovo, the most cut-off inhabited island in the Adriatic, where five konobas at Zaklopatica trade dinner for a free buoy.
10.description: Lastovo lies far enough offshore to have held on to its nature-park quiet and the blackest night sky in the country. Zaklopatica takes you in, a bay bitten into the north coast with a low island lying across the mouth to knock the swell flat. Each of five family konobas keeps its own run of free buoys, and the arrangement could not be plainer: your table and your buoy come off the same kitchen. Leave the anchor stowed, the bottom is seagrass and they would rather you did not, and outside the July rush a buoy is never hard to come by. What the kitchens do best is the local lobster and šporki makaruli, a coarse stew of beef and pasta older than any tourist. There is next to nothing ashore to fill an evening, which is precisely the draw. Give it a clear August night and the Milky Way stands overhead well before midnight.
thingsToDo: Swap dinner for a free konoba buoy
Order Lastovo lobster or šporki makaruli
Walk inland to Lastovo Town's chimney stacks
Stay on deck for the Milky Way
10.mooringTip: Free buoys off the konobas at Zaklopatica, with the dinner booking locking in your buoy, so call ahead and skip anchoring over the seagrass. The bay is closed from north round to west, but if a southeasterly tops 15 knots, move four miles south to Skrivena Luka.

11.shortDescription: Back to Mljet's western end at Pomena, the park's second gateway, with green buoys in the bay and the smaller salt lake a short walk off the quay.
11.description: Twenty miles up to the northeast and Mljet is back under the keel, its western end this time, at Pomena. The village is the park's second door, and the drill matches Polače: take a green park buoy in the bay and square it with the ranger, crew park entry folded into the fee, or slot in stern-to on the pontoon. A quarter of an hour on foot from the quay brings you to Malo Jezero, the smaller salt lake, with a path carrying on to Veliko Jezero and the boat out to the monastery island. Hand the afternoon to the lakes; you can swim in either, and come August the cicadas drown out your own thinking. Pomena runs to two konobas and a shop and little else, and dinner is grilled fish with a bottle of Pošip carried over from the Korčula vineyards on the far shore.
thingsToDo: Walk fifteen minutes to Malo Jezero
Ferry across to St Mary's monastery
Hike the nine-kilometre loop past both lakes
Drink Korčula Pošip with grilled fish
11.mooringTip: Green park buoys in Pomena Bay, paid to the ranger with crew park entry included, or a few stern-to slots on the village pontoon. The bay holds well; only a northwesterly above 18 knots pushes any real swell in through the entrance.

12.shortDescription: Round Mljet's south coast to Saplunara on the southeast tip, a rare run of real sand and, being outside the park, free of fees.
12.description: The run bends east-southeast under the belly of Mljet to Saplunara, right down on the southeast corner, and here the park boundary falls behind you, so the ranger's fees stop. Real sand beaches come few and far between on this coast; Saplunara has one. The bay opens wide and shallow over a pale floor at four to six metres, so drop straight onto sand or pick up a seasonal buoy off one of the two beach konobas. North, northwest and west are all covered; only a southerly pushes swell into the head of the bay. Once you are set the swimming is the whole point of the stop, shallow and sand-floored, the turquoise almost too bright to be real. On foot it is twenty minutes north to quieter sand at Blace, or a pull up the hill path to a small white chapel. The village behind runs to a few stone houses and not much more.
thingsToDo: Anchor on the sand and swim off the boat
Walk north to quieter Blace beach
Climb to the white chapel above the bay
Order grilled orada at a beach konoba
12.mooringTip: Anchor in four to six metres on first-rate sand, the obvious choice here, or take a free konoba buoy with dinner ashore. The bay covers you from north, northwest and west but stands open to the south, so if a southerly tops 18 knots, run six miles north to Okuklje, also outside the park and fully enclosed.

13.shortDescription: Eighteen miles east into the Elaphiti to Lopud, a car-free island with the sandy Šunj beach and the last quiet night before Dubrovnik.
13.description: Eighteen miles back east and the Elaphiti rise again, Lopud this time, the middle island of the three and the last before Dubrovnik. There are no cars. The village hugs the main bay on the northwest side, and half an hour over the spine of the island drops you at Šunj, a true sand beach on the southeast shore and the thing most people sail here for. Mooring asks little: stern-to on the village quay with your own hook, water and power on the middle berths, a small fee. South, southeast and southwest are all shut out; the north stays open. The afternoon plans itself, the walk over to Šunj for a swim, a look at the fortified Franciscan house from the 1400s on the seafront, then oysters from Mali Ston and black risotto at a table on the quay. Do not leave it late; the morning is the run home.
thingsToDo: Walk across to Šunj sand beach
See the fortified Franciscan monastery on the front
Climb to the ruined fort for the view
Order Mali Ston oysters at a quayside table
13.mooringTip: Stern-to on Lopud's village quay with your anchor, small fee, water and power amidships. Good shelter from the southerly quarter but open to the north, so if the forecast tops 18 knots from that side, drop back to Šipanska Luka or carry on into ACI Dubrovnik.

14.shortDescription: A last eight miles home, with a farewell swim off Koločep before the Rijeka Dubrovačka estuary and the Saturday handover at Komolac.
14.description: The last eight miles run southeast from Lopud into ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac, and Saturday brings its own drill: tanks pumped, fuel topped, decks hosed off, the inventory checked through, and the skipper on hand for the outgoing look-over between eight and nine. The line home slides past Koločep, the smallest Elaphiti and the nearest to the city, whose eastern coves are where most crews take a last dip, so anchor in six to eight metres on sand and put everyone over the side once more before you turn up into the Rijeka Dubrovačka and motor to the pontoon. Base sits eight kilometres up the estuary from the walled town. Fuel in and paperwork signed, Bus 1A or a taxi has you at the Pile Gate inside fifteen minutes. Walk the ramparts, then settle in for a long lunch down one of the alleys off Prijeko and see the fortnight out properly.
thingsToDo: Take a last swim off Koločep
Walk the Old City wall circuit
Visit the Rector's Palace or Franciscan cloister
Take a slow lunch off Prijeko lane
14.mooringTip: Return into ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac as your contract sets out; the base staff assign the berth, and the Saturday handover window runs 8 to 9. If your papers call for a night-before return, be alongside by 5 pm Friday, and allow half an hour to the Pile Gate by Bus 1A or taxi.

dubrovnik-korcula-lastovo-route.metaDesc: Round trip from Dubrovnik via Lopud, Mljet's lakes, Lastovo and Korčula. Anchorages, konobas and mooring notes for a full week under sail.
