1.shortDescription: A gentle first hop out of the marina to car-free Lopud, where the walk to Šunj sand ends every afternoon in the sea.
1.description: We slip out of Komolac and down the Rijeka Dubrovačka with the engine ticking over, past the Old Port, which the day boats use for a tender drop but nobody lies to overnight. Eight miles on, Lopud opens up, the second-largest of the inhabited Elaphiti islands and blissfully free of cars. Tie stern-to on the village quay and the afternoon writes itself. Behind the harbour the 15th-century Franciscan monastery guards a thirty-metre bell tower and a sea wall you can walk. Then comes the crossing, half an hour on foot over the spine of the island to Šunj, one of the few real sand beaches on this coast, shallow enough for children to wade out forever. Back at a konoba it is grilled squid and a cold Pošip. Leave the swimming until the light goes soft.
thingsToDo: Cross to Šunj beach on foot
Tour the 15th-century Franciscan monastery
Order grilled squid at a village konoba
Try a chilled local Pošip white
Climb to the ruined hilltop fort
1.mooringTip: Berth stern-to on the town quay and lay your own hook off the bow; the fee is cheap and the middle berths have water and power run to them. Southerlies glance off the headland, but anything out of the north drives straight in, so once it pushes past 18 knots, cross to Šipanska Luka on neighbouring Šipan.

2.shortDescription: A fourteen-mile reach west to Saplunara, Mljet's sandy corner beyond the park gate, where the anchor bites clean sand and the water turns pale.
2.description: The southeastern toe of Mljet falls outside the national park line, so nobody collects a fee at Saplunara and no ranger comes looking. What pulls boats in is the beach, a long shallow run of true sand and one of only a handful left on the whole coast. Drop the hook in four to six metres and the holding is first-rate, or pick up a restaurant buoy laid by one of the two konobas above the sand. We usually anchor and swim straight off the transom, the pale floor throwing the water that milky turquoise you only get over sand. In the afternoon, walk the twenty minutes north to Blace, a smaller and quieter cove. This is the open Adriatic side, so the stars after dinner are worth staying on deck for.
thingsToDo: Anchor on the sand and swim off the transom
Walk north to quiet Blace cove
Order grilled orada at a beach konoba
Climb the path to the hillside chapel
Stargaze on deck after dinner
2.mooringTip: Anchor in four to six metres over first-class sand, the standard call here, or take a free konoba buoy if you are dining ashore. The bay is tucked from north through west but open to a southerly swell; if the sirocco builds past 18 knots, run six miles north to Okuklje, which closes in completely.

3.shortDescription: Around Mljet's western end to Pomena, the little park village where a fifteen-minute walk lands you at the saltwater lakes and their island monastery.
3.description: Round the western end of the island and Pomena is as close as a keel gets to the national park. Grab one of the green park buoys in the bay, pay the ranger, and the fee covers the crew's park entry too. From the pontoon it is a quarter of an hour on foot to Malo Jezero, the smaller of the two salt lakes, and the path carries on to the big lake where a shuttle boat runs out to the Benedictine monastery on its islet. Give the afternoon to the lakes. The full loop is roughly nine kilometres of shaded trail, swimming is allowed wherever you fancy, and the cicadas never let up. Bicycles are for hire in the village if the legs prefer wheels. Two konobas and a small shop cover dinner, usually grilled fish and a glass of Pošip.
thingsToDo: Walk to Malo Jezero salt lake
Take the shuttle to the island monastery
Cycle or hike the nine-kilometre lake loop
Pay the ranger for a park buoy
Dine on grilled fish and Pošip
3.mooringTip: The buoys in the bay are park-run and settled at the ranger's kiosk, about forty euros a night with the crew's park entry rolled in. Shelter is good all round; it takes a northwesterly over 18 knots to work any swell as far as the mouth. The village pontoon has a handful of stern-to spots if you would rather lie to your own anchor.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles southwest to remote Lastovo, where the konobas of Zaklopatica hand out buoys with dinner and the night sky is the darkest in Croatia.
4.description: The run down to Lastovo is twenty miles southwest, and out here it feels like the last stop on the line, a nature park the day boats desert the moment their engines fire for home. Everyone funnels into Zaklopatica, a bight bitten into the north coast with a low islet lying across the mouth to knock the swell flat. Family konobas ring the water, each hanging its own line of buoys, Augusta Insula, Triton and Santor among them. The deal could not be simpler. Book a buoy and a table at the same place and the mooring costs you nothing but the meal. Skip anchoring here, the bottom is seagrass and the hold is poor. What you come for is lobster, hauled off the traps north of the island that morning, and the rustic pasta stew the locals call šporki makaruli. After dark there is nothing to do, which is exactly right. The Milky Way stands overhead well before midnight.
thingsToDo: Book a konoba buoy with dinner
Eat fresh Lastovo lobster off the traps
Try šporki makaruli pasta stew
Snorkel the islet across the entrance
Watch the Milky Way after dark
4.mooringTip: Pick up whichever konoba's buoy you like the look of, but ring ahead in July; there are only fifteen or so and the dinner booking is what holds one for you. Don't bother anchoring, the seabed is seagrass and won't grip. The bight closes off everything from north to west, but a sirocco over 15 knots reaches in, and then it's a four-mile hop south to Skrivena Luka.

5.shortDescription: Fourteen miles northeast to walled Korčula town, red roofs on a spur of stone, with the Moreška sword dance staged on Monday and Thursday nights.
5.description: Korčula announces itself long before you arrive, a compact peninsula of terracotta roofs and a lone bell tower rising off the eastern end of the island. The ACI marina on the east flank of the spur is the no-fuss berth in any weather, lazy lines and every service laid on. Tie up on the town quay to the west and you land nearer the gates, but the afternoon Maestral comes at you head-on and the swell starts working once the channel funnels around two. The old town is tiny, laid out in a herringbone of lanes built to catch the breeze and break the wind. Climb the bell tower of St. Mark's for the run down the channel, poke into the disputed Marco Polo house, then find a table for the Moreška, the costumed sword play that runs Monday and Thursday at nine. Order a glass of Grk, the white grape grown only on the sands at Lumbarda just east of town.
thingsToDo: Watch the Moreška sword dance at nine
Climb St. Mark's cathedral bell tower
Wander the herringbone lanes at dusk
Visit the disputed Marco Polo house
Drink a glass of Grk from Lumbarda
5.mooringTip: The ACI marina on the eastern flank is the berth that holds in any blow, lazy lines and every service. The town quay opposite drops you at the gates but sits open to the west Maestral and turns rolly past two in the afternoon. If it is full, calm water waits three miles southeast at Lumbarda.

6.shortDescription: Polače lies eighteen miles back southeast, the most sheltered bay on the coast, where you drop the hook beside the walls of a Roman palace.
6.description: Back on Mljet's north coast, eighteen miles southeast of Korčula, Polače is the landing for the park's western half, a long inlet screened by a scatter of low islands that leaves it almost eerily still. First-timers routinely sail right past the entrance and only spot it once they are through. The bay is shut off from north the whole way round to southeast, about as safe an overnight as you will find down here. Pick up a green park buoy or slip stern-to on the village pontoon. The real curiosity is ashore: the walls of a fourth-century Roman complex stand a few steps from the water, basilica and corner tower still legible, and you can walk straight through at no charge. For the afternoon, climb Montokuc at 253 metres for the full sweep over the park, or ride the shuttle across to the monastery. Black cuttlefish risotto is the village supper.
thingsToDo: Walk through the fourth-century Roman ruins
Climb Montokuc for the park panorama
Ride the shuttle to St. Mary's monastery
Order black cuttlefish risotto ashore
Pick up a green park buoy
6.mooringTip: The buoys are park-run, about forty euros a night with the crew's entry included, settled with the ranger on the waterfront. Few bays in the south Adriatic hold you as snugly, blocked from north the whole way clockwise to southeast, so the forecast rarely troubles your sleep.

7.shortDescription: The long run home, twenty-four miles southeast into the marina by nine on Saturday, with a last Elaphiti swim before the handover.
7.description: Twenty-four miles of open water stand between Polače and the run back up the Rijeka Dubrovačka to Komolac, all of it southeast. Saturday rules apply: the boat wants to be alongside by nine, fuel topped, tanks pumped, decks hosed and the inventory ticked, with the skipper standing by for the checkout between eight and nine. Time it to leave early. The course threads back past the Elaphiti chain, so one more swim in Šipanska Luka or off Lopud is the traditional full stop before the river narrows to the marina. The base sits eight kilometres inland from the walled city, but once you are cleared it is fifteen minutes by the 1A bus or a taxi to the Pile Gate. Walk the ramparts, then lose an afternoon over lunch in the back lanes off Prijeko.
thingsToDo: Take a last swim in the Elaphiti
Walk the Old City wall circuit
See the Rector's Palace courtyard
Lunch in a Prijeko back lane
Ride the 1A bus to Pile Gate
7.mooringTip: Bring her back into ACI Marina Komolac as the contract says and the base will point you to a slot. Checkout is eight to nine on the Saturday; if your paperwork wants the boat in the night before, be tied up by five on the Friday. Reckon half an hour door to door from the pontoon to the Pile Gate.

dubrovnik-split-oneway-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Dubrovnik to Split One-Way Sail | Boat4You
dubrovnik-split-oneway-route.metaDesc: Sail one-way from Dubrovnik to Split in a week: Elaphiti, Mljet, Korčula, Lastovo, Vis and Hvar, with leg distances and where to moor each night.

1.shortDescription: A gentle eight-mile start out of Komolac into the Elaphiti chain, where Šipan's quays and Lopud's sandy Šunj make the easiest first night.
1.description: Day one is the shakedown, and the Elaphiti chain is built for it. Clear ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac, follow the old flooded river out to sea, and the whole hop to these low green islands runs about eight miles. Most of the traffic settles on Šipan, the largest of the three. Its northern inlet at Šipanska Luka takes maybe ten boats stern-to with water on the pier, and Konoba Kod Marka, a few steps up from the quay, grills fish the way it ought to be done. Lopud is the calmer call: no cars, a Franciscan monastery over the harbour, and a track that climbs across the island to Šunj. Šunj is proper sand with a warm, wade-in shelf, which you almost never find this far south. Crew after a swim, we point the bow at Lopud; crew after a long konoba dinner, Šipan. Either way the anchorage stays settled with any south in the wind.
thingsToDo: Cross Lopud on foot to the sand at Šunj
Grilled fish at Konoba Kod Marka, Šipan
Visit the Franciscan monastery above Lopud harbour
Berth at Šipanska Luka town pier
Swim off the Šipan olive-grove coast
1.mooringTip: Šipanska Luka is the safest berth in the group, roughly ten boats stern-to on your own anchor with water on the 40 m pier; grab a slot by mid-afternoon in July. Lopud's village quay works the same way but empties badly in a northerly, so if the forecast turns above 18 knots from the north, run back to ACI Dubrovnik for the night.

2.shortDescription: Fifteen miles west with a lunch swim at Kobaš on Pelješac, then a Park mooring at Pomena and the walk to Mljet's saltwater lakes.
2.description: Split the run to Mljet at Kobaš, a cove backed by pine on the Pelješac side of the channel. A couple of konobas here keep their own jetties and will wave you stern-to as long as you stay for lunch; if it's only a swim you want, drop the hook over sand in six to eight metres and go over the side. Gastro Mare and Konoba Kobaš both feed you well. Come afternoon, work the last miles up to Pomena, the western entrance to Mljet National Park. Take one of the ranger's buoys in the bay and pay him where you lie, and that same ticket counts as the crew's Park entry. Fifteen minutes on foot from the pontoon lands you at Malo Jezero, first of the two salt lakes. Give the rest of the day to the circuit: a footpath rings both lakes, a launch runs out to the Benedictine monastery on its islet in Veliko Jezero, and you can swim anywhere you please.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Kobaš for a midday swim over sand
Tie stern-to at a Kobaš konoba jetty
Settle the buoy fee with the Pomena ranger
Stroll the pontoon path to Malo Jezero
Ferry across Veliko Jezero to the island monastery
2.mooringTip: The only mooring in Pomena is a Park buoy, running about €40 a night in season with the crew's Park entry built into the price; the ranger collects when you tie on. Shelter is good all round, and it takes a stiff northwesterly before any swell noses into the entrance. If the buoys are all taken, a few boats can lie stern-to off the village pontoon on their own hook instead.

3.shortDescription: Across the Pelješac Channel, fourteen miles to walled Korčula town, with the all-weather ACI marina or a stern-to slot right under the medieval gates.
3.description: Korčula announces itself a long way out: a fist of stone pushing off the island's eastern tip, one campanile over the roofs, the sea wall wrapping three sides. Fourteen miles of open channel from Pomena get you across, most days an easy beam reach. You have two beds for the night. The ACI marina on the peninsula's east flank gives you lazy lines, power and water and holds in any weather; the town quay on the west flank drops you at the very foot of the walls but pays for it, taking the afternoon Maestral on the nose and rolling from two o'clock on. Inside, the old town is tiny, a ten-minute walk end to end, its side lanes fanned off the spine like ribs so the summer wind can't drive straight through. Go up St Mark's campanile for the channel view, put your head into the house they call Marco Polo's, and on a Monday or Thursday night stay for the Moreška, the old sword dance. Fancy a wine detour, the Grk vineyards at Lumbarda are a short reach to the southeast.
thingsToDo: Take the campanile stairs at St Mark's for the channel view
Look in at the Marco Polo house and the Land Gate
Stay for the Moreška sword dance on a Monday or Thursday
Taste Grk white in the Lumbarda vineyards
Track down a bowl of fish brodet off the back lanes
3.mooringTip: For a night that ignores the weather, take the ACI marina on the peninsula's east side, lazy lines and full services. The quay under the walls is the postcard berth but sits open to the west Maestral and starts rolling by mid-afternoon, so save it for a flat-calm forecast. When the channel gets up, duck three miles southeast into Lumbarda, which holds quiet.

4.shortDescription: Fourteen miles south to Lastovo, a Nature Park so far out it barely catches a house light; the Zaklopatica konobas swap a mooring buoy for a seat at dinner.
4.description: Nowhere in this reach of the Adriatic goes as quiet as Lastovo. It's a Nature Park, the sky at night is about as black as Croatia gets, and there's next to nothing to do ashore, which is the whole reason to come. Count on fourteen miles down from Korčula. Zaklopatica bites into the north coast in a tight horseshoe, with a scrap of an islet across the mouth to knock the swell flat. The family konobas around the water, four or five of them, each keep a row of buoys for anyone eating in, so there's no puzzle to it: call ahead, book the table, the buoy comes with it. Triton and Aragosta are the ones we lean on. Leave the anchor stowed, the bed is seagrass and won't hold, and the Park would rather you left it be. Eat the lobster, or the šporki makaruli, that rough beef-and-macaroni stew they do out here. Then cut the cabin lights and sit out on deck; on a clear night the stars stack up overhead well before midnight.
thingsToDo: Trade a dinner booking for a night on the buoy
Eat the island lobster or a bowl of šporki makaruli
Hike up to Lastovo Town for the fumari chimneys
Swim out and snorkel the islet off the entrance
Sit out for the stars once the konoba closes
4.mooringTip: The Zaklopatica buoys cost nothing so long as you dine at the konoba that owns them, so phone ahead and let the table booking pin your buoy down; Triton keeps about six and fills earliest. Don't bother with the anchor over that seagrass. Everything but a hard southeasterly is covered here, and if the scirocco is due over 15 knots, it's four miles south through the channel to Skrivena Luka, which shrugs off anything.

5.shortDescription: Komiža on Vis is twenty-two miles northwest, a working fishing port beneath Mount Hum that still carries the feel of the 1980s Adriatic.
5.description: Vis stayed shut to outsiders, a military island, right up to 1989, and you can still read that in how little has been built on it. This is the week's long leg, twenty-two miles up from Lastovo, so we like to slip the buoy by nine. Komiža sits out on the west coast, folded into a bay under Hum, which rears 587 metres straight off the water behind the town. Lie stern-to in the inner basin on your own hook; the bed is sand over weed and grips well enough most nights. Don't get talked onto the outer wall after dark, it takes the southwest swell full on. A quarter-hour on foot covers the whole town: fishing boats moored down the east side, the Fishermen's Museum tucked in the old Venetian tower, and konobas that turn on grilled sardines and a cold Vugava, the island's own white. If anyone's legs are still working, the climb to Tito's wartime cave on Hum hands over the best view in central Dalmatia.
thingsToDo: Berth stern-to in Komiža's inner basin
Step into the Fishermen's Museum in the old tower
Walk up Hum to the cave Tito hid out in
Pair a cold Vugava with grilled sardines
Catch the sun drop into open water off the mole
5.mooringTip: Best bet is stern-to on your own anchor inside Komiža's inner basin; the sand-and-weed bottom does the job, and you'll pay a harbour fee. Steer clear of the outer wall overnight, it's wide open to any southwest swell. Should a southwesterly build past 15 knots, it's a six-mile run north to Vis Town, where St George's Bay gives you cover on all sides.

6.shortDescription: Twenty miles east across the Hvar Channel to Palmižana on Sveti Klement, marina slots on one side and quiet line-ashore coves on the other.
6.description: Sveti Klement is the biggest of the Pakleni Islands, a low green spine that keeps Hvar town out of sight of the open channel. It's twenty miles east off Komiža to the ACI marina on the Palmižana shore, and its berths go early through July and August, so reserve online or be ready to swing on the hook. With the marina full, I'd sooner be on a line ashore in Vinogradišće on the south coast, which stays calmer once the afternoon westerly gets going. Days here fall into a pattern: the Meneghello botanical garden the family has kept since 1906, a scatter of konobas at the water's edge, and a mask-and-fins drift over the Roman amphorae sunk off the western point. Hvar town, and the crowds that come with it, is one water taxi east for anyone chasing the evening; stay put and the quiet is yours.
thingsToDo: Drift over the sunken amphorae off the western point
Wander the Meneghello family's century-old garden
Run a line ashore in Vinogradišće for the night
Hop the water taxi over to Hvar town after dark
Walk the goat track to Vlaka on the far shore
6.mooringTip: Palmižana's ACI marina is stern-to on lazy lines, and through high summer you'll want that booking made online before you turn up. No room, and Vinogradišće on the south coast is the fallback, sand and weed with a line run ashore, and it sits better under the afternoon westerly. Don't lie out in the open main bay overnight once the southwest gradient sets in.

7.shortDescription: A final twenty-two miles northeast into the Split marinas for the Saturday nine o'clock handover, ending on the Riva or in old Trogir.
7.description: One last hop, twenty-two miles northeast off Palmižana, brings you into the ring of charter bases around Split. Saturday is handover day and it runs to a clock: alongside by nine with the fuel topped, the tanks pumped down, the decks hosed and someone aboard to walk the boat through with the base between eight and nine. Which base is written into your one-way papers. ACI Marina Split lands you nearest the centre and Diocletian's Palace; Marina Kaštela is the largest and sits a quarter-hour's drive west; ACI Trogir tucks against the old walled town and closest to the runway. None of the three is any bother to enter, day or night. Once the checkout is signed, the airport is ten to fifteen minutes from Trogir or Kaštela and a touch longer from Split. Time to spare before the transfer, spend it over a slow lunch on the Split Riva or in Trogir, and let a week's sailing settle.
thingsToDo: Top the tanks and empty the heads before checkout
Take one more turn through Diocletian's Palace
Poke around Trogir's walled old town if that's your drop
Get a last swim in at Bačvice below the old town
Sit out a slow lunch on the Split waterfront
7.mooringTip: Hand the boat back at whichever base your one-way paperwork names, be it ACI Marina Split, Marina Kaštela or ACI Trogir; the dockmaster there will put you stern-to or alongside. Checkout runs eight to nine on the Saturday, so if your contract wants the boat in the night before, aim to be tied up by five on the Friday.

kastela-dubrovnik-kastela-14day-route.metaDesc: A 14-day round-trip yacht itinerary from Kaštela down to Dubrovnik and back, taking in Šolta, Vis, Lastovo, Mljet, Korčula and Hvar with mooring notes.

1.shortDescription: First leg is an easy one. Šolta lies just off Split but keeps to itself, and Maslinica on its western tip is the only harbour on the island worth the name, thirteen miles southwest of the base.
1.description: We tend to slip out of Marina Kaštela around mid-morning and swing the bow southwest, Split falling away over the port rail as we settle onto the thirteen-mile line for Šolta. The entrance to Maslinica hides behind a scatter of low islets that flatten whatever the open Adriatic is throwing at the coast. Inside, the bay kinks back on itself under a wall of hills on the village side and holds well in four to eight metres of mud and sand. Two ways to spend the night: lazy lines and full service at Martinis Marchi, tucked beneath a castle first raised in 1708, or the village quay stern-to on your own hook for a few euros. Both put the brakes on the moment you are fast. Konoba Kamenar has stayed in one family across three generations, and the Dobričić red they pour barely makes it off the island.
thingsToDo: Sundowner on the Martinis Marchi castle terrace
Ask for the Dobričić red at Konoba Kamenar
Row over to Stipanska islet and swim
Grab a tin of Šoltansko olive oil in the village
1.mooringTip: Book Martinis Marchi on the south side well ahead for July and August; it has the lazy lines and shore power. Prefer the village quay and you go stern-to on your own anchor for a small fee. The ring of islets across the mouth means the only wind that reaches you is a northerly, and those are rare in summer.

2.shortDescription: The trip's shortest hop, barely twelve miles across the Brač Channel. Milna cuts deep into the west side of Brač, a harbour going back to Venetian times, and its narrow throat holds the water dead flat.
2.description: Milna is a lunch stop and nothing grander, which is exactly what a fortnight afloat wants now and then. The crossing lands you at a slot in the western shore of Brač where a parish church from the sixteen-hundreds stands hard on the waterline and points you round into the inner basin. Go stern-to on your own anchor anywhere along the town wall, or hang back in the outer half in six to ten metres of sand with a line run to the rocks for when the Maestral shows up after lunch. The stone under your feet here built half of Split: Brač limestone went into Diocletian's Palace and plenty besides. Food is a single decision. Put the order in for the lamb peka before two, because you cannot hurry three hours over the coals, and swim off the transom while it cooks.
thingsToDo: Put the lamb peka on before 2pm, eat it at six
Stroll out to the parish church on the quay
Anchor the outer bay with a line to the rocks
Dinghy up to Bobovišća for a sunset swim
2.mooringTip: The inner quay is stern-to on your own anchor, with water and power on the middle pontoon and a small fee to the harbourmaster. Most charter crews end up anchoring the outer half and running a line to the rocks, which costs nothing. When the afternoon Maestral pushes past fifteen knots the inner berths still sit quiet.

3.shortDescription: The Pakleni Islands sit a couple of miles off Hvar Town, fifteen miles southeast of Milna. Anchor-and-swim country, except at Palmižana, which holds the only marina in the chain.
3.description: A low chain of pine-covered rock lies a couple of miles off the south side of Hvar Town, and that is the Pakleni. Sv. Klement is the big one at the western end, with Palmižana on its eastern flank holding the only berths in the group. That ACI marina packs out in high summer, so book online if you would rather pick up a lazy line than dig the anchor in. Come early afternoon the Maestral funnels straight into the main bay and starts it rolling; when it does, we shift south to Vinogradišće, where forty buoys and a line ashore under the pines keep things steady. The Meneghello family laid out a botanical garden here in 1906, and their kitchen still trades alongside Laganini and a scatter of smaller konobas. Ten minutes by water taxi puts you in Hvar Town if the evening asks for it.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the sunken Roman amphorae off the west shore
Poke around Meneghello's 1906 botanical garden
Grab a Vinogradišće buoy when ACI fills
Hop the water taxi to Hvar Town for dinner
3.mooringTip: ACI Palmižana is stern-to on lazy lines, and the fairways run tight for a modern charter yacht, so reserve online for July and August. If it is full, or the main bay gets rolly on the afternoon westerly, move round to Vinogradišće and run a line ashore.

4.shortDescription: The long leg of the week: twenty-three miles southwest out to Vis, the last inhabited island before open sea. Komiža, its fishing port, stayed off-limits behind the navy until 1989.
4.description: No inhabited Croatian island sits further out than Vis, and getting there means twenty-three miles of open water off the Pakleni. The place was sealed off as a naval base under Yugoslavia right up to 1989, and you can still read that in the total absence of resort sprawl. Komiža curls around a bay under Mount Hum, fishing boats along the eastern side and a Venetian tower from the fifteen-hundreds watching the entrance. Get onto the inner quay before two if you can, because the good stern-to spots over sand and weed go early, and the outer wall takes any southwesterly swell straight on the nose overnight. Dinner is whatever the boats landed at dawn. Konoba Jastožera perches over the water in a converted lobster store, and the Vugava white in your glass grows on no other patch of earth.
thingsToDo: Eat lobster over the water at Konoba Jastožera
Hike Mount Hum to Tito's wartime cave
Look in on the Fishermen's Museum in the old tower
Order grilled anchovies with a glass of Vugava
4.mooringTip: Take a stern-to spot on Komiža's inner quay on your own anchor over sand and weed, and get in before two in high season. The outer wall is exposed to southwesterly swell; if that is forecast above fifteen knots, run six miles north to the fully sheltered St. George Bay at Vis Town.

5.shortDescription: Two caves before the sun climbs, then eighteen miles east to Vela Luka at the west end of Korčula. The Blue Cave off Biševo only turns properly blue for a couple of hours, so the day pivots on that window.
5.description: Everything today keys off where the sun is. The Blue Cave on Biševo only burns that deep ultramarine for a stretch mid-morning, so we are out of Komiža early and tied to the concession quay by the mouth. Your own dinghy stays put; the licensed boats run you in for a quick forty minutes, and the queue earns its keep. A short leg on brings you to Ravnik and the Green Cave, lit from a gap in the roof instead of below the surface, and here you take the tender straight in and mostly skip the crowd. Early afternoon the course bends eighteen miles southeast to Vela Luka, a deep harbour on the tail of Korčula with fuel, full services and mud that grabs the anchor first go. Dinner is žrnovski makaruni, rolled out by hand the slow way it is meant to be.
thingsToDo: Line up for the Blue Cave tender before 10:30
Nose the dinghy into Ravnik's Green Cave
Order žrnovski makaruni at a Vela Luka konoba
Walk the Vela Luka waterfront at dusk
5.mooringTip: Vela Luka is stern-to on your own anchor along the town quay, with water, power and a fuel pump at the western end over excellent mud and sand. The bay shuts out everything from the north and east; only a strong southerly nudges any swell into the outer harbour, and the inner pontoon stays quiet.

6.shortDescription: Lastovo is the darkest-skied island in the country and about as cut off as the middle Adriatic gets, fourteen miles south of Vela Luka. Tie up at Zaklopatica, where the konobas lend a buoy to anyone booking a table.
6.description: Lastovo is a Nature Park and roughly the end of the line for the central Adriatic, which is the whole reason it holds onto its quiet through the season. Drop fourteen miles south from Vela Luka and the shore folds open at Zaklopatica, a bay bent like a horseshoe with a low islet lying across its throat to knock the swell down. Five family konobas work the water here, and each keeps a run of buoys it lends free to anyone eating ashore, so the deal writes itself: take a buoy, book the table under it, done for the night. Leave the seabed alone, though, because the seagrass gives poor holding and the park would sooner you did not. The kitchens run on lobster off the traps north of the island and šporki makaruli, a beef ragù cooked down slow over pasta. Do not turn in straight after dinner. Few places are left where the Milky Way reads this sharp.
thingsToDo: Trade dinner for a free konoba buoy
Order Lastovo lobster or šporki makaruli
Walk up to the Lastovo Town fumari chimneys
Stay on deck for the Milky Way
6.mooringTip: The konoba buoys at Zaklopatica are free with a dinner booking, so confirm with the restaurant as you arrive. Holding on the bottom is poor over seagrass; if a southeasterly is forecast above fifteen knots, slip four miles south through the channel to the all-weather cover of Skrivena Luka.

7.shortDescription: Mljet's draw is Polače, a deep walled inlet twenty-two miles northeast, with Roman ruins on the quay and the park's saltwater lakes a short walk inland.
7.description: Twenty-two miles of open water lie between Lastovo and Mljet, and the reward sits at the far end. Polače reveals itself late, a long inlet walled by land on both sides, its opening so understated you are almost in before the water goes glassy, and glassy it stays through the night. Along the shorefront run the remains of a Roman estate from the fourth century, near enough the quay that you can wander the basilica and the corner tower without paying a cent. This is the western way into Mljet National Park, so grab a green park buoy and square up with the ranger, whose fee rolls in the crew's park entry. Half an hour on foot gets you to Veliko Jezero, where a little ferry crosses to a monastery from the twelfth century on its own islet. Circle both lakes and you have walked about nine kilometres with nothing but cicadas for company.
thingsToDo: Walk the fourth-century Roman ruins on the quay
Ferry to St. Mary's monastery on Veliko Jezero
Cycle or hike the nine-kilometre lake loop
Swim in the cooler saltwater lakes
7.mooringTip: Polače uses paid national park buoys, settled with the ranger on the waterfront, and the fee covers park entry for the crew. It is among the best-sheltered bays on the whole south coast, wide open only to the west; the village pontoon also takes a few stern-to boats on their own anchor.

8.shortDescription: The day runs twenty-four miles down the coast to Dubrovnik, but the bed for the night is inland at Komolac up the river, with a bus into the walled Old City.
8.description: Give it a settled morning and the westerly gets up around eleven to carry you the twenty-four miles down to Dubrovnik. The city is the point, but the mooring is well back from it: charter boats lie at ACI Marina Dubrovnik in Komolac, eight kilometres inland up the Rijeka Dubrovačka, where you find lazy lines, fuel and the rest of it. Skip the old harbour on the seafront altogether. It takes day visitors only, lies open to a southerly, and never empties of tour tenders all summer. From the marina the 1A bus or a cab has you at the Pile Gate in under fifteen minutes. The one thing you do not miss is the wall circuit, a shade under two kilometres that soaks up an hour and a half with a few stops. Afterwards drop into the lanes behind Prijeko for dinner, clear of the Stradun scrum.
thingsToDo: Walk the full Old City wall circuit
Ride the cable car up to Mount Srđ
Dine down a Prijeko side lane
Have a drink at Buža bar on the rocks
8.mooringTip: Book ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac ahead in season; it is the only realistic overnight, with lazy lines and full services. Treat the Old Port as a daytime tender stop and nothing else. Reckon on half an hour door to door out to the Pile Gate on the 1A bus or by cab.

9.shortDescription: Okuklje is the deliberate anti-Dubrovnik: twenty miles back up the coast to a townless bay on Mljet's southeast side, outside the park line, no fees, three konobas and their free buoys.
9.description: The way home opens with twenty miles northwest, and Okuklje is picked as everything Dubrovnik is not. It lies beyond the national park line on Mljet's southeast shore, which means no fee, no town and no day-boats, just a pinched crescent that holds maybe twenty yachts and three family konobas dangling their own free buoys. The routine repeats: grab a buoy, get ashore in the tender, settle on a konoba, and hold it by reserving the table. What to order is black risotto, inked dark with cuttlefish and the day's catch, with grilled bream if you want a second plate. Hills a hundred metres high close the bay off on three sides, so whatever is blowing outside, the wind quits at dusk and all you hear after dark is the sea chewing the rocks at the mouth. Take a swim before lunch, then follow the headland track round for the view down toward Korčula.
thingsToDo: Take a free konoba buoy and dine ashore
Order black risotto cooked with cuttlefish ink
Walk the headland path toward Korčula
Swim off the boat in flat water
9.mooringTip: Okuklje's buoys are free once you book a table ashore, so check with the konoba when you pick one up. Bottom holding is patchy in the seagrass; the bay covers you from north, east and south, and only a strong northwesterly works any swell into the mouth.

10.shortDescription: Korčula Town waits fifteen miles up the Pelješac Channel, a walled medieval port laid out on a fishbone plan, best walked once the day ferries clear out.
10.description: Fifteen miles along the Pelješac Channel and Korčula Town is in sight long before you close it, red roofs stacked on a little peninsula behind one bell tower, the old wall dropping sheer into the sea. ACI Marina Korčula lies on the east side of the point, lazy lines and everything laid on. The town quay across on the west sits nearer the gates, but the Maestral finds it and it rolls once the channel breeze builds in the afternoon, so the marina usually wins. You cross the old town in ten minutes flat, its lanes cut in a herringbone that steers the wind through and throws shade down the alleys. Go up St. Mark's tower for the run of the channel, put your head into the house that lays claim to Marco Polo, and save the wander for dusk, once the Split day-ferries have cast off. A fish brodet in a courtyard konoba shuts the day down.
thingsToDo: Climb the bell tower of St. Mark's Cathedral
Walk the herringbone lanes at dusk
Look in on the Marco Polo house
Catch a Moreška sword dance in season
Order fish brodet in a courtyard konoba
10.mooringTip: The east-side ACI Marina Korčula is your any-weather pick, lazy lines and full services included. The west-side town quay sits closer to the gates but opens to the Maestral and starts rolling by two in the afternoon; when the channel builds, run three miles southeast into the shelter of Lumbarda.

11.shortDescription: Jelsa sits on Hvar's north coast, twenty-three miles back over the Hvar Channel, the quiet counterweight to Hvar Town, with the lavender fields behind it and the old Stari Grad Plain nearby.
11.description: Jelsa is the north-coast town on Hvar, third-biggest on the island and a far softer landing than the noise down at Hvar Town. Twenty-three miles over the channel from Korčula drop you into a broad bay that shrugs off the southerlies. Lie stern-to on the town quay on your own anchor over decent mud and sand, or slip onto a lazy line at the little marina east of it if you want power through the night. Farming runs this part of the island. The lavender still climbs the inland road toward Vrboska and Stari Grad and peaks through July, while the konobas down the back lanes pour Bogdanjuša, the local white. Two ways to burn the afternoon sit close by: three miles on the bike along the shore to the canal bridges at Vrboska, or a walk out into the Stari Grad Plain, tilled without a break since Greek settlers pegged out its field walls in 384 BC.
thingsToDo: Bike the coast road to Vrboska's canals
Walk into the UNESCO Stari Grad Plain
Try the local Bogdanjuša white at a konoba
Climb to the Tor watchtower for the view
11.mooringTip: Jelsa's town quay is stern-to on your own anchor with water and power on the central section for a modest fee. The bay is well covered from the south but open to a northerly, which is rare in summer; the small marina on the east side is the lazy-line alternative when you want it.

12.shortDescription: Ten miles north brings a string of empty coves at Lučice, tucked under Brač's white cliffs. Two seasonal restaurants, no town, and the clearest water you will swim in all fortnight.
12.description: Ten miles north over the channel sets you on Brač's south coast at Lučice, a knot of little coves just along from the quarry town of Pučišća. Nobody lives out on the headland. Limestone cliffs and pine drop to the waterline, and the only walls standing are two summer restaurants that hang free buoys for anyone eating in. Take a buoy in the deepest cove, go over the side, and that is the afternoon accounted for. The water is the whole draw: Brač's pale stone floors the seabed, and on a flat day you see twenty metres straight down. The kitchens keep it plain, grilled fish and a jug of Plavac Mali, and with no shop and no bar there is nothing to pull you off the boat. Should the buoys all be gone, the bottom takes an anchor happily in eight to twelve metres of sand and weed.
thingsToDo: Take a restaurant buoy in the central cove
Snorkel the pale limestone seabed
Eat grilled fish with Plavac Mali ashore
Dinghy west to the Pučišća stone yards
12.mooringTip: The buoys at Lučice are free with dinner ashore, so confirm with the restaurant on arrival; if they are full, anchor in eight to twelve metres of sand and weed. The coves are covered from the north and east but open to a southerly, so in a fresh south wind move a mile west into the enclosed harbour at Pučišća.

13.shortDescription: Krknjaši is the last anchorage, a glass-water swim stop between the two Drvenik islands, fifteen miles northwest and a short final hop from base.
13.description: You finish on the hook at Krknjaši, a fifteen-mile run northwest of Lučice into the strait dividing Veli and Mali Drvenik, two empty islets four miles off the Trogir base. This one is a swim stop and nothing more. The bottom is pale sand at three to six metres, the anchor sets first go, and on a still day the whole bay turns turquoise and shallow enough to read the floor. Three restaurants on Veli Drvenik, no road to any of them, put out free buoys, but on a bottom this kind most crews just drop the hook. There is a small chapel on the east shore, a path that rings the headland in thirty minutes, and that is the entire inventory. Stopping here for the last night is pure logistics: Saturday's run back is only four miles east into Kaštela, so nobody is rushed in the morning and the boat comes in fuelled and easy. Sunset over the open sea is the route's quiet goodbye.
thingsToDo: Anchor on sand in four to six metres
Swim the turquoise shallows off the boat
Walk the half-hour headland loop on Veli Drvenik
Eat grilled fish at a Drvenik beach restaurant
13.mooringTip: Krknjaši is a straightforward anchor on excellent sand in four to six metres, which most crews prefer to the free restaurant buoys. The bay is covered from almost every direction except the west and northwest; if a fresh westerly is forecast, run the last four miles east into Marina Kaštela a day early.

14.shortDescription: Just four miles east wraps it up, docking at Marina Kaštela for Saturday's handover, the morning slack enough for a final swim and a coffee.
14.description: The final hop is the shortest of the whole fortnight: four miles east off Krknjaši into Marina Kaštela, between Split and Trogir, where the trip began. The Saturday handover follows a fixed script, boat back alongside by nine, tanks pumped, fuel aboard, decks hosed and inventory ticked off, and someone left on board for the walk-through between eight and nine. Kaštela ranks among the largest marinas on the Adriatic and reads clearly from any approach, its control tower rising over the breakwater. Once the fuel is in and the papers are signed, the airport is a quarter of an hour away and central Split around twenty-five minutes. Most crews fill the gap the same way, an unhurried loop through Diocletian's Palace and a coffee on the Riva before the car comes. Two weeks and better than two hundred miles behind you, that last one goes down well.
thingsToDo: Top up fuel before the 9am inspection
Walk Diocletian's Palace one last time
Take a final swim at Bačvice beach
Have a long coffee on the Riva
14.mooringTip: Bring the boat back into Marina Kaštela as your contract specifies, and the base manager will point you to the slot. The handover window is eight to nine on Saturday morning; if your contract asks for a night-before return, aim to be in by five on Friday. The fuel pontoon is on the western entrance side.

kastela-kornati-krka-route.metaDesc: A 7-day sail from Kaštela north into Kornati National Park and up the Krka River to Skradin, looping back via Primošten and Rogoznica.

1.shortDescription: A short four-mile hop west from the base into a shallow sand-bottomed bay where the crew learns the boat before the real sailing starts.
1.description: Day one is deliberately gentle. We motor four miles out of Marina Kaštela into the strait dividing Veli Drvenik from its smaller sister, and drop the hook where the water shades from deep blue to a pale green over clean sand. Locals call this the Blue Lagoon, and on a still afternoon the name earns itself. Depths run three to six metres, holding is reliable, and the seabed is soft enough to forgive a sloppy set. A trio of little restaurants sits on the shore, each laying free buoys for anyone booking dinner, though most crews simply anchor and swim. There is a chapel on the far side and a footpath that rings the headland in half an hour. Nothing here demands your attention, which is exactly the point on a first night. Use the light air to practise reefing and check the sail trim before turning north.
thingsToDo: Anchor over sand in the Blue Lagoon
Swim the shallows between the two Drvenik islands
Walk the half-hour loop past the chapel
Book a shore table for grilled fish
Practise reefing in the afternoon breeze
1.mooringTip: Anchor in three to six metres on sand that holds well; it is the standard call here. Restaurant buoys are free with dinner, but the bay opens to the west, so if a westerly above 18 knots is forecast, slip two miles into ACI Trogir instead.

2.shortDescription: Fourteen miles up the coast to a terracotta old town wrapped around a hilltop church, with an all-weather marina just south for the night.
2.description: The run to Primošten is fourteen miles of open water, past Rogoznica which you will visit on the way home. Primošten reads clearly from a few miles out: a knuckle of land tied to the mainland by a thin sandy neck, red roofs stacked up to the church of St. George at the summit. The old town has stayed a working place rather than a resort, and its narrow lanes hide a handful of konobas worth the walk. Overnight, take ACI Marina Kremik two miles south, where lazy lines and a shuttle bus handle the logistics; the town quay is a daytime affair only. Above the houses climb the Bukovac terraces, dry-stone vineyards that yield the local Babić red. Come evening, take a glass of it on the quayside; the coastal whites do not come close.
thingsToDo: Climb to St. George's church at the peak
Taste Babić red from the Bukovac terraces
Wander the lanes for a konoba dinner
Swim off Velika Raduča beach
Ride the shuttle in from Marina Kremik
2.mooringTip: Kremik, two miles south, is the reliable overnight: lazy lines, fuel, showers, and a bus into town. The town quay only works for a daytime stop and sits open to the west. If you take it in settled weather, clear the berth by five before the fishing boats come back.

3.shortDescription: Twenty miles northwest into Kornati, a maze of nearly ninety bare islands and the only marina inside the national park.
3.description: Nothing else on the Croatian coast looks like Kornati, and the twenty miles northwest are the price of admission. It is a scatter of some ninety islands, almost none inhabited, stripped down to bare limestone and the dry-stone walls that shepherds ran up their spines centuries ago. The overnight is ACI Marina Piškera, sitting on the islet of Panitula Vela and the only berth inside the park. It runs 150 places with lazy lines, water, power, and one restaurant perched on the rocks above. Pay the park fee online before you arrive; it is cheaper than buying it at the kiosk, and dearer in high summer. With the boat secured, the afternoon is for the dinghy. Motor across to snorkel the channel toward Lavsa, or point south to the crowns, the sheer cliffs of Mana and Kornat where the rock falls straight into deep water. Sundown over the outer islands is the reason people keep coming back.
thingsToDo: Buy the park ticket online before arrival
Snorkel the channel toward Lavsa
Dinghy to the cliffs of Mana and Kornat
Dine at the marina restaurant above the rocks
Walk the karst spine of Panitula Vela
3.mooringTip: Book Piškera online for July and August; every berth has lazy lines. Overnight anchoring inside the park is limited to a few bays, Lavsa, Lojena, Vrulje and Statival among them, where konobas lay free buoys with dinner. Ranger boats patrol, so pay your entry either way.

4.shortDescription: Fifteen miles east out of Kornati to a car-free island with one village and seven centuries of red-coral diving in its past.
4.description: Zlarin lies fifteen miles east of the park, one working village on the eastern shore and no second settlement to speak of. The island made its name on red coral. Divers here worked the offshore beds for something like seven hundred years, and a small museum by the square keeps the old gear, the weighted ropes and the frame cages they lowered by hand before engines took over. Tie stern-to on the village quay with your own anchor; the fee is modest and the middle berths have water and power. No cars run on Zlarin. The lanes are for walking, the pine and cypress on the western slopes give up a shady hour, and the konobas keep it simple with whatever came off the boats that morning. Later, the harbour wall does the work of a bar: a few crews, the fishermen, and the cicadas going flat out in the dark.
thingsToDo: See the coral gear at the village museum
Walk the pine slopes on the western side
Swim off the rocks south of the village
Eat the day's catch at a harbour konoba
Buy hand-made coral jewellery in the village
4.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Zlarin quay with your own anchor works well; central berths have water and power for a small fee. The bay covers you from the west and north but stands open to a southeasterly. If one over 15 knots is forecast, run four miles up to Šibenik and tuck into ACI Mandalina.

5.shortDescription: A rare twelve-mile passage upriver past Šibenik and through a rock-cut channel into the freshwater heart of Krka and its waterfalls.
5.description: This is the leg crews remember. Twelve miles take you off the sea entirely, up the Krka estuary past the old city of Šibenik, through the St. Anthony channel, a 600-metre slot cut in the rock with plenty of air under the bridge for any charter rig. Beyond it the river widens into a deep, hill-lined reach that runs fresh and green up to ACI Marina Skradin. The marina puts you at the doorstep of Krka National Park. Pay at the kiosk on the seawall, then take the park boat, which leaves every half hour in season and rides fifteen minutes upstream to Skradinski Buk. Skradinski Buk itself is seventeen travertine ledges stacked into a forty-six-metre fall, with a boardwalk threading both banks and the pools caught between them. Back at the quay, Skradin cooks its own dish, a slow beef risotto stained with saffron that keeps a cook busy for hours, washed down with the local Babić.
thingsToDo: Ride the park boat to Skradinski Buk
Loop the boardwalk over the seventeen cascades
Order Skradin risotto with the local Babić
Climb the lookout above the falls
Walk the medieval streets behind the marina
5.mooringTip: ACI Skradin gives you lazy lines and full services, with a fuel pontoon at the river-mouth end. The channel in is well buoyed and deep for any draft, but budget ninety minutes at displacement speed from Šibenik. There is no anchoring in the park stretch, so it is the marina or back downriver.

6.shortDescription: Twenty miles back down to the sea and along the coast to a peninsula town guarding a tidal saltwater lake called the Dragon's Eye.
6.description: Retrace the river, then bear southeast down twenty miles of open coast to Rogoznica. It stands on a low peninsula, roughly halfway from Šibenik to Split. The southern tip holds the Dragon's Eye, a saltwater pool trapped in the karst that the sea refills through a buried channel on every tide, twice a day. At low water the surface sits several metres below the surrounding sea, ringed by pale cliffs, and a signed path from town reaches it in twenty minutes. Marina Frapa handles the night from the south side of the peninsula, one of the biggest bases in central Dalmatia, with lazy lines, fuel, a hotel and a run of restaurants. Smaller boats can go stern-to on the north quay, though it catches the northwesterly and the marina is the safer bet in season. The town itself stays small: a fishing fleet, a few konobas, an old quarter on the point.
thingsToDo: Walk the path to the Dragon's Eye
Look down at the lake from the cliffs
Eat brudet at an old-quarter konoba
Wander the headland's old stone quarter
Dinghy to Smokvica islet for a swim
6.mooringTip: Marina Frapa on the south side is the all-weather choice, with lazy lines, fuel at the entrance and services on tap. The north quay takes stern-to with your own anchor for a small fee, but it is open to the northwest; move round to Frapa if a northwesterly over 15 knots shows on the forecast.

7.shortDescription: A last fifteen miles home past Čiovo's swimming bays, timed for a final dip before the Saturday nine o'clock handover at base.
7.description: Fifteen miles southeast closes the loop back to Marina Kaštela. You track the south side of Čiovo the whole way in, the long island that shelters Trogir, and its bays hand you a last swim: Slatine, Saldun or Mavarstica, each a short nudge off the rhumb line. Keep an eye on the clock, though. Saturday handovers run tight, and the boat needs to be back by nine with the tanks pumped, the fuel topped and the decks rinsed, ready for the base check between eight and nine. The run into Kaštela is simple in any light; the marina's control tower stands out from well offshore. Once the inspection clears you are fifteen minutes from Split airport. If the flight allows, spend the last hours in Trogir, a five-minute walk from the berth, where the UNESCO old town and the carved Radovan portal on the cathedral earn a slow lunch on the Riva before the transfer.
thingsToDo: Take a last swim in Saldun bay
Top up fuel before the base check
See the Radovan portal on Trogir cathedral
Walk Trogir's UNESCO old town
Linger over lunch on the Trogir Riva
7.mooringTip: Return to Marina Kaštela as your contract specifies; the base manager assigns the slot. The Saturday check runs from eight to nine, so arrive by five on Friday if your paperwork calls for a night-before return. The fuel pontoon is on the western side of the entrance.

kastela-vis-korcula-hvar-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Vis, Hvar & Korčula Yacht Loop | Boat4You
kastela-vis-korcula-hvar-route.metaDesc: Seven-day yacht loop from Kaštela taking in Šolta, Vis, the Blue Cave, Korčula, the Pakleni Islands, Hvar and Brač, with distances and mooring notes.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 13-mile hop across the Split Channel to Maslinica, Šolta's one real harbour, so the crew settles in before the longer days south.
1.description: We usually cast off from Marina Kaštela mid-morning and lay a course southwest, thirteen miles across to the western tip of Šolta. Maslinica opens up behind a scatter of low islets that knock the worst of any swell down before it reaches the inlet, so the water inside sits calm even when it's blowing outside. The village is small and unhurried. Baroque Martinis Marchi castle anchors the south shore, restored now into a marina and hotel, while a handful of konobas line the square and pour Šolta's own olive oil and the local Dobričić red. It's the closest island to Split yet somehow skips the day-boat crowds. Late afternoon, walk the shore path or dinghy over to the entrance islets for a swim off the rocks. An early dinner ashore sets you up for the open crossing tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Tour the Martinis Marchi castle grounds
Try Dobričić, Šolta's native red wine
Swim off the rocks at Stipanska islet
Buy Šoltansko olive oil in the village
Catch the sunset from the entrance islets
1.mooringTip: On the south side, Martinis Marchi carries full services and laid lines; book it for July and August. Its village quay is stern-to on your own hook for a modest charge, and the ring of islets keeps the bay well protected in all but a rare northerly.

2.shortDescription: The week's longest open-water leg, 24 miles south past Brač into Komiža, the salt-worn fishing town on the far west of Vis.
2.description: Point the bow south and get comfortable: Komiža sits a full twenty-four miles off, and there's little to do but hold the course. Brač slides past to port, the Hvar Channel falls away astern, and no other day this week keeps you out on open water this long. Vis stayed a Yugoslav military zone until 1989, and the lack of resort sprawl still shows. Komiža wraps a small bay on the island's western shore, a proper working port with nets drying on the quay and fishing boats rafted three deep. The old Venetian tower holds the Fishermen's Museum, worth an afternoon hour. For dinner, Jastožera serves lobster in a converted lobster-pot house over the water, and the konobas nearby grill the day's catch alongside the island's Vugava white. Stay for the sunset. The horizon runs unbroken west toward Italy, and Komiža does that hour better than most.
thingsToDo: Eat lobster at Jastožera over the water
See the Fishermen's Museum in the Venetian tower
Order grilled fish with a glass of Vugava
Hike Mount Hum for the island panorama
Watch the sun drop toward Italy
2.mooringTip: Berth stern-to with your own anchor in the inner basin off the town quay; the bottom is sand and weed with mostly reliable holding, and there's a harbour fee. The outer wall catches southwest swell and isn't safe overnight, so if a strong SW is forecast, slip six miles north to sheltered Vis Town in St. George's Bay.

3.shortDescription: Blue Cave at opening time, coffee back aboard, and by late afternoon Vela Luka's huge natural harbour swallows the boat whole.
3.description: Everything hinges on the light. The sun only floods the Blue Cave on Biševo with that deep blue glow between roughly nine and eleven in the morning, so we time the day around it. Tie up at the little concession quay outside, pay at the kiosk, and wait for an official tender; private dinghies stay out. Reckon on forty minutes all in. Then it's eighteen miles southeast to Vela Luka, tucked into the western end of Korčula. The long town quay has fuel, water and power, and the bay is among the best-protected on the south coast, shrugging off wind from the north and east. Dinner ashore leans on žrnovski makaruni, the island's hand-rolled pasta, and whatever came off the boats that morning. Walk up to the Vela Spila cave above town if you've the legs for it.
thingsToDo: Join the official Blue Cave tender early
Order žrnovski makaruni in a Vela Luka konoba
Climb to the prehistoric Vela Spila cave
Stroll the Vela Luka waterfront at dusk
3.mooringTip: Vela Luka takes stern-to on your own anchor along the town quay, with water, power and a fuel pump at the western end and excellent holding in mud and sand. The bay is shut off from north, northeast and east; only a hard southerly nudges any swell inside, and the inner pontoon still stays comfortable.

4.shortDescription: The week's long leg, 30 miles east-northeast to the Pakleni anchorage of Palmižana, with the afternoon Maestral filling in on the beam.
4.description: Set the alarm early for this one. Thirty miles east-northeast is the biggest single haul of the week, and you want to be well clear of the Korčula Channel by early afternoon, when the westerly Maestral starts to build. Play it right and that wind swings onto the beam, driving you into the Pakleni cluster at a proper clip. Palmižana lies on Sv. Klement, the only islet here with a marina, and its slots fill fast in high summer, so book ahead or plan to anchor. The Meneghello family's botanical garden, laid out back in 1906, is the classic wander, and Zori and the other konobas around Vinogradišće handle dinner. Snorkellers should look for the scattered Roman amphorae off the western shore. A water taxi runs across to Hvar Town if the crew wants a livelier night.
thingsToDo: Wander Meneghello's 1906 botanical garden
Dine at Zori above Vinogradišće cove
Snorkel the Roman amphorae off the west shore
Take the water taxi to Hvar Town
Walk across Sv. Klement to Vlaka
4.mooringTip: ACI Marina Palmižana offers stern-to lazy lines, but summer capacity is tight, so book online well ahead. If it's full, anchor in Vinogradišće on the south side with a line ashore; the holding in sand and weed is fair and the cove sits better out of the afternoon westerly than the main bay, which turns rolly once the Maestral funnels through.

5.shortDescription: A short 12-mile hop round western Hvar into Stari Grad, Croatia's oldest town and gateway to the UNESCO-listed plain behind it.
5.description: After yesterday's slog, twelve easy miles feels like a rest day. Round the western end of Hvar and run down a long, fjord-like inlet to Stari Grad. Greek colonists from Paros founded the place as Pharos in 384 BC, and their street grid still lines the old centre. Behind town spreads the Stari Grad Plain, a UNESCO landscape whose field boundaries have been worked continuously for well over two thousand years. If you head indoors anywhere, make it Tvrdalj Castle, the poet Petar Hektorović's fortified house built around a saltwater fishpond he stocked with mullet. Best of the afternoon is to rent a bike and roll east through the plain, past the drystone walls and lavender rows, or simply lose an hour in the medieval lanes. The bay itself stays calm in most weather, one of the surest overnights on Hvar.
thingsToDo: Cycle the UNESCO Stari Grad Plain
Visit Tvrdalj Castle and its fishpond
Walk the ancient Greek street grid
Order peka at a plain konoba, booked ahead
5.mooringTip: The town quay takes stern-to on your own anchor for a modest fee, with water and power along the central stretch. For an all-weather berth, ACI Marina Stari Grad on the east side has lazy lines. The inlet is deep and shut in on every side, so wind direction barely matters here.

6.shortDescription: Twelve miles north over the Brač Channel to Milna, the snuggest harbour on the island's west coast and an easy set-up for the run home.
6.description: Milna sits twelve miles north across the Brač Channel, a long, protected cut into the island's west coast and a safe haven since Venetian days. You'll pick out the seventeenth-century parish church on the inner quay from well offshore, stone houses stacked up the slope behind it. Berth stern-to on your own anchor along the inner quay, or drop the hook in the outer half over sand and run a stern line to the rocks if the Maestral gets up. The island trades on its white limestone; Diocletian's Palace was raised from it, and the quarries at Pučišća up the coast are still cutting it today. The other local institution is peka, lamb slow-baked under an iron bell, so order it the minute you're tied up. Then walk out to the headland church and wander back for a slow dinner.
thingsToDo: Order lamb peka three hours ahead
Spot the 17th-century church from offshore
Anchor and line ashore in the outer bay
Dinghy north to Bobovišća for a quiet swim
6.mooringTip: Stern-to on the inner quay with your own anchor, small fee, water and power mid-quay. The outer bay is free anchoring with a stern line to the rocks. It's open from southwest to west, so if the afternoon Maestral climbs above fifteen knots the inner quay is the calmer bet; Bobovišća, three miles north, is the fallback.

7.shortDescription: A 15-mile morning run back across the Split Channel to Marina Kaštela for the Saturday handover, fuelled up and decks rinsed by nine.
7.description: Home stretch, and a short one. Fifteen miles east-northeast across the open Split Channel drops you back where the week started. Charter Saturdays run to a script: boat alongside by nine with tanks topped, holding pumped out, decks hosed and inventory ticked off, and the skipper on hand for the handover check between eight and nine. The marina fills the shoreline between Split and Trogir, and you'll spot its control tower long before you pick out the breakwater. Once the paperwork's signed you're fifteen minutes from the airport and twenty-five from the old town. There's a reason this loop works so well over seven days: it strings together Vis, Korčula and Hvar without the forced march you'd need to reach Dubrovnik and get back. Send the week off with a long lunch on the Trogir riva or inside Diocletian's Palace.
thingsToDo: Top up fuel and pump out before nine
Take a last swim at Bačvice beach
Wander Diocletian's Palace before the transfer
Stand a long lunch on Trogir riva
7.mooringTip: Return her to Marina Kaštela as your charter contract states; the base crew will point out the slot. The window runs eight to nine on the Saturday, though a few contracts want a Friday-evening return by five. You will find the fuel pontoon over on the entrance's western side.

pomer-14days.metaDesc: Two weeks from Pomer, Istria: Susak sands, the Lošinj channels, NP Kornati, the Krka falls, Zadar's Sea Organ and car-free Silba. Mooring tips inside.

1.shortDescription: Check in at ACI Marina Pomer, run the boat over with the base, load the lockers, then slip just three miles out to anchor in Medulin bay. A deliberately soft start, with time to shake down the systems and get the crew talking before the long hop to Susak tomorrow.
1.description: Nobody remembers day one for the sailing, and that is fine. The morning goes on forms, a walk round the catamaran with the base crew, and a serious shop in Medulin or Pula, because you will not see a supermarket this size again for two weeks; load for seven days and refill as chances come. Once the briefing wraps, it is a three-mile motor over Medulin bay, shallow and screened by a scatter of islets, about as gentle a place as exists to test the windlass and take the first swim. Off to the south the pines of Cape Kamenjak run out to the end of Istria. You meet that headland twice more, outbound in the morning and again on the way home, so take a good look now. Spend the evening on the dull essentials: engine hours, fuel logged, a watch and galley rota agreed, and a hard read of the Kvarner forecast, because tomorrow starts early.
thingsToDo: Do the big provisioning run in Medulin or Pula
Run the crew through an anchoring and windlass drill
Swim off the transom while dinner cooks
Walk the coast trails of Lower Kamenjak
1.mooringTip: Drop the hook in 3 to 5 m over sand in Medulin bay, using the islets as a windbreak on whichever side is blowing. If you would rather not move, keep the ACI Pomer berth for the night and cast off at first light.

2.shortDescription: Now the trip earns its name. Twenty-four miles of open Kvarner separate Medulin from Susak, so clear Cape Kamenjak in the morning quiet and hold a course to the southeast. Four hours out, the most peculiar island of the whole fortnight climbs out of the haze.
2.description: Get the anchor up while the Kvarner is still asleep. The channel behaves in the early hours; leave it till afternoon and the wind puts a nasty little sea between Istria and the islands. Give Cape Kamenjak plenty of offing on the way out, since the shoals off the point trip up even in light air. From there you are committed to open water for the best part of four hours, the Lošinj hills lifting a fraction higher off the bow each time you look up. Then Susak, which fits nowhere in the usual picture of this coast: a heap of sand and windblown loess almost a hundred metres tall, laced together by reeds and stepped vineyards, with none of the grey rock that armours every other island. Feel your way in from the north where the ground rises kindly, keeping the leadline honest, because the shallows run a long way out. Ashore, the walk up to Gornje Selo pays back every step with the whole terraced bowl laid out below.
thingsToDo: Hike up to Gornje Selo for the terrace view
Feel real sand underfoot at Spiaza beach
Try Trojišćina, the wine off Susak's sand terraces
Swim off the shallow shelf on the north shore
2.mooringTip: That little mole shoals quickly and there is rarely room, so crews mostly lie at anchor in Bok bay or along the north shore in 3 to 6 m of sand. It bites well, but there is nothing between you and a northerly, so clear out if bura is in the forecast.

3.shortDescription: Seven miles, and gentle ones, curling round the bottom of Lošinj to Ilovik. You will be moored inside the hour, so the day belongs to the flower-lined channel and the water, not the chartplotter.
3.description: Call today a reward for yesterday: seven miles, a bit over an hour, and no reason on earth to rush any of it. Have your morning swim off Susak, then potter south along the low islets strung off the bottom of Lošinj. Ilovik and the islet of Sveti Petar sit either side of a slim channel, and that channel is basically the town, doubling as harbour, anchorage and evening promenade all at once. Grab one of the numbered buoys in the middle and run the tender in. They call the place the island of flowers and mean it, with oleander, palms and gnarled eucalyptus crowding the front of nearly every house. On foot, the low saddle takes you over to Paržine, a proper sandy beach on the southeast shore; by dinghy, Sveti Petar offers an old Benedictine site and a cemetery tucked in the pines. Dinner is whatever the boats brought in, grilled at a konoba on the waterfront.
thingsToDo: Pick up a numbered buoy in the channel
Hike over the saddle to Paržine beach
Dinghy across to the Benedictine ruins on Sveti Petar
Eat grilled fish at Konoba Oliva
3.mooringTip: Take one of the eighty-odd numbered buoys strung down the channel; you settle up ashore when you eat. A cable runs along the bottom and the swinging room is next to nothing, so in July and August turn up early rather than hoping to drop the hook.

4.shortDescription: Passage day again. The Maknare channel points you off toward Dugi Otok, Molat sliding by to port, roughly three hours all told. At the end sits Brbinj, a drowsy pair of coves ringed with olive woods.
4.description: Here the water changes owners. Twenty miles carry you out of the Kvarner and into the Zadar archipelago. Point southeast out of Ilovik for the Maknare channel, the slot between Molat and the top corner of Dugi Otok. Coming up on it, look to starboard among the pines for the Veli Rat lighthouse, over forty metres of it and the tallest on this coast, throwing its light since 1849. Once you are through, Dugi Otok's long backbone keeps you company for the next two days. Brbinj hides in a twin bay, its pair of coves parted by a wooded spur and hemmed with olive trees old enough to have seen empires come and go. Next to nothing happens here, and that is the reason to come: swim off the stern, follow the path through the groves, and watch the Zadar ferry work its timetable. Buy only what you must ashore, as the shop runs to the modest side.
thingsToDo: Frame the Veli Rat lighthouse from the channel
Swim between Brbinj's two sheltered coves
Follow the shore path through the old olives
Watch the Zadar ferry from the cockpit at dusk
4.mooringTip: Grab a buoy in Lučina, the southern of the two coves, or lay the hook in 4 to 8 m of sand and mud, which holds well. Give the ferry pier a wide margin so you are never in the way when it swings.

5.shortDescription: Twelve miles of lazy coasting down the green side of Dugi Otok, past hamlet after hamlet, bring you to Zaglav, one bay short of Sali and home to the island's lone fuel berth. Two hours, no drama.
5.description: You spend the whole day in Dugi Otok's shadow, hugging the northeast coast for a dozen easy miles. The sheltered flank is a row of small places, Savar and Luka and Žman, each just a few houses gathered round a church at the water, and the navigation asks nothing more than a sensible distance off. Zaglav earns its keep and still has charm. The concrete quay here feeds the only fuel pump on the island, so brim the tanks; the next simple fill-up is several days down the line. Behind the harbour, a fifteenth-century Franciscan house keeps watch from the slope. If the evening wants more life, Sali, the island's biggest village and a fishing port with a thousand years on it, is a half-hour stroll or a short dinghy ride to the south. Tomorrow's Kornati crossing is brief, so no early alarm is needed, but water and fuel taken on tonight buy you a slow morning.
thingsToDo: Fill the tanks at Zaglav's lone pump
Walk up to the St Michael Franciscan house
Stroll south to Sali's old fishing port
Buy island olive oil straight from a grower
5.mooringTip: Room on the village quay is tight, so come in bow or stern-to by mid-afternoon, or else lie at anchor off it in 4 to 6 m. The fuel berth is strictly for fuelling; move away the moment the tanks are full.

6.shortDescription: Round the foot of Dugi Otok and steer fourteen miles into the back rows of the Kornati, past Telašćica's entrance and over to Žut. A couple of hours put you among bare rock and terraces at ACI Marina Žut, the last berth outside the park.
6.description: Clear the bottom end of Dugi Otok and the whole scene swaps out. The olive greens fall away behind, and in front the Kornati stand up out of the water, stone ridges the colour of dust and old gold. The fourteen-mile run cuts across the throat of Telašćica, that enormous natural harbour at the island's foot, a nature park in its own right with laid buoys and well worth stopping in for lunch if the clock is kind. After that it is a straight hop over to Žut. Second-biggest of the whole chain, Žut lies just outside the park line, so tonight costs you no ticket. There is no village here and not a drop of fresh water, only rock, scrub, olive terraces still worked by Murter families, and a handful of konobas that open for the season. ACI Marina Žut runs the season from the back of a broad bay, lazy lines laid on, a restaurant terrace over the pontoons. Stay up for it: the ridges burn copper as the sun drops, and nowhere are the Kornati emptier.
thingsToDo: Swing into Telašćica for a swim under the cliffs
Hike the ridge above the marina
Eat lamb or fresh-caught fish at a Žut konoba
Book NP Kornati tickets online before bed
6.mooringTip: Being seasonal and much in demand, ACI Marina Žut wants a booking well ahead in July and August. With no fresh water on the island, both water and power come rationed; the lines are already laid for you.

7.shortDescription: The set-piece of the fortnight. Fifteen miles carry you clean through NP Kornati and spit you out at Kaprije in Šibenik water, with Kornat and its islets sliding past on the way. Keep the tickets handy for the ranger boat, and leave the day loose enough for a swim or two.
7.description: Below Žut the real park begins, and the rules bite: some ninety islands, islets and reefs with hardly a tree standing, and a ticket required by law for every boat afloat. Sort yours online before you leave, well under what the ranger launch will charge. This is a day to dawdle. The Kornatski kanal threads down the flank of Kornat, the island that lends the group its name, past dry-stone walls stitched across the hillsides and the lone huts Murter farmers still keep out here. High over Tarac cove, the Byzantine fort of Tureta has held its watch since the sixth century, fourteen hundred years of it. Anchor where the wind lets you for a swim and a bite; the water over those pale seabeds hardly looks real. Come mid-afternoon, slip past the southern rim of the park and cross into Šibenik's islands. Kaprije rounds off the day: one village, a real fishing fleet, no cars worth mentioning, and a bay that goes still not long after dark.
thingsToDo: Run the Kornatski kanal under Tureta fort
Drop anchor for a swim over pale seabed
Pick out the Murter farmers' dry-stone walls
Amble Kaprije's car-free waterfront
7.mooringTip: There are buoys off Kaprije and space to anchor in 5 to 8 m of sand that holds dependably; if the bay is full or the wind swings, drop back into deep Medoš bay. Buy your park ticket online first, since the on-the-water price stings.

8.shortDescription: The knockout leg, fourteen miles of it. You thread the whole approach in one go, sea-fort to city to lake to river canyon, and by nightfall you are tied up at ACI Marina Skradin with fresh water under the hulls.
8.description: This is the arrival everyone remembers. A short reach off Kaprije brings the mainland close, and the way into Šibenik's inner water is marked by St Nicholas Fortress, a low Venetian sea-fort of the sixteenth century, now on the UNESCO roll. Past it the St Anthony channel pinches in between pine banks, and since ships use it too, keep to the right and keep your eyes up. Then the town spreads out, the cathedral dome and the piled old quarter off to starboard. Hold on north beneath the road bridge, over the wide brackish sheet of Prokljan lake, into the last forested kinks of the Krka. Skradin waits at the top: a small old town under the falls with ACI Marina Skradin along the bank. The water has gone nearly sweet by now, an odd treat after seven days of brine. Before you eat, book the first park boat for the morning.
thingsToDo: Slip past St Nicholas Fortress into the channel
Spot Šibenik's UNESCO cathedral from the deck
Pre-order Skradin's slow-cooked risotto
Book NP Krka tickets and the morning boat
8.mooringTip: Sitting up the river, ACI Marina Skradin is sheltered from every quarter but heavily booked in summer, so reserve early. Failing that, anchor a little downstream of town in 5 to 10 m of mud, with an eye on the current and the tripper-boat lane.

9.shortDescription: Nothing to sail today, and that is the point. The park boat carries you up to Skradinski Buk, where the Krka tumbles down a staircase of travertine, while the catamaran sits quietly in Skradin. Boardwalks, mills and viewpoints fill the hours; the log stays blank.
9.description: Zero on the log today, and the skipper draws a day off as well. Park boats leave Skradin's front and run up the last stretch of river no keel can manage, to Skradinski Buk, the Krka's grand closing cascade: seventeen travertine steps spilling some forty-five metres down a wooded gorge. The ride comes with the park ticket. In high summer, take the earliest boat and the boardwalks are all but yours. Give it two or three hours; the walkways lace over the rapids and pools, viewpoints line the gorge, and old restored mills show how the water once ran the local economy. Swimming under the Buk has been off the table since 2021, so pack walking shoes rather than towels. You are back in Skradin by afternoon with time on your hands, enough for the fortress viewpoint, an ice cream by the river, and a proper sit-down dinner.
thingsToDo: Catch the first boat up to Skradinski Buk
Loop the boardwalks past the old watermills
Climb to Turina fortress over Skradin
Add the Visovac island monastery excursion
9.mooringTip: Keep the ACI Skradin berth for a second night; it beats casting off and hunting a new one. Get the Krka tickets online the evening before, and note the Skradin park boat is already covered by them.

10.shortDescription: Back down the Krka you go, ten miles retracing yesterday's water to St Nicholas Fortress, then a quick northwest hop to Tribunj. The village crowds onto its own little islet; the marina alongside takes the boat.
10.description: Taking the Krka canyon the other way is no chore. Light drops into the gorge from a fresh angle in the morning, and Šibenik's waterfront gives you a slow, easy pass on the way through. Once St Nicholas Fortress is behind you, swing northwest for the short leg to Tribunj, ten miles or so out of Skradin. The old part of town is jammed onto a scrap of islet linked by a stone bridge, fishing boats rafted three-deep along both quays, and this is still one of the harder-working small fleets around Šibenik.
