Tribunj has never lost its soft spot for donkeys, once the muscle of these parched hills, and races them every summer. Berth in the marina by the village, then climb the little St Nicholas rise behind it for a sunset thrown across the outer islands. Fresh fish for dinner is not a choice here so much as a duty.
thingsToDo: Walk the stone bridge onto Tribunj's islet
Climb St Nicholas hill for the sunset
Watch the fleet unload its catch on the quay
Order the day's fish, priced by the kilo
10.mooringTip: Marina Tribunj is right on the village doorstep, lazy lines and every service laid on. The old quay is the fishermen's, so the marina is really your only berth; ring ahead when the season peaks.

11.shortDescription: The longest single run of the back nine days, and one of the gentlest. Twenty-five sheltered miles by the Murter Sea and Pašman channel land you in Zadar; roll in before dark and the Sea Organ plays you into the evening.
11.description: Today links up some of the calmest water on the whole coast. Out of Tribunj the course runs northwest over the Murter Sea. Leave Murter and bridge-linked Tisno to landward and feel your way into the Pašman channel, a long green trough between the mainland and the twin islands of Pašman and Ugljan. Watch the marked fairway: the channel shoals in places and the tide sets through it harder than it does anywhere close by. Biograd's front and a constant dribble of local traffic carry you through the middle of the day. Zadar shows first as a knot of bell-towers on a walled spit of land. Berth over the water from the old town and hand the evening to the city: the Roman forum, the round ninth-century church of St Donatus, and the Riva at dusk, where the Sea Organ works the swell into slow chords and the Greeting to the Sun glows underfoot. See how the sunset stacks up for yourself.
thingsToDo: Hear the Sea Organ play the evening swell
Stand over the lit Greeting to the Sun
Round the Roman forum and St Donatus
Load up at Zadar's markets
11.mooringTip: Marina Zadar faces the old-town peninsula head-on and puts catamarans on lazy lines, so reserve in summer; ferries and trip boats own the city quay. If it is full, D-Marin Borik lies two miles to the northwest.

12.shortDescription: A half-day northwest, roughly four hours and twenty-four miles, ends at car-free Silba by way of Ugljan's villages and the last low islands. Save the afternoon for the sandy coves and the Toreta climb.
12.description: Once you shake off Zadar's channel, the run bends northwest past Ugljan and Rivanj, the sea thinning of islands as you go, until Sestrunj, Molat and finally Silba pull themselves into focus. Silba is the sort of island they simply do not build now: not a single car, no hotel worth the name, and through July and August even a bicycle is unwelcome in the village lanes. The one village straddles a thin waist of land with a landing to either hand, Mul on the east, Žalić on the west, so a lee is always going somewhere. Over the roofs rises the Toreta, a nineteenth-century six-sided tower with its stair spiralling up the outside, put there by a sea captain and bound up in an old tale of love and waiting. Climb it and the northern archipelago fans out beneath you. After that, do as the island does: sandy coves, pine shade, and a village dinner eaten at a walk, because Silba has no faster gear.
thingsToDo: Climb the Toreta's outer spiral stair
Swim off the sand at Šotorišće
Wander the car-free village lanes
Dinghy to an empty cove on the west shore
12.mooringTip: Pick up a buoy at either Mul or Žalić, whichever the wind favours, since swell reaches both; or drop the hook in Sveti Ante bay just south of the village, sand in 4 to 8 m. There is no marina on Silba.

13.shortDescription: This is the leap between the Zadar and Kvarner island groups. Twenty miles of open water bring Silba's crowd across to Veli Lošinj and its thimble of a harbour under the pastel houses, three hours in which a dolphin sighting is very much on the cards.
13.description: The hop from Silba over to Lošinj runs through some of the finest dolphin water anywhere in the Adriatic. A resident pod of bottlenose lives out here, followed for better than thirty-five years by the Blue World Institute up in Veli Lošinj, so put someone on watch the moment you are in open sea. Lošinj's southeast face comes up steep and green. Sail made this island rich, and by the eighteen-hundreds the captains of Veli Lošinj were raising the tall pastel houses that still elbow for room around its ridiculously small harbour. The entrance is about one boat wide, the pink church of St Anthony standing guard over the quay. Inside is cramped, so most crews prefer neighbouring Rovenska, sheltered behind its old breakwater and a ten-minute walk off. Ashore, climb the lanes to the Venetian tower of 1455, a small museum these days.
thingsToDo: Keep watch for the resident bottlenose pod
Tour the 1455 Venetian tower museum
Walk the coast path in from Rovenska
Take a coffee below St Anthony's church
13.mooringTip: Most catamarans will not fit the inner harbour at Veli Lošinj, so take Rovenska instead, either the quay or the buoys tucked behind the breakwater. Everything but a southeasterly leaves it calm, and the coast path walks you round to the village.

14.shortDescription: Homeward, and it is the biggest single passage of the fortnight, twenty-seven miles of open Kvarner from Veli Lošinj. Round Cape Kamenjak, ease into Medulin bay, and finish at ACI Marina Pomer. Leave early and keep an eye to windward so you are alongside in good time.
14.description: Keep something in reserve for the last day, because it is the longest single passage of the lot and almost all of it is open water. Slip out of Veli Lošinj early, round the island's southern tip and run up the outer coast past Unije, then lay off across the Kvarner for the point of Istria, the same water you crossed going south on day two, only now with thirteen days of boat under your belt. Cape Kamenjak still asks to be treated seriously: stand well off the outlying shoals, expect the sea to jump where wind meets current, and only then bear away into the calm of Medulin bay. A quiet last mile follows, up to ACI Marina Pomer. Fuel where the base tells you, run the checkout inventory, and then get to the part that matters, a final swim, a final dinner with the crew, and the yearly row over which of the fourteen anchorages was the best of them.
thingsToDo: Round Cape Kamenjak giving the shoals room
Take a last swim in Medulin bay
Refuel and square the boat for handover
Toast the fortnight over dinner in Pomer
14.mooringTip: Aim to be on the ACI Pomer berth by late afternoon, as the check-out inspection comes early next day. Work the fuel stop into your timing; the base will point you to the pump they want used before handover.

pomer-portic-bay-cape-kamenjak.metaTitle: 7-Day Kvarner Island Hop from Pomer | Boat4You
pomer-portic-bay-cape-kamenjak.metaDesc: A 7-day sail from Pomer around the Kvarner islands: Cape Kamenjak, Mali Lošinj, Ilovik, Rab, Krk and Cres, with dolphins, anchorages and konobas.

1.shortDescription: An easy first-day run to the wild tip of Istria, where you anchor under Kamenjak's cliffs and walk to real dinosaur tracks.
1.description: The first leg barely counts as a passage — an hour or so south from Pomer and you're rounding the last headland of Istria. Veliki Portić is the roomiest bay in the Kamenjak reserve, so we usually aim for there and drop the hook on sand at five or six metres. No road reaches this stretch of coast, which keeps the crowds thin once the day-trip boats head home. Take the dinghy ashore and follow the marked trail near the park entrance; the footprints pressed into the limestone are easy to walk past if you don't know where to look. Swim off the low cliffs, snorkel the clear water at the bay's edge, then settle in for the evening. Just read the forecast first: this anchorage is wide open to the Bora, with nothing behind you if it fills in from the northeast.
thingsToDo: Follow the marked dinosaur-footprint trail
Snorkel the clear water off Veliki Portić
Cliff-jump from the low Kamenjak rocks
Sundowner swim before the day boats leave
1.mooringTip: Anchor free on sand and weed in five to eight metres; holding is decent, but check your set through the seagrass. It sits snug in southerly and southeast wind and stays open to the Bora, so clear out for Pomer or Medulin if the northeast is forecast.

2.shortDescription: The longest crossing of the week: twenty-five miles over open Kvarner to Mali Lošinj, keeping an eye out for the resident dolphins.
2.description: This is the day that earns its miles. Twenty-five nautical miles of open water separate Kamenjak from Lošinj, so we like to slip the anchor by nine and use the morning before the afternoon chop builds. The Kvarner holds a resident pod of roughly two hundred bottlenose dolphins, studied for decades by the Blue World Institute over in Veli Lošinj, and they often come to ride the bow on this stretch. Mali Lošinj is one of the busiest harbours up here, its long pastel Riva lined with cafés and gelato counters. Anchoring in Čikat is off the table these days, so plan on a marina berth or a slot on the town quay. Once you're tied up, walk the waterfront, poke into the galleries, and eat scampi buzara at a harbourside table while the boats keep arriving.
thingsToDo: Watch for bow-riding bottlenose dolphins
Stroll the pastel Mali Lošinj Riva
Scampi buzara at a harbourside table
Visit the Blue World centre in Veli Lošinj
2.mooringTip: Čikat's buoys are for hotel guests and the bay bans anchoring, so book a berth at Marina Lošinj in high season or take a first-come slot on the town Riva. For a quiet night instead, Krivica bay on the southwest coast is enclosed on every side.

3.shortDescription: A gentle six-mile hop to Ilovik, a car-free island of barely a hundred souls, and the sheltered channel it shares with Sveti Petar.
3.description: After yesterday's slog, day three is a treat: six miles down the coast and you're off Ilovik. Fewer than a hundred people live here, there are no cars, and the waterfront runs thick with oleander and palms. The real draw is the channel between Ilovik and the uninhabited Sveti Petar islet, one of the most protected pieces of water in the whole Kvarner. Around eighty buoys fill it; depth sits near six metres in the middle and shoals fast toward each shore, so read your chart before you swing in. Pick up a restaurant buoy, kayak over to Sveti Petar to find the old Benedictine ruins and the cemetery chapel, then swim in the shallows before dinner. Ashore, Konoba Oliva and Dalmatinka both feed you well; take whichever buoy belongs to the place you choose.
thingsToDo: Kayak over to the Sveti Petar ruins
Pick up a restaurant buoy in the channel
Swim the shallow water off Ilovik
Dinner at Konoba Oliva or Dalmatinka
3.mooringTip: The channel buoys are laid by the tavernas: tie to the one owned by the restaurant you'll eat at, and the mooring is free with dinner. It's sheltered from every direction, but both sides shoal quickly, so favour the deeper middle and don't cut the corners.

4.shortDescription: A full day's sail east to Rab, the medieval town whose four bell towers stand over one of Croatia's rare sandy coasts.
4.description: Rab is a proper day's sail from Ilovik, roughly twenty-five miles, so treat it as a passage day and leave early. The payoff comes into view long before you arrive: four Romanesque bell towers rising off a thin peninsula, the skyline that put the town on every postcard. Berth at ACI Marina Rab, which takes boats up to twenty metres, or drop anchor west of town in Sveta Eufemija bay for a calmer, cheaper night. Rab is unusual for these waters in having real sand. The Lopar dunes on the north shore, Sahara among them, are a short tender ride or a quick bus hop away when you want a proper beach afternoon. Climb the Sveti Ivan tower for the rooftop view, wander the three old streets, and finish with a slice of Rapska torta, the almond cake the island's nuns have baked for centuries.
thingsToDo: Climb the Sveti Ivan bell tower
Beach afternoon on the Lopar dunes
Walk Rab's medieval Upper Street
Almond Rapska torta near the cathedral
4.mooringTip: ACI Marina Rab has 134 berths and fills through July and August, so reserve ahead. For a quieter, free alternative, anchor in Sveta Eufemija bay just west of the old town, well sheltered and a five-minute dinghy from the walls.

5.shortDescription: Twenty miles north to Baška on Krk, where a two-kilometre pebble beach curls beneath bare mountains at the island's southern end.
5.description: Twenty miles brings you up to Baška, tucked into the southeast corner of Krk under steep, treeless ridges. Vela Plaža is why most crews stop: nearly two kilometres of pale pebbles in a long arc, a Blue Flag beach that keeps turning up on Europe's best-of lists. The bay is open to southerly and southeast wind, and the Bora can push a real swell into the harbour, so keep half an eye on the sky. Baška has a small marina and a town quay; both fill in peak weeks, so call ahead or plan to anchor southeast of the breakwater in deeper water. Ashore, stretch your legs on the Moon Plateau trail behind town, or take a taxi up to Vrbnik, the clifftop village whose vineyards make the crisp white žlahtina. Dinner is šurlice, the hand-rolled Krk pasta, in a stone-cellar konoba.
thingsToDo: Beach day on Vela Plaža
Hike the Moon Plateau trail
Žlahtina tasting up in Vrbnik
Šurlice pasta in a cellar konoba
5.mooringTip: Baška's inner harbour is snug and usually taken by local boats; the pier and the inner side of the breakwater are the next bet. In settled weather anchor about 400 metres southeast of the breakwater in 20 to 30 metres, but move if the Bora or a southerly gets up.

6.shortDescription: West across the channel to Valun on Cres, a fishing hamlet under vast cliffs with the medieval village of Lubenice on the ridge above.
6.description: Cross the Krk-Cres channel and work down the western shore to Valun, a handful of stone houses at the foot of a horseshoe bay. The village lays about ten buoys off its waterfront for boats eating ashore; beyond them you can anchor free on sand and weed in six to ten metres. It's a calm-weather spot, well worth it when the forecast cooperates. High on the cliff above sits Lubenice, a settlement people have lived in for some three thousand years, now down to a few dozen residents and reached by a steep road or a hard climb. In the village church at Valun, look for the eleventh-century stone tablet carved in both Glagolitic and Latin. Eat where the buoys are, at Na moru or MaMaLu on the seafront, and order the Cres lamb if it's on the board.
thingsToDo: Anchor under the Valun cliffs
See the Glagolitic Valun Tablet
Climb up to clifftop Lubenice
Cres lamb at a Valun konoba
6.mooringTip: Valun's waterfront buoys are free if you dine at the taverna that owns them, so grab one early on a summer afternoon. Otherwise anchor further out on sand in six to ten metres, and only stay the night in settled weather, since the bay opens to the west.

7.shortDescription: The long haul home: twenty-five miles back across Kvarner to Pomer, with a swim stop at Plavnik's Moon Bay if the wind allows.
7.description: The last day is the mirror of day two: twenty-five miles of open Kvarner back to Pomer. We usually wait for the mid-morning northwesterly, the thermal breeze that fills in most summer afternoons, and let it carry us home on a fair wind. If the sea stays kind, break the passage at Plavnik, the little island off the Cres coast whose Moon Bay makes a fine last swim. Keep an eye on the cliffs on the way through; peregrines nest along this coast and griffon vultures ride the thermals over Cres. Aim to reach Marina Pomer with daylight to spare, so you can top up fuel and settle the boat before handover. Then there's one thing left: a glass of Istrian Malvazija on deck as the light goes, the boat quiet under you for the first time all week.
thingsToDo: Last swim at Plavnik's Moon Bay
Spot griffon vultures over Cres
Refuel and settle at Marina Pomer
Farewell Malvazija on deck at dusk
7.mooringTip: Call Marina Pomer a day ahead to lock in your berth and a fuel slot, since handover mornings get busy. If the evening is calm you can anchor free just outside the marina for a last quiet night before going in.

primosten-dubrovnik-primosten-route.metaDesc: Two-week sailing loop from Primošten to Dubrovnik and back: Brač, Hvar, Vis, Lastovo, Mljet and Korčula, with mooring notes for every night.

1.shortDescription: An easy 15-mile shakedown southeast to Krknjaši, the shallow sand-floored bay wedged between the two Drvenik islands — a quiet first night.
1.description: Marina Kremik sits two miles south of Primošten town, and it makes a calm base to load the boat before the first leg. From there it's fifteen miles down the open coast into the channel that separates Veli Drvenik from its smaller sister. Nobody sails to Krknjaši for the shore — you come to get in the water: pale sand at three to six metres, an anchor that bites first go, and a channel that turns proper turquoise the moment the breeze quits. A trio of tavernas on Veli Drvenik put out free buoys for anyone dining ashore, though plenty of crews just set the hook and stay put. There's no road to this side of the island, so the evenings stay quiet. Day one earns its keep by being dull: sort the galley, run the reefing lines through, let everyone claim a bunk. The serious miles start tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Drop anchor on sand and swim
Order grilled fish at a Drvenik buoy restaurant
Walk the short headland loop above Krknjaši
Run reefing drills in the light channel breeze
Watch the sun drop over the open Adriatic
1.mooringTip: Anchor in four to six metres on firm sand, or take a restaurant buoy if you plan to eat ashore. The bay covers you from north through east and south; only a building westerly is a problem, in which case slip two miles east into Marina Trogir.

2.shortDescription: Twelve miles east across the Brač Channel to Milna, a sheltered Venetian-era harbour where the lamb peka needs ordering hours ahead.
2.description: The crossing to Milna runs twelve miles east-southeast over the Brač Channel, usually a comfortable reach once the morning wind fills. The town hides at the head of a long, kinked fjord on Brač's west side; you won't see the quay until you're nearly on it, though the 17th-century belfry gives the position away. Boats have wintered in here since Venice ran the coast. Take the inner quay stern-to on your own anchor, where the central run carries water and power for a small fee. The island's money was always in stone: the white blocks that built Diocletian's Palace were cut here, and its cooking turns on peka, meat and potatoes slow-roasted under an iron bell of embers. That last part is really a timing problem. Reckon on three hours, so find a konoba and put the order in before you so much as swim.
thingsToDo: Order lamb peka three hours before dinner
Find the 17th-century church on the inner quay
Taste local olive oil at the co-op
Dinghy north to Bobovišća for a swim
Wander the stone lanes above the harbour
2.mooringTip: Go stern-to on the inner town quay with your own anchor; water and power sit on the central section for a small harbour fee. The inlet opens to the southwest, but even when the afternoon Maestral pushes past 15 knots the inner quay stays settled.

3.shortDescription: Fifteen miles southeast into the Pakleni Islands and Palmižana, the only islet in the cluster with a marina and a botanical garden.
3.description: The Pakleni Islands lie a fifteen-mile reach southeast off Hvar, and Palmižana is the one to aim for. It's tucked on the east flank of Sv. Klement — the largest islet out here and the only one that runs a marina. The ACI berths are stern-to on lazy lines and go early, so book online for July and August or resign yourself to the anchor. Anchoring holds fine at five to ten metres in sand and weed until about two o'clock, when the Maestral starts funnelling down the channel and the swell finds you. That's the cue to slip round to Vinogradišće or Tarsce on the south side and run a line ashore for a settled night. The set-piece ashore is the Meneghello garden, planted by the family in 1906; after that it's dinner at a konoba up the slope and a snorkel over the Roman amphorae still lying off the west shore.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Roman amphora field, western shore
Walk the Meneghello botanical garden from 1906
Take a line ashore in Vinogradišće cove
Water-taxi across to Hvar Town for dinner
Cross the island footpath to Vlaka Bay
3.mooringTip: ACI Palmižana is stern-to on lazy lines with online booking all but essential in high summer. If it's full, anchor in Vinogradišće on the south side, where a line ashore over sand and weed is the normal drill.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles southwest to Komiža on Vis, a working fishing port with konoba lobster pots and the finest sunset on the route.
4.description: The leg out to Komiža covers twenty miles southwest, open water most of the way. Croatia has no inhabited island further west than Vis, and because the navy kept it off-limits until 1989 it still reads a step apart from the usual charter run. In the inner basin you moor stern-to on your own anchor over sand and weed; holding is mostly sound, though the outer wall feels any swell out of the southwest. Dinner leans on the harbour's own catch, grilled anchovies and the island's Vugava white, and Konoba Jastožera lays its tables over the water in an old lobster pound. Save an afternoon for the climb up Mount Hum to Tito's wartime cave, where British commandos and the Partisans ran operations; the view from the top reaches half of central Dalmatia.
thingsToDo: Climb Mount Hum to Tito's wartime cave
Eat over the water at Konoba Jastožera
Order grilled anchovies with a glass of Vugava
See the Fishermen's Museum in the Venetian tower
Watch the sunset from the harbour wall
4.mooringTip: Anchor stern-to in the inner basin on your own hook — sand and weed, holding mostly good, harbour fee paid ashore. The outer wall lies open to southwesterly swell, so once a SW gradient pushes past 15 knots, slip the six miles across to St. George Bay at Vis Town.

5.shortDescription: An early Blue Cave call at Biševo, then an eighteen-mile hop east to reach Vela Luka on Korčula's sheltered western end.
5.description: This day runs on the sun. The Blue Cave on Biševo only turns that improbable electric blue when low morning light angles in through its underwater entrance, roughly between nine and eleven. You tie up at the little concession quay outside (a daytime fee, no overnight berthing) and take one of the licensed tenders that run people in and back out in about forty minutes. Once you're back aboard, point east and cover the eighteen miles to Vela Luka at the far western tip of Korčula. The long bay shelters well from north through east, has full town services, and keeps a fuel pump at the western end of the quay. Dinner is žrnovski makaruni, the hand-rolled island pasta, at a konoba a few steps back from the water.
thingsToDo: Queue for the Blue Cave tender by 10:30
Dinghy into the Green Cave at Ravnik
Order žrnovski makaruni at a Vela Luka konoba
Top up fuel at the western quay
Stroll the waterfront promenade at dusk
5.mooringTip: Vela Luka takes you stern-to with your own anchor along the long town quay for a modest fee, water and power laid on, fuel pump at the western end. The bay is fully covered from north, northeast and east.

6.shortDescription: Fourteen miles south to Lastovo, as far offshore as inhabited Croatia gets; Zaklopatica's tavernas put out free buoys under some of the darkest skies in the Adriatic.
6.description: Lastovo is fourteen miles further south, about the outer limit of where charter boats bother to go. The night stop is Zaklopatica, a bay bitten into the north shore in a tight U. Five family konobas ring it, and each keeps a run of free buoys for anyone coming in to eat — fifteen or so in all, with room on the quay too when it's calm. The kitchens lean on Lastovo lobster and šporki makaruli, pasta in a long-cooked meat sugo. There's next to no artificial light out here, and it shows: on a moonless night the Milky Way is up and obvious well before midnight. Sit out on deck for it. Not much else is competing for your attention, which is more or less the reason to come.
thingsToDo: Take a konoba buoy in return for dinner
Order Lastovo lobster or šporki makaruli
Walk inland to the Lastovo chimney stacks
Snorkel the islet at the bay mouth
Watch the Milky Way rise after dark
6.mooringTip: Zaklopatica's free buoys go with a dinner booking, so confirm with the konoba as you pick one up. The bay covers you from north round to west; if a southeasterly above 15 knots is forecast, drop four miles south to Skrivena Luka.

7.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles northeast to Mljet, where the Polače inlet sets Roman ruins on the waterfront and opens onto the national park's saltwater lakes.
7.description: Polače is twenty-two miles up to the northeast, a long finger of water on Mljet's north coast so hemmed in it barely registers the weather outside. Green park buoys line the bay; pay the ranger at the kiosk and the fee covers park entry for the crew. The oddity here is the ruin a few metres back from the shoreline, a 4th-century Roman palace whose walls still stand roof-high in stretches. It's about half an hour on foot to Veliko Jezero, the larger of the park's two saltwater lakes, where a small park boat ferries visitors out to the 12th-century Benedictine monastery on the islet mid-lake. A flat trail loops both lakes if you want the legs stretched, and the swimming is warm and still.
thingsToDo: Pick up a park buoy, pay the ranger
Walk the 4th-century Roman walls on the shore
Ferry to St. Mary's monastery on the lake
Cycle the flat loop around Veliko Jezero
Swim in the warm saltwater lakes
7.mooringTip: Polače is green park mooring buoys only, paid to the ranger on arrival (around €40 a night in 2025, crew park entry included). Holding elsewhere in the bay is patchy, so the buoys are the sensible choice.

8.shortDescription: Twenty-four miles southeast to ACI Dubrovnik in Komolac, then a short bus into the Old City for the wall walk.
8.description: Dubrovnik shows itself twenty-four miles down the coast to the southeast, though you carry on another eight up the Rijeka Dubrovačka estuary to ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac. That's the practical overnight: lazy lines, water and power on every berth, a fuel pontoon. The Old Port on the seafront takes tenders by day only and sits open to any southerly. From Komolac, Bus 1A or a taxi reaches the Pile Gate in about fifteen minutes. The walls are the thing you don't skip — a 1.94-kilometre circuit that runs roughly ninety minutes and lays out the whole warren of terracotta roofs with the sea beyond. Go early or near closing to dodge the cruise-ship crush. In the evening, eat up a Prijeko side lane rather than on the Stradun, and finish with a drink at Buža, out on the rocks below the south wall.
thingsToDo: Walk the 1.94 km wall circuit early
Ride the cable car up Mt. Srđ
Dine up a quiet Prijeko lane
See the Rector's Palace and Franciscan cloister
Have a drink on the rocks at Buža
8.mooringTip: ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac is really the only workable overnight, so book ahead in season; lazy lines and full services throughout. Treat the Old Port purely as a daytime tender stop.

9.shortDescription: Okuklje sits twenty miles northwest on Mljet's southeast shore, beyond the park boundary — a wind-proof cove where three konobas string up free buoys for diners.
9.description: The return starts with a twenty-mile hop northwest to Okuklje, on Mljet's southeast coast — this stop sits outside the national park, so the fees don't apply. The bay is a narrow crescent good for maybe twenty boats, boxed in on three sides by hills the better part of a hundred metres tall. Three family konobas run the place and each hangs its own free buoys for guests eating ashore. The standby dish is crni rižot, black risotto stained with cuttlefish ink, and it's usually excellent. Those steep walls do something useful at day's end: whatever the wind is up to out in the channel, the anchorage goes glassy by sunset. This tends to be the quietest night of the whole loop, no town noise, no ferry wake, just water against the hull.
thingsToDo: Take a free konoba buoy, book dinner
Order crni rižot cooked with cuttlefish ink
Walk the headland path toward Korčula's view
Swim straight off the boat in flat water
Sleep through the quietest night of the trip
9.mooringTip: Okuklje's konoba buoys are free with a dinner reservation, which locks your spot. The bay covers you fully from north, east and south; only a strong northwesterly over 18 knots sends any swell in.

10.shortDescription: Fifteen miles northwest up the Pelješac Channel to walled Korčula Town, where the Moreška sword dance runs Monday and Thursday evenings.
10.description: A short run up the Pelješac Channel, fifteen miles northwest, sets you down beneath the ramparts of Korčula Town. Two options for the night: ACI Marina Korčula on the east side of the peninsula, full services and lazy lines, or the town quay on the west side, stern-to with your own anchor for a harbour fee but open to the afternoon Maestral. The old town is tiny, a fishbone of lanes laid out to funnel the wind and shade the streets, and it reads as a rehearsal for the radial grids Venice later built. Try to be here on a Monday or Thursday: the Moreška, a clashing two-sword battle dance, is staged those evenings through the season. For dinner, pour a glass of Grk, the white grape grown almost nowhere but the sandy vineyards of nearby Lumbarda.
thingsToDo: Catch the Moreška sword dance, Mon or Thu
Walk the old town once the ferries leave
Climb St. Mark's Cathedral bell tower
Look in at the disputed Marco Polo house
Drink a glass of Lumbarda Grk white
10.mooringTip: Take ACI Marina Korčula on the eastern side for any weather — lazy lines, full services. The town quay looks nicer but sits open to the westerly Maestral, so use it only when the forecast is settled.

11.shortDescription: Jelsa lies twenty-three miles northwest over the Hvar Channel — a calmer counterpart to Hvar Town, framed by lavender fields and a UNESCO-listed plain.
11.description: Across the Hvar Channel, twenty-three miles on, Jelsa makes a slower-paced stand-in for the crowds of Hvar Town. Stern-to on your own anchor on the town quay holds well in mud and sand, and if you'd rather have lazy lines there's a small marina on the east side. This is the agricultural middle of the island. Lavender still climbs the road inland toward Vrboska and Stari Grad and gets cut commercially every July, while the konobas down the back lanes pour Bogdanjuša, a white that barely leaves Hvar. With a bike you can reach the UNESCO-listed Stari Grad Plain, a grid of dry-stone field walls the Greeks laid out 2,400 years ago and farmers still work today.
thingsToDo: Cycle the coast road to Vrboska's canals
Walk the ancient Stari Grad Plain grid
Order Bogdanjuša white at a back-lane konoba
Buy lavender oil from a July producer
Climb to the Tor tower for the channel view
11.mooringTip: Drop your own anchor and back onto the Jelsa town quay; the mud-and-sand bottom grips well, with water and power along the central section. Need laid lines instead? The little marina on the eastern side has them.

12.shortDescription: Lučice sits ten miles north over the Brač Channel — a run of empty coves beneath limestone cliffs, with two summer-only beach restaurants for dinner.
12.description: It's a short one today — ten miles north over the Brač Channel to Lučice, a string of linked coves on Brač's south shore just east of Pučišća. No village here, no road, just white limestone cliffs and pine coming down to the water. Two seasonal restaurants work the coves, each with its own free buoys for guests, turning out grilled fish and the local Plavac Mali. The pale limestone carries on under the surface, so on a still summer day the bottom shows clear at twenty metres. If you fancy a leg-stretch, dinghy the mile west into Pučišća, where the stone-masonry school and its yards still cut the white blocks that built palaces up and down the coast. Otherwise this is a swimming-and-nothing afternoon, which after twelve days of miles is no bad thing.
thingsToDo: Take a free restaurant buoy in the central cove
Snorkel the pale limestone floor, 20 m down
Order grilled fish with Plavac Mali ashore
Dinghy to Pučišća's working stone-mason yards
Paddle the cliff coast at first light
12.mooringTip: The restaurant buoys at Lučice are free if you eat ashore. If they're taken, anchor on the bottom in eight to twelve metres of sand and weed. The coves shelter you from north, northeast and east but stand open to a southerly.

13.shortDescription: Fourteen miles northwest brings Maslinica, on the western tip of Šolta — a natural harbour sheltered behind a line of seven little islets.
13.description: The outer edge of the loop finishes at Maslinica, fourteen miles northwest, in the deep S-bend of a harbour on Šolta's western point. A scatter of islets guards the mouth, Polebrnjak and Stipanska among them, and they knock the flat off any Adriatic swell, which leaves one of the calmest natural harbours in the central islands. You've a choice of berths: Martinis Marchi Marina on the south side, lazy lines below an 18th-century baroque castle now run as a hotel, or the village quay opposite, stern-to on your own anchor for a modest fee. For all that it's the closest island to Split, barely fifteen miles off, Šolta stays the most low-key of the central group. Ask for Dobričić, the island's own dark grape, and watch the light go off Polebrnjak at the mouth.
thingsToDo: Step into the Martinis Marchi castle courtyard
Order Dobričić, Šolta's own red grape
Dinghy to Stipanska island's swim path
Buy Šolta olive oil at the village shop
Watch sunset from Polebrnjak at the bay mouth
13.mooringTip: On the south side, Martinis Marchi runs full services and laid lines, worth reserving ahead in season. Opposite, the village quay is stern-to on your own hook. Screened by its islets, the bay holds calm from the south the whole way round to northwest.

14.shortDescription: The final twenty miles northwest back into Marina Kremik for the Saturday handover, past Šolta's south coast and the Drvenik islands.
14.description: One leg left: twenty miles northwest from Maslinica into ACI Marina Kremik, the Primošten base a couple of miles below the old town. Saturday turnarounds keep to the clock — alongside by 09:00, tanks pumped, fuel topped, decks rinsed and the inventory walked through. The course north passes the south coast of Šolta and slips back between the Drvenik islands you left on day one, so it closes the circle neatly. Once the base manager has signed off there's usually time to kill before transfers. Walk the path up to the 16th-century St. George church on the tip of the Primošten peninsula, or settle in for a long harbour-wall lunch with a last glass of the local Babić red. Split airport is about forty-five minutes north.
thingsToDo: Top up fuel and pump out before 09:00
Walk up to St. George church on the peninsula
Take a long lunch on the harbour wall
Order a last glass of Babić red
Ride the shuttle into Primošten old town
14.mooringTip: Return into Marina Kremik as your charter contract specifies; the base manager will assign the slot. The handover window is 08:00 to 09:00 Saturday, so if your contract asks for a night-before return, be in by 17:00 on Friday.

primosten-np-kornati-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Kornati & Krka Sail from Primošten | Boat4You
primosten-np-kornati-route.metaDesc: Seven-day sailing route from Primošten: Zlarin and the Šibenik islands, Krka's waterfalls at Skradin, Telašćica cliffs and Kornati National Park.

1.shortDescription: Twelve miles north from Kremik to Zlarin, a car-free island where local divers have worked the red-coral beds since 1412.
1.description: We usually clear ACI Marina Kremik mid-morning and point the bow north, twelve miles of open coast before the Šibenik islands close in around you. Zlarin sits low and green off the estuary mouth, a single village strung along its eastern shore and not a car on the whole island. The place made its name on coral. Divers here have pulled red coral off the offshore beds since 1412, and the small museum on the square lays out six centuries of the trade in a couple of rooms. Tie up stern-to on the main pier, where water and power run to the central berths for a modest harbour fee. The forested interior is worth an hour on foot. Come evening, Konoba Ivana at the top of the harbour fills with locals, and dinner is whatever the boats brought in.
thingsToDo: See the coral museum on the main square
Walk the pine interior to the north end
Dinner at Konoba Ivana above the harbour
Swim off the rocks below the village
Browse the two coral workshops in the village
1.mooringTip: The 140-metre pier holds around 40 boats stern-to on your own anchor, with water and power on the inner berths; it shelters from the west and north but sits open to the SE. If a sirocco is forecast over 15 knots, duck 4 miles up into the Šibenik estuary for the night.

2.shortDescription: An upriver run past Šibenik and under the St Anthony narrows into Krka's freshwater canyon, tying up where the river turns sweet at Skradin.
2.description: This is a passage most crews remember. From Zlarin you thread the channel past Šibenik's old town, slip under the road bridge with its 49 metres of clearance, and follow the St Anthony narrows into a walled freshwater canyon with no real parallel on this coast. Allow ninety minutes at displacement speed from the river mouth; the entrance is well buoyed and deep enough for any charter draft. ACI Marina Skradin waits at the head of the river, lazy lines on every berth and fuel by the entrance. Buy your park ticket at the seawall kiosk and take the included shuttle up to Skradinski Buk, where the water drops 46 metres over seventeen travertine steps. A boardwalk loops both banks. Back in town, order the Skradin risotto, beef and saffron cooked down over hours.
thingsToDo: Ride the park shuttle to Skradinski Buk
Walk the boardwalk loop below the falls
Order Skradin risotto in the old town
Hike the 3.4 km canyon path upriver
Take on fuel at the marina pontoon
2.mooringTip: ACI Marina Skradin has lazy lines and full services, with the fuel pontoon right at the entrance. The river channel is well marked and deep, but budget 90 minutes up from the Šibenik mouth at slow speed, and remember there is no anchoring anywhere inside the park.

3.shortDescription: Back down the Krka and out west to Kaprije, a quiet caper-grown island of 140 people, one harbour and a chapel on the ridge.
3.description: Retrace the river, back under the St Anthony narrows, then bear west for open water and Kaprije. The island took its name from the caper bushes that still grow wild across it, and its 140 residents all live around the one bay on the eastern side. No cars here either. The harbourmaster works the moorings along the mole, and you berth stern-to on your own anchor; water reaches the central berths but there is no shore power, so charge up before you arrive. Ninety minutes on foot gets you round the whole island. The path up to the little chapel on the spine opens out over the Kornati cluster offshore, your ground for the next two days. Konoba kod Kate, a handful of tables, cooks whatever came in that morning.
thingsToDo: Climb the ridge path to the chapel
Eat the day's catch at Konoba kod Kate
Swim off the rocks south of the harbour
Dinghy out to the islets off the bay
Walk the full island in ninety minutes
3.mooringTip: Berth stern-to with your own anchor on the Kaprije mole; water on the central berths, no power, so top up the batteries beforehand. The bay is snug in southerlies and westerlies but wide open to a NE bora; if one is forecast above 18 knots, run 4 miles east into Mikavica bay on Žirje.

4.shortDescription: Northwest to Telašćica, the great sheltered bay at the foot of Dugi Otok, walled by 160-metre cliffs with a warm salt lake behind them.
4.description: Fourteen miles northwest brings you into Telašćica, cut deep into the southern end of Dugi Otok and reckoned among the safest natural harbours in the Adriatic. The western arm runs under pine and rock; the eastern side breaks into a scatter of coves and islets. Pick up one of the park buoys, which is mandatory here, and the ranger comes round by tender for the fee and your rubbish. Two things pull people ashore. Mir, the salt lake behind the isthmus, warms well above sea temperature and swims like a bath; a short path leads over to it, and the island donkeys usually wander the shore. And the Grpašćak cliffs on the seaward side stand 160 metres over the water, with a marked trail along the top and the Kornati laid out below. Stay aboard after dark for the cicadas.
thingsToDo: Take a park buoy and pay the ranger
Walk over to Mir salt lake for a swim
Hike the Grpašćak cliff trail for the view
Snorkel the coves on the eastern shore
Watch for the free-roaming island donkeys
4.mooringTip: A park mooring buoy is compulsory in Telašćica, and the overnight fee collected by the ranger covers park entry. The bay holds well in every wind direction, which makes it one of the few genuinely all-weather nights on this route.

5.shortDescription: A ten-mile push south to Piškera, the lone marina inside Kornati National Park, where bare-karst peaks plunge 80 metres just offshore.
5.description: Only ten miles today, but they run through the heart of the park. ACI Marina Piškera sits on Panitula Vela at the western edge, a shelf of lazy-line berths with water, power and a restaurant built onto the rock; a second place across the water sends its own tender for guests. Book online in July and August, when the marina fills. Pay your park entry online too if you can, since it comes cheaper than at the kiosk, and berthing here folds in the ticket for the day and the next. The afternoon is for the water. Take the tender across to snorkel the channel between Piškera and Lavsa, or motor down to the southern crowns off Mana and Kornat, where the naked karst falls eighty metres sheer into the sea. Nothing grows out on those crowns, just rock and salt and glare off the water.
thingsToDo: Pre-pay park entry online before you arrive
Snorkel the channel between Piškera and Lavsa
Motor out to the Mana and Kornat cliffs
Eat at the restaurant above the marina
Walk the karst spine of Panitula Vela
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Piškera runs lazy lines on every berth; book ahead for July and August. Overnight anchoring inside the park is limited to five bays only, Lavsa, Lojena, Vrulje, Stiniva and Statival, so plan for the marina or one of those and nothing in between.

6.shortDescription: East out of the park to Jezera on Murter, the old charter gateway to Kornati, with salt pans, a sand beach and slow-cooked pašticada.
6.description: Twelve miles east and you are back among inhabited islands. Murter is the closest land to the Kornati and has launched charter crews toward the park for generations; strictly it is a peninsula, joined to the mainland by the small bridge at Tisno. ACI Marina Jezera lies on the south coast, full services, lazy lines, fuel at the entrance, and the village folds around the bay behind it. The good konobas hide in the back lanes, where pašticada, beef braised down in red wine, is the dish to order. Off the boat there is more here than most crews expect. Walk out to the Tisno salt pans, worked since Roman times and still producing, or take the short hop to Slanica for a rare sand beach on this coast. Betina, next door, keeps a small museum of Murter's wooden-boat building.
thingsToDo: Order pašticada in a Jezera back-lane konoba
Walk to the Roman-era Tisno salt pans
Swim at Slanica sand beach
See the wooden-boat museum in Betina
Taste local Murter olive oil quayside
6.mooringTip: ACI Marina Jezera brings laid lines, a full service list and a fuelling pontoon by the entrance; with the bay calm in most conditions, it makes a dependable all-weather hold before the long last push home.

7.shortDescription: Twenty miles south back to Kremik for the Saturday handover, past the Vodice and Tribunj shoals, with time for a last climb to St George's.
7.description: The last leg is the longest, twenty miles south back to ACI Marina Kremik, and worth leaving by nine to have the day at the far end. The course tracks past the shoals off Vodice and Tribunj, all well marked but worth a proper look at the chart before you settle on a line. Saturday handover runs early: boat alongside by nine, fuel topped, tanks pumped, decks rinsed and the inventory checked through. With the paperwork signed off, the peninsula is yours for a few hours. Climb up to the sixteenth-century church of St George on the point above the old town, where the Babić vineyards run down the slopes, or hold the harbour wall over a long lunch. Split airport is forty-five minutes up the road when the transfer calls.
thingsToDo: Climb to St George's church on the point
Take the last Babić red on the peninsula
Lunch long on the Primošten harbour wall
Ride the shuttle from Kremik to the old town
Top up fuel before the nine o'clock check
7.mooringTip: Return into ACI Marina Kremik as your contract sets out, with the base manager assigning the berth; the handover window is usually eight to nine on Saturday. If your paperwork asks for a night-before return, aim to be alongside by 17:00 on the Friday.

primosten-vis-korcula-hvar-solta-route.metaDesc: Seven days from Primošten through Vis, the Blue Cave, Hvar Town, ancient Stari Grad and Maslinica on Šolta. A working skipper's day-by-day sailing plan.

1.shortDescription: A short 15-mile hop from Kremik to the Blue Lagoon off Drvenik Veli, sand-bottom swimming and two shore konobas for the first night.
1.description: Kremik empties out slowly on a Saturday, so we usually clear the fuel dock and point southeast without rushing. Fifteen miles down the coast, the channel between Veli and Mali Drvenik opens into Krknjaši, the shallow water most people know as the Blue Lagoon. Sand bottom at three to six metres, water you can read your anchor through. Come in from the south; the gap between the smaller islets shoals to under three metres and catches the careless. By early afternoon the day-tripper boats from Trogir crowd the middle, so settling in early pays off. Two places ashore on Veli Drvenik, Skoy Bar and Konoba Krknjaši, do fresh fish if the crew wants a table. Fine for a first night when the forecast is quiet, less so in any western push.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the sand shallows off Krknjaši islets
Fresh fish dinner at Konoba Krknjaši
Sundowner drinks at Skoy Bar, Veli Drvenik
Swim across to the smaller Krknjaš islet
1.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in four to six metres; holding is reliable and there's room to swing. Approach only from the south, and treat it as a settled-weather overnight since it opens to the west and northwest. If you want a shore table, take a buoy off the konoba and eat in.

2.shortDescription: The week's first real sail, southwest to Komiža under Mount Hum, where the lobster comes out of the harbour pots.
2.description: The run to Komiža is the first proper sail of the week, twenty-four miles southwest across open water with the maestral usually filling in after noon. Komiža sits under Mount Hum on the western face of Vis, and it still works for a living; nets dry on the quay and the fishing fleet comes and goes at odd hours. Get in before two in high season if you want a spot on the town quay, which has lines, water and power for maybe thirty boats. The bay holds well against north and east, but a southerly jugo rolls swell straight in. Dinner is the reason to be here. Konoba Bako pulls lobster from pots in the harbour, and Jastožera, built over an old lobster pen, lets a small tender slip under its arch.
thingsToDo: Lobster dinner at Konoba Jastožera
Climb Mount Hum for the ridge view
Walk the working fishing quay at dusk
Local Vugava white at Konoba Barba
2.mooringTip: Stern-to on the town quay with lines, water and power, but arrive before 1400 in July and August or the wall fills. Komiža shelters from north and east; if the jugo builds from the south, move six miles round to Vis Town in St George Bay for the night.

3.shortDescription: Beat the RIBs into the Blue Cave, swim off Budihovac, then an easy afternoon into St George Bay at Vis Town.
3.description: Get the Blue Cave done first. The light inside only comes good through the late morning, and the official tenders out of Komiža run on a 24-hour booking, so we like to be first in line rather than fighting the Split RIBs later. Fifteen minutes inside, then south to Budihovac, a pair of islets off the south coast with sand at six to eight metres and some of the cleanest water on Vis for a pre-lunch swim. From there it's a lazy afternoon leg, working round the western tip and up into Vis Town. St George Bay is the safest overnight on the island in any wind. Ashore, the Roman colony of Issa lies under the seafront, Fort George crowns the point above the entrance, and Pojoda does proper Dalmatian cooking in a walled garden.
thingsToDo: First tender into the Blue Cave
Pre-lunch swim at the Budihovac islets
Sunset drink up at Fort George
Dinner in Pojoda's walled garden
See the Roman Issa ruins on the seafront
3.mooringTip: Take the Blue Cave's 24-hour booking seriously; private dinghies are not allowed and only the official tenders go in. In Vis Town, moor stern-to on the Luka quay on the western side, or grab one of the sixty buoys. St George Bay is sheltered from south through west, the reliable choice whatever the forecast.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles east across the Hvar Channel to Hvar Town, all noise and crowds in July, where a booked ACI berth is the only quiet night going.
4.description: The hop east across the channel to Hvar is about twenty miles, and it drops you straight into the most crowded port in Dalmatia. Hvar Town wears it well: the Fortica fortress on the hill, the long stone Arsenal on the water, St Stephen's closing off the main square. The sailing decision here is simple. Book ACI Marina Hvar on the western side of the bay ahead of time, because for a twelve-metre boat it's the only sensible overnight once there's any wind, and the western gusts make the town quay a daytime-only proposition. Lazy lines, water and power on the berths. Climb the Fortica early, before the heat and the crowds, for the view out over the Pakleni Islands. Then it's the old routine: walk the Arsenal, stand a round in the back lanes, and let the crew loose on the town.
thingsToDo: Climb the Fortica for the Pakleni view
Walk the Arsenal and St Stephen's square
Evening drinks in the back lanes
Tender across to the Pakleni Islands
4.mooringTip: Book ACI Marina Hvar in advance; in high summer it fills days out, and it is the only workable overnight for a mid-size yacht when the wind is up. The town quay takes boats by the hour for a steep fee but sits open to western gusts and tender wash, so don't plan to sleep there.

5.shortDescription: A sheltered dozen miles into the deep bay at Stari Grad, older than any town in Croatia and backed by the Greeks' UNESCO field plain.
5.description: Only a short, sheltered leg today, maybe a dozen miles: you slip round the western end of Hvar and run deep into the long neck of bay that shuts Stari Grad off from the open sea. Nowhere in Croatia has been lived in longer; settlers from Paros founded it as Pharos in 384 BC, and their street grid still runs through the lanes behind the front. The bay is properly protected, closed in like a fjord, so it holds in any wind. Moor stern-to on the town quay with its laid lines, or take the ACI marina on the eastern side. Give the afternoon to Tvrdalj, the poet Hektorović's walled house with its saltwater fishpond, and to the Stari Grad Plain inland, still farmed on the field lines the Greeks measured out. Book a table at Jurin Podrum, running since 1917, for the wild boar and a jug of Plavac.
thingsToDo: Wander Tvrdalj Castle's saltwater fishpond
Walk the Greek field lines on Stari Grad Plain
Wild boar and Plavac at Jurin Podrum
Trace the old Greek grid streets
5.mooringTip: The town quay on the south side has laid moorings, water and power for around sixty boats, with a stretch of buoys behind. ACI Marina Stari Grad on the eastern shore is the all-weather backup. The bay is fjord-shaped and sheltered from every direction, so overnight here is easy in any forecast.

6.shortDescription: Northwest to Maslinica, the one harbour worth the name on Šolta, screened by a row of islets with a castle-hotel on the quay.
6.description: It's a run back toward the mainland to reach Maslinica, but don't file it as a mere convenience stop. It's the one genuine natural harbour the island has, an S-shaped inlet screened by a scatter of islets across the mouth, so it lies quiet when the open coast is anything but. Two ways to tie up: lazy lines at Martinis Marchi on the south side, where an 18th-century castle now runs as a hotel with its terrace open for a drink, or stern-to on the village quay opposite. Šolta is the nearest island to Split and somehow still the quietest of the group. The konobas around the square pour the local Dobričić red and cook with the island's own olive oil. If there's time before dinner, take the tender round to Šešula bay or across to Stipanska for a last swim.
thingsToDo: Drink on the Martinis Marchi castle terrace
Try the local Dobričić red in the square
Tender to Šešula bay for a swim
Swim off Stipanska island at the entrance
6.mooringTip: On the south side, Martinis Marchi offers lazy lines and full services — its boutique berths fill fast, so reserve early in summer. The north-side village quay is stern-to on your own anchor. Next door, Šešula bay makes a fine anchor-and-eat stop, with Konoba Šišmiš laying free buoys for anyone dining ashore.

7.shortDescription: Home run to Kremik along the underside of Šolta and past the Drvenik islands, aiming to be alongside for the Saturday handover.
7.description: Last leg, and it's a working morning. Homeward now, retracing the way out along the south side of Šolta and past the Drvenik islands into ACI Kremik. Handovers here run early, so plan to be alongside by nine with the fuel topped, the tanks pumped out and the decks rinsed. Check your contract, though; some bases want the boat back the evening before, by around five on Friday. Once the inspection is signed off, there's usually an hour to spare before the airport transfer, and Split is only forty-five minutes north. Walk up to the little St George church on the tip of the old Primošten peninsula, or take a long table on the harbour wall with a glass of the local Babić red to close out the week.
thingsToDo: Walk up to St George church, Primošten point
Long lunch on the Primošten harbour wall
Glass of local Babić red before the transfer
Pump out and fuel up before handover
7.mooringTip: Return into ACI Marina Kremik as your charter contract specifies; the base manager assigns the slot on arrival. The base runs handovers early, in the 0800 to 0900 window, and a contract that wants the boat back the night before means being tied up by 1700 on the Friday.

pula-14days.metaTitle: 14-Day Pula to the Zadar Islands Loop | Boat4You
pula-14days.metaDesc: A two-week sailing route from Pula through Istria, Kvarner and the Zadar islands: Cres, Unije, Premuda, Dugi Otok, Pag and Lošinj, with mooring tips.

1.shortDescription: Thirty-five miles, the longest leg of the fortnight, and it lands on day one: Pula to Rabac by way of Cape Kamenjak and the open Kvarner. Rabac sits under Mount Učka, a working fishing harbour that added hotels without ballooning into a strip.
1.description: Pula sends you off past its Roman arena, and then the day is mostly open water. This is the longest single leg of the fortnight, so we usually slip the lines by eight and settle in for the round of Cape Kamenjak before the Kvarner opens up ahead. Rabac curls into a shallow bay at the foot of Labin, the medieval town stacked on the ridge above. Mount Učka stands behind it, catching the last light. Ashore, the promenade runs the length of the shingle, and the konobas do scampi na buzaru properly, shells and all, with bread for the sauce. Walk up to Labin next morning if your legs are willing; the alleys and the belltower view repay the climb.
thingsToDo: Swim off Girandella beach
Climb to Labin's old town and belltower
Scampi na buzaru at a harbour konoba
Drive up Učka for the summit view
1.mooringTip: Rabac's port has only a handful of laid moorings for boats up to about 14 m, and the breakwater shelters north and east but leaves you open to a southerly. If a jugo is forecast, take a berth rather than the bay, and anchor off the northeast corner only in settled weather.

2.shortDescription: Twelve miles east brings you to Valun on Cres, a row of stone houses under the cliff village of Lubenice. Griffon vultures work the thermals over the Tramuntana woods above.
2.description: Short and easy after yesterday's slog. Valun sits in a notch of the western shore, a row of stone houses, a church, and a pebble beach that shelves quickly into clear green water. The restaurants along the front keep mooring buoys for guests, so book a table and the night's berth is settled at once. The real reason to stop is above your head: Lubenice, perched on a 378-metre ridge with the whole Kvarner laid out below. It's an hour's walk up or a short taxi if the heat is fierce. Cres lamb, slow-cooked with island herbs, is the dish to order. Keep an eye out for griffon vultures riding the thermals over the Tramuntana woods to the north.
thingsToDo: Hike the ridge trail up to Lubenice
Swim off Valun's pebble beach
See the Glagolitic Valun Tablet
Cres lamb dinner at a stone konoba
2.mooringTip: Valun's waterfront restaurants lay their own buoys and give them free to anyone dining ashore, which is the easy play here. If the field is full, drop the hook on sand a little further out in seven to ten metres; the bay is calm in the prevailing summer northwesterly.

3.shortDescription: Unije next, southwest and car-free, with fewer than a hundred residents. Maračol Bay gives up a stretch of true sand, which is rare this far up the Adriatic.
3.description: Unije is a slip of an island with fewer than a hundred residents and not a car on it. The single village climbs a low saddle, whitewashed and quiet, with a working fishing quay below. Maračol, the big bay on the western side, is the draw: a broad sandy bottom that holds an anchor beautifully and gives up one of the rare stretches of real sand in these waters. Wind can funnel in from the west by afternoon, so tuck well in. Evenings here are slow. Wander up to the old lighthouse for sunset, then find the family konoba doing brudet, the Adriatic fish stew that every household makes a little differently. Bring cash; there isn't much in the way of card machines.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in sandy Maračol Bay
Walk the car-free path across the island
Sunset from the old lighthouse
Brudet fish stew at a family konoba
3.mooringTip: The village quay is tiny and first-come, so if you want it, arrive by early afternoon. Most crews just anchor in Maračol on good holding sand in four to eight metres; it's snug from the north and northeast but open to the west, so watch the afternoon sea breeze.

4.shortDescription: Seventy people and two tavernas share Premuda, a bare white ridge you could cross on foot in an hour. The pull is underwater: the SMS Szent István lies off the southwest at sixty-five metres, the most famous wreck dive in these waters.
4.description: Premuda is barely more than a limestone spine with a scatter of houses, but it draws divers from across the region. Somewhere off the southwest, at around sixty-five metres, lies the SMS Szent István, the Austro-Hungarian dreadnought torpedoed in 1918 and filmed as she rolled over. That's technical-diving territory, but the shallower Katedrala cave nearby, where light pours through the rock in shafts, is within reach of a guided dive. Above water, Krijal is the anchorage, an open bight facing the sea with a field of buoys and a couple of konobas serving pršut and figs. Sunset over bare white rock has a stark quality you don't get on the greener islands. Settle in early; the buoys go fast in July.
thingsToDo: Guided dive into the Katedrala cave
Walk the limestone ridge trail
Pršut and figs at a Krijal konoba
Watch sunset over bare white rock
4.mooringTip: Krijal has roughly fifty buoys set north and south of the little harbour, and it sits more exposed to open water than most Adriatic anchorages, so a swell can work in. Take a restaurant buoy and eat ashore, and don't linger if a strong southwesterly is on the charts.

5.shortDescription: Molat runs quiet even at the height of summer. We bring up in Zapuntel Bay on the north shore, where twenty winter residents turn into two hundred by August and the coves stay pebble and pine.
5.description: Molat runs quiet even in August. Three villages, Molat, Brgulje, and Zapuntel, are strung together by one narrow lane, and Zapuntel's bay on the north shore is where we like to bring up for the night: pebble coves, a fringe of pine, and water that stays clear over a sandy bottom. There's not a great deal to do, which is rather the point. Stroll the lane between Brgulje and Zapuntel, poke along the shingle for weathered sea glass, and settle in for gregada, the peppery fish-and-potato stew, at whichever konoba is open. After dark the sky here is genuinely black, and the stars come out thick. One caveat: the bay is soft in a southerly but wide open to the bora.
thingsToDo: Walk the lane between Brgulje and Zapuntel
Beachcomb the coves for sea glass
Gregada fish stew at the village konoba
Stargaze under a genuinely dark sky
5.mooringTip: Free anchoring on sand in five to eight metres, with good cover from the south and southeast. The catch is the bora out of the northeast; if one is forecast, don't get caught here overnight, run for the shelter of Zadar or a proper lee bay instead.

6.shortDescription: Round to the north end of Dugi Otok and the shelter of Božava. A mile west, Sakarun shelves over pale sand into milky water; just past it stands Veli Rat, the tallest lighthouse on the Adriatic.
6.description: Dugi Otok, the 'long island', stretches away to the south, and Božava makes a calm base at its northern end. The village itself is small and unhurried, with a shaded harbour and, oddly, a Cold War tunnel bored into the hillside to explore. A mile west lies Sakarun, a shallow bay of pale sand and milky turquoise water that ranks among the best-loved beaches in the country; go early before the day boats arrive. Round the point, Veli Rat lighthouse has stood since 1849 and is the tallest on the Adriatic, its ochre tower worth the walk through the pines. Back in the harbour, order grilled orada straight off the boats. Enjoy the calm; tomorrow's leg turns south down the outer coast.
thingsToDo: Swim the pale sand of Sakarun Bay
Walk to the 1849 Veli Rat lighthouse
Explore Božava's Cold War tunnel
Grilled orada on the harbour front
6.mooringTip: Božava's harbour quay is small and fills in season, so call ahead or plan to anchor. Sakarun, a mile west, gives lovely holding on sand in four to six metres but is exposed to the north, making it a daytime stop rather than an overnight in unsettled weather.

7.shortDescription: Down the outer shore to Sali, the biggest village on Dugi Otok and the way into Telašćica park. Hit the first weekend of August and you land in the Saljske užance, donkey races and all.
7.description: Sali is the busiest place on Dugi Otok, which on this island still means a fishing town with a couple of quays and a handful of konobas. It's the natural staging post for Telašćica just to the south, so many crews arrive mid-afternoon, provision, and set up for an early park run tomorrow. If your dates fall on the first weekend of August, you land in the middle of Saljske užance, the village's own festival, with donkey races along the quay and the famous tovareća mužika, a band that plays ox horns and cowbells and takes itself not at all seriously. Order pašticada, the sweet-and-sour braised beef, and let the evening run long. Book a marina slot ahead in peak weeks; the town quay fills and empties all day.
thingsToDo: Provision for a Telašćica day-trip
Catch the Saljske užance donkey races
Hear the tovareća mužika ox-horn band
Pašticada braised beef at a konoba
7.mooringTip: Sali's port takes around ninety boats across two quays with laid lines, water, and power, and there is an ACI berth option too. In July and August book ahead; the quay turns over through the day, so a slot that's full at noon may open by evening.

8.shortDescription: Into Telašćica itself: a fjord of an inlet, the Stene cliffs falling over 160 metres, and the warm Mir salt lake behind them. Four miles on lies Žut, where the Kornati chain begins.
8.description: Telašćica is the payoff for the run south. The inlet cuts eight kilometres into Dugi Otok, sheltered and mild on its inner side, while the seaward flank drops off the Stene cliffs, some 160 metres straight into deep blue. Take the short walk over the ridge to Mir, a saltwater lake warmer and heavier than the sea, its muddy shallows said to be good for the skin. You'll pay a park fee at the buoy, and it's money well spent. In the afternoon we usually cross the few miles east to Žut, the top island of the Kornati chain, and pick up a restaurant buoy in one of its inlets. Grill whatever the konoba sells you, and stay up for the Milky Way; there's no light out here to dim it.
thingsToDo: Swim the warm saltwater Mir lake
See the Stene cliffs from below
Pick up a restaurant buoy in Žut
Grill fresh fish under the Milky Way
8.mooringTip: Inside the park the buoys and anchorages are free to use, but you'll buy a Telašćica entry ticket at the mooring. Over on Žut there is no marina; take a konoba buoy and the fee comes off with dinner, and reserve by radio in high summer when the fields fill early.

9.shortDescription: Back to the mainland at Biograd, a coronation town for medieval Croatian kings and now one of the coast's busiest charter bases. Two marinas share a single breakwater, so a berth is rarely the worry here.
9.description: Biograd sits on a low peninsula facing the Pašman channel, and after a run of tiny islands it feels almost metropolitan. The name means 'white town', and it once crowned Croatian kings; these days it's one of the biggest charter bases on the coast, with Marina Kornati and Marina Šangulin sharing a single entrance and around a thousand berths between them. Walk the paved Riva in the evening, look into the stone churches and the small heritage museum, and if you've a spare morning, drive inland to Vrana Lake, the largest natural lake in the country and a haven for herons and marsh birds. For dinner, crni rižot, the inky squid-ink risotto, is the local order. Provision properly here; the shops are good and the next islands are lean.
thingsToDo: Stroll the stone-paved Biograd Riva
Look into the town heritage museum
Drive to Vrana Lake for the birdlife
Crni rižot at Konoba Barbara
9.mooringTip: Marina Kornati and Marina Šangulin sit side by side inside the town breakwater and share a thousand-odd berths, so there's usually room, but reserve for July and August when the fleet turns over on Saturdays. Both are full-service, which makes this a sensible spot to refuel and re-provision.

10.shortDescription: North to Zadar, the old capital of upper Dalmatia. The Sea Organ groans under the marble steps and the Greeting to the Sun lights the pavement after dark, and both pull a crowd at sunset.
10.description: Zadar is the old capital of northern Dalmatia, and it wears its history plainly: a Roman forum, a round pre-Romanesque church, and Venetian gates all within a short walk. What everyone comes for sits at the tip of the peninsula. The Sea Organ, a set of pipes built under the marble steps, turns the swell into a low, wandering drone that changes with the sea. Beside it, the Greeting to the Sun, a disc of solar panels set flush in the pavement, lights up after dark. Both draw a crowd at sunset, and it's worth being there. This is a good town for provisioning and a proper dinner. Try a glass of Maraschino, the cherry liqueur that Zadar has distilled for centuries, before you head back out to the islands.
thingsToDo: Hear the Sea Organ at sunset
Watch the Greeting to the Sun light up
Walk the Roman Forum and St Donatus
Maraschino tasting at Konoba Skoblar
10.mooringTip: The usual berth is Marina Borik, a couple of kilometres northwest of the old town, with Tankerkomerc as a second option. The quay along the old town is for ferries and fishing boats, not visiting yachts, so don't plan on lying alongside there.

11.shortDescription: Twelve miles to Olib, which most crews sail straight past: under two hundred residents, sandy bays at Slatina and Banve, and olibške makarule, a pasta rolled nowhere else.
11.description: Olib gets missed by most itineraries, which is exactly why it's worth the detour. Under two hundred people live here year-round, scattered among stone villages that empty out the moment summer ends. The coast is unusually soft for the Adriatic: Slatina and Banve are proper sandy bays over a pale, bright seabed, shallow and warm for swimming. Rent or borrow a bike and ride the interior lanes past drystone walls and abandoned houses reclaimed by fig and pomegranate. At lunch, hunt down olibške makarule, a hand-rolled pasta the older women still make by the hour, dressed simply with island lamb or a tomato sauce. It's the kind of day where nothing is scheduled and that's the whole appeal. Anchor off Slatina and swim before dinner.
thingsToDo: Swim the sandy Slatina and Banve bays
Cycle the lanes past empty stone hamlets
Olibške makarule pasta for lunch
Beachcomb the shallows for sea glass
11.mooringTip: Olib's fishing harbour has only a few visitor slots, so treat it as a maybe rather than a plan. Most crews anchor off Slatina on good sand in four to six metres, well tucked from the north and northeast, and it's a fine place to lie for the night in settled summer weather.

12.shortDescription: Fourteen miles to Novalja on Pag, longest and barest of Croatia's islands. Sheep cheese and sea salt on one hand, the all-night clubs of Zrće a short taxi away on the other.
12.description: Pag is a study in contrasts, and Novalja sits right at the hinge of them. The island's western hills are stripped bare by the bora into a pale, stony moonscape, grazed by sheep that give Pag its two famous exports: paški sir, the hard, salty sheep's cheese, and salt itself. Drive out to the Gligora dairy for a proper tasting, or up to Lun to walk among wild olive trees that have stood for centuries. Then there's Zrće, a fifteen-minute taxi from the harbour, a shingle strip of open-air clubs that runs until sunrise and pulls crowds from across the continent. You can do one, the other, or both. Sober up with a plate of grilled lamb; Pag's is reckoned the best in Croatia.
thingsToDo: Taste paški sir at the Gligora dairy
Walk the ancient olive grove at Lun
Dance until sunrise on Zrće Beach
Grilled Pag lamb for dinner
12.mooringTip: Novalja has a town quay and a small marina, both of which fill in July and August, so book if you can. For a quiet night away from the club noise, anchor in one of the coves outside town and take a taxi in and out of Zrće.

13.shortDescription: Mali Lošinj lies twenty miles off, back in busy water and the liveliest port up this end of the Adriatic. Pastel captains' houses ring the inlet, and the pine air earned the island a 'climatic spa' name more than a century ago.
13.description: Back into busier waters. Mali Lošinj wraps around a long, narrow inlet, its quay lined with the pastel townhouses that captains built when this was a shipbuilding power. It's the liveliest harbour on the upper Adriatic, and after a week of empty bays the bustle is rather welcome. Make time for the Museum of Apoxyomenos, purpose-built around a single bronze athlete pulled from the seabed after two thousand years down there; it's beautifully done. The island trades on its air, all Aleppo pine and sea salt, and long ago earned a reputation as a climate cure. Walk or cycle the Čikat woods to the west, paddle the harbour front at dusk, and put together one last seafood spread. Tomorrow is the long haul home.
thingsToDo: See the bronze Apoxyomenos in its museum
Cycle the pine-scented Čikat woods
Paddleboard the harbour at dusk
A last seafood spread on the Riva
13.mooringTip: ACI Marina Mali Lošinj is the reliable booking in summer and worth reserving well ahead; town-quay places are first-come and go early. If you'd rather anchor, Krivica and Balvanida bays on the southwest coast hold well and stay calm even in a bora.

14.shortDescription: The run home mirrors day one: thirty-five miles over the Kvarner under the west cliffs of Cres. Susak, roughly halfway, offers a last swim off real sand dunes before Pula and handover.
14.description: The last day is the mirror of the first, a long passage back across the Kvarner Gulf under the tall western cliffs of Cres. Get the anchor up early and use the morning breeze before the afternoon maestral builds against you. Susak lies roughly halfway and makes a fine final swim: it's the one island in Croatia made of deep, fine sand rather than rock, its low hills draped in reed grass and old vineyards. From there it's a straight run in to Pula. Most crews hand back at Marina Veruda just south of the city, though the town marina takes charter boats too. Leave time to refuel, and if you can, walk up to the Roman amphitheatre for a last look before you fly out.
thingsToDo: Final swim off Susak's sand dunes
Sail the morning breeze before the maestral
Tour the Pula Roman amphitheatre
Clean and pack for handover
14.mooringTip: Plan the handover at Marina Veruda or the city marina in Pula, both full-service and used to charter turnarounds. Book your fuel-dock slot a day ahead if you can, since the queue backs up on Friday and Saturday mornings when the whole fleet is coming in.

pula.metaDesc: A seven-day yacht route from Pula through the Kvarner islands: Medulin, Unije, Vele Srakane, Cres and Rabac, with distances, moorings and konoba tips.

1.shortDescription: A short eight-mile shakedown south from Pula around Cape Kamenjak into the broad, shallow sweep of Medulin Bay.
1.description: The first leg is deliberately gentle: eight miles south from the base, rounding the tip of the Premantura peninsula into Medulin Bay. Kamenjak is a protected reserve, so keep clear of the seagrass meadows when you drop the hook. We usually berth stern-to at Marina Medulin, which takes boats up to twenty metres and has fuel, water and 220-volt power on the pontoons. If the bay is calm, anchoring outside in four to eight metres of sand works well and buys a quieter swim. Ashore there is not much beyond a low-key village, which suits a first evening. Walk out onto the cape at dusk; over thirty small coves cut into the limestone, and the light going down behind Brijuni is worth the stroll. Order the brodet at a waterfront table.
thingsToDo: Walk the Cape Kamenjak coastal path at dusk
Anchor off the marina for a quiet swim
Order brodet at a Medulin waterfront table
Watch the sunset behind the Brijuni islands
1.mooringTip: Marina Medulin is the reliable choice in any wind and worth booking ahead in July and August; it holds boats to twenty metres with fuel on site. Anchoring outside on sand at four to eight metres is fine except in a southerly, and keep off the protected seagrass.

2.shortDescription: The long open leg of the week: Unije sits twenty miles out in the Kvarner, a car-free island of olive terraces with one uncommon patch of sand on its far shore.
2.description: This is the day for open water. Unije lies twenty miles off to the southeast with nothing to duck behind, so I like to slip the lines by nine and let the maestral fill in behind us through the afternoon. It's the biggest of the Lošinj outer islands and refreshingly plain: no cars, one village stacked above its harbour, olive terraces running down to the rock. Tie stern-to on the village quay with your own anchor for a small fee; there is water on the central berths but no power, so charge up before you arrive. The island's best swim is the sandy cove on the far shore, a lunchtime anchorage rather than an overnight. Walk the track up to the little chapel on the spine for the view, then eat grilled lamb by the water.
thingsToDo: Swim at the sandy cove across the island
Eat grilled lamb at a quayside konoba
Walk the track to the hilltop chapel
Snorkel the clear rock ledges below the village
2.mooringTip: Back stern-to onto the Unije village quay under your own anchor; expect a small charge, water at the central berths, and no shore power. Shelter comes off the western quarter while the southeast stays open, so if a jugo above fifteen knots is forecast, motor six miles over to Mali Lošinj.

3.shortDescription: Six miles to Vele Srakane, one of Croatia's smallest inhabited islands — a lunchtime swim, then on to a sheltered night.
3.description: Barely an hour of sailing, but a genuine oddity. Vele Srakane covers just over a square kilometre and counts a handful of permanent residents; there are no roads, no shops, no running water, and the whole place is worked as one organic smallholding. There is no proper harbour either, only a couple of rough concrete piers and a lot of reef, so treat it as a daytime stop and nothing more. Anchor off the eastern shore in five to eight metres over sand and weed, and swim in water so clear you can count the anchor chain on the bottom. Wander the single path through the olives if the heat allows. By late afternoon, lift the hook and make for the protected harbour at Mali Lošinj, or double back the six miles to Unije for the night.
thingsToDo: Anchor off the east shore for swimming
Snorkel the exceptionally clear, reef-lined shallows
Walk the single path through the olive groves
Move on to Mali Lošinj before evening
3.mooringTip: Vele Srakane is a daytime anchorage only, with no shelter from the west or northwest and reefs close in, so keep a good lookout on the approach. Plan to overnight five miles south at Marina Mali Lošinj or back at Unije.

4.shortDescription: Martinšćica is a working fishing village on the west coast of Cres, twelve miles up the water and the way in to the Tramuntana forest and its vultures.
4.description: Cres is twelve miles to the northeast, the biggest island in the country and shaped like a long thin splinter of rock. Martinšćica is a working village with a stone quay where you moor stern-to for a modest fee, water on the central berths. Holding in the bay runs three to six metres and it tucks in nicely from the bora, though a southwesterly sends swell straight in. The reason to stop is inland.
