The north of the island holds the Tramuntana, an old oak and holm forest laced with something like a hundred kilometres of marked trail, and one of Europe's last colonies of griffon vulture nests on the cliffs above Beli. For dinner, book a table and drive over to Konoba Bukaleta at Loznati, where the family raises its own lambs and roasts them on the spit.
thingsToDo: Hike the Tramuntana forest trails from Beli
See the griffon vultures at the Beli centre
Book Cres lamb at Konoba Bukaleta, Loznati
Swim off the rocks below the village
4.mooringTip: Go stern-to at Martinšćica's stone quay on your own anchor; the central berths carry water, and the fee is small. Shelter runs from north through east, but the southwest is exposed, so read the forecast before committing to the night.

5.shortDescription: Cres Town sits at the head of a deep, all-weather bay round the north end of the island, fifteen miles on, with an ACI marina hard against the old Venetian quarter.
5.description: Round the top of the island, another fifteen miles, and you slip into one of the safest harbours in the Kvarner, a long bay that shuts out the weather from every quarter. ACI Marina Cres sits right at the town wall with lazy lines, fuel and full services, open the year round; the town quay only takes visitors for the day. Cres wears its Venetian centuries openly. The old core still sits inside its medieval walls, and you come in through the stone gates onto Frane Petrić square. Climb the clock tower for the look down over the masts and red roofs. The fifteenth-century loggia still stands where the town once read out its notices. Eat the island janjetina in a courtyard, and swim from the pebble coves a short walk south of the marina.
thingsToDo: Climb the sixteenth-century clock tower for the view
Walk the walls and gates onto Frane Petrić square
See the old loggia in the town centre
Swim from the pebble coves south of the marina
Order Cres janjetina in a courtyard konoba
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Cres is the all-weather berth, lazy lines and fuel at the town wall, open all year; the town quay is daytime visitors only. The bay is well shut in from every wind, so this is the place to sit out a bora night if one is brewing.

6.shortDescription: Rabac is a pebble-beach resort bay on the Istrian side, fifteen miles back across the Kvarner, with medieval Labin perched on the ridge behind it.
6.description: Point the bow due west and it's fifteen miles back to the Istrian mainland at Rabac. It is a small harbour rather than a big marina, on the south side of the bay, with lazy lines, water and power and enough services for an easy night. The swimming here is the draw: pale pebble beaches strung along the Lungomare, the shelving stone quick to deep water. The real detour is uphill. Labin, the old town on the ridge behind the resort, is ten minutes by taxi or the shuttle bus, and its fourteenth-century walls and narrow lanes make a good late-afternoon wander once the sun drops off the water. Back down at sea level, order scampi buzara at a table over the bay, and pick up a jar of Istrian truffle paste before you leave.
thingsToDo: Ride up to medieval Labin old town
Walk the Lungomare between the pebble beaches
Order scampi buzara over the bay
Buy Istrian truffle products up in Labin
6.mooringTip: Marina Rabac on the south side takes you stern-to with lazy lines and full services. The bay shelters from the north, northeast and west but opens to the south, so it is fine in the usual summer maestral and less so in a southerly blow.

7.shortDescription: The run home to Pula for the Saturday handover, twenty miles southwest, with a last swim off Trget in the long Raša inlet on the way.
7.description: The last leg is twenty miles southwest to the base. Most contracts want the boat back by nine on Saturday with fuel topped, tanks pumped and the inventory checked, so leave enough of a margin and do the chores the evening before if you can. The course runs past the mouth of the Raša, a long thin inlet that cuts deep into the coast like a fjord; a final swim off Trget on its western side is the traditional way to close the week. Once the handover is signed off, you are five minutes on foot from the Roman amphitheatre, one of the largest still standing anywhere, and a short walk further to the Temple of Augustus on the old forum. Pula airport is a quarter of an hour by car if you are flying out the same day.
thingsToDo: Take a last swim off Trget in Raša Bay
Top up fuel and pump out before handover
Walk to the Roman amphitheatre in Pula
See the Temple of Augustus on the forum
7.mooringTip: Return into ACI Marina Pula as your charter contract sets out and let the base manager place you; the Saturday handover window is usually eight to nine. If your contract asks for a night-before return, aim to be alongside by late Friday afternoon.

punat-14days.metaDesc: Two weeks round-trip from Punat through Kvarner and Istria: Rab, Pag, Susak, Lošinj, Pula and Opatija, with real anchorages and konoba picks.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 18-mile shakedown south from Punat to Rab's Lopar peninsula, where the Adriatic hands you a rare stretch of real sand.
1.description: The first day is deliberately short, which is how a first day should be. You clear Marina Punat, turn south down the Krk coast, and by early afternoon the Lopar headland opens up ahead. This corner of Rab is unusual for the north Adriatic: proper sand, not pebble. Rajska plaža runs about a kilometre and a half and shelves so gently that kids can wade out fifty metres. West of it, the quieter Sahara coves draw the clothing-optional crowd. Ashore, the bakeries sell Rapska torta, a coiled almond cake the island has made for centuries. We usually anchor off San Marino bay and take the tender in. If the crew wants a proper town evening, Rab itself, with its four bell towers, is a short hop further.
thingsToDo: Wade out along Rajska plaža sand
Swim the quieter Sahara coves
Rapska torta from a Lopar bakery
Evening walk under Rab's four campaniles
1.mooringTip: Anchor off San Marino (Crnika) bay on sand in 4–6 m, well held and shielded from the northeast. Skip the Lopar pier; it works for the ferries, and for a proper town berth you carry on to Rab.

2.shortDescription: A short skip across to Pag, where Novalja runs the Adriatic's loudest summer nightlife against a backdrop of bare limestone and prized sheep cheese.
2.description: Pag looks like nowhere else on the coast. The bora scours its northeast flank bare, leaving pale rock and low sage where you expect pine. Sheep graze on salt-dusted herbs, and that is the whole secret of Paški sir, the hard, piquant cheese the Gligora dairy up in Kolan will happily let you taste through a dozen ages. Novalja fills up fast in July. Zrće beach, a short taxi north of the harbour, runs on a different clock; clubs like Papaya and Aquarius only warm up after midnight. If that is not your crew, the Lun peninsula at the island's tip hides a grove of wild olives, some of them over a thousand years old, and stays blissfully quiet. Provision here while you can; the supermarket near the quay is well stocked.
thingsToDo: Taste aged Paški sir at Gligora dairy
Night out at Papaya or Aquarius
Walk the ancient Lun olive grove
Provision at the Novalja quay market
2.mooringTip: Novalja has a town quay plus a small marina, and both fill in summer, so call a day or two ahead. For a calm night, anchor in one of the coves just south of the entrance.

3.shortDescription: The longest open-water leg, 25 miles west to Susak, a soft yellow hump of sand rather than rock, with under a hundred people living on it.
3.description: Leave early. Twenty-five miles is a full sailing day if the maestral fills in late, and you want to be anchored before it pipes up in the afternoon. Susak stands alone off the western edge of the Lošinj group, piled up from wind-carried loess instead of the limestone every other island here is cut from, which is why its paths run soft and yellow underfoot and its hills roll instead of jut. The permanent population would fill a small café. On feast days the older women still turn out in the short, vivid skirts the island is known for, and šokol, the local cured meat, lands on most tables. There is no glamour here, and that is the point. Swim off the shallow Bok bay beach, walk up to the lighthouse, and watch a sunset with almost no one else around.
thingsToDo: Swim the shallow Bok bay shelf
Walk the soft loess footpaths
Try šokol with the islanders
Sunset from the Susak lighthouse
3.mooringTip: The village harbour holds only a handful of boats, so most crews anchor in Bok bay on sand in 4–6 m. It shelters you from the jugo but stays open to the bora and the afternoon maestral, so watch the forecast.

4.shortDescription: A quick six miles east to Mali Lošinj, the scented harbour town whose museum guards a single 2,000-year-old bronze athlete.
4.description: After Susak's stillness, Mali Lošinj feels like a city. The deep bay funnels you past pastel captains' houses to one of the north Adriatic's busiest waterfronts. Give the morning to the Apoxyomenos museum in the old Kvarner Palace: one room, one exhibit, a life-size bronze of an athlete scraping oil from his skin, hauled from the seabed in 1999 and worth every minute. Lošinj sells itself as the island of vitality, a claim the pine-scented air at Čikat has backed up since Habsburg doctors began sending patients here in the 1890s. Walk the aromatic trail there in the late afternoon. Back on the riva, the fish restaurants know their trade; ask what came in that morning rather than reading the menu. Refuel and top up water while you have a proper marina.
thingsToDo: See the Apoxyomenos bronze up close
Walk the Čikat pine-scented trail
Dinner from the day's catch on the riva
Top up water and fuel in town
4.mooringTip: ACI Marina Mali Lošinj and Marina Čikat both take charter boats, so book in high season. Note that anchoring in Čikat bay itself is not permitted; plan on a berth.

5.shortDescription: Seven gentle miles northwest to Unije, a low, car-free island of olive terraces with a golden bay at Maracol that has no business being this far up the Adriatic.
5.description: Unije is the sort of place that talks people into extending charters. No cars, one village of white houses above a small port, and a shoreline of olive terraces running down to the water. Most crews come for Maracol bay on the west side, where the sand is genuine and rare enough this far north to feel like a small event. The single road across the island doubles as a walking track through fig and rosemary. Time it right and you can eat brudet, the slow fisherman's stew, at one of the family konobas near the harbour while the light goes copper over the channel. Cover the leg in the morning; by lunch you can be swimming. It is a short day on purpose, and nobody complains.
thingsToDo: Swim the golden sand at Maracol
Walk the island's single cross-path
Brudet stew at a harbour konoba
Sunset over the Unije channel
5.mooringTip: The little port takes a few boats first-come and can be full by evening. Anchoring in Maracol bay on sand in 4–6 m is the safer bet, well protected except from the west.

6.shortDescription: Twenty-five open miles from the islands across to the Istrian mainland and Pula, the region's Roman capital, where a 2,000-year-old amphitheatre still fills for summer concerts.
6.description: This is the second long hop, and it changes the character of the trip. You leave the islands behind and close on the Istrian mainland, with Pula's skyline announced from miles out by the pale ring of its arena. The amphitheatre is one of the best-preserved anywhere, still standing to its full height and still in use, film festival in July, concerts through the summer. Walk up from the harbour through the old town and you pass the Arch of the Sergii and the little Temple of Augustus almost in passing. Swim off the Verudela peninsula in the afternoon; the coves there are easy to reach by tender. Istrian cooking leans on truffle and the local Malvazija white, and plenty of inland konobas will run a taxi down to the marina for you.
thingsToDo: Tour the Roman arena in Pula
Find the Arch of the Sergii
Swim the Verudela peninsula coves
Truffle pasta with a Malvazija white
6.mooringTip: Marina Veruda and Marina Pula are both full-service and the sensible overnight, so reserve in July and August. The city quay is for short daytime stops only.

7.shortDescription: Just six miles down the coast to Medulin, gateway to the wild Kamenjak headland and one of Croatia's most talked-about fish taverns.
7.description: Medulin is a short reach around the southern tip of Istria, and the draw is the land more than the town. Cape Kamenjak, the reserve that ends the peninsula, is a tangle of low scrub, dirt tracks and small emerald coves like Kolombarica, with actual dinosaur footprints set in the rock near the shore. Rent bikes or just walk; the swimming is the reward. The evening belongs to Konoba Batelina over in Banjole, run by the Skoko fishing family and cooked by David Skoko from whatever the boat landed that day. It opens at five, takes cash only, closes on Sundays, and books out days ahead in season, so ring first. Order the offcuts and odd fish the menu barely names; that is where the kitchen shows off.
thingsToDo: Hike Cape Kamenjak's coastal tracks
Find the dinosaur footprints in rock
Swim the emerald Kolombarica cove
Book dinner at Konoba Batelina
7.mooringTip: Berth at Medulin's marina or nearby Marina Veruda; for a quiet night, anchor in Bijeca bay on sand in 3–5 m. Reserve Batelina before you arrive, not after.

8.shortDescription: A 22-mile leg around Istria's southern cape and up its eastern shore to Rabac, a bright cove village under the bulk of Mount Učka.
8.description: Round the cape at Kamenjak and the coast turns green and steep. Rabac sits in a sheltered bay at the foot of Labin, its little harbour tucked behind a long natural breakwater that keeps the northerlies out. The village itself is holiday-bright, with a curving seafront path and pebble coves like Girandella good for an afternoon swim. What makes the anchorage memorable is the backdrop: Učka rises almost straight up behind, the tallest thing on this stretch of the Istrian coast. Spend an hour walking up to Labin, the old town on the hill above, all steep lanes and a view back down to your boat. Come evening, the seafront bars pour Istrian Malvazija, and the water in the bay usually flattens to glass once the day breeze drops.
thingsToDo: Swim the pebbles at Girandella cove
Climb to medieval Labin old town
Walk the curving Rabac seafront path
Malvazija on the harbour at dusk
8.mooringTip: Rabac's small marina is the overnight option; the town quay is short-stay only. The bay is well shielded from north and east, but book a berth in peak weeks as space is tight.

9.shortDescription: North around the Kvarner shore to Opatija, the grand old Habsburg spa town, and its famous twelve-kilometre seafront promenade.
9.description: Opatija was where Vienna came to take the sea air in the 1800s, and it still wears that inheritance: ornate villas, manicured parks, a promenade built for slow evening walks in good clothes. The Lungomare runs twelve kilometres along the water from the fishing quay at Volosko, through Opatija and Ičići, down to Lovran, and an hour of it before dinner is time well spent. Villa Angiolina and its botanical garden sit right above the front. The kitchens here take Istrian cooking a notch smarter than the island konobas; fuzi pasta with truffle is the thing to order on a terrace over the water. The marina at Ičići is a short walk or taxi from the centre, so it is easy to leave the boat and make a proper night of it ashore.
thingsToDo: Walk the Lungomare toward Lovran
Stroll Villa Angiolina's botanical garden
Fuzi with truffle on a seafront terrace
Evening drinks along the Opatija front
9.mooringTip: ACI Marina Opatija sits in Ičići, a few minutes south of the centre and open year-round. It is the standard berth here; the Opatija town front is not set up for overnight charter stays.

10.shortDescription: A short hop across the bay to Rijeka, Croatia's gritty third city, a working port with a hilltop castle and a real urban pulse.
10.description: Rijeka is a change of gear, and a welcome one after a week of small harbours. This is a real city, a container port with cranes and cargo ships, and a cultural streak it earned as European Capital of Culture in 2020. Climb to Trsat castle on the ridge above the river for the long view down over the rooftops and the water. Back at sea level, the Korzo is the pedestrian spine where the whole town seems to drink coffee at once. Head to the covered fish market near the centre for Kvarner scampi, the sweet local prawns that show up in half the kitchens along this coast. Rijeka rewards curiosity more than a checklist; give it an afternoon of wandering the side streets and reading the Austrian, Italian and Yugoslav layers in the facades.
thingsToDo: Climb to Trsat castle above the river
Coffee on the Korzo pedestrian street
Kvarner scampi at the covered fish market
Wander the layered old-town facades
10.mooringTip: Central charter berthing in Rijeka is limited and sits inside a busy commercial port, so confirm space before you commit. Many crews instead run back to ACI Marina Opatija at Ičići for a calmer, protected night.

11.shortDescription: Twelve miles south to Crikvenica, a mild seaside town backed by the Vinodol valley's vineyards, with Roman ruins lying just off the shore.
11.description: Crikvenica has a gentle, unhurried feel after the port bustle of Rijeka. The Vinodol valley opens behind it, a strip of vineyards and citrus worked since the middle ages; the 1288 Vinodol charter signed nearby is one of the oldest legal texts written in Croatian. Off the Kaštel headland, the submerged Roman settlement of Ad Turres still shows on the seabed in a few metres of clear water, an easy snorkel if the day is calm. The town beaches shelve slowly and suit a lazy afternoon. Ashore, order škampi na buzaru, prawns simmered in white wine, garlic and tomato, with bread to mop the pan. It is the kind of stop that lowers everyone's pulse before the more exposed run down to Senj tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the sunken Ad Turres ruins
Visit a Vinodol valley winery
Škampi na buzaru at a harbour konoba
Slow afternoon on the town beach
11.mooringTip: Crikvenica's marina is small, so book ahead in summer. In settled, light air you can lie at anchor off the town, but move inside or push on if any jugo is forecast.

12.shortDescription: A 20-mile run under the Velebit wall to Senj, the old Uskok stronghold and the most bura-exposed anchorage on the whole loop.
12.description: Read the forecast carefully before this one. Senj sits at the mouth of the Velebit channel, the single most bura-prone stretch of the whole Adriatic, where cold air pours off the mountains and can go from calm to fifty knots in an hour. On a settled day the run down is fine and the town is worth it. Nehaj fortress, a square stone keep built in 1558, stands on the hill above the harbour; from its walls the Uskok raiders once watched for Ottoman and Venetian ships. Inside is a small museum that tells their story well. The old lanes below are made for slow wandering, and the konobas serve maneštra, a thick bean-and-vegetable stew that suits a cool evening under the Velebit wall. Keep the boat ready to move if the wind shifts.
thingsToDo: Tour the Nehaj fortress museum
Wander Senj's medieval stone lanes
Maneštra bean stew at a konoba
Read the bura forecast twice
12.mooringTip: Senj's harbour quay is really only for short daytime stops and offers little protection once the bura fills in. For a secure night, plan a marina at Stinica or back at Crikvenica, and never sit here through a bura warning.

13.shortDescription: Cross back over to Baška on Krk's south coast, where a vast pebble beach sits beside one of the earliest stones inscribed in Croatian.
13.description: Crossing back toward Krk, the scenery softens again. Baška sits in a wide bay on the island's southern shore, closed in by bare hills that catch the evening light. Its beach, Vela plaža, runs close to two kilometres of fine pebble and is one of the longest on the Adriatic, so even a busy day rarely feels crowded. Behind the town, marked trails climb onto the stony Moon plateau, where medieval hermits left Glagolitic inscriptions carved into the rock. The Baška tablet, one of the earliest records of the Croatian language, was found in the little church of St Lucy at nearby Jurandvor; a copy stands where the original once did. Eat lamb here, ideally slow-cooked under the peka, and pair it with Žlahtina, the crisp white from Vrbnik across the island.
thingsToDo: Walk the long Vela plaža pebbles
Hike the Glagolitic Moon plateau trail
See the Baška tablet copy at Jurandvor
Lamb under the peka with Žlahtina
13.mooringTip: Baška has a small marina and a town quay, so reserve in peak weeks. Anchoring off the beach works only in light air, since the bay lies open to the south and southeast and gets rolly fast.

14.shortDescription: The final 18 miles, a last swim off Rab and a curl back into the huge sheltered bay of Punat where the trip began.
14.description: Two weeks aboard end where they started, but there is no need to rush the last morning. Most crews swing past Rab for a final sandy swim near Lopar and a walk under the town's four bell towers before the run home. From there it is a straightforward passage back toward Krk. Punat lies inside one of the largest natural harbours on the Adriatic, a near-enclosed bay with the wooded islet of Košljun and its Franciscan monastery sitting in the middle of it. Marina Punat, the base you left on day one, is among the oldest and biggest in the country, working since 1964, with its own boatyard and fuel quay. Fit in a last toast of Žlahtina from Vrbnik before the handover, then leave the cleaning to tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Final sandy swim off Rab's Lopar
Walk under Rab's four bell towers
Pass the Košljun islet monastery
Žlahtina toast before the Punat handover
14.mooringTip: Marina Punat is full-service with its own boatyard and fuel dock. Request your fuel slot about a day ahead and confirm the return berth before your final approach, since the marina is large and busy.

punat-mali-losinj-route.metaDesc: Sail a 7-day loop from Punat through Rab, Pag, Susak, Mali Lošinj, Unije and Cres: Kvarner's sandy beaches, car-free islands and quiet anchorages.

1.shortDescription: An easy first leg south from Marina Punat to Rab's sandy northern tip, where Paradise Beach runs a full kilometre and a half.
1.description: Most crews cast off from Marina Punat by mid-morning, once the fuel and water are sorted. The run down to Lopar is short and forgiving, roughly three hours if the Maestral fills in behind you. Rab's northern tip is unusual for this coast: real sand, not shingle. Rajska plaža, the Paradise Beach, stretches a kilometre and a half and shelves so gently that families wade far out before it reaches their waists. Behind it the Sahara dunes hide a quieter, wilder stretch for anyone willing to walk. Climb the low hill to the old cross for a wide view over the Kvarner, then head into the village for a slice of Rapska torta, the almond cake local families have baked for generations. Not a demanding day. That is rather the point on day one.
thingsToDo: Wade out along Paradise Beach shallows
Find quiet sand at the Sahara dunes
Walk up to Lopar's hilltop cross
Slice of Rapska torta in the village
1.mooringTip: Overnight at anchor in San Marino Bay, which shelters well from the northerly Bura; leave the Lopar pier to the ferries and taxi boats rather than counting on a charter berth.

2.shortDescription: A short southwest crossing to Novalja, Pag's summer capital, where salt-bleached hills, ancient olives and Zrće's nightlife sit side by side.
2.description: Pag reveals itself long before you dock. The hills run bare and pale, scoured by the Bura until little grows but sage and low scrub, and the salt in the wind is part of why the island's cheese tastes the way it does. Novalja sits on a deep western inlet behind a long breakwater; the town keeps the party crowd busy at Zrće, a short hop across the neck of the island. There is a gentler Pag too. Drive or taxi up to Lun, where a grove of wild olives has stood for centuries, some trunks twisted past recognition. Book a tasting at the Gligora dairy near Kolan and you will understand why Paški sir wins medals abroad. Pair it with the island's roast lamb in the evening. The contrast between the two Novaljas is the whole appeal.
thingsToDo: Taste Paški sir at Gligora dairy
Walk the ancient olive grove at Lun
Beach day or nightlife at Zrće
Slow-roasted Pag lamb for dinner
2.mooringTip: The town quay behind Novalja's breakwater has power and water but fills fast in July and August, so radio ahead or arrive early. The bay opens to the west, so a secure berth beats anchoring if a storm threatens.

3.shortDescription: The route's longest passage, a 25-mile haul west to Susak, the Adriatic's one true sand island, built entirely from windblown soil.
3.description: This is the day to leave early. Twenty-five miles is the longest single hop on the route, and you want the passage done before the afternoon Maestral kicks up chop across the open Kvarner. Susak rewards the effort. There is nothing else quite like it on this coast: no rock, just deep loess sand that the wind piled here over millennia, threaded with paths through reed and vine. Fewer than a hundred people live here year-round, and on feast days the older women still wear the short, bright skirts the island is known for. Anchor off Bok, swim in water that shelves gently over pale sand, then climb to the little chapel above the harbour for the light going down. Dinner is whatever the konoba landed that morning, with a šokol starter and a shot of homemade rakija.
thingsToDo: Swim the shallow sand at Bok Bay
Wander the reed-lined sand paths
Climb to the harbour chapel at sunset
Šokol and rakija at a family konoba
3.mooringTip: Susak's fishing harbour has only a handful of slots, so most crews anchor in Bok Bay on good-holding sand in four to six metres. It covers you from the Bura and Jugo but stays open to the west, so watch the forecast.

4.shortDescription: A quick six-mile hop east into Mali Lošinj, the sheltered harbour town whose pine-scented Čikat air earned it a climatic-spa reputation.
4.description: After Susak's quiet, Mali Lošinj feels almost metropolitan, and the six miles across take barely an hour. The long, protected harbour has been a sailors' town for two centuries, its captains' houses painted in faded ochre and pink along the waterfront. Drop the boat and give the afternoon to the Apoxyomenos museum, built around a single bronze: a Greek athlete hauled from the seabed off the island in 1999 after two thousand years underwater. It is worth the entrance fee. Later, follow the shore path or take a bike out to Čikat, the pine-fringed bay whose resin-heavy air first drew health travellers here over a century ago. If the legs are willing, carry on to Veli Lošinj, tighter and prettier than its bigger neighbour. Finish with scampi buzara at a harbourside table, the shells worth the mess.
thingsToDo: See the Apoxyomenos bronze up close
Cycle the pine trails around Čikat bay
Stroll the harbour at Veli Lošinj
Scampi buzara on the waterfront
4.mooringTip: Y/C Marina Mali Lošinj is the reliable summer booking, with a restaurant and shop on site. The town's city-port quay takes walk-ins first-come, so arrive by early afternoon if you want a spot there.

5.shortDescription: Car-free Unije is a lazy seven miles northwest: under a hundred residents, no roads to speak of, and a Maracol bay that hides one of the north Adriatic's rare sand beaches.
5.description: Unije is the sort of island crews remember long after the flights home. Under a hundred residents, no cars, a single village of stone houses looking west, and a coastline of quiet inlets. The sail across from Lošinj is a lazy hour, so there is no rush to leave. Tuck into Maracol, the deepest and best-sheltered of the island's eastern bays, and you have most of the day to swim, kayak the smaller coves, or simply do nothing on the boat. When hunger sets in, it is a twenty-minute walk over the spine of the island to the village. Konoba Kod Joze does an honest brudet, the tomato-rich fish stew of these waters, and the pace is set by whoever is cooking. Come evening, the lack of streetlights turns the sky over the anchorage genuinely dark.
thingsToDo: Swim and kayak the coves at Maracol
Walk over the island to Unije village
Brudet at Konoba Kod Joze
Stargaze from the dark anchorage
5.mooringTip: Maracol's head is filled with paid mooring buoys on heavy concrete blocks that hold well; take one with bow and stern lines, or anchor clear of them in eight to ten metres. The bay funnels a strong Jugo from the southeast, so reconsider if one is forecast.

6.shortDescription: Twelve easy miles east to Cres Town, a Venetian-walled harbour on a big, wild island of oak forest, griffon-vulture cliffs and one stubborn clifftop hamlet.
6.description: Cres is a big, empty island, and the crossing from Unije gives you a first sense of it: dry ridges, oak forest, and somewhere above, the griffon vultures that nest on the cliffs near Beli. Cres Town sits in a snug bay behind a Venetian gateway, its harbour ringed by arched stone houses and a Romanesque bell tower. It is an easy place to lose an afternoon among the narrow lanes and cafés on the quay. With energy to spare, drive up to Lubenice, a huddle of forty souls on a ridge nearly four hundred metres straight above the sea, with a cove below that only makes sense to visit in settled weather. Back down, Slatina near Martinišćica is the swim stop. Dinner is Cres lamb, grazed on wild herbs and grilled simply, and it needs little else.
thingsToDo: Wander the walled lanes of Cres Town
Drive up to clifftop Lubenice
Swim at Slatina near Martinišćica
Herb-grazed Cres lamb, simply grilled
6.mooringTip: ACI Marina Cres is the largest in the Kvarner and the safe bet in season, with the town quay alongside. For a free night, the sheltered water between the marina and the old shipyard holds well on sand.

7.shortDescription: The final leg back across to Krk, with a swim off Malinska on the west shore before the run home to Marina Punat.
7.description: Handover day, but there is time for one more stop before you hand back the keys. Cross the channel to Krk and anchor off Malinska on the western shore, a shallow, easy bay for a last long swim while someone starts on the boat. If the timing works, it is a short trip inland to the Glavotok monastery, Franciscan and five centuries old, standing among the pines at the water's edge. This is also Žlahtina country: the crisp white from Vrbnik across the island pairs well with a final konoba lunch. From Malinska it is a straightforward sail south and east back to Marina Punat, the oldest charter base in the country and still one of its biggest. Aim to be alongside with fuel taken and the boat rinsed in good time for the afternoon check-out.
thingsToDo: Last swim in Malinska's shallows
Visit the Glavotok monastery
Vrbnik Žlahtina with konoba lunch
Refuel and rinse before check-out
7.mooringTip: Marina Punat runs its own boatyard and fuel quay, so book a fuel slot roughly a day ahead and confirm your return berth before the final approach. It gets busy on Saturday turnaround.

punat-rijeka.metaDesc: Round-trip from Punat through the Kvarner Gulf: Malinska vineyards, Opatija's Lungomare, Rijeka, Senj under Velebit and Rab's beaches before home.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 8-mile shakedown down the west coast of Krk to Malinska, a pine-lined resort with the Žlahtina vineyards a short ride inland.
1.description: Most crews use this first leg to shake out the boat rather than cover ground. It's only eight miles down Krk's western shore, so there's no rush to leave the base. Malinska has a long pine-shaded waterfront and a small harbour that fills up fast in July, so aim to be tied off by mid-afternoon. The town itself is easygoing. What sells the stop is what sits inland: Vrbnik, the cliff village that grows Žlahtina, Krk's own white. Konoba Nada up there has been cooking for over fifty years and pours wine from its own cellar; the monkfish in Žlahtina sauce is the dish to order. Back at the boat, a quiet swim off Porto Bay before dinner settles everyone into charter rhythm.
thingsToDo: Taste Žlahtina at Konoba Nada, Vrbnik
Stroll the pine-shaded Malinska waterfront
Swim off Porto Bay before dinner
Ride inland to the Vrbnik cliffs
1.mooringTip: Marina Malinska is compact and books out in summer, so reserve ahead. If it's full or you want a quieter night, anchor off Porto Bay just outside and dinghy in.

2.shortDescription: Twelve miles north across the gulf to Opatija, the old Habsburg riviera, with its 12 km Lungomare walk and Volosko's fish taverns next door.
2.description: Opatija is where nineteenth-century Vienna came to winter, and the town still wears that money well: grand villas, manicured parks, a promenade that runs for miles. You berth at ACI Opatija in Ičići, a couple of kilometres south of the centre and one of the better-sheltered marinas in the northern Adriatic. From there the Lungomare threads the whole coast, twelve kilometres of it, from the fishing hamlet of Volosko down to Lovran. Walk at least the Volosko end. That's where the good seafood is, in small konobas tucked behind the harbour rather than on the tourist strip. Order the fuži with truffle if it's on. Later, a cocktail at Hemingway Bar with the water going pink is a fine way to close the day.
thingsToDo: Walk the Lungomare from Volosko to Lovran
Eat fuži in a Volosko konoba
Sundowner cocktail at Hemingway Bar
Wander Villa Angiolina's seafront park
2.mooringTip: ACI Marina Opatija at Ičići is the working berth here and holds well; the town quay in Opatija itself is short-stay only. Book ACI in high season.

3.shortDescription: A short six-mile hop east into Rijeka, Croatia's big working port and a 2020 Capital of Culture, with Trsat castle above the harbour.
3.description: Nobody sails to Rijeka for the postcards. It's a real port, third-largest city in the country, with shipyards, café culture and an edge the polished resorts don't have. The sail in from Opatija takes barely an hour. Berthing is the catch: the city has only a small charter quay near the centre and space is tight, so call ahead or plan to overnight back at Ičići. Once ashore, climb to Trsat castle for the view down over the Rječina gorge and the bay. Then work back down to the Korzo, the pedestrian spine, and the covered fish market off it. Kvarner scampi are the local prize, sweet and small; find them na buzaru at a market-side kitchen and you'll understand the fuss.
thingsToDo: Climb to Trsat castle for the view
Buy Kvarner scampi at the Dolac market
Coffee and people-watching on the Korzo
Tour the Maritime and History Museum
3.mooringTip: Rijeka's central charter quay is small and often full; confirm a spot before you commit to the passage. ACI Opatija at Ičići is a reliable protected fallback for the night.

4.shortDescription: Twelve miles south down the Vinodol shore to Crikvenica, where Roman ruins lie under the water off the old Kaštel headland.
4.description: The run south hugs the Vinodol coast, vineyards and old citrus terraces climbing off the water behind you. Crikvenica grew out of a Roman posting station called Ad Turres, and the ruins never fully left; parts of the old harbour still lie submerged off the Kaštel point, shallow enough to snorkel over on a calm afternoon. The Kaštel itself began as a Pauline monastery in 1412 and is a hotel now, sitting right on the pebble town beach. This is an easy, low-key stop. Provision here if you're running short, since the town has proper shops. For dinner, a konoba plate of škampi na buzaru and a glass of Vinodol white does the job before the longer leg south tomorrow.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the submerged Ad Turres ruins
Swim off the Kaštel pebble beach
Provision at the town market
Škampi na buzaru at a local konoba
4.mooringTip: The marina at Crikvenica is small, so book ahead in season. When it's calm you may anchor off the town's beach, though the bay stays open and is no spot to ride out heavy weather.

5.shortDescription: Twenty miles south into the Velebit channel to Senj, the windiest town on the coast, guarded by the Nehaj fortress above the harbour.
5.description: Read the forecast before you slip lines. This is the leg that runs down the Velebit channel, the most bura-prone water in the Adriatic, and Senj catches it worse than anywhere; the town sees strong wind well over a hundred days a year. On a settled morning it's a straightforward twenty-mile reach. When the bura is forecast, it isn't, and the sensible call is to wait a day. Ashore, the Nehaj fortress from 1558 stands on its hill above the port, built by the Uskoks who ran their raiding fleet from here against the Ottomans and Venetians. The museum inside is worth an hour. Down in the old lanes, a bowl of maneštra, the thick local bean-and-vegetable stew, is exactly right after a passage.
thingsToDo: Tour the Nehaj fortress and its museum
Walk the medieval Uskok old town
Try maneštra bean stew at a konoba
Check the bura forecast before leaving
5.mooringTip: Senj's harbour quay is really only short-stay and exposed, so plan your overnight elsewhere and treat any bura forecast as a hard stop. If it's blowing, stay put or run for shelter early rather than push down the channel.

6.shortDescription: A twelve-mile crossing back over the channel to Baška, at the foot of Krk's mountains, fronted by the mile-long Vela Plaža.
6.description: Timing matters more than distance here. The crossing back to Krk is only a dozen miles, but you're cutting across the mouth of the bura channel, so most crews leave early while it's quiet. Baška sits in an amphitheatre of bare grey mountains at the island's southern tip, and the approach alone is worth the passage. Its beach, Vela Plaža, runs close to two kilometres of pale pebble and picks up a swell that swimmers love and skippers should respect at anchor. Behind the town, a marked trail climbs onto the stark limestone plateau where the Bašćanska Ploča was found, one of the oldest surviving texts in Croatian. Book a farmhouse peka in the afternoon and you'll eat lamb under the bell that evening.
thingsToDo: Swim the two-kilometre Vela Plaža
Hike the trail up the Moon Plateau
See the Bašćanska Ploča cast in town
Order lamb peka at a hillside konoba
6.mooringTip: Baška has a small marina plus town quay, so book ahead at peak. Anchoring off the beach works in light air, but the bay is open to the south and southeast, so don't count on it if any swell is running.

7.shortDescription: A final eighteen-mile loop past Rab Town and Lopar's sandy Rajska Plaža before turning back into the great enclosed bay at Punat.
7.description: Save the softest swim for last. Lopar, on the northern tip of Rab, has Rajska Plaža, a rare stretch of genuine sand nearly two kilometres long and shelving so gently you can wade a long way out. Anchor off, drop the swim ladder, and let the crew have a slow morning. On the way back you can duck into Rab Town for a short walk under its four campaniles, then point north for home. Punat sits inside one of the largest natural harbours on the coast, almost fully ringed by Krk, which is why the charter base has run here since 1964. Radio ahead for a fuel slot and confirm your berth before the final approach. A last glass of Žlahtina on deck closes the week.
thingsToDo: Last swim off Lopar's Rajska Plaža
Short walk under Rab Town's campaniles
Radio ahead for a Marina Punat fuel slot
Toast the week with Vrbnik Žlahtina
7.mooringTip: Marina Punat is a full-service base with its own yard and fuel quay. Request a fuel slot about a day ahead and confirm your return berth before your final approach, since arrivals bunch up on turnaround days.

rogoznica-dubrovnik-rogoznica-route-14-d.metaDesc: Two weeks from Rogoznica down to Dubrovnik and back: Brač, Hvar, Vis, Lastovo, Mljet and Korčula, with anchorages, konobas and mooring notes for each day.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 15-mile shakedown leg southeast to the pale sand shallows of Krknjaši, where three roadless konobas keep buoys for dinner guests.
1.description: The first leg is deliberately easy: fifteen miles out of Marina Frapa, tracking the open coast southeast until the channel between Veli and Mali Drvenik opens up. Krknjaši is all about the water. The floor is bright sand at three to six metres, the holding is reliable, and by midday the anchorage glows that improbable turquoise you came for. Veli Drvenik has no road, so the handful of family konobas reach guests by tender or on foot, and each lays a few free buoys for anyone booking a table. We usually drop the hook early, swim before the day boats thin out, then walk the short headland loop while the grill gets going ashore. It shelters well from every quarter except the west and northwest, so check the afternoon forecast before you commit to the night.
thingsToDo: Dive off the bow into the turquoise shallows before the day boats arrive
Tender across to roadless Veli Drvenik for grilled fish at a family konoba
Stretch your legs on the footpath around the headland above the anchorage
Snorkel the channel between Veli and Mali Drvenik
1.mooringTip: Drop anchor in four to six metres on firm sand, or take one of the konoba buoys if you plan to eat ashore. Fine in settled weather, but the bay opens to the west and northwest, so move on if a sea breeze is forecast to back that way overnight.

2.shortDescription: A twelve-mile crossing of the Brač Channel drops you into Milna, tucked so far up its bay that Venetian galleys sheltered here; order the peka lamb the minute you tie up.
2.description: Milna sits deep in a bay near the western tip of Brač, and it has been sheltering boats since the Venetians ran this coast. The bell tower of the parish church guides you in, visible well before the harbour mouth resolves, with stone houses stacked up the slope behind. Mooring is stern-to with your own anchor along the town quay, water and power on the central stretch, a modest fee at the end of the day. Brač built its reputation on white limestone, the same stone quarried for Diocletian's Palace in Split. The island's other signature is slower: lamb baked under the peka, a bell of iron and coals that needs three hours, so order it when you arrive rather than when you are hungry. Quieter than Hvar or Bol, and all the better for it.
thingsToDo: Put in your order for the peka lamb the moment you tie up; it needs three hours
Look up at the parish bell tower that marks the harbour mouth
Pick up island olive oil at the growers' cooperative
Row the dinghy up to Bobovišća for an evening swim
2.mooringTip: Berth stern-to with your own anchor on the inner town quay; the central section has water and power for a modest fee. The harbour opens toward the southwest, but the inner quay stays comfortable even when the afternoon Maestral pipes up over fifteen knots.

3.shortDescription: Fifteen miles southeast into the Pakleni Islands, where Palmižana pairs a lazy-line marina with a botanical garden and a clutch of art-house konobas.
3.description: The Pakleni chain scatters across the water just off Hvar Town, and Palmižana is the one islet in it set up for boats. Slots at the ACI marina take lazy lines, and in July and August you book them online or you don't get in. When the marina fills, the Vinogradišće buoy field on the south side is the fallback; the cove is ringed with pine and anchoring there is no longer allowed, so pick up a mooring. Ashore, the Meneghello family has been running this place for over a century, and their botanical garden is worth an hour before dinner. Konobas here lean toward long, unhurried evenings and local wine. When the crew wants nightlife, a water taxi runs across to Hvar Town in minutes and saves you the harbour scrum.
thingsToDo: Lose an hour in the Meneghello family's botanical garden before dinner
Snorkel over the sunken Roman amphorae off the western shore
Hop the water taxi to Hvar Town when the crew wants a late one
Cross Sv. Klement on foot to the hamlet of Vlaka
3.mooringTip: The ACI marina takes lazy lines and books out for July and August, so reserve online well ahead. If it is full, take a mooring in the Vinogradišće buoy field on the south side rather than trying to anchor, which is no longer permitted there.

4.shortDescription: The longest hop yet, twenty miles southwest to Komiža, a working fish town under Mount Hum where the whole waterfront turns out for sundown.
4.description: Vis is the farthest out of the inhabited islands, and it feels it. The military kept it closed until 1989, which is a large part of why the place still runs on fishing rather than tourism. Komiža tucks into a deep bay at the foot of Mount Hum, protected from the north, and its quay offers stern-to mooring with water and power if you arrive before the early afternoon rush. Get in, tie up, then climb Hum for the view or poke around the fishing fleet on the eastern side of the harbour. The konobas do brodet, grilled octopus and the local pogača, washed down with Vugava, the island's own white. Come evening the whole waterfront turns west and waits for the sun to drop into open sea. Nothing on this route beats it.
thingsToDo: Climb Hum to Tito's wartime cave for the run of the island
Poke around the fishing boats moored along the eastern harbour
Drink a glass of Vugava with brodet or grilled octopus at a konoba
Track down the Falkuša replicas at the Fishermen's Museum
4.mooringTip: Stern-to with your own anchor on the inner town quay, and aim to arrive before early afternoon in season to find a spot. The outer wall catches southwest swell; if it is blowing hard from that quarter, run six miles up to Vis Town in St George's Bay instead.

5.shortDescription: A dawn run to the Blue Cave on Biševo, then eighteen miles east to Vela Luka for full town services and homemade makaruni.
5.description: This is a morning-driven day. The Blue Cave on Biševo only lights up when the sun sits at the right angle, roughly nine to eleven, and private dinghies aren't allowed inside, so you queue for the official tender and time your arrival accordingly. Once you've seen it, the Green Cave over on Ravnik is an easier stop that you can enter under your own power, usually with far fewer boats around. From there it's a straightforward eighteen miles east to Vela Luka at the tail of Korčula. The bay is well tucked from north through east, there's a fuel pump at the western end, and the town has everything you need to restock. For dinner, order the žrnovski makaruni, the pasta they still roll by hand here. Leave the caves early; the tender line only grows as the morning goes.
thingsToDo: Line up early for the official tender into Biševo's Blue Cave
Slip your own dinghy into the quieter Green Cave on Ravnik
Stock the galley from the Vela Luka shops before the next leg
Sit down to a plate of žrnovski makaruni, the hand-rolled island pasta
5.mooringTip: Vela Luka has a long town quay for stern-to mooring with your own anchor, plus water, power and a fuel pump at the western end. The bay is well protected from north through east, so it is a dependable overnight in most summer conditions.

6.shortDescription: Fourteen miles south lands you on Lastovo, far enough offshore that few boats bother; at Zaklopatica the konoba buoys come with Europe's darkest sky overhead.
6.description: Lastovo takes some getting to, which is exactly its appeal. Few boats bother, and the ones that do tend to be crewed by people who've sailed here before. Most crews tuck into Zaklopatica for the night. Fifteen buoys sit in the narrow bay, laid by family konobas like Augusta Insula and Triton, and the mooring costs nothing once you have booked a table. The kitchens here are serious about lobster, jastog on the menu, alongside šporki makaruli in a slow-cooked meat sauce. What sets the island apart comes after dark. Lastovo was made a Dark Sky Park in 2018, and on a clear night the Milky Way stands out so sharply you can pick out satellite tracks. If a southeasterly is forecast above fifteen knots, drop down to Skrivena Luka instead, where thirty buoys sit inside a bay you can't even see from open water.
thingsToDo: Grab a konoba buoy in Zaklopatica and settle in for the night
Split a Lastovo lobster or the šporki makaruli in its slow meat sauce
Kill the deck lights and let the Dark Sky Park do the rest
Hike inland to see Lastovo's oddly grand chimney stacks, the fumari
6.mooringTip: Zaklopatica has around fifteen konoba buoys; call ahead or agree one on arrival, and a dinner booking holds it for the night. The bay covers you from north to west, but for a southeasterly blow move four miles south to fully enclosed Skrivena Luka.

7.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles northeast into Polače, a long finger of calm water on Mljet's north coast; Roman palace walls line the quay and the park's salt lakes sit a short stroll inland.
7.description: Polače is one of those anchorages that makes the passage worth it. The inlet runs long and narrow into Mljet's north coast, hills close on both sides, and it holds calm when much of the coast is bouncing around. The name comes from palatium: the ruins of a fourth-century Roman palace still stand along the shoreline, close enough that you moor almost inside them. Overnight is on the green national park buoys, paid at the ranger kiosk, which folds in park entry for the crew. Once you're settled, a half-hour walk brings you to Veliko Jezero, the larger of the two saltwater lakes, where a park boat ferries you out to the islet and its twelfth-century Benedictine monastery. Bring bikes or good shoes; the loop trail around the lakes runs to nine kilometres and rewards the effort with quiet coves.
thingsToDo: Moor a stone's throw from the fourth-century Roman palace on the shore
Catch the park launch out to the Benedictine monastery on Veliko Jezero
Ride a bike the whole way round the nine-kilometre lake trail
Cool off with a swim in the warm saltwater lakes
7.mooringTip: Polače uses paid national park mooring buoys, settled with the ranger on arrival at roughly forty euros a night with crew park entry included. It is one of the most sheltered overnights on the southern coast, so it rarely disappoints.

8.shortDescription: The turning point: twenty-four miles southeast to the marina at Komolac, then a short hop to the walls of the old city itself.
8.description: Twenty-four miles down the coast brings you to ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac, set well up the Rijeka Dubrovačka estuary where the water goes flat and river-quiet. Every berth has lazy lines, water and power, and there's a fuel pontoon on the way in. Don't plan on the Old Port for the night; it's a daytime tender stop only and lies open to any southerly. From Komolac the 1A bus or a taxi gets you to the Pile Gate in about a quarter of an hour. Then it's the city. Walking the walls is the thing everyone does and everyone should, a two-kilometre circuit that takes ninety minutes and shows you the town from every angle. Afterwards, skip the Stradun crowds and eat down a Prijeko side street, or take a drink at Buža, wedged into the rocks outside the south wall.
thingsToDo: Walk the whole two-kilometre wall circuit; give it a good ninety minutes
Ride the cable car up Mount Srđ and look back down on the walls
Eat on a Prijeko back lane and skip the Stradun crowds
Take a drink at Buža, clinging to the rocks below the south wall
8.mooringTip: ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac is the only sensible place to leave the boat overnight; book ahead in high season as it fills fast. The Old Port inside the walls is fine for a daytime tender visit but exposed and off-limits for staying.

9.shortDescription: Northwest again to Okuklje, a tight bay just past the park boundary where mooring costs nothing and the night is the quietest of the fortnight.
9.description: After the noise of Dubrovnik, Okuklje is the antidote. It sits on Mljet's southeast shore, just outside the national park line, which means no buoy fees and no ranger. The bay is a tight little U with room for twenty-odd boats, and the three family konobas string out their own moorings for free when you dine with them. There's not much to do here, and that's the point. Order the crni rižot, the black cuttlefish-ink risotto that turns everything the same shade, then swim off the stern into flat water. Later, walk the headland path for a look back toward Korčula. No town, no traffic, no late ferries thumping through. The bay stays snug from north, east and south; only a strong northwesterly, over about eighteen knots, sends any swell worth noticing into the anchorage.
thingsToDo: Pick a konoba, book a table, and its buoy is yours for the night
Order the crni rižot, black with cuttlefish ink and worth the mess
Follow the headland path for the long look back to Korčula
Go over the side straight off the stern into water flat as glass
9.mooringTip: Okuklje's konobas lay their own free buoys, held for the night by a dinner reservation. The bay shelters you from north, east and south, and only a northwesterly above eighteen knots or so pushes in any noticeable swell.

10.shortDescription: Fifteen miles up the Pelješac Channel to walled Korčula Town, its fishbone streets, sword dancers and the sandy-soil Grk wine of nearby Lumbarda.
10.description: Korčula Town announces itself from a way off, a compact walled peninsula bristling with towers. The medieval planners laid the streets in a herringbone so the summer Maestral could reach the lanes while winter gusts were kept out, and it still works. For the night, take the ACI marina on the east side, which holds calm in any wind and gives you lazy lines. The prettier town quay on the west is stern-to, but the afternoon Maestral blows straight onto it, so save it for a settled evening. Wander the old town after the day ferries have gone and it empties out beautifully. Climb the bell tower of St Mark's for the rooftops, argue about whether Marco Polo was really born here, and time your visit for a Monday or Thursday evening when the Moreška sword dance is performed. Pair dinner with a bottle of Grk from the sandy Lumbarda vineyards down the coast.
thingsToDo: Catch the Moreška sword dance on a Monday or Thursday night in season
Haul yourself up the St Mark's belfry for the rooftop view
Walk the herringbone lanes once the day ferries have cleared out
Order a bottle of Grk, the white grown on Lumbarda's sandy plots
10.mooringTip: The ACI marina on the eastern side of the peninsula is the reliable, all-weather berth with lazy lines. The western town quay is more central but exposed to the afternoon Maestral, so take it only in settled conditions.

11.shortDescription: A longer twenty-three-mile leg over the Hvar Channel to Jelsa, a working town that grows lavender and old-vine wine and lets the Hvar Town crowds pass it by.
11.description: Jelsa is where you go when you've had enough of the Hvar Town scene. It's a working town on the island's north coast, wrapped around a green harbour, and it gets on with growing lavender, olives and wine rather than chasing yachts. Berth stern-to on the town quay with your own anchor, where the mud and sand give a good grip, or take the small marina on the eastern side if you'd rather have lazy lines. The land behind town is the real draw. The Stari Grad Plain, a Greek field system still farmed after twenty-four centuries, carries UNESCO status and makes for a fine walk or ride. Cycle the coast road west to Vrboska and its little bridged canal, climb to the Tor watchtower for the channel view, and pour a glass of Bogdanjuša, the local white that barely leaves the island.
thingsToDo: Wander into the Stari Grad Plain, worked on the same Greek grid for 24 centuries
Freewheel the coast road over to canal-cut Vrboska
Climb up to the Tor watchtower and look out over the channel
Ask for a glass of Bogdanjuša, the white that rarely leaves Hvar
11.mooringTip: Lie stern-to at the Jelsa town quay on your own anchor; the mud-and-sand seabed grips well, water and power laid on for a small charge. Prefer laid lines? The little marina on the bay's east side takes you instead.

12.shortDescription: A short ten-mile skip north to Lučice, a run of empty coves under white cliffs, where a couple of summer beach kitchens keep buoys and you can read the bottom at twenty metres.
12.description: Ten miles is barely a morning's work, so there's no rush leaving Jelsa. Lučice lies just east of Pučišća on Brač's north coast, a chain of small linked coves hemmed by pale cliffs and pine, with not a single house on the shore. Two seasonal beach restaurants operate here in summer and keep a few buoys for guests. The seabed is that bright Brač limestone again, and on a calm day you can see twenty metres down to it. This is a swimming and snorkelling stop more than a sightseeing one. Paddle the shoreline at first light, then dinghy the short way into Pučišća to watch the stonemasons work the same white rock that built palaces up and down the coast. The coves cover you from north through east but stand open to the south, so pick your night by the forecast.
thingsToDo: Drop into water clear enough to count the stones twenty metres down
Tie onto a beach-restaurant buoy in one of the linked coves
Nip round to Pučišća and watch the masons cut white Brač stone
Take the paddleboard along the cliffs while the light is still soft
12.mooringTip: Lučice's two seasonal beach restaurants keep a handful of buoys, secured by eating ashore. The coves shelter you from north through east but lie open to the south, so avoid an overnight here if a southerly is on the way.

13.shortDescription: Fourteen miles northwest to Maslinica on the tip of Šolta, where a fan of islets shelters a curling inlet; tie up at the castle-hotel marina or keep it simple on the village quay.
13.description: Maslinica sits at the western end of Šolta, and its harbour is one of the prettiest on this stretch, an S-shaped inlet tucked behind a screen of small islets that knock down anything from the west. Mooring splits two ways. The south side belongs to Martinis Marchi Marina, fifty berths on lazy lines beside a converted seventeenth-century palace that's now a hotel; the north side is the village quay, stern-to with your own anchor and a simpler affair. For all that Split sits just fifteen miles off, Šolta stays the sleepiest of the central Dalmatian islands, and it grows a grape you won't find elsewhere: Dobričić, an inky red locals call the darkest wine in Dalmatia, and a genetic parent of both Zinfandel and Plavac Mali. Dinghy across to Stipanska for a swim, or watch the sun go down behind the outer islets.
thingsToDo: Wander into the courtyard of the Martinis Marchi castle-hotel
Try Dobričić, the near-black Šolta red behind both Zinfandel and Plavac Mali
Run the dinghy over to Stipanska for a swim
Watch the sun drop behind the screen of outer islets
13.mooringTip: You have two choices here. To the south, Martinis Marchi Marina keeps fifty lazy-line berths and accepts advance booking; the north-side village quay is stern-to on your own anchor. Those islets keep the bay settled right across the compass's western half.

14.shortDescription: The last fifteen miles home to Marina Frapa, rounding the Drvenik islands and Šolta's south shore, timed to put you alongside for the Saturday-morning handover.
14.description: The last leg is a gentle fifteen miles, tracking past the south coast of Šolta and the Drvenik islands you left behind on day one. Most charters want the boat back at Marina Frapa by nine on Saturday morning, so the smart move is to close the distance the evening before or leave Maslinica early. Come in with fuel topped up, holding tanks pumped out, decks rinsed and the inventory squared away; it makes the handover quick and the deposit comes back cleanly. If you land with time to spare, walk out to the Dragon's Eye, the strange sinkhole lake on the tip of the Rogoznica peninsula, connected to the sea underground and steeped in local legend. A long lunch at the marina restaurant is a fair way to close out two weeks.
thingsToDo: Square away fuel, tanks and decks before the Saturday handover
Stroll out to the Dragon's Eye, the eerie sinkhole lake on the peninsula
Round it all off with a long lunch at the marina restaurant
Poke through the old quarter of Rogoznica town
14.mooringTip: Bring the boat back into Marina Frapa as your charter contract requires, usually by nine on Saturday morning. If the paperwork calls for a night-before return, plan to be alongside by late afternoon Friday to beat the weekend queue at the fuel dock.

rogoznica-np-kornati-route.metaDesc: Seven days from Rogoznica through the Šibenik islands to Krka's waterfalls, Telašćica and the Kornati archipelago, then home for the Saturday handover.

1.shortDescription: Fifteen miles north from Marina Frapa to car-free Zlarin, the Šibenik islands' red-coral village, with a quiet quay under the cypresses.
1.description: Marina Frapa sits on the south side of the Rogoznica peninsula, ten piers deep and about as sheltered as any base on this stretch of coast. Fuel up, take the lines, and point the bow north. It's roughly fifteen miles of open water before you slip into the Šibenik island group and pick up Zlarin. The island runs on coral. Divers here have worked the deep red-coral beds offshore since medieval times, and the trade still shapes the village and its little museum. Tie stern-to along the south side of the 140-metre pier, where the central berths carry water and power for a small fee. The bay holds well in a westerly but opens to the southeast, so read the evening forecast before you settle. We usually eat ashore at Konoba Prslika.
thingsToDo: Tour the Zlarin coral museum on the square
Dinner at Konoba Prslika or Restoran Koralj
Swim off the rocks south of the village
Walk the pine and cypress paths inland
Buy handmade coral jewellery from a workshop
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on the south side of the pier; the central berths carry water and power, the outer ones don't. Shelter is good from W and NW, but the quay turns uncomfortable in a southeasterly, so arriving early lets you pick a spot before the afternoon boats crowd in.

2.shortDescription: Twelve miles up the Krka estuary past Šibenik to ACI Marina Skradin, where the park ferry runs up to the Skradinski Buk falls.
2.description: From Zlarin it's a short twelve miles, but the character changes completely. You round Šibenik old town, pass under the bridge with its 49 metres of clearance, and motor into the freshwater reach of the Krka. Sailing is banned above the river mouth, so it's engine-on at displacement speed the rest of the way to ACI Marina Skradin. The marina sits two miles below the falls; buy park entry at the seawall kiosk, then catch the national-park launch that shuttles up to Skradinski Buk every half hour in season. Seventeen travertine steps drop the river forty-odd metres, and a boardwalk loops both banks. Back in town, order the Skradin risotto, a beef and saffron affair the locals stir for the best part of a day. Prefer the walk? A 3.4-kilometre canyon path reaches the falls on foot.
thingsToDo: Ride the park launch to Skradinski Buk
Loop the boardwalks on both riverbanks
Order Skradin risotto in the old town
Walk the 3.4 km canyon path to the falls
Take the upstream boat to Visovac island
2.mooringTip: ACI Marina Skradin has laid lines on every berth, full services and a fuel pontoon by the entrance. Coming up from the river mouth, budget about ninety minutes at displacement speed, and call ahead for July and August when the freshwater berths book out fast.

3.shortDescription: Back down the estuary and out to car-free Kaprije, a one-village island with a spine walk that opens onto the Kornati.
3.description: Retrace the freshwater reach, clear the river mouth, and turn southwest for Kaprije. All told it works out around fifteen miles, most of them easy once you're back in open water. This is a small place: one village, roughly 140 people, no cars, a shop and a post office and a couple of bars. Walk it end to end in an hour and a half. The path climbs to a little chapel on the island's spine, and from up there the whole Kornati cluster lays out on the horizon. Tie stern-to on the village quay, where the inside of the jetty holds ten or twelve boats on laid lines. Water reaches the central berths but there's no shore power, so charge up before you arrive. Konoba Kunjka does the fish, with Babić red from the Primošten terraces to go beside it.
thingsToDo: Climb to the hilltop chapel for Kornati views
Fish supper at family-run Konoba Kunjka
Swim off the rocks south of the harbour
Try local Babić red at the quay
Wander the olive groves and old vineyards
3.mooringTip: Stern-to on the inside of the concrete jetty, laid lines for ten or twelve boats; water on the central berths but no power. The harbour is snug in anything from S through W, though a NE Bora sends swell straight in, so treat it as a fair-weather stop.

4.shortDescription: Fourteen miles northwest set you inside Telašćica Nature Park off Dugi Otok, where the warm Mir salt lake meets the soaring Grpašćak cliffs.
4.description: Telašćica bites a deep bay into the foot of Dugi Otok, fourteen miles northwest of Kaprije. It's one of the largest natural harbours in the Adriatic and about the safest, folded so far into the island that wind barely reaches you. A ranger comes round to the buoys; the park fee is separate from Kornati and you settle it on the water. Two things pull people ashore. The Mir salt lake sits a short walk over the ridge, cut off from the sea and warm enough to swim in weeks before the open water is. Round on the seaward side, the land drops away at Grpašćak, a wall of rock 160 metres tall with a footpath running its edge to an old lookout. Stay aboard after dark and the cicadas take over.
thingsToDo: Swim the warm Mir salt lake over the ridge
Hike the Grpašćak clifftop trail and lookout
Pay the ranger and pick up a buoy
Snorkel the sheltered eastern shore
Listen to the cicadas from the deck
4.mooringTip: A park buoy is compulsory here, paid to the ranger on his rounds, and the fee covers park entry too. There are around thirty buoys in the Mir arm; the bay lies dead calm in any wind, so this is the one night on the route you can count on a still deck.

5.shortDescription: A ten-mile leg south lands at seasonal ACI Marina Piškera within Kornati National Park — bare rock, a single restaurant, and 80-metre cliffs close at hand.
5.description: Ten miles south drops you into the heart of the Kornati, that maze of stone islands with almost nothing green on them. ACI Marina Piškera sits on Panitula Vela, open April to October, with roughly 120 to 150 berths on laid lines and a single restaurant perched on the rocks above. A berth includes park entry, but it's cheaper bought online or in advance, so sort it before you arrive. Once you're tied up, the afternoon is yours. Take the tender across to the Piškera–Lavsa channel for the clearest snorkelling of the week, or run the boat down to the park's southern rim, where Mana and Kornat present eighty-metre walls of bare karst to the open sea. No village noise out here, just rock and water and the marina lights.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the channel between Piškera and Lavsa
Sail the sheer cliffs of Mana and Kornat
Dinner at the rock-top marina restaurant
Buy park entry online before you arrive
Walk the bare karst ridge of Panitula Vela
5.mooringTip: Book ACI Piškera online for July and August; it's the only marina inside the park and it fills. If you'd rather anchor, five bays within the boundary allow it overnight: Lavsa, Lojena, Vrulje, Stiniva and Statival. Anywhere else the rangers will move you on.

6.shortDescription: Twelve miles east out of the park to Jezera on Murter, the closest inhabited island to Kornati and a full-service ACI marina.
6.description: Twelve miles east and you're back among people. Murter is the nearest lived-in island to the Kornati, close enough that many of the park's boats are owned in its villages, and it joins the mainland by a small swing bridge at Tisno. ACI Marina Jezera runs along the south coast with laid lines, full services and fuel by the entrance. Off the boat, it's an easy afternoon. Walk over to the salt pans at Tisno, Roman in origin and still worked, or take the dinghy round to Slanica for a swim on the sandy south shore. Betina, next door, keeps a maritime museum and a living tradition of wooden boatbuilding worth an hour of anyone's time. Dinner ashore is pašticada, beef braised slow in red wine and prunes, the Dalmatian Sunday dish.
thingsToDo: Swim the sandy shallows at Slanica beach
See the working Roman salt pans at Tisno
Visit Betina's boatbuilding maritime museum
Order pašticada at a Jezera konoba
Taste Murter olive oil on the quay
6.mooringTip: ACI Marina Jezera is the all-weather choice on this stretch, laid lines throughout and a fuel berth at the mouth. It's well tucked from most directions; on a settled night you could lie at anchor off Slanica instead, but the marina is the safe call before a long final leg.

7.shortDescription: Twenty miles south back to Marina Frapa for the Saturday handover, with a last swim off Primošten and Split airport an hour north.
7.description: The longest leg of the week, twenty miles back down to Rogoznica, so we usually slip the lines by nine. The handover is the standard Saturday drill: boat on the base berth, tanks pumped, fuel topped, decks rinsed and the inventory checked off before the manager comes down. You'll pass Primošten on the way down; leave a little slack in the schedule and Raduča beach makes a last swim before the base. Marina Frapa folds into a deep, cliff-ringed bay that shrugs off Bora, Jugo and the afternoon maestral alike, which is why so many charters begin and end here. With the boat signed off, walk the peninsula to the Dragon's Eye, a saltwater lake trapped in the rock, or stretch a long lunch at the marina hotel. Split airport is about an hour north for the run home.
thingsToDo: Final swim at Primošten's Raduča beach
Walk the peninsula to the Dragon's Eye lake
Long lunch at the Marina Frapa hotel
Top fuel and pump out before inspection
Stroll Rogoznica's small old quarter
7.mooringTip: Come back into Marina Frapa as your contract states; the base manager will point you to a slot. The Saturday window is tight, usually 08:00 to 09:00, so if the paperwork calls for a night-before return, aim to be alongside by late Friday afternoon rather than pushing it in the dark.

rogoznica-vis-hvar-korcula-solta-route.metaDesc: Seven-day sail from Rogoznica out to Vis and Hvar: Komiža, the Blue Cave, Stari Grad and Šolta, with mooring notes and konobas for each night.

1.shortDescription: A short first hop to the Blue Lagoon off Drvenik Veliki, sandy holding for the settle-in swim and dinner ashore at a Drvenik konoba.
1.description:
We usually push off from Marina Frapa by mid-afternoon and run the fifteen miles southeast at an easy pace. There's no reason to rush day one. Krknjaši is the target, the shallow lagoon in the channel between Drvenik Veliki and its smaller neighbour, where the sand turns the water an improbable pale blue.

Come in from the south. The gaps between the islets shoal to under three metres, so keep the depth sounder honest and drop the hook in five to seven metres on sand. By late afternoon the day-tripper boats from Trogir have mostly cleared out and the bay goes quiet. Row ashore to Drvenik Veliki for grilled fish at Konoba Krknjaši or a drink at Skoy, then back aboard for a first night that sets the crew up for the longer legs ahead.
thingsToDo: Anchor over the sand for the first swim
Grilled fish at Konoba Krknjaši, Drvenik Veliki
Sundowner at Skoy Bar on the shore
Snorkel the shallows between the two islets
1.mooringTip: Approach only from the south, since the passages between the islets drop below three metres. Anchor in five to seven metres on good sand, and arrive after four in the afternoon once the tour boats leave to have your pick of space.

2.shortDescription: A long open-water leg out to Komiža, Vis's working fishing town, for lobster at Jastožera and a glass of island Vugava.
2.description:
Twenty-four miles of open water lie between the lagoon and Vis, so it pays to leave by nine and let the morning breeze fill in. Komiža sits on the far western side of the island, the last inhabited port before the open Adriatic, and it kept its closed-military status right up to 1989. Being shut off for so long kept the developers away. The fishing fleet still works out of the eastern harbour and the konobas cook what comes off the boats.

Berth stern-to on the inner basin, dropping your own anchor into sand over weed, and the hold is usually sound. Stay off the outer wall, where any southwest swell rolls straight in. Book a table at Jastožera, built out over the water in an old lobster store, and pour the local Vugava.
thingsToDo: Hike Mount Hum to Tito's wartime cave
Dinner over the water at Konoba Jastožera
Walk the working fishing fleet at dawn
Fishermen's Museum in the Venetian tower
2.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth with your own anchor on the inner basin; the outer wall is exposed to southwest swell. Vis Town, six miles up the coast in St George Bay, is your bolt-hole if a southwesterly builds past fifteen knots.

3.shortDescription: Blue Cave at first light, a swim stop at Budihovac, then a short hop to Vis Town and its Roman ruins.
3.description:
The Blue Cave on Biševo is the reason most crews come this far, and the light inside is at its best late morning when the sun fires up through the underwater opening. The catch is the queue. Only the licensed tenders go in, so get across early and take your place before the day boats from Split arrive.

Afterwards, swing east to Budihovac for lunch on the hook. The lagoon behind the little islet shoals to a metre or two over pale sand, so read the water carefully and post someone on the bow. From there it's a gentle six miles clockwise to Vis Town, sat deep in St George Bay. The old colony of Issa stood here, and you can still walk past Roman baths and Hellenistic walls on the seafront.
thingsToDo: Beat the queue into the Blue Cave
Anchor and swim at Budihovac lagoon
Walk the Hellenistic walls on Vis seafront
Climb to Austro-Hungarian Fort George
Book peka octopus at a Vis konoba
3.mooringTip: Vis Town takes you stern-to with your own anchor along the seafront promenade, with water and power on the central berths for a small fee. St George Bay shelters you from south through west, making it the steadiest overnight on the island.

4.shortDescription: A twenty-mile channel crossing to Hvar Town, the busiest harbour on the coast, with the Fortica climb and Pakleni sunsets.
4.description:
The crossing runs about twenty miles east-northeast, and the summer maestral usually fills in from the northwest after midday to give you a proper sail across the channel. Hvar Town is the loudest stop on this route, packed and glamorous through July and August. Somewhere quieter waits at either end, but the town earns its evening.

A word on mooring: the town quay is exposed to west gusts and jammed with tenders, so it won't do overnight. Book a lazy-line berth ahead, or run across to ACI Marina Palmižana on the Pakleni islands and tender back in. Climb the Fortica early, before the stone throws heat, for the view down over the red roofs and the scatter of Pakleni islets. Save the evening for the Arsenal, St Stephen's square, and a konoba in the back lanes.
thingsToDo: Climb the Fortica before the midday heat
Sunset drink over the Pakleni channel
Wander the Arsenal and St Stephen's square
Late round at a back-lane konoba
4.mooringTip: The town quay is exposed and impossible overnight in any wind. Reserve a lazy-line berth well ahead in season, or take a berth at ACI Marina Palmižana across on the Pakleni islands and run the tender back into town.

5.shortDescription: An easy dozen miles around Hvar's tip to Stari Grad, the country's oldest town, edged by the drystone Greek field boundaries gridding its UNESCO-listed plain.
5.description:
After Hvar's noise, Stari Grad is a change of gear. It's a twelve-mile run clockwise around the island's western tip and into a long, fjord-like inlet that shelters the town from every wind. The Greeks founded the place as Pharos in 384 BC, which makes it the oldest town in the country, and you can still trace their grid in the lanes of the old centre.

Walk out of town into the Stari Grad Plain, the UNESCO-listed patchwork of dry-stone walls the Greeks laid out to divide the fields. Farmers have worked those same plots for well over two thousand years. Back in town, look in on Tvrdalj, the poet Hektorović's fortified house, with its walled seawater fishpond at the centre. Tie up on the town quay or take lazy lines at the ACI marina on the eastern shore.
thingsToDo: Walk the dry-stone walls of Stari Grad Plain
Visit Tvrdalj and Hektorović's seawater fishpond
Trace the Greek grid in the old town
Cycle the lavender road east of town
5.mooringTip: The inlet is deep and fully protected, so any berth here rides easy. Tie stern-to with your own anchor on the town quay for a modest fee, or cross to ACI Marina Stari Grad on the eastern side for lazy lines and full services.

6.shortDescription: Twenty miles northwest to Maslinica, Šolta's only real harbour, screened behind a line of islets and quiet despite Split being close.
6.description:
The final full day runs twenty miles northwest to Maslinica, out at the western end of Šolta. It is the one natural harbour on the island — an S-bent notch protected by seven little islets strung across its entrance — so it keeps its calm even when the afternoon breeze rises out at sea. Split is a mere fifteen miles distant, yet no island in this group stays as sleepy as Šolta.

Mooring gives you a choice. Set below an eighteenth-century castle now run as a hotel, the south-side Martinis Marchi marina lays laid lines and full services. Across on the northern side, you take the village quay stern-to under your own anchor. Whichever you pick, supper is the island's own red Dobričić and the local olive oil, then a dinghy paddle over to Stipanska if the light holds.
thingsToDo: Step into the Martinis Marchi castle courtyard
Try Dobričić, Šolta's own red grape
Dinghy across to Stipanska for a swim
Taste Šoltansko olive oil at the village shop
6.mooringTip: Book the Martinis Marchi marina on the south side ahead if you want lazy lines and shore power. The village quay opposite is stern-to on your own anchor and cheaper. The islets across the entrance keep the whole basin settled in the prevailing wind.

7.shortDescription: A last fifteen miles back to Marina Frapa, with time for one more swim off Šolta before the Saturday handover.
7.description:
