The run home is a short one, fifteen miles northwest from Maslinica back to Marina Frapa. That leaves room to slow down. The course passes the south coast of Šolta and the Drvenik islands, and there's usually a quiet bay to drop into for a final swim before the week ends.

Most bases want the boat alongside by nine on Saturday morning, so if your contract allows a night-before return, it often pays to come back into Rogoznica on the Friday evening and hand over relaxed. Top up the fuel, pump out the tanks, rinse the decks and square away the inventory before the check. With the paperwork done, walk the peninsula path to the Dragon's Eye, the collapsed karst lake linked to the sea by an underground channel, for a last look at the coast you've just sailed.
thingsToDo: One last swim off the Šolta coast
Walk the path to the Dragon's Eye lake
Long lunch at the Frapa marina restaurant
Wander old Rogoznica on the peninsula
7.mooringTip: Return into Marina Frapa per your charter contract, where the base staff assign your slot. The Saturday handover window is tight, usually eight to nine, so if the contract allows it, come back Friday afternoon and hand the boat over unhurried.

sibenik-dubrovnik-sibenik-route.metaDesc: Sail two weeks round-trip from Šibenik to Dubrovnik via Brač, Hvar, Vis, Lastovo, Mljet and Korčula, with anchorage, konoba and mooring notes.

1.shortDescription: A 25-mile hop southeast to open the trip: down through the St. Anthony narrows below Šibenik and out to the sand-floored swim anchorage at Krknjaši, between the two Drvenik islands.
1.description: The first leg is really a shakedown. You drop lines at ACI Marina Šibenik, run down the river to the St. Anthony channel, a rock-cut gap barely 600 metres wide where the current can push either way, then out past Zlarin into open water. From there it's a straight reach southeast to Krknjaši, the shallow bowl between Veli and Mali Drvenik. Nobody comes here for the village — they come for the floor. Pale sand sits at three to six metres and the holding is excellent, so the anchor bites first time and the water turns the colour of a swimming pool. Three small tavernas on the Drvenik side lay free buoys for dinner guests, though most crews just hook into the sand. A quiet night, sails trimmed, before the route turns properly south.
thingsToDo: Anchor on the sand floor in 4–6 m
Walk the headland loop above Krknjaši
Order grilled fish at a Drvenik taverna
Practise reefing in the open-channel breeze
Watch the sun drop over the open Adriatic
1.mooringTip: Anchor in 4–6 m on first-class sand holding, or pick up a taverna buoy if you're eating ashore. The bowl is well sheltered except from the west and northwest; if a westerly above 18 knots is forecast, duck into ACI Marina Trogir two miles east for the night.

2.shortDescription: A twelve-mile reach east over the Split and Brač channels lands you in Milna, the one west-Brač inlet that shrugs off every wind, and a village that slow-cooks its lamb under the bell.
2.description: A short hop today, twelve miles east across two channels, which leaves the afternoon free. Milna curls a long way inland from Brač's western coast, and that geometry is the whole point: bura, jugo, maestral, none of them get a proper run at you once you're inside. The seventeenth-century church on the inner quay marks the old Venetian harbour, still the heart of the village. Go stern-to on the town quay with your own anchor for a small fee, water and power on the central stretch, or drop the hook in the outer bay and run a line to the rocks if the maestral fills in after lunch. Brač built Diocletian's Palace out of its white stone, and it cooks lamb slowly under the peka. Order it the moment you tie up; three hours later it's dinner.
thingsToDo: Order lamb under the peka on arrival
Find the 17th-century church on the inner quay
Run a stern line in the outer bay
Taste Brač olive oil at the co-op
Dinghy north to Bobovišća for a swim
2.mooringTip: Stern-to on the town quay with your own anchor, small harbour fee, water and power mid-quay. The outer bay takes a free anchor with a line ashore. Milna is open only to the southwest and west; if the afternoon maestral pipes up past 15 knots the inner quay stays calm, and Bobovišća three miles north is the all-weather backup.

3.shortDescription: Palmižana sits on Sv. Klement, fifteen miles southeast over the Hvar Channel: the only Pakleni islet with a marina, and a botanical garden planted in 1906.
3.description: The Pakleni Islands are a low green chain strung off Hvar Town, fifteen miles southeast of Milna. Palmižana sits on the east side of Sv. Klement and is the one islet in the group with a marina, so the lazy-line berths fill fast in July and August; book online or plan to anchor. The main bay holds in five to ten metres over sand and weed, but it rolls once the maestral funnels through around two in the afternoon, so most crews slip round to Vinogradišće on the south side and pick up a buoy with a line ashore. Afternoons here have a rhythm: a wander through the Meneghello family's 1906 garden, a snorkel over the Roman amphorae off the western shore, dinner at one of the konobas along the bay. If the crew wants a late night, a water taxi runs across to Hvar Town in minutes.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Roman amphora field offshore
Wander the Meneghello 1906 botanical garden
Pick up a buoy in Vinogradišće cove
Water-taxi across to Hvar Town at night
Walk the track over to Vlaka Bay
3.mooringTip: ACI Marina Palmižana runs stern-to on lazy lines and books out for July and August, so reserve online. If it's full, take a buoy in Vinogradišće on the south side, sand and weed with a line ashore, and skip the main bay overnight once the southwesterly builds.

4.shortDescription: Komiža is a working fishing port under Mount Hum on the far side of Vis, twenty miles southwest — and, most nights, the best sunset of the whole trip.
4.description: Vis sits further out to sea than any other inhabited Croatian island, and Komiža, the fishing port on its western shore, is twenty miles southwest of the Pakleni chain. The island stayed a closed military zone until 1989, which is why it never grew a resort strip and still feels like itself. You can walk the whole town in a quarter of an hour: the fishing fleet along the eastern wall, the Fishermen's Museum in the old Venetian tower, konobas built around grilled sardines and the local Vugava white. Grab a spot on the breakwater quay before two in the afternoon in high season or you'll be left anchoring off. Dinner is easy to sort. Bako serves lobster from that morning's pots among the pines, and Jastožera sits right out over the water in a converted lobster trap. If the crew has legs left, the hike up Hum to Tito's wartime cave pays out the finest view in central Dalmatia.
thingsToDo: Hike Mount Hum to Tito's wartime cave
Eat lobster at Konoba Bako among the pines
Dine over the water at Jastožera
See the Fishermen's Museum in the Venetian tower
Order a glass of local Vugava white
4.mooringTip: Berth stern-to with your own anchor on the breakwater quay, and arrive before 14:00 in July and August; the 20-odd laid moorings go early. Buoys sit on the east side in 6–8 m. The quay is open to southwest swell, so if that's forecast above 15 knots, run six miles north to fully sheltered Vis Town.

5.shortDescription: Catch the Blue Cave on Biševo while the low morning sun still lights it that impossible blue, then run eighteen miles east to Vela Luka, the deep harbour at Korčula's western end.
5.description: The clock runs the day. Sunlight only lights the Blue Cave on Biševo to that electric blue between about nine and eleven, when the low angle drives light up through the underwater opening. Tie alongside the little concession quay outside, pay at the kiosk, and wait for one of the official tenders to run you in; forty minutes, in and out. On the way east it's worth ducking into the Green Cave at Ravnik, which you can enter in your own dinghy and usually have to yourself. By early afternoon the route settles into an eighteen-mile run to Vela Luka, at the sheltered western tip of Korčula. The bay is protected from north through east, the town has full services and a fuel pump at the western end, and dinner runs on žrnovski makaruni, the pasta they roll by hand here.
thingsToDo: Take the official tender into the Blue Cave
Dinghy into Ravnik's Green Cave yourself
Sail the south face of Vis heading east
Order žrnovski makaruni at a Vela Luka konoba
Stroll the Vela Luka waterfront at dusk
5.mooringTip: Along Vela Luka's long town quay you go stern-to on your own anchor for a small fee, with water, power and fuel at the western end. The mud-and-sand holding is first-rate, and the bay shuts out north, northeast and east, so nights stay calm.

6.shortDescription: Lastovo lies fourteen miles south, a Nature Park with the least light pollution in the country; at Zaklopatica the konobas lend free buoys to anyone booking a table.
6.description: Lastovo sits far enough offshore that the crowds never found it, fourteen miles south of Vela Luka and a Nature Park in its own right. After dark the sky over it goes as black as anywhere in the Adriatic. Zaklopatica is where boats spend the night: a U-shaped notch in the north coast with a low islet across the mouth that knocks down any swell. Four family konobas ring the bay, Augusta Insula, Santor, Triton and Aragosta, and each keeps a line of free buoys for guests who eat ashore, so you moor, dinghy in, and pick your table. Skip anchoring on the bottom; it's seagrass and they'd rather you didn't. The kitchens lean on Lastovo lobster and šporki makaruli, a rich stew of beef and macaroni. Stay on deck after dinner. On a clear night the Milky Way stands straight overhead before midnight.
thingsToDo: Take a konoba buoy at Konoba Augusta Insula
Order Lastovo lobster or šporki makaruli
Walk inland to Lastovo's chimney-stack houses
Snorkel the islet across the bay mouth
Stay up for the Milky Way overhead
6.mooringTip: Zaklopatica's konobas each hang free buoys, so book a table on arrival and that locks your mooring. There are fifteen or so buoys plus quay space, but Zaklopatica is poor in a bora. If a strong southeasterly is forecast, move four miles south through the channel to Skrivena Luka.

7.shortDescription: Polače, twenty-two miles northeast on Mljet's north coast, has a Roman palace at the water's edge and a footpath running straight to the national park's salt lakes.
7.description: Mljet's north coast folds inward at Polače, twenty-two miles northeast, and the slot stays hidden until you're nearly inside it. It's the western gate to the national park and one of the most protected overnights on the south coast, shut off from north, northeast, east and southeast. Pick up a green park buoy, pay the ranger at the waterfront kiosk (the fee covers park entry for the crew), or take a stern-to slot at the village pontoon. A fourth-century Roman palace stands a few metres back from the water, walls still up. A half-hour walk from the bay reaches Veliko Jezero, the bigger of the island's two salt lakes, where a small park boat ferries out to the Benedictine monastery on its own islet. The lake water is warmer than the sea and worth the swim.
thingsToDo: Pick up a park buoy and pay the ranger
Walk the 4th-century Roman palace on the shore
Take the park boat to St. Mary's monastery
Hike the lake loop through the park
Swim the warm water of Veliko Jezero
7.mooringTip: Polače runs on paid national-park buoys, around €40 a night in 2025 with crew park entry included; pay the ranger when you arrive. It's among the best-sheltered bays on the south coast, so it's the safe pick if the forecast looks unsettled.

8.shortDescription: Dubrovnik lies twenty-four miles southeast, but the boat sleeps at Komolac, up the Rijeka Dubrovačka estuary and a fifteen-minute bus from the Pile Gate.
8.description: Day eight is Dubrovnik, though not the Dubrovnik of the postcards. Charter boats overnight at ACI Marina Dubrovnik in Komolac, eight kilometres inland up the Rijeka Dubrovačka, a fjord-like estuary with lazy lines, fuel and full services on every berth. The Old Port on the seafront is beautiful and takes daytime visitors only; it's exposed to the south and packed with tour tenders all season, so it's a stop, not a berth. From the marina, Bus 1A or a taxi has you at the Pile Gate in about a quarter of an hour. Do the walls first, before the cruise crowds and the afternoon heat: a mile and a bit of circuit, ninety minutes at a steady pace, the best angle on the whole city. Later, end the night at Buža, the bar wedged into the rocks below the southern wall.
thingsToDo: Walk the full Old City wall circuit early
Ride the cable car up Mount Srđ
Eat on a Prijeko side street off the Stradun
See the Rector's Palace and Franciscan cloister
Drink at Buža bar on the sea rocks
8.mooringTip: For an overnight, ACI Marina Dubrovnik up at Komolac is really the only call — lazy lines, full services, reserve early in season. The Old Port takes daytime visits only. From the marina pontoon, allow about half an hour to the Pile Gate on Bus 1A or by taxi.

9.shortDescription: Back northwest along Mljet, Okuklje is a near-circular bolt-hole outside the park line — twenty miles on, its family konobas hanging free buoys for anyone eating ashore.
9.description: Okuklje sits back up the southeast coast of Mljet, this time beyond the park line, so there's nothing to pay. It's a tight, almost round bay that takes maybe twenty boats, and the narrow mouth blocks nearly everything the sea throws at it; holding is superb in five to ten metres of mud. Three family konobas run the shore, each with its own free buoys for guests, so the drill is simple: grab a buoy, dinghy in, book a table, done. Black risotto is the thing to order, crni rižot cooked dark with cuttlefish ink and whatever came in that day. The hills stand a hundred metres straight up on three sides, which means the wind quits at sunset no matter what's blowing outside. It's the quietest night on the route.
thingsToDo: Grab a free konoba buoy and book dinner
Order crni rižot cooked with cuttlefish ink
Walk the headland path toward Korčula's view
Swim straight off the boat in flat water
Sleep with no town noise at all
9.mooringTip: Free konoba buoys again; a dinner booking secures yours. Okuklje is shut off from north, east and south, and only a northwesterly above 18 knots pushes any real swell through the entrance, so it's a dependable overnight.

10.shortDescription: A walled town on a spit of rock, Korčula guards the Pelješac Channel fifteen miles northwest — its Moreška sword dance staged on Monday and Thursday nights.
10.description: You can pick Korčula out from a long way off — a wedge of red roofs on a low spit, one campanile above them, the old walls dropping sheer to the water on three sides. The channel run up from Okuklje is fifteen miles northwest. ACI Marina Korčula on the eastern side of the peninsula has full services and lazy lines; the town quay on the western side takes a handful of stern-to boats with their own anchor for a fee, but it's exposed to the maestral and gets rolly after two. The old town is tiny, laid out in a herringbone of lanes designed to trap the breeze and shed the wind. Climb St. Mark's bell tower for the channel view, look in on the disputed Marco Polo house, walk the Land Gate steps. On Monday and Thursday evenings the Moreška sword dance plays out at nine, blades and all. Pour a glass of Grk, the white grape from nearby Lumbarda.
thingsToDo: Watch the Moreška sword dance at 21:00
Climb the bell tower of St. Mark's Cathedral
Walk the lanes after the day-ferries leave
Look in on the disputed Marco Polo house
Drink Grk white from nearby Lumbarda
10.mooringTip: On the east side, ACI Marina Korčula covers any weather — full services, laid lines. Its western town quay is stern-to on your own anchor but faces the maestral, so reckon on roll past 14:00 and choose the marina whenever wind threatens.

11.shortDescription: Skip the Hvar Town circus for Jelsa, the island's quiet farming town on the north coast — twenty-three miles northwest over the channel, lavender rows climbing the hills behind it.
11.description: Jelsa is Hvar's third town, and nothing like the first. It sits on the north coast, twenty-three miles northwest across the channel from Korčula. The town quay takes stern-to with your own anchor for a fee, good holding in mud and sand, and a small marina on the east side handles boats that want lazy lines. This is the farming heart of the island. The lavender belt still runs commercial rows up the road toward Vrboska and Stari Grad, cut each July, and the back-lane konobas pour the local Bogdanjuša white. Two good ways to spend the afternoon: cycle the three coastal miles to Vrboska and its canal-bridged old centre, or walk inland across the Stari Grad Plain, a UNESCO site and one of the oldest farmed landscapes in Europe, its field walls laid out by Greek colonists.
thingsToDo: Cycle the coast road to Vrboska's canals
Walk the UNESCO Stari Grad Plain inland
Order Bogdanjuša white at a back-lane konoba
Buy Hvar lavender oil from a grower
Climb to the Tor watchtower above town
11.mooringTip: Take the Jelsa town quay stern-to on your own hook for a small fee, water and power at mid-quay. The bay is well shielded across south, southeast and southwest; a small east-side marina lays lines if you would sooner skip the anchor.

12.shortDescription: Lučice is a twin-lobed, uninhabited cove just east of Pučišća, ten miles north over the Brač Channel — and the clearest water you'll swim in all fortnight.
12.description: Lučice notches into Brač's south coast just east of Pučišća, a ten-mile skip north over the channel. There's no town here at all, just white limestone cliffs, pine, and two seasonal beach restaurants that lay free buoys for guests. The bay forks into two pine-hugged lobes, and the water is the reason you come: Brač's pale stone floor throws visibility out to twenty metres on a still summer day, so the snorkelling is the best you'll get. Konoba Dupini runs the water's edge, sixteen buoys and grilled fish straight off Šime's boat, octopus and meat under the bell, a jug of Plavac Mali. Take the dinghy the last mile into Pučišća to see the stonemasons' yards, where the school still cuts white Brač stone by hand.
thingsToDo: Pick up a free buoy off Konoba Dupini
Snorkel the pale limestone floor, 20 m clear
Eat grilled fish and Plavac Mali ashore
Dinghy into Pučišća's stonemason yards
Paddle the headland at first light
12.mooringTip: Free restaurant buoys at Lučice, secured by eating ashore; if they're taken you can anchor in 8–12 m over sand and weed. The lobes shelter from north, northeast and east but stand open to the south, so watch for a southerly and move on if one's building.

13.shortDescription: A chain of seven islets guards the mouth of Maslinica, the only proper harbour on Šolta and fourteen miles northwest of Lučice.
13.description: Maslinica curls into an S at the western tip of Šolta, the one genuine harbour on the island. The leg up from Lučice is fourteen miles northwest. A row of islets, Polebrnjak, Stipanska and the rest, sits across the entrance and soaks up whatever the open Adriatic sends in, so it's calm inside almost regardless. Mooring splits two ways: Martinis Marchi Marina on the south side, lazy lines with an eighteenth-century baroque castle turned hotel on the quay, or the village quay opposite, stern-to with your own anchor for a modest fee. Šolta is the island closest to Split, fifteen miles off, and also the sleepiest of the central bunch. The square konobas run on Šoltansko olive oil and Dobričić, the island's own red grape. A dinghy across to Stipanska buys you a last clean swim.
thingsToDo: Walk into the Martinis Marchi castle courtyard
Order Dobričić, Šolta's own red grape
Dinghy to Stipanska island for a swim
Buy Šoltansko olive oil in the village
Watch sunset from Polebrnjak at the mouth
13.mooringTip: South-side Martinis Marchi Marina runs full services and laid lines; reserve in season. Facing it, the cheaper village quay is stern-to on your own hook. A line of islets across the mouth screens the bay across the arc from south through southwest and west to northwest.

14.shortDescription: The last day is the long one: thirty miles northwest back to Šibenik for the Saturday handover, an early start with the prevailing summer breeze mostly behind you.
14.description: Maslinica to the Šibenik base is the longest hop of the fortnight, thirty miles and all of it northwest. Saturday handover rules apply, so the boat needs to be alongside by nine with the fuel topped, tanks pumped out, decks rinsed and inventory checked. That means an early line-off. The good news is the direction: the prevailing summer breeze usually sits fair on this heading, and the buoyed channel into the river estuary is easy to read even tired. Most Šibenik-based boats lie either at ACI Marina Šibenik, in the inner harbour within walking distance of St. James' Cathedral, or at D-Marin Mandalina across the estuary with full yard services. If the schedule allows, steal one more swim off the south coast of Brač or Šolta on the way north before the season closes out.
thingsToDo: Top up fuel and pump out before nine
Walk to UNESCO-listed St. James' Cathedral
Climb to St. Michael's fortress over town
Grab a last swim off south Brač
Take a long lunch on the Šibenik Riva
14.mooringTip: Return to whichever base is on your charter contract, ACI Marina Šibenik or D-Marin Mandalina. The Saturday handover window is tight, usually 08:00–09:00, so if your contract asks for a night-before return, aim to be alongside by around 17:00 on Friday.

sibenik-hvar-vis-solta-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Šibenik to Vis & Blue Cave | Boat4You
sibenik-hvar-vis-solta-route.metaDesc: Round-trip sailing route from Šibenik: Primošten, Komiža on Vis, the Blue Cave, the Pakleni Islands and Šolta, with distances, marinas and mooring notes.

1.shortDescription: A short 14-mile hop south past the St. Anthony narrows to Primošten, a church-topped peninsula ringed by the terraced Babić vineyards.
1.description: Clear the breakwater at ACI Šibenik and the first stretch runs you down through the St. Anthony channel, a tight limestone gut with the road bridge overhead before the coast opens out. Primošten opens up fourteen miles down the coast. It hangs off the shore on one thin sandy neck, red roofs stacked to the waterline, with the St. George church planted on the very top. Tie up in town for the afternoon only; the harbour wall is open to the west and gets uncomfortable once the sea builds. For the night we drop two miles down to Kremik, deep in its inlet and about as sheltered as marinas get on this coast. The shuttle runs back into town. Behind Primošten the Bukovac terraces climb the slope in dry-stone squares, still worked for the Babić red poured on every quay.
thingsToDo: Climb to St. George church on the peak
Walk the Bukovac dry-stone vineyard terraces
Taste local Babić red on the quay
Take the Kremik shuttle in for dinner
Swim off the town's sandy neck
1.mooringTip: Kremik, two miles south, is the night berth: lazy lines, full services and a deep, all-weather inlet with a shuttle into Primošten. Use the town quay for daytime stops only, since it lies open to westerlies.

2.shortDescription: Twelve miles down to the Krknjaši lagoon, a shallow turquoise pool between the Drvenik islets, good for a swim stop and a quiet night.
2.description: The run across to Krknjaši is a lazy twelve miles, ending in the strip of water between Veli and Mali Drvenik with the pair of low Krknjaš islets closing off the swell. This is a swimming stop first and everything else second. The bottom is pale sand at three to six metres, the holding is reliable, and the anchor sits so clearly you can read it from the bow. Two of the little tavernas on Veli Drvenik hang free buoys for guests, though most crews simply drop the hook on the sand. Ashore there is no road, just a footpath over the headland and a handful of tables serving grilled orada and a carafe of Plavac Mali. Time it for a June-to-September call; the kitchens shut outside high summer. We usually swim, walk the half-hour loop, then eat late.
thingsToDo: Anchor on the sand in 4-6 m
Walk the half-hour headland loop
Eat grilled orada at a Drvenik taverna
Order a carafe of Plavac Mali
Snorkel the shallows off the Krknjaš islets
2.mooringTip: Drop the hook on the sand floor in four to six metres for reliable holding, or pick up a free taverna buoy if you plan to eat ashore. The lagoon covers you from north through south and east; only westerlies send swell in.

3.shortDescription: The long leg of the week: twenty-five miles southwest to the working fishing town of Komiža on Vis, which owns the week's best sunset.
3.description: Point southwest and settle in; this is the week's biggest single passage at twenty-five miles, most of it open water with Vis rising slowly out of the haze. The island stayed a closed military zone until 1989, which is why you find so little resort sprawl and so many stone lanes left as they were. Komiža wraps around a bay on the west coast, the fishing fleet still working the eastern side. Berth stern-to on the inner quay, sand and weed under you and mostly firm holding; leave the outer wall alone when any southwest swell is running. The set piece is dinner at Jastožera, an old lobster house from 1883 where small boats slip under the arch into an inner pool. Order the grilled bogueroni and a bottle of Vugava, then take the sunset from the wall.
thingsToDo: Dine at Jastožera, the 1883 lobster house
Hike Mount Hum to Tito's cave
Order grilled bogueroni with a Vugava white
See the Fishermen's Museum in the Venetian tower
Take the sunset from the harbour wall
3.mooringTip: Aim to reach the inner quay before early afternoon in high season, and berth stern-to on the sand-and-weed bottom for mostly firm holding. The outer wall takes any southwest swell; if it builds past fifteen knots, run the six miles north to Vis Town in St. George Bay.

4.shortDescription: A dawn call at the Blue Cave, then twenty miles east to Palmižana, the only marina in the pine-covered Pakleni Islands off Hvar.
4.description: Get away early. The Blue Cave on Biševo only glows its full ultramarine when the low sun angles through the underwater mouth, roughly nine to eleven in the morning, and the queue lengthens fast after that. Tie to the concession quay by the entrance and wait for an official tender; private dinghies stay outside, and the whole visit runs about fifteen minutes. From there it is twenty miles east-northeast to Palmižana on Sv. Klement, the largest and only inhabited islet of the Pakleni cluster. ACI Marina Palmižana takes you stern-to on lazy lines, and a July or August berth needs booking online well ahead. The main bay grows rolly once the Maestral funnels through around two o'clock, so for a settled night slip round to Vinogradišće on the south side. Ashore, walk the Meneghello garden, planted in 1906, and eat at Zori or Laganini.
thingsToDo: Take the tender into the Blue Cave
Snorkel the Roman amphora field off Sv. Klement
Walk the Meneghello garden from 1906
Dine at Zori or Laganini in Vinogradišće
Water-taxi across to Hvar Town at night
4.mooringTip: Book the ACI Palmižana lazy-line berth online for July and August; it fills. If the marina is full or the main bay turns rolly under the afternoon Maestral, anchor with a line ashore in Vinogradišće cove on the south side.

5.shortDescription: Maslinica, on Šolta's western tip, is eighteen miles northwest and the island's one true harbour, screened from the open sea by a line of seven islets.
5.description: Šolta's only proper harbour is Maslinica, at the western tip of the island and eighteen miles up from Palmižana. Seven low islets lie strung across the entrance like a breakwater, so the basin stays flat while the open Adriatic works itself up outside. Two berths to choose from: Martinis Marchi on the south shore, lazy lines under a converted eighteenth-century castle, or the village quay across the water, stern-to on your own hook for a small fee. For all that it sits barely fifteen miles off Split, Šolta stays the quiet one of the group, its square konobas trading on home-pressed Šoltansko oil and Dobričić, a dark old grape that sired Plavac Mali. Late on we run the dinghy round to Šešula, swim off the rock ledges, then carry the sunset home from Polebrnjak at the harbour mouth.
thingsToDo: Berth under the Martinis Marchi castle
Taste the indigenous Dobričić red
Dinghy to Šešula cove for a swim
Buy Šoltansko olive oil in the village
Watch sunset from Polebrnjak islet
5.mooringTip: Martinis Marchi on the south side offers lazy lines and full services, worth booking ahead in season; the north village quay takes stern-to with your own anchor. The chain of islets at the mouth keeps the basin calm in south, southwest, west and northwest winds.

6.shortDescription: Rogoznica sits fifteen miles northwest, a peninsula town with the saltwater Dragon's Eye karst lake and the big, sheltered Marina Frapa.
6.description: Rogoznica comes next, a low peninsula town set about halfway up the coast between Šibenik and Split, fifteen miles on. Its oddity is inland: the Dragon's Eye, a round saltwater lake dropped into the karst on the Gradina headland, fed through a concealed underwater passage that swaps its water with the tide. The basin runs deep, sixty metres in places, and the deeper layers hold hydrogen sulphide that sometimes sets the surface fizzing. Marina Frapa spreads across the south side, among the biggest berthing basins on this stretch of the Adriatic, with lazy lines, full services and a fuel pontoon right at the entrance. That makes it a sensible night to top up tanks before the last hop. Smaller boats can lie stern-to on the north village quay, though the northwest gradient makes the marina the safer call in season. Walk out to the lake late afternoon.
thingsToDo: Walk out to the Dragon's Eye lake
Look down from the karst cliffs
Top up fuel at the Frapa pontoon
Dinghy to Smokvica islet for a swim
Eat brudet at a peninsula konoba
6.mooringTip: On the south side, Marina Frapa is the any-weather berth — laid lines, the full service list, and a fuelling pontoon by the mouth. The northern village quay faces the northwest gradient head-on, so move onto the marina as the afternoon sea breeze builds.

7.shortDescription: The last fifteen miles back into Šibenik, past the car-free coral island of Zlarin and the St. Nicholas fort, for the Saturday handover.
7.description: The last leg runs fifteen miles back to base and closes the loop. Saturday handover keeps its own timetable, so plan to be alongside by nine, tanks pumped out, decks hosed down, fuel topped and the inventory squared away. If the clock allows, the course lets you pause at Zlarin, a car-free island that made its living from red coral for centuries; the small museum on the square opens in the afternoons and rewards a half-hour ashore. From there the channel narrows into the Šibenik estuary, past the sixteenth-century St. Nicholas fort standing guard at the river mouth, and up to ACI Šibenik or D-Marin Mandalina. Once the boat is signed off, St. James' Cathedral, carved entirely in stone and UNESCO-listed, is a five-minute walk from the pontoon. A long lunch on the Riva is the right way to end the week.
thingsToDo: Stop at Zlarin's coral museum
Top up fuel before the 09:00 inspection
Walk to St. James' Cathedral
Climb St. Michael's fortress over the old town
Take a long lunch on the Riva
7.mooringTip: Bring the boat back into your booked base, ACI Šibenik or D-Marin Mandalina, for the 08:00 to 09:00 Saturday handover. If your contract calls for a night-before return, aim to be alongside by around five on the Friday.

sibenik-kornati-molat-route.metaDesc: A week-long sail from Šibenik through Kornati and Telašćica to the quiet outer islands of Iž, Molat and Ugljan, with mooring notes for every night.

1.shortDescription: Car-free Kaprije lies fifteen miles west, out past the fortress channel: one quiet village around a working harbour and the cleanest swimming in the Šibenik islands.
1.description: You leave the marina and thread down the St. Anthony channel, under the bridge and past the low-lying salt-farmers' island of Krapanj, before the water opens west toward Kaprije. The island runs on caper plants and quiet; there are no cars, and the single village folds around its harbour on the eastern shore. We usually tie stern-to on the village quay, own anchor down, water on the central berths but nothing for shore power. Depths hold at five to six metres with decent sand-and-weed holding. Afternoon is for the footpath inland to the chapel on the ridge, where the whole Kornati cluster spreads out offshore. Dinner is grilled fish and a glass of Babić, the dark local red from the Šibenik hills. There's no wrong konoba on Kaprije; ask on the quay which one landed fish that day.
thingsToDo: Walk the ridge path to the hilltop chapel
Order grilled fish at a Kaprije konoba
Swim off the rocks south of the village
Take the dinghy to the islets at the bay entrance
Sample Babić red at a quayside table
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on the village quay with your own anchor for a small fee; expect water on the central berths but no power. If the quay fills, pick up one of the roughly twenty buoys in the bay or anchor in six to eight metres; shelter is good from everything except a northeasterly.

2.shortDescription: ACI Piškera sits ten miles northwest inside Kornati National Park, the only marina within the boundary, bare karst and empty islets in every direction.
2.description: The run northwest carries you into the Kornati archipelago, close to ninety islands of bare stone with no residents, no fishing, and dry-stone walls climbing every ridge. The park charges a daily entry fee, cheaper if you buy it online before you arrive rather than at the kiosk. Your berth is ACI Marina Piškera, tucked in the channel behind Panitula Vela on the western rim of the park, with lazy lines, water, power and a single restaurant up on the rocks. Book ahead in July and August; the pontoons fill by mid-afternoon. Once tied up, take the dinghy across to snorkel the shallow channel toward Lavsa, or motor south to the crowns of Mana and Kornat where the cliffs fall eighty metres straight into the blue.
thingsToDo: Buy the park ticket online before arrival
Snorkel the channel between Piškera and Lavsa
Sail past the 80-metre cliffs of Mana
Eat at the marina's clifftop restaurant
Walk the karst spine of Panitula Vela
2.mooringTip: ACI Piškera runs lazy lines on every berth, so book online for July and August when it fills early. Overnight anchoring inside the park is limited to a handful of licensed bays such as Lavsa, Lojena and Vrulje; rangers patrol and check tickets.

3.shortDescription: Telašćica bites into the south tip of Dugi Otok, eight miles north: cliff-walled, with a warm salt lake ashore and a hundred-and-sixty-metre wall over the anchorage.
3.description: A short hop north brings you into Telašćica, a deep bay biting into the southern end of Dugi Otok, pine and grey cliff on one side, a scatter of smaller coves on the other. This is a nature park in its own right, with a separate fee and a rule that you pick up one of the park buoys for the night. Two things pull crews ashore. The first is Mir, a shallow salt lake a short walk from the bay that warms far quicker than the sea, so the swimming stays soft into the evening. The second is the Grpašćak lookout on the western wall, where the rock stands a sheer hundred and sixty metres above the water and the view runs clear across the outer Kornati. Stay aboard after dark for the cicadas and a genuinely still anchorage.
thingsToDo: Walk to the Mir salt lake for a warm swim
Hike the Grpašćak clifftop for the panorama
Pick up a park buoy and pay the ranger
Snorkel the sheltered eastern coves
3.mooringTip: Telašćica is a park, so you take one of the marked buoys in the southern arm of the bay and pay the ranger, entry included. Shelter here is close to total in any wind, which makes it one of the safest overnight bays on the central coast.

4.shortDescription: Cross the Iž channel fifteen miles northeast to Veli Iž, a low-key potters' village where the Zadar islands ease into fjaka.
4.description: Northeast across the Iž channel sits Veli Iž, the larger of the island's two villages and a place that has traded on clay for generations. The old craft still runs here; a small workshop off the square turns hand-thrown pots much as it did a century ago. Tie stern-to on the village quay with your anchor, or take a lazy-line berth at Marina Veli Iž on the southern side of the bay if you want power and a shower. The bay covers you from west, southwest and south, but a Bora lays straight in from the northeast. Afternoons go one of two ways. Climb the signed track to Korinjak at a hundred and sixty-eight metres for the long view over the archipelago, or stay down at sea level: swim off the rocks south of the bay, then eat pašticada at a konoba on the front.
thingsToDo: Visit the potter's workshop off the village square
Hike to Korinjak for the archipelago view
Sample local figs and olive oil at a small farm
Swim from the rocks south of the bay
4.mooringTip: Stern-to on the village quay with your own anchor and a modest fee, or lazy lines and full services at Marina Veli Iž on the south side of the bay. The bay holds well in west through south winds; if a strong Bora is forecast from the northeast, the marina is the safer bet.

5.shortDescription: Molat, twelve miles northwest, is a thin outer island of three small villages, empty coves and next to no charter boats.
5.description: Molat sits out toward the open sea, a thin ridge of an island with three villages and space to breathe. Most crews come here precisely because it stays quiet; the yachts thin out and the coves go still. Zapuntel, on the eastern side, is the natural overnight. A dogleg breakwater shelters the little harbour, with mooring buoys laid in the bay in seven to eight metres and clean sand-and-weed holding if you'd rather anchor. Shore power isn't part of the deal, so top up water before you come. With the boat settled, follow the marked track up the spine to the overgrown remains of the wartime airstrip the Italian air force cut into the island, still readable in the scrub. Then swim off the rocks by the bay mouth while the light drops. Dinner is whatever came up that morning.
thingsToDo: Hike the spine to the wartime airstrip ruins
Pick up a buoy in Zapuntel Bay
Swim off the rocks at the bay mouth
Snorkel the rocky eastern shoreline by dinghy
Order grilled dentex at the village konoba
5.mooringTip: Pick up one of the buoys in Zapuntel Bay in seven to eight metres, or anchor on sand-and-weed; the harbour quay is small and free but has no services. Cover is good from west through north; if the wind swings into the southeast, shift a couple of miles round to Brgulje.

6.shortDescription: Sutomišćica opens on Ugljan, fourteen miles southeast, a broad calm bay with a small family marina and olive terraces down to the shore.
6.description: Ugljan is the green barrier island that screens Zadar from the open water, and locals call it the town's garden for the olive terraces and vineyards that cover it. Sutomišćica opens on the eastern side, a broad bay that stays calm in almost any weather. Olive Island Marina sits on the north shore, a small family-run place with lazy lines and full services; its restaurant does honest Dalmatian cooking if you don't feel like walking ashore. The village strings along the southern shore with a daytime pontoon and a few konobas on the front. In the afternoon, walk the headland path to the little chapel of St. Peter for the view back over the Zadar channel, or paddle out to the Sveti Petar islet just offshore for lunch on your own patch of rock.
thingsToDo: Walk to the St. Peter chapel for the channel view
Paddleboard out to Sveti Petar islet
Dinner at the marina restaurant on the north shore
Swim from the rocks on the bay's east side
Taste local olive oil from the Ugljan groves
6.mooringTip: On the north shore, Olive Island Marina is your any-weather berth — lazy lines plus water and power all year. The south-side village pontoon is a daytime stop only, though the bay itself gives shelter from every quarter.

7.shortDescription: The long haul home: twenty-five miles southeast to Šibenik for the Saturday handover, with a last swim off sandy Vrgada before the estuary.
7.description: The last leg is the longest, twenty-five miles southeast back to base, so we usually slip the lines by nine to leave room for one more swim. Saturday handover means the boat wants to be alongside early, gauge full, black-water pumped down, salt hosed off the deck and the gear checked against the list, so shape the day around it rather than against it. Vrgada makes the natural break on the way down; it's one of the few genuinely sandy islands on this coast, and the reddish beach on its southern side shelves gently into warm shallows. Anchor off in five to eight metres, swim, eat, then push on. From there it's into the Šibenik estuary, past the St. Nicholas fortress guarding the river mouth, and up the channel to your marina, a short walk from St. James' cathedral in the old town.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Vrgada's sandy south beach for a swim
Top up fuel and pump out before handover
Walk to St. James' Cathedral in the old town
See the St. Nicholas fortress at the river mouth
Climb to St. Michael's fortress over the old town
7.mooringTip: Return into your booked Šibenik base, either ACI Marina Šibenik or D-Marin Mandalina, and aim to be alongside inside the 08:00–09:00 handover window. If your contract asks for a night-before return, get in by Friday evening rather than pushing the last miles at dawn.

skradin-dugi-otok-route.metaDesc: A seven-day loop from Skradin: down the Krka, through the Šibenik islands into Kornati and Telašćica, then back upriver. Anchorages and mooring notes.

1.shortDescription: A gentle fifteen miles down the Krka and past Šibenik to a car-free coral island where one working village faces the sea.
1.description: Cast off from ACI Marina Skradin and you spend the first hour idling down the freshwater Krka at five knots, past mussel lines and the walls of Šibenik old town. The St. Anthony channel funnels you out under the road bridge, then it opens up for the short hop south to Zlarin. No cars here, just one village strung along the eastern shore. What made the place was red coral: divers have been going down for it here the best part of seven hundred years, and a one-room museum on the square lays the whole trade out. We usually take the south side of the long pier, stern-to with our own anchor, water and power on the berths. Dinner is whatever came up in the nets that morning, washed down with a jug of local Babić.
thingsToDo: See the coral trade at Zlarin's museum
Order the day's catch at Konoba Ivana
Swim off the rocks south of the harbour
Browse a village coral-jewellery workshop
Walk the pine paths inland from the village
1.mooringTip: Take the south side of the village pier, stern-to on your own anchor, with water and power laid on. The harbour holds well against anything off the land (W, NW or N) but lies open to a SE blow; if that fills in, motor back into the Šibenik estuary for cover.

2.shortDescription: Fifteen miles west to the fringe of Kornati National Park and a bare islet with one konoba, a breakwater berth and no lights after dark.
2.description: The passage west runs about fifteen miles into the southern reaches of Kornati National Park. Pay the park fee online before you go; the ranger kiosk gets busy in season. Ravni Žakan is a low, uninhabited islet with a single konoba above the water and a sturdy breakwater where guests who eat ashore can lie stern-to with water and power. The whole place walks end to end in half an hour, up to a spine that opens the horizon in every direction. Save the mask and fins for the northwest shore. The seabed there steps down through sand and broken rock to about fifteen metres, clear as gin, and no other stop all week comes close. After the konoba closes there is nothing but the slap of water on the hull and, on a clear night, the Milky Way smeared across the sky.
thingsToDo: Book the park ticket online before arrival
Eat grilled fish at Konoba Žakan
Snorkel the northwest shore over sand and rock
Walk the half-hour spine for the panorama
Watch the Milky Way from the cockpit
2.mooringTip: Berths at Ravni Žakan are few and tied to the konoba, so plan to arrive by early afternoon in July and August, or call ahead. Overnight anchoring inside the park is restricted to a short list of licensed bays, so the breakwater is your surest bet here.

3.shortDescription: Twelve miles northwest into Telašćica's deep bay, where a warm salt lake and 160-metre cliffs sit within a short walk of the buoy field.
3.description: Twelve miles up the coast brings you onto the southern tip of Dugi Otok and into Telašćica, one of the safest natural harbours on this coast. This is a separate nature park, so budget a second entry fee; the rangers come round in a launch to collect it and take your rubbish. Pick up one of the roughly thirty buoys in the inner bay. Two walks are worth the legs. A path leads over to Lake Mir, a salt lake that runs several degrees warmer than the sea and rises and falls with the moon. The other climbs to the Grpašćak lookout, a 160-metre wall of rock with the whole southern archipelago laid out under your boots. Come evening the cicadas take over and the anchorage falls dead calm.
thingsToDo: Swim the warm water of Lake Mir
Climb to the Grpašćak cliff lookout
Pay the ranger from your buoy
Snorkel the sheltered eastern shore
Listen for cicadas at dusk on deck
3.mooringTip: The inner bay carries around thirty park buoys; pick one up and settle the fee with the ranger, which covers park entry. The bay stays calm in almost any wind, which makes it one of the most dependable overnight stops on the route.

4.shortDescription: Eight miles southeast back into Kornati to Žut, a bare karst island ringed with konoba bays and one marina at Pristanišće.
4.description: A short eight-mile reach carries you back over the park boundary to Žut. Only Kornat outsizes it in the whole park, and nobody lives on it year-round. What you get instead is a run of deep bays along the south coast, several with a family konoba on the rocks and free buoys for anyone who books a table. Marina Žut at Pristanišće is the option with lazy lines and water if you would rather have services. Ashore it's stone and little else: karst ridges seamed with old dry-stone walls and a handful of waymarked paths, good for stretching the legs before dinner. Order the grilled fish and a bottle of Pošip, and watch the light go long over the bare stone. On the way in, the southern cliffs of Kornat make a fine backdrop.
thingsToDo: Take a free konoba buoy in a south-coast bay
Order grilled fish with a Pošip white
Walk the dry-stone trails along the ridge
Snorkel the clear water off the east coast
Sail beneath the southern cliffs of Kornat
4.mooringTip: The south-coast konoba bays lay free buoys for guests who dine ashore; Marina Žut at Pristanišće has lazy lines and water if you want full services. Anchoring on the seabed is regulated inside the park, so plan on a buoy or a berth rather than dropping the hook.

5.shortDescription: Twelve miles east out of the park to Murter, the gateway island, and Betina's working harbour of wooden boatbuilders and quayside konobas.
5.description: Twelve miles east and you leave the national park behind, back to inhabited coast at Murter. Crews have used it as their jumping-off point for the Kornati for generations. Strictly it's a peninsula: a low bridge at Tisno hooks it back to the mainland, near enough to walk over. Betina is where most boats tie up, a working fishing port where the town quay takes you stern-to on your own hook, with water and power on the middle berths. The village has kept its trade in wooden boatbuilding alive for four hundred years, and the little maritime museum tells that story well. Ashore, wander over to the old salt pans at Tisno, worked since Roman times and still producing, or swim off Slanica on the south coast. The konobas run on local fish, Pag cheese and Murter olive oil, with a Babić red to finish. ACI Marina Jezera is the all-weather fallback.
thingsToDo: See the wooden-boat museum in Betina
Walk the Roman-era salt pans at Tisno
Swim at Slanica beach on the south coast
Taste Pag cheese and Murter olive oil
Order pašticada at a Betina konoba
5.mooringTip: Betina town quay is stern-to on your own anchor, with water and power on the central berths and a short walk to the konobas. If the forecast turns, ACI Marina Jezera nearby has lazy lines and full services in any wind.

6.shortDescription: A lazy six miles to Tribunj, where the old town sits on its own islet and the marina fills the bay just south.
6.description: Only six miles today, southeast to Tribunj on the mainland just north of Vodice, so there is no rush to leave. The marina sits on the south side of the village with lazy lines, water and power on every berth and a fuel pontoon by the entrance, handy for topping up before the run home. The old town is the reason to stop: a knot of lanes and courtyards out on its own islet, reached by one stone footbridge, fig trees leaning over the walls and a couple of good konobas tucked in among them. Try the crni rižot, squid-ink risotto black as tar, at Konoba Bepo. It's a snug enough bay in most winds; put a southerly in and you'll feel some swell work across the berths. On summer evenings you may catch a klapa group singing on the harbour wall, unaccompanied and unannounced.
thingsToDo: Wander the islet old town over the stone bridge
Order crni rižot at Konoba Bepo
Swim at Čista Bay east of the marina
Catch klapa singing on the harbour wall
Pick up local Vodice olive oil
6.mooringTip: The marina on the south side of Tribunj has lazy lines, full services and fuel at the entrance; book ahead in high season. The bay is well protected in most directions, with only a southerly bringing any swell onto the berths.

7.shortDescription: A final twelve miles back up the Šibenik estuary to Skradin, with a last shuttle to the Krka waterfalls before the Saturday handover.
7.description: The run home is twelve miles, south into the Šibenik estuary then up the river to ACI Marina Skradin. Plan the morning around a nine o'clock handover on Saturday: decks hosed off, holding tanks emptied, fuel topped up and the inventory back where it started. The approach is one of the more memorable on the coast. You come in past the St. Nicholas fortress on its rock at the mouth, duck under the Šibenik bridge in the St. Anthony narrows, run below the old town, and then the river closes in green and deep between the hills the whole way to the pontoons. Keep the speed down; the whole river is a five-knot limit past the mussel lines. With an hour to spare before handover, you can still hop the park boat up to Skradinski Buk for one more dip under the falls. A plate of Skradin risotto sends you off.
thingsToDo: Ride the park ferry to Skradinski Buk falls
Swim below the waterfalls one last time
Fuel and pump out before the 09:00 handover
Order slow-cooked Skradin risotto ashore
Walk the medieval streets behind the marina
7.mooringTip: Return into ACI Marina Skradin as your contract directs; the base team will point you to a slot. The Saturday window is tight, usually eight to nine, so if your paperwork asks for a Friday-night return, be back on the river by late afternoon.

skradin-hvar-route.metaDesc: Seven-day yacht route from Skradin: Krka, Primošten, Šolta, Hvar, Milna and Šibenik, with distances, mooring and konoba picks for each leg.

1.shortDescription: A 20-mile run down the Krka's brackish lower reach, out past Šibenik, then south to the church-topped peninsula of Primošten.
1.description: Leaving ACI Marina Skradin means motoring the freshwater top of the Krka before the river opens out. You pass under the Šibenik bridge and thread the St. Anthony channel, where the current can push you around a little, then break into open water for the eight-mile hop south. You will pick out Primošten well before you close it: the town caps a little peninsula slung off the mainland on a sandbar you could almost wade across, red roofs spilling to the sea and the St. George church pinned at the top. We usually take a slip at Marina Kremik, two miles down the coast, because it sits deep in a protected inlet and holds in any weather. A shuttle runs into the old town for dinner. Uphill behind the last houses the Bukovac terraces climb in hand-laid dry-stone walls, the shelter that ripens the Babić vines into the heavy local red.
thingsToDo: Climb to St. George church on the peak
Drink a glass of Babić on the quay
Hike the hand-stacked Bukovac wine terraces
Sit down to a bowl of brudet fish stew
Ride the Marina Kremik shuttle into town
1.mooringTip: Book a berth at Marina Kremik rather than the town quay: the inlet is one of the best-sheltered on this coast, and the town front is open to the west. Lazy lines and a shuttle bus are the payoff.

2.shortDescription: Twenty miles southeast to Maslinica, a deep V-shaped harbour on Šolta's western end, screened by a run of seven small islets.
2.description: The crossing is an easy reach in the usual afternoon Maestral, twenty miles down to the far tip of Šolta. Maslinica is the island's one genuine harbour, and the string of islets across its mouth flattens whatever the open Adriatic sends in. You can lie stern-to on the village quay with your own anchor, or take a lazy line at Martinis Marchi on the south side, where an old baroque castle now runs as a hotel. Šolta stays quiet even in August, closer to Split than most crews expect but skipped by the crowds. Ashore, the konobas pour Dobričić, the dark island grape, and cook with Šolta olive oil pressed a few streets away. If the marina is full, Šešula bay half a mile round the corner is a dependable anchorage in most winds.
thingsToDo: Anchor for a swim off Stipanska islet
Drink the local Dobričić, a dark Šolta red
Poke around the Martinis Marchi castle courtyard
Buy cold-pressed Šolta olive oil
Duck round to Šešula bay for shelter
2.mooringTip: Martinis Marchi takes lazy lines but fills fast in July and August, so reserve ahead; the village quay is stern-to with your own anchor for a smaller fee. Šešula bay, half a mile east, holds well if you would rather swing on the hook.

3.shortDescription: Fifteen miles down to Hvar Town, the busiest harbour on the coast, where ACI Marina Hvar is the only safe overnight berth in a blow.
3.description: The leg off Šolta to Hvar Town runs fifteen miles and lands you in the loudest port in Dalmatia through the July and August crush. You read the skyline before you read the chart: Fortica fortress up on the hill, the Arsenal's arcade along the front, St. Stephen's bell tower over the main square. Make straight for ACI Marina Hvar on the west side of the bay, the one berth that holds a mid-size yacht when it pipes up, which is why it needs booking well ahead; every slip has lazy lines, water and power. The town quay bills by the hour, packs out with tour boats and tenders by noon, and sits wide open to the Maestral that pours in each afternoon. Get into the marina, walk ashore, and take the Fortica climb early while the steps are still cool. Eat inland, in the lanes behind the square, where the bill runs half what the seafront charges.
thingsToDo: Take the Fortica steps early, before the heat
See the Arsenal arcade and the cathedral on the square
Hop by taxi-boat to Palmižana in the Pakleni
Find a back-lane konoba off the square
Watch the superyacht circus from the marina wall at dusk
3.mooringTip: Reserve ACI Marina Hvar as early as you can; in season it is the only berth that holds a mid-size yacht through an afternoon blow. If it is full, ACI Palmižana across in the Pakleni is calmer, with a ten-minute taxi-boat into town.

4.shortDescription: Twelve miles north over the Hvar Channel lies Milna, the cosiest harbour along Brač's western shore and the place for lamb baked under the peka.
4.description: Milna is a short twelve miles north over the Hvar Channel, a deep slot of water knifed into the west coast of Brač. The Venetians called it the bay of a thousand ships, and it has been a working port ever since; the 17th-century parish church stands on the inner quay, grey stone terraces climbing the slope behind it. Lie stern-to on the inner town quay with your own anchor for a modest fee, water and power along the middle stretch. The outer bay is the anchorage, sand in six to ten metres, and a line to the rocks pays off if the Maestral gets up past mid-afternoon. Brač built Diocletian's Palace out of its white limestone, and its other export is lamb roasted slow under the bell. Order it the minute you tie up, then walk the headland north while it cooks.
thingsToDo: Order peka lamb the moment you moor
Find the 17th-century church on the quayside
Drop the hook in the outer bay with a line ashore
Dinghy north to Bobovišća for a quiet swim
Buy island olive oil from the village co-op
4.mooringTip: Take the inner town quay stern-to with your own anchor; water and power run along the central section for a modest harbour fee. The bay opens southwest to west, so if the afternoon Maestral pipes up, Bobovišća three miles north is the all-weather fallback.

5.shortDescription: Rogoznica lies twenty miles northwest, a peninsula town known for the strange Dragon's Eye salt lake plus the huge, all-weather Marina Frapa.
5.description: It is twenty miles back up the open coast to Rogoznica. The oddity here sits a short walk from the marina: the Dragon's Eye, a saltwater karst lake ringed by rock that trades water with the sea through a submerged fissure you cannot see from the bank. It runs to about fifteen metres deep, and the steep limestone rim makes a natural diving board for anyone brave enough. Marina Frapa fills the south side of the peninsula, among the biggest berthing complexes on this stretch of coast, with lazy lines, a fuel dock at the mouth and a run of its own restaurants. For a plainer supper, Konoba Maslina and Konoba Mario in the old village do grilled fish and slow stews the way the town has long cooked them. The bay holds well in almost any wind.
thingsToDo: Hike over to the Dragon's Eye and its salt lake
Cliff-jump from the karst lake's rim
Eat grilled fish at Konoba Maslina
Fuel up at the Marina Frapa dock
Row the tender out to Smokvica islet for a swim
5.mooringTip: Marina Frapa on the south side is the safe overnight, with lazy lines and a fuel pontoon at the mouth; the naturally closed bay holds in almost any weather. The north-side village quay catches the northwest gradient and is no place to lie overnight.

6.shortDescription: The run back into the Šibenik estuary is twelve miles, past the UNESCO St. Nicholas fortress and through the St. Anthony channel to the old town.
6.description: Twelve miles north and you turn in off the coast into the mouth of the Šibenik estuary. Few approaches on this coast match it. First comes the St. Nicholas fortress, a squat Venetian sea fort planted on a spit at the river mouth, restored in recent years and walkable from the town side. Then the St. Anthony channel narrows to a rock-cut passage barely six hundred metres long before the estuary opens and the city stacks up the hillside. Berth at ACI Marina Šibenik in the inner harbour, a few minutes' walk from the cathedral, or across at D-Marin Mandalina on the south shore. St. James' Cathedral, built in interlocking stone without mortar, is the set piece; the climb to St. Michael's Fortress pays back the effort with the whole estuary laid out below. Pelegrini, on the cathedral steps, is the splurge.
thingsToDo: Thread the old-town alleys up to St. James' Cathedral
Take the climb to St. Michael's Fortress for the estuary view
Eat at Michelin-starred Pelegrini by the cathedral
Cool off at Banj Beach just east of the walls
Step into the monastery garden at St. Lawrence
6.mooringTip: ACI Marina Šibenik in the inner harbour puts you at the foot of the old town; D-Marin Mandalina on the south side has more room and full services. The buoyed estuary channel is deep enough for any charter draft, so the entrance is straightforward in daylight.

7.shortDescription: A short eight-mile run up the Krka back to Skradin for the Saturday handover, with a last stop at Skradinski Buk if time allows.
7.description: Home is eight easy miles up the Krka into ACI Marina Skradin. Saturday mornings the base runs tight: they want the boat alongside by nine with the tanks pumped out, the fuel gauge full, the decks rinsed and the inventory ticked off, so slip your lines at Šibenik by seven and leave yourself room. The run itself is unlike anywhere else on the coast, up the deep freshwater reach of the river between wooded hills, and it takes about ninety minutes at displacement speed. If you have an hour spare before the base manager waves you in, catch the national park shuttle boat from the marina pontoon up to Skradinski Buk. The waterfalls are fifteen minutes upstream, covered by the park ticket, and the boardwalk loop makes a fitting last swim. Split airport is roughly an hour's drive south when you are done.
thingsToDo: Ride the park shuttle to Skradinski Buk
Do the boardwalk circuit under the falls
Fill the tanks before the nine o'clock handover
Have the Skradin risotto at a quay table before you drive off
Lose an hour in the old town behind the marina
7.mooringTip: Return into ACI Marina Skradin as your charter contract sets out, and let the base manager point you to the slot. The Saturday window runs roughly eight to nine; if your paperwork asks for a night-before return, aim to be in by late Friday afternoon.

slano-hvar-lastovo-korcula-route.metaDesc: A 7-day yacht route from Slano through Mljet, Lastovo, Vela Luka, the Pakleni Islands and Korčula, with distances and mooring notes for each night.

1.shortDescription: A 14-mile hop southwest to Saplunara, where one of the Adriatic's rare sand beaches shelves gently off Mljet's quiet southeastern corner.
1.description: Marina Frapa Resort Slano sits at the back of a long, well-protected bay about 25 miles up the coast from Dubrovnik, which makes it a calmer place to take over a boat than the marinas nearer the city. The first leg runs 14 miles southwest to Saplunara, on the far southeastern tip of Mljet and outside the national park line, so no park ticket is needed here. What draws crews in is the beach: a wide, shallow shelf of real sand, unusual on this coast, with the bottom staying pale and clear well out from the shore. Anchor in four to six metres on sand, or take one of the buoys the beach konobas lay in season. Once the hook is set, the swimming is superb. A short walk north brings you to the smaller cove at Blace.
thingsToDo: Swim off the boat over the sand shelf
Follow the shore path north to Blace cove
Grab a beach-konoba table for fresh grilled fish
Paddleboard the shallows before the day-trippers arrive
Sit out on deck under a sky with no shore glow
1.mooringTip: Anchor in 4-6 m on good sand holding, or pick up a konoba buoy and eat ashore. The bay opens to the south, so if a southerly is forecast above 18 knots, shift six miles north to Okuklje, which is fully enclosed.

2.shortDescription: Fifteen miles west to Skrivena Luka, a cliff-walled inlet on Lastovo's south coast and about the safest overnight in the whole south Adriatic.
2.description: The name translates as hidden harbour, and it earns it: from offshore the entrance is almost impossible to pick out until you are nearly on top of it. Inside, the bay opens into a long finger of water walled by hills on three sides, protected from every direction the wind can come from. Holding is good on sand and mud, and there is a small pontoon with water and power on the eastern shore, plus buoys laid by the Porto Rosso restaurant. The afternoon job is the walk up to the Struga lighthouse, built in 1839 on the cliff edge above the southern coast, one of the oldest on the Croatian coast. On a clear evening you can make out the Italian shore across the water. Lastovo is a nature park with almost no light pollution, so stay on deck after dark.
thingsToDo: Take a Porto Rosso buoy and dine ashore
Climb to the 1839 Struga lighthouse on the cliff
Take a dip in the flat water at the head of the inlet
Split a Lastovo jastog straight from the tank
Trace the whole Milky Way from the cockpit
2.mooringTip: Buoys and a small water-and-power pontoon sit off the Porto Rosso restaurant on the east shore; overnight is billed separately from dinner. Anchoring on sand and mud also holds well. The bay takes wind from any quarter, so it is the fallback when Zaklopatica on the north coast turns nasty.

3.shortDescription: Fourteen miles north brings Vela Luka at Korčula's west end — a deep, protected harbour with a town quay, fuelling and a clifftop 20,000-year-old cave.
3.description: Vela Luka means big port, and the long west-facing inlet at Korčula's western end lives up to it. The harbour takes wind from the north, northeast and east without trouble; only a strong southerly pushes swell into the inner basin. Tie up stern-to with your own anchor along the town quay for a modest fee, with water and power on the central stretch and a fuel pump at the western end. Three konobas line the waterfront, and this is the place to try žrnovski makaruni, the hand-rolled pasta that a couple of the local kitchens still shape by hand. In the afternoon, it's a half-hour climb up to Vela Spila, a cave that people have lived in on and off for twenty thousand years and one of the oldest inhabited spots on this coast. If there is time, take the dinghy out to Proizd.
thingsToDo: Hike up to the 20,000-year-old Vela Spila cave
Eat žrnovski makaruni, still hand-rolled in the village
Stroll the promenade as the light drops
Top off the tank at the pump on the west quay
Run the tender over to Proizd for a swim
3.mooringTip: Stern-to with your own anchor on the town quay, modest fee, water and power midway along, fuel pump at the west end. Holding is reliable in mud and sand. Shelter is solid from north through east; only a southerly over about 18 knots sends swell into the inner harbour.

4.shortDescription: The week's longest leg, 30 miles east-northeast to the Pakleni Islands; be off the dock by dawn to beat the afternoon Maestral across the channel.
4.description: This is the long one, thirty miles east-northeast, and it pays to be off the dock early. The prevailing westerly Maestral usually fills in around two in the afternoon, and once it does the wind sits on the beam for the last stretch into the Hvar Channel, so we normally slip lines not long after dawn. Palmižana lies on the eastern side of Sv. Klement, the biggest of the Pakleni islets and the only one with a marina. The ACI berths fill fast in July and August, so book ahead or plan to anchor. The main bay holds in five to ten metres but rolls once the Maestral funnels through, which is why the south-side coves of Vinogradišće and Tarsce, both taken with a line ashore, make the more restful night. On the island itself the Meneghello family have kept a botanical garden going since 1906, and a handful of konobas along the bay handle dinner. Off the western point, a scatter of Roman amphorae sits in the shallows for anyone who brings a mask.
thingsToDo: Free-dive the Roman amphorae off the western point
Walk the Meneghello family's garden, planted 1906
Slip round to Vinogradišće and tie a line ashore
Ride the shuttle boat over to Hvar Town after dark
Walk the track over Sv. Klement to Vlaka hamlet
4.mooringTip: Book the ACI marina online for July and August; there is a rock off the entrance to give a wide berth. If it is full, work into Vinogradišće on the south side, sand and weed with a line ashore, which also handles the afternoon westerly better than the exposed main bay.

5.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles back southeast to walled Korčula Town, red roofs and a bell tower, with the Moreška sword dance on Monday and Thursday nights.
5.description: Twenty-two miles back across the Hvar Channel brings you to Korčula Town, the walled port on the island's eastern point. You will recognise it a long way off: a small peninsula of terracotta roofs, a single bell tower, and the pale curtain wall dropping straight to the sea. ACI Marina Korčula sits on the east side of the peninsula with lazy lines and full services, the all-weather choice. The town quay on the west side takes a few boats stern-to with your own anchor for a harbour fee, closer to the old gates but open to the westerly and rolly by mid-afternoon. The Old Town is tiny, laid out in a herringbone of lanes designed to break the wind. In season the Moreška, a staged sword battle four centuries old, plays on Monday and Thursday evenings. The wine to ask for is Grk, a white that really only grows on the sandflats at Lumbarda a few miles east.
thingsToDo: Catch the Moreška sword dance on a Monday or Thursday
Walk the medieval lanes once the day ferries clear out
Go up the cathedral bell tower for the rooftops
See the house they claim was Marco Polo's
Order a bottle of Grk from the Lumbarda sandflats
5.mooringTip: In settled weather, ACI Marina Korčula on its eastern flank is the berth — laid lines, full services throughout. Its west town quay sits nearer the gates but meets the westerly and grows rolly by mid-afternoon; in a hard blow, Lumbarda, three miles southeast, stays calm.

6.shortDescription: A short ten miles east into Kobaš, a pine-fringed cove with one waterside konoba and the Pelješac wine cellars a taxi ride away.
6.description: Ten easy miles east lands you in Kobaš, a small cove on the south shore of the Pelješac peninsula, ringed by pines and quiet once the day boats leave. The waterside konoba lays free buoys for anyone eating ashore, so pick one up and book a table to hold it. Pelješac is the home of Plavac Mali, the dense red that fathered Zinfandel, and the reason to come is the wine country on the slopes above. The steep, south-facing vineyards around Dingač and Postup turn out the most concentrated reds in the country, with Dingač insisting on old-vine fruit off the cliff terraces. A short taxi runs you up to cellars like Bartulović or Saints Hills for a tasting; call a day ahead. Back aboard, the konoba does oysters from Mali Ston in the next bay and a slow-cooked pašticada worth the wait.
thingsToDo: Grab a free buoy off the konoba and reserve a table
Ride up to a Dingač or Postup cellar for a tasting
Start dinner with Mali Ston oysters and a local red
Swim off the boat in the shade of the pines
Follow the goat track up to the chapel on the hill
6.mooringTip: The konoba lays free buoys held by a dinner booking, so confirm on arrival. If they are taken, anchor in eight to twelve metres on sand and weed. The cove is closed off from north, northwest and west but open south; if a southerly over 15 knots is coming, run six miles east into Mali Ston bay.

7.shortDescription: A final twelve miles southeast back to Marina Frapa Slano for the Saturday handover, with time for one last swim off Pelješac on the way.
7.description: The run home is twelve miles southeast from Kobaš back to Marina Frapa Slano. The Saturday routine is fixed. You want the boat back alongside by 09:00, fuel topped, waste pumped out, decks hosed down and the inventory ticked off, with someone from the crew around for the walk-through the base runs between 08:00 and 09:00. The course tracks the southeast shore of Pelješac past a string of small bays, and there is usually time for a last swim in one of them before turning up into Slano. The marina sits at the head of a long, deep, well-sheltered bay, one of the better natural harbours on this stretch, with lazy lines, water, power and a fuel pontoon by the entrance. Once the inspection is signed off, Dubrovnik airport is about 45 minutes away by road. A slow lunch at the marina or in Slano village closes the week.
thingsToDo: Squeeze in one last swim off the Pelješac shore
Fill the tank and empty the holding before check-out
Settle in for a slow lunch at the marina
Wander into Slano village for a coffee
7.mooringTip: Return into Marina Frapa Slano as your contract sets out; the base manager assigns the slot. The handover window is 08:00 to 09:00 Saturday, though some contracts ask for a Friday-evening return by 17:00. The fuel pontoon is on the western side of the marina entrance.

slano-korcula-hvar-vis-lastovo-14day-rou.metaTitle: 14-Day Vis, Hvar & Lastovo Sail Loop | Boat4You
slano-korcula-hvar-vis-lastovo-14day-rou.metaDesc: Two weeks under sail from Slano: Mljet's lakes, Korčula's walls, Vis and Blue Cave, Hvar's Pakleni coves and quiet Lastovo. NM and mooring notes inside.

1.shortDescription: A gentle six-mile shakedown southwest from Marina Frapa into Šipanska Luka, the working heart of Šipan and the largest Elaphiti island.
1.description: Day one is a warm-up, and that suits most crews after the flight in. Six miles southwest of Marina Frapa, the bay at Šipanska Luka opens on the northwest side of the island, deep and quiet, with room to settle stern-to on the village quay for a small fee. Water and power sit on the central berths. Šipan grows olives and citrus rather than tourists, so the pace is slow by design. In the afternoon we usually walk the old road inland to Suđurađ, roughly forty-five minutes through the groves, where the fortified Skočibuha summer palace still stands from the 1500s. Swim off the rocks south of the harbour before dinner. Black risotto and a local red close the first night without any fuss.
thingsToDo: Walk the grove road to Suđurađ and Skočibuha palace
Order black risotto on the Šipanska Luka quay
Swim off the rocks south of the harbour
Taste island olive oil at a small farm
Dinghy across to Lopud for an hour
1.mooringTip: Stern-to with your own anchor on the Šipanska Luka quay; small fee, water and power amidships. Well tucked from south through southwest, but a rare summer northerly over 18 knots is a cue to drop back into Marina Frapa.

2.shortDescription: Polače lies twenty miles west, the fjord-like western door to Mljet National Park; Roman ruins stand on its quay, the lakes a short walk inland.
2.description: Polače is the sort of anchorage you remember. The inlet bends in behind a screen of islets, and once you're inside there is almost no weather that reaches you; protection runs from north right round to southeast. Green Park buoys fill the middle of the bay, and the ranger comes by the kiosk to take the overnight fee, which covers the crew's Park entry too. A stone's throw from the water sit the walls of a fourth-century Roman villa, the corner tower still reading clearly against the pines. Give the afternoon to the lakes. It's twenty minutes on foot to Veliko Jezero, where a Park launch runs out to the Benedictine monastery on its small island, and the full loop around both lakes comes to about nine kilometres. Swim; the lake water is cooler than the sea.
thingsToDo: Pick up a green Park buoy off the kiosk
Walk the fourth-century Roman villa walls
Ferry to St Mary's monastery on Veliko Jezero
Hike the nine-kilometre loop around both lakes
Swim in the cool saltwater lakes
2.mooringTip: Polače runs on paid Park buoys, around €40 a night in 2025 with crew Park entry included; settle up with the ranger when he comes round. Shelter here is among the best on the whole south coast, solid from north through southeast.

3.shortDescription: Eighteen miles up the Pelješac Channel to walled Korčula Town, red roofs and a bell tower, with the Moreška sword dance on summer evenings.
3.description: You clock Korčula from a good way off, a stone peninsula jutting into the Pelješac Channel with the old town packed onto its tip. ACI Marina Korčula sits just south of the old centre behind a two-hundred-metre breakwater, lazy lines and full services, and it holds in everything short of a hard southeasterly. The town quay across the way is prettier but takes the afternoon Maestral on the nose and rolls by mid-afternoon. Inside the walls you can cross the whole place in ten minutes, though the fishbone street plan repays a slow wander. Climb St Mark's bell tower for the channel view, look in on the Land Gate, argue about the Marco Polo house. On Monday and Thursday nights in season the Moreška sword dance plays out in the open air, and it is worth staying for.
thingsToDo: Watch the Moreška sword dance on Mon or Thu
Climb the St Mark's Cathedral bell tower
Wander the old town once the ferries leave
Dinner at Michelin-listed LD or Filippi
Duck through the Land Gate at dusk
3.mooringTip: ACI Marina Korčula behind its breakwater is the all-weather choice, safe in anything but a strong southeasterly. The west-side town quay looks the part but catches the afternoon Maestral and turns rolly; if it's blowing, Lumbarda three miles southeast is calmer.

4.shortDescription: Just twelve miles north into the Korčula–Hvar channel to uninhabited Šćedro, where a couple of konobas hang free buoys for anyone who eats ashore.
4.description: Šćedro is the quiet one. No village, no road, no shop, just pine and stone and a handful of seasonal konobas that lay buoys for their dinner guests. Lovišće cuts into the north coast and makes the natural overnight; grab a buoy, or drop the hook in four to ten metres on sand and mud if they're taken. Konoba Klanac has been at it longest, the Kordić family cooking slow octopus and pouring Plavac Mali off their own vines above the bay. Confirm the buoy when you tie a dinner table to it. Afternoon is for the water. Snorkel straight off the boat, or walk the path over to Mostir on the south side, where a ruined Dominican monastery sits at the shoreline. Visibility runs to twenty-five metres on a still day, the clearest on the route.
thingsToDo: Take a free buoy at Konoba Klanac, Lovišće
Eat slow-cooked octopus with local Plavac Mali
Snorkel the boat in 25-metre visibility
Walk to the Dominican ruins at Mostir
Sleep with no town noise at all
4.mooringTip: Lovišće's buoys belong to the konobas, so book a table and confirm the mooring's included when you arrive. If the field is full, anchor in eight to twelve metres on sand and weed. A northerly over 15 knots means slipping round to Mostir on the south side.

5.shortDescription: Twenty miles out to Vis Town, deep in sheltered St George Bay, once the Greek colony of Issa and the far outpost of the Adriatic.
5.description: The run out to Vis is the first proper open-water leg, twenty miles across to the island that sat furthest from the mainland and stayed the better for it. Vis Town wraps the long curve of St George Bay, sheltered from south through northwest, with only a rare northerly finding a way in. Tie stern-to along the seafront for a modest fee, or pick up one of the roughly hundred and fifty buoys the harbourmaster now lays. This was Issa, the oldest Greek settlement on the eastern Adriatic, and the Hellenistic wall and Roman baths still edge the promenade. Walk up to Austro-Hungarian Fort George for the view back over the harbour.
