For dinner, the garden at Villa Kaliopa in Kut is the classic, candlelit under old palms; order the Vugava white and octopus from under the peka.
thingsToDo: Dinner in the Villa Kaliopa garden, Kut
Walk the Hellenistic walls on the promenade
Climb to Fort George above the harbour
See the Roman baths on the seafront
Try Vugava white with peka octopus
5.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Vis Town seafront with your own anchor, or take one of the harbourmaster's buoys off Church Bay to save the hassle. St George Bay shelters you from south through northwest; only a rare northerly reaches in, which makes this the most dependable overnight on Vis.

6.shortDescription: A short six-mile hop around the west end of Vis to Komiža, the most honest working fishing port left in central Dalmatia.
6.description: You barely sail today. Komiža lies six miles off on the far western shore of Vis, an easy clockwise loop out of St George Bay, so leave late and take a swim on the way over. Komiža has kept its working fleet on the east side of the basin and shrugged off the resort trade, and it is the better for it. Moor stern-to in the inner basin, sand and weed, mostly good holding, for a harbour fee. Dinner is the reason people come back. At Jastožera, built in 1883 as a lobster store, a small tender can motor straight under the arch into the old holding pool. Ashore, Fabrika pours island wine and tapas. If you've the legs, the trail up Mount Hum climbs through dry-stone vineyards in ninety minutes to the best view in Dalmatia, with Tito's war cave a short detour off it.
thingsToDo: Dinner at Jastožera, the 1883 lobster store
Wine and tapas at Fabrika on the quay
Hike Mount Hum to Tito's wartime cave
Walk the working fleet on the east basin
Watch the open-sea sunset from the mole
6.mooringTip: Stern-to with your own anchor in Komiža's inner basin, sand and weed, generally good holding, harbour fee payable ashore. The outer wall takes any southwest swell and is no place to spend the night; if it's forecast over 15 knots, run the six miles back to St George Bay.

7.shortDescription: Take the tender into Biševo's Blue Cave early, then a twenty-five-mile run northeast into Stari Grad, the country's oldest settlement and its UNESCO plain's gateway.
7.description: Timing runs the morning. The Blue Cave on Biševo only lights up that impossible blue when the sun is low behind it, roughly nine to eleven, so we aim to be first in the queue. Tie alongside the concession quay outside, pay at the kiosk, and take an official tender through the low mouth; forty minutes does it. Then it's twenty-five miles east-northeast to Stari Grad, founded by Greeks as Pharos in 384 BC and the oldest town in the country. The old grid of streets is still legible, and behind the town the Stari Grad Plain carries UNESCO protection as one of Europe's longest-farmed landscapes. The bay reaches deep inland and shelters you in any wind. Tie stern-to on the town quay, or take lazy lines at the small ACI marina on the east side.
thingsToDo: Be first in the Blue Cave tender queue
Walk the UNESCO Stari Grad Plain vineyards
See Tvrdalj Castle and Hektorović's fishpond
Trace the Greek street grid downtown
Book peka at a plain-side konoba
7.mooringTip: Stari Grad town quay is stern-to with your own anchor, modest fee, water and power midships; the ACI marina on the east side has lazy lines if you'd rather. The bay is fjord-shaped and holds you in any wind direction, one of the safest overnights on Hvar.

8.shortDescription: Fourteen miles round the tip of Hvar to Palmižana on Sv. Klement, the one Pakleni island with a marina and a century-old garden.
8.description: Palmižana is the target today, a sheltered notch in Sv. Klement and the biggest of the Pakleni islets, fourteen miles off round the western cape of Hvar. The ACI marina here is small and books out through July and August, so reserve online or plan to anchor. The main bay holds in five to ten metres on sand and weed, but the Maestral funnels in around two in the afternoon and the swell finds you, so for a settled night the south-side coves of Vinogradišće and Tarsce are the smart call, line ashore the usual rig. Ashore, wander the Meneghello family's botanical garden, planted in 1906, and eat at one of the konobas strung along the water. Snorkellers head for the western shore, where Roman amphorae still lie on the bottom. A water taxi runs across to Hvar Town after dark.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Roman amphorae off the west shore
Walk the Meneghello botanical garden from 1906
Dinner at a Palmižana bay konoba
Anchor line-ashore in Vinogradišće if full
Water taxi over to Hvar Town at night
8.mooringTip: Reserve the ACI berth online if you want July or August — walk-ups get turned away. Should it fill, duck across to Vinogradišće on the southern shore: sand and weed, a line run ashore, and far steadier in the afternoon westerly than the open main bay.

9.shortDescription: A long reach south to the rock pools of little Proizd for a swim and lunch, then a short skip into Vela Luka to moor.
9.description: Today is built around Proizd, a scrap of uninhabited rock a few miles off the western tip of Korčula and a fixture on the local charter circuit. Its draw is the run of wave-cut basins on the west side, natural pools that fill and warm in the sun, over some of the clearest water on the coast. The islet is a lunchtime stop only; there is no shelter for the night anywhere on it. Anchor in six to eight metres on the sand patch on the east side, swim, walk the half-hour path that circles the rock, then move the short four miles into Vela Luka. The deep harbour at the west end of Korčula has the lot, fuel, water and power along the town quay, and the local žrnovski makaruni on the konoba tables.
thingsToDo: Swim the natural rock pools on Proizd
Circle Proizd on the half-hour footpath
Snorkel the west shore in clear water
Order žrnovski makaruni in Vela Luka
Top up fuel on the town quay
9.mooringTip: Proizd is a daytime anchorage only, open to every direction with no overnight shelter, so plan to move off it by late afternoon. Vela Luka takes you stern-to on the long town quay, modest fee, water, power and a fuel pump at the west end, well shut off from north through east.

10.shortDescription: Fourteen miles south to Lastovo, the loneliest inhabited island in the Adriatic, where Zaklopatica's konobas hang free buoys under Croatia's darkest sky.
10.description: Lastovo sits apart, a Nature Park and about as far from a city glow as Croatia gets. Everyone puts in at Zaklopatica, a deep notch on the north coast, with a scrap of island parked across the way to flatten any swell. Five family konobas ring the bay, each with its own line of buoys held for dinner guests, so the arithmetic is simple: book the table, keep the mooring. Triton runs six berths and is the steadier reservation; in July and August the bay is full by six, so don't dawdle up from Vela Luka. Lastovo lives on lobster, worked daily off the traps to the north, and on šporki makaruli, a slow beef ragù over pasta. There is little else to do, which is the point. On a clear night the Milky Way stands right overhead before midnight.
thingsToDo: Take a konoba buoy in trade for dinner
Eat Lastovo lobster or šporki makaruli
Book ahead at Konoba Triton for a berth
Walk up to the Lastovo Town chimneys
Stay on deck for the Milky Way
10.mooringTip: Zaklopatica's buoys go with a dinner booking, so call a konoba ahead, Triton being the most reliable for a berth. The bay packs out by 18:00 in high season. Holding on the bottom is poor over seagrass; if a strong southeasterly is coming, run four miles south to Skrivena Luka.

11.shortDescription: Twenty miles back northeast to Pomena at Mljet's western tip, the park's other gateway, with the small saltwater lake a quarter-hour walk away.
11.description: Back to Mljet, this time to Pomena on the western tip, the park's other front door. Green buoys sit in the bay and the ranger takes the fee, crew Park entry folded in as before, or you can lie stern-to at the village pontoon. Malo Jezero, the smaller of the two lakes, is a quarter-hour walk inland, and a footpath links it to Veliko Jezero where the launch runs out to the island monastery. Give the afternoon to the circuit; the loop around both lakes is about nine kilometres, the swimming is good, and the cicadas are deafening in August. Pomena itself is a couple of konobas and a small shop, no more. Dinner runs to grilled fish and a glass of Pošip off the Korčula vines across the channel.
thingsToDo: Walk fifteen minutes to Malo Jezero
Ferry to St Mary's monastery on Veliko Jezero
Circle both lakes on the nine-kilometre trail
Swim the cool lake water at midday
Grilled fish and Pošip at a Pomena konoba
11.mooringTip: Pomena's Park buoys run about €40 a night in 2025 with crew Park entry, paid to the ranger on arrival. The bay is well tucked in; only a northwesterly over 18 knots pushes any real swell through the entrance.

12.shortDescription: Twenty miles around Mljet's south coast to Saplunara, outside the park and its fees, one of the rare true sand beaches on the whole coast.
12.description: Saplunara sits on Mljet's southeast corner, twenty miles east-southeast around the island's underside, and better yet it lies past the Park line where the fees quit. The reward is a genuine sand beach, uncommon on this coast, with a pale floor shelving through four to six metres. Drop the hook straight onto the sand, which holds beautifully, or pick up one of the buoys the two beach konobas lay in season. The bay covers you from north, northwest and west; only a southerly sends swell into the inner corner. Once you're set the swimming is the best of the fortnight, the shallow sand turning the water that clean turquoise. Walk the path north to Blace, a smaller, quieter sand cove twenty minutes on foot, or just stay put and grill orada ashore as the open-sea stars come out.
thingsToDo: Anchor on the sand and swim off the boat
Walk twenty minutes north to Blace cove
Climb to the white chapel above the bay
Order grilled orada at a beach konoba
Watch open-Adriatic stars after dark
12.mooringTip: Anchor in four to six metres on first-rate sand, the obvious call here, or take a free konoba buoy with dinner ashore. Sheltered from north, northwest and west but open to the south; if a southerly over 18 knots is due, move six miles north into Okuklje, also outside the Park and fully shut in.

13.shortDescription: Eighteen miles east to car-free Lopud, its village on one shore and the rare sand of Šunj beach a half-hour walk over the other side.
13.description: Day thirteen carries you back into the Elaphiti, eighteen miles east to car-free Lopud, the middle child of the three inhabited islands. The village curls round the northwest bay, and a half-hour walk across the island lands you at Šunj, a proper sand beach on the southeast shore and the island's headline. Mooring is easy: stern-to on the village quay with your own anchor, small fee, water and power amidships. The bay covers you from south through southwest, with only the rare summer northerly a worry. The rhythm here is the walk over to Šunj for an afternoon swim, a look at the fortified fifteenth-century Franciscan friary on the seafront, and a quayside dinner of black risotto and Mali Ston oysters. Climb the ruined hill fort above town for the last big view of the trip.
thingsToDo: Walk half an hour over to Šunj beach
Visit the fortified 15th-century Franciscan friary
Eat Mali Ston oysters at a quay table
Order black risotto in the village
Climb the hill fort above town
13.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Lopud village quay with your own anchor, small fee, water and power on the central berths. Cover runs from south through southwest; a summer northerly over 18 knots is the cue to slip four miles to Šipanska Luka or back toward Slano.

14.shortDescription: The last ten miles northeast to Marina Frapa Slano, with a farewell swim off Koločep before the Saturday morning handover.
14.description: The last hop is a short one, up to Marina Frapa at the head of Slano Bay, ten miles northeast of Lopud. The Saturday checkout runs to a routine: alongside at base by nine, waste tanks pumped down, a full tank of fuel, decks hosed and the inventory ticked through, and the skipper aboard for the walk-around, eight till nine. The course slips past Koločep, smallest and nearest of the Elaphiti, and the little bays on its eastern side make the standard last swim; anchor in six to eight metres on sand, dive in one more time, then push up into Slano Bay. The marina sits at the head of a long, deep, well-shut inlet, so the run in is calm whatever the morning brings. Once you're checked out, Dubrovnik airport is forty-five minutes south by road. A long lunch at the marina or in Slano village is the right way to end it.
thingsToDo: Last swim in a Koločep bay
Top up fuel before the 09:00 checkout
Square the inventory for handover
Long lunch at the marina restaurant
Wander Slano village before the drive out
14.mooringTip: Come back into Marina Frapa per your contract; the base manager assigns the slot. The Saturday checkout window is 08:00–09:00, so if your paperwork calls for a night-before return, be alongside by 17:00 Friday. Fuel pontoon is on the west side of the entrance.

slano-korcula-lastovo-route.metaDesc: Sail 7 days from Slano through the Elaphiti islands, Mljet National Park, Lastovo and Korčula. Anchorages, konoba moorings and daily NM, skipper-tested.

1.shortDescription: A short 10-mile hop southwest from Slano lands you on car-free Lopud, where a footpath crosses to the sandy shallows of Šunj.
1.description: The first leg is gentle, ten miles southwest out of Marina Frapa and down into the Elaphiti chain. Lopud has no cars, so the village strings itself around the northwest bay and you moor stern-to on the quay with your own anchor for a small fee. Water and power reach the central berths. The reason everyone comes is Šunj, a genuine sand beach on the far side of the island, maybe half an hour on foot through olive terraces. We usually swim there in the late afternoon once the day-trip boats have gone. Back in the village, the fortified Franciscan monastery on the seafront dates to the 15th century and is worth a wander. Dinner is grilled squid and a cold Pošip from the Korčula vineyards across the water.
thingsToDo: Walk the olive path to Šunj sand beach
See the 15th-century fortified Franciscan monastery
Order grilled squid at a village konoba
Climb to the ruined fort above town
Pour a cold Pošip from Korčula vineyards
1.mooringTip: Moor stern-to on the village quay, dropping your own anchor, for a modest harbour fee with water and power on the inner berths. Good shelter from S through W, but a northerly gradient blows straight in — if it tops 18 knots, slip 4 miles up to Šipanska Luka.

2.shortDescription: Fourteen miles west drops you at Saplunara on Mljet's southeast tip, outside the park, anchored over pale sand off one of the few real sand beaches on this coast.
2.description: West again, fourteen miles to Saplunara at the bottom corner of Mljet. You are outside the National Park boundary here, so no park fees. The bay fronts a real sand beach, a rarity on this rocky coast, and the water over that pale floor comes up a milky green you don't often see this far down the Adriatic. Anchor in four to six metres, where the sand gives excellent holding, or grab one of the seasonal buoys the beach konobas lay out. Konoba Saplunara is the long-running family place if you want dinner ashore; grilled orada and a carafe of house white is the order. Shelter is solid from north, northwest and west. Only a southerly gradient sends swell into the inner bay, and if that builds, Okuklje six miles north is bomb-proof.
thingsToDo: Anchor over sand and swim off the boat
Walk to the Blace lagoon just southeast
Order grilled orada at Konoba Saplunara
Climb to the chapel above the village
Watch the open-Adriatic stars after dark
2.mooringTip: Anchor in 4–6 m over sand for the best holding, or pick up a free konoba buoy if you are dining ashore. The bay covers you from north, northwest and west; a southerly is the weakness, so if it forecasts over 18 knots, run six miles up to Okuklje, which shuts out everything.

3.shortDescription: Fifteen miles round Mljet's western end to Pomena, the park's back door, with the saltwater lakes and St Mary's monastery a short walk away.
3.description: Round the western end of Mljet, fifteen miles, and Pomena sits at the far tip as the quieter entrance to the National Park. Pick up a green park buoy in the bay and settle the fee with the ranger, which folds in park entry for the crew, or take a stern-to slot at the village pontoon. Fifteen minutes on foot puts you at Malo Jezero, the smaller of the two saltwater lakes; keep going and the shore path meets Veliko Jezero, where the little park boat shuttles out to the Benedictine monastery on its own islet. The lakes are worth a whole afternoon. You can swim off the rocks the whole way round, the full circuit runs about nine kilometres, and by mid-August the cicadas get loud enough to drown out whoever you're walking with. Grilled fish and a Pošip close the day.
thingsToDo: Take a park buoy and pay the ranger
Walk fifteen minutes to Malo Jezero lake
Ferry across to St Mary's island monastery
Hike the nine-kilometre lake loop
Order grilled fish at a Pomena konoba
3.mooringTip: Pomena uses paid park mooring buoys, around €40 a night in 2025, which includes park entry for the crew; pay the ranger when you arrive. The bay is well protected, and only a northwesterly over 18 knots pushes any real swell through the entrance.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles southwest to Lastovo, the most remote inhabited island out here, where Zaklopatica's konobas hang free buoys for guests who dine ashore.
4.description: Twenty miles southwest, out to Lastovo, and the traffic thins to almost nothing. This is a Nature Park with the darkest skies in Croatia, and you tuck in for the night at Zaklopatica, a tight horseshoe on the north coast where a low islet sits square across the mouth and flattens whatever swell tries to get in. Enter through the eastern channel, which carries eight metres; the western gap shoals to barely a metre and a half. Five family konobas ring the water, and each one keeps a run of free buoys for anyone eating at their table. The deal is simple: book the buoy and a dinner table together. Lastovo's kitchen leans on lobster and šporki makaruli, a slow beef-and-macaroni stew. Come a clear night, you'll have the whole Milky Way arched overhead long before the konobas close.
thingsToDo: Take a konoba buoy in return for dinner
Order Lastovo lobster or šporki makaruli
Walk up to Lastovo Town's fumari chimneys
Snorkel the islet across the bay mouth
Watch the Milky Way after midnight
4.mooringTip: The konoba buoys at Zaklopatica are free with dinner, so confirm your table on arrival and the buoy is yours. Come in through the eastern channel (about 8 m); the western entrance is only 1.4 m. Anchoring is discouraged over the seagrass, and if a fresh SE is forecast, drop down to Skrivena Luka on the south coast.

5.shortDescription: Fourteen miles northeast to walled Korčula Town, all red roofs and one bell tower, with the Moreška sword dance twice a week in season.
5.description: Korčula Town is one you pick out early. Fourteen miles off to the northeast it's already there: a wedge of red roofs on a stub of peninsula, a single bell tower standing over them, the old walls dropping into the sea on three sides. ACI Marina sits on the eastern side of the old town with lazy lines and full services, and it fills fast in July and August, so book ahead. The town quay on the west takes a few stern-to boats with own anchor for a fee, but it goes rolly once the Maestral fills after lunch. Inside the walls the streets fan out in a herringbone that the Venetians later copied elsewhere. The Moreška, a sword drama between two armies that dates back four centuries, plays Monday and Thursday evenings in season. Dinner calls for a glass of Grk, the white grown on Lumbarda's sandy vineyards just east.
thingsToDo: Catch the Moreška sword dance, Mon or Thu
Wander the old town once the ferries leave
Climb St Mark's Cathedral bell tower
Look in on the Marco Polo house
Taste Grk white from Lumbarda's sands
5.mooringTip: East-side ACI Marina Korčula is the all-weather bet, offering laid lines plus water, power and fuel; reserve ahead in high season. The western town quay accepts stern-to boats but stands exposed to the afternoon Maestral, rolling past 14:00. For calm, head three miles southeast to sheltered Lumbarda.

6.shortDescription: Eighteen miles back southeast to Polače on Mljet's north shore, a deep, all-round-sheltered inlet with Roman ruins right on the waterfront.
6.description: The run back to Mljet is eighteen miles, up onto the north coast this time and into Polače, a long narrow gash of an inlet that doubles as the park's western gate. Nothing on this stretch of coast shelters you better. It's shut off from north, northeast, east and southeast, and the mouth is so tight you don't clock it until you're already through. Take a green park buoy and pay the ranger at the waterfront kiosk, crew park entry included, or slot stern-to at the small village pontoon. The ruins of a fourth-century Roman palace sit metres from where you tie up. With the afternoon spare, climb Montokuc, at 253 metres the high point of western Mljet, for a full turn of view over the park, or take the ferry across Veliko Jezero to the monastery. Black risotto is the local order for dinner.
thingsToDo: Tie to a park buoy at the kiosk
Walk through the fourth-century Roman palace ruins
Climb Montokuc, 253 m, for the panorama
Ferry across Veliko Jezero to the monastery
Order black risotto at a village konoba
6.mooringTip: Polače also runs paid park buoys, roughly €40 a night in 2025 with crew park entry rolled in; pay the ranger on arrival. This is one of the most sheltered anchorages on the south coast, fully closed off from north, northeast, east and southeast, so it holds even when the forecast turns.

7.shortDescription: Twenty miles east-northeast back to Marina Frapa Slano for the Saturday handover, with a last Elaphiti swim stop before you push into the bay.
7.description: Last day, and it's twenty miles east-northeast from Polače straight back to Marina Frapa Slano. This is a Saturday changeover, so the clock matters: the base wants the boat alongside by 09:00, fuelled up, holding tanks pumped, decks hosed off and the gear checked against the list, with you aboard for the hand-back between 08:00 and 09:00. Since the track threads the Elaphiti anyway, most crews steal a last swim on the way in, at Šipanska Luka, off Lopud, or in one of the little bites of coast at Koločep, before rounding into Slano Bay. Leave Polače at first light and there's still time to cut the engine for half an hour over sand and reach the marina without rushing. Paperwork signed and tanks topped, Dubrovnik airport is about 45 minutes down the coast by car, and a slow lunch at the marina restaurant is the right way to shut the week.
thingsToDo: Take a last swim in the Elaphiti islands
Top up fuel and pump out before 09:00
Have a final dip off the pontoon
Stand a long lunch at the marina restaurant
Walk into Slano village for its konobas
7.mooringTip: Bring her back into Marina Frapa Slano as your contract states; the base manager will point you to a slot. The Saturday handover window is 08:00 to 09:00, so if your paperwork asks for a night-before return, be alongside by 17:00 Friday. The fuel pontoon is on the western side of the marina entrance.

split-dubrovnik-split-14day-route.metaDesc: A 14-day sailing route from Split down to Dubrovnik and back, via Hvar, Vis, Lastovo, Mljet and Korčula, with distances and mooring notes for each night.

1.shortDescription: A forgiving fifteen-mile opener southwest to Milna, deep in the quietest reach of Brač's west shore. Swim off the anchor first, then let the evening turn on the peka.
1.description: Nobody's in a hurry on day one. The crossing to Milna is fifteen miles of open water off Split, enough to hoist everything, sort the reefing and let people who haven't sailed in a year remember how it works. The inlet cuts deep into Brač's western shore, and you spot Milna by its baroque church tower long before the houses resolve behind it. Fishermen have worked this harbour since the Venetians ran the coast, and it still keeps that unhurried, lived-in feel. Anchor out in the wide part first, take the season's first swim, and hold off the town quay until the afternoon boats start clearing. The one thing to sort the minute your feet hit the stones: lamb under the peka wants three hours over the coals, so walk straight to the konoba and order it. Do that and the rest of the night runs itself.
thingsToDo: Order the peka the minute you tie up, not after your swim
Take the trip's first swim off the anchor outside town
Walk up to the baroque parish church over the quay
Run the dinghy round to Osibova for the sunset
Grab a bottle of Brač olive oil before the shop shuts
1.mooringTip: Stern-to on the town quay on your own anchor; the fee is small and water and power run along the central section. Most days the inlet stays flat, but a hard afternoon Maestral pushes chop into the outer bay, so if the quay is full, run three miles north to Bobovišća, which sits quiet in any westerly.

2.shortDescription: Fifteen miles southeast to the Pakleni Islands, where Palmižana holds the middle of the chain. Berth on the marina pontoon or drop south into Vinogradišće when the westerly gets to work.
2.description: Coffee first, then cast off for the fifteen-mile reach across the Hvar Channel to the Pakleni Islands, a low chain of pine islets that screen the run into Hvar Town. Palmižana is the biggest of them and the only one with a marina, sat about halfway along. The main bay is sand and weed in five to ten metres, decent holding until the westerly builds around two in the afternoon and starts the boats rolling. When that happens, take a lazy-line slot on the ACI pontoon or slip south into Vinogradišće, where a line ashore is the norm and the swell can't reach. The Meneghello family planted a botanical garden here back in 1906, and it's still the thing to see; lunch drags on happily at Zori or Toto's along the water. Save the snorkelling gear for the amphorae scattered off the western shore, best in the flat light of late afternoon.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the sunken amphorae off the western shore
Walk the Meneghello garden, planted back in 1906
Settle in for a long lunch at Zori or Toto's
Take the dinghy to Marinkovac when you want an emptier beach
Jump the water taxi over to Hvar Town after dark
2.mooringTip: The ACI pontoon fills every day in July and August, so reserve a lazy-line berth online well before you arrive. If it's gone, tuck into Vinogradišće on the south side and run a line ashore, which is how everyone does it there. Once the southwest gradient sets in, don't spend the night in the main bay.

3.shortDescription: The short five-mile leg back into Hvar Town, which in July is bedlam. Only the ACI marina stays calm when the wind fills; treat the riva as a lunch stop and nothing more.
3.description: Barely an hour under sail today, five miles straight back over the channel, and you fetch up in the busiest port in Dalmatia. You'll know Hvar Town from well offshore: the Fortica sitting on its hill, the long stone arcade of the Arsenal on the front, St Stephen's tower marking the square behind. Anything bigger than a daysailer wants ACI Marina Hvar on the west side of the bay, which is really the only place that stays calm once the afternoon breeze arrives. The riva will take you by the hour for real money, but the tour boats and tenders own it by lunchtime and it gives no cover from the Maestral. Get the Fortica done early, before the sun turns the climb into a slog. Then let the night sort itself out among the konobas in the lanes off the square.
thingsToDo: Climb the Fortica early, before the midday heat sets in
Look over the Arsenal arcade and St Stephen's on the square
Stand a round at a konoba in the back lanes
Browse the lavender stalls near Trg Sv. Stjepana
Watch the harbour from the marina wall once it's dark
3.mooringTip: Book ACI Marina Hvar ahead in season; every slot comes with lazy lines, water and power. Treat the riva as a lunch mooring only, exposed to the west gusts and packed with tenders, never a berth for the night.

4.shortDescription: Twenty-five miles out to Vis, breaking for a swim in cliff-ringed Stiniva. The day ends at Komiža, a working fishing port that pulls the best sunset on this coast.
4.description: Point the bow west off Hvar mid-morning for the long haul, twenty-five miles out to Vis. This is the farthest of the inhabited islands from the mainland, sealed off as a military base until 1989 and all the emptier for it. Break the passage at Stiniva on the south coast, a pool of a cove almost closed off by cliffs, the gap in them no wider than the boat. Drop the hook on the sand in eight to ten metres and stay clear of the grass. The tripper boats start pouring through that gap around eleven, so get there before them or wait them out into the evening. From there it's a quick run west to Komiža, which still lands fish rather than tourists. Tie stern-to in the inner basin, then eat at Jastožera, the lobster house built on stilts over the water, with a cold Vugava from the island's own vines. Stay up for the sunset; out here it earns its reputation.
thingsToDo: Get into Stiniva before the tripper boats do
Eat lobster at Jastožera out over the water
Ask for a glass of the island's Vugava white
Hike Mount Hum for the run of the archipelago
Walk through Tito's Cave above the harbour
4.mooringTip: Stern-to on your own anchor in Komiža's inner basin; the sand and weed hold well, though the outer wall catches any southwest swell. If the forecast shows a fresh westerly or southwesterly, motor the six miles north to Vis Town in St George's Bay, which stays shut off from all of it.

5.shortDescription: Two caves in the morning, then a twenty-mile run east to Vela Luka, about the safest bay to lie in on this stretch. Catch the Blue Cave early, while the sun still lights it.
5.description: Set an alarm. The Blue Cave on Biševo only fires up from about nine until noon, when the sun slants in under the rock and floods the chamber a hard electric blue. You tie to the concession quay outside and wait your turn for a licensed tender, because private dinghies don't go in. Half an hour on, cross to Ravnik for the Green Cave, where daylight drops through a hole in the roof and the water goes emerald; that one you take yourself by dinghy, and it's usually near-empty. By early afternoon, turn east and log the eighteen miles to Vela Luka at the western end of Korčula, a deep harbour with full services that shuts out anything from the north or east. Ashore, order žrnovski makaruni, still rolled by hand here, and a bottle of Pošip from the vineyards up the road at Čara and Smokvica.
thingsToDo: Get in the tender line for the Blue Cave before eleven
Take your own dinghy into Ravnik's Green Cave
Order žrnovski makaruni at a quay-side konoba
Drink a Pošip grown up the road at Čara
Walk the Vela Luka waterfront as the light goes
5.mooringTip: Vela Luka takes you stern-to on your own anchor along the long town quay, with water, power and a fuel pump at the west end. The mud and sand hold beautifully and the bay is closed off to the north and east. Only a strong southerly works any swell inside, and even then the inner pontoon rides easy.

6.shortDescription: Fourteen miles down to Lastovo, as cut off as islands get out here. Pick a free buoy at Zaklopatica in return for dinner ashore, and stay up for a sky with no light in it.
6.description: South from Vela Luka, a fourteen-mile crossing drops you at Lastovo, which is about as far as you can get from anywhere out here. The whole island is a nature park, and with next to no light on it the sky at night is the darkest in the country. Head for Zaklopatica, a bay bitten into the north coast with a low islet parked across the mouth to soak up the swell. The konobas ashore each hang a few buoys for nothing, on the understanding you'll eat with them, so grab one, run the dinghy in and book a table. Lobster is the splurge; the honest order is šporki makaruli, a heavy beef-and-macaroni stew. There's almost nothing to do here, which is the whole point of coming. Long after the plates are cleared the Milky Way is still hanging over the masthead. Should a southeasterly get up, Skrivena Luka around on the south coast holds in anything.
thingsToDo: Take a konoba buoy and pay for it in dinner
Order the šporki makaruli, or lobster if you're flush
Run the dinghy to the islet across the mouth
Walk up to Lastovo's odd chimney stacks
Stay on deck for the Milky Way
6.mooringTip: Zaklopatica's konoba buoys cost nothing once you've booked dinner ashore, so sort it with them as you pick up. Leave the anchor stowed, since the seagrass holds badly. The bay shelters you from north around to west; if a southeasterly over fifteen knots shows on the forecast, shift four miles south to Skrivena Luka.

7.shortDescription: Northeast to Mljet, twenty-two miles, with Polače waiting at the head of a bay walled in by hills. Roman ruins sit on the shore and the salt lakes are a short walk inland.
7.description: The Mljet leg is twenty-two miles to the northeast, and it saves the best arrival on the whole trip for the end. Polače is tucked so far up its inlet, behind pine ridges, that you're nearly on top of it before the bay opens. A fourth-century Roman ruin stands right on the shore, close enough to moor beside; the walls and a corner tower are still up, and you can wander them for nothing. Everything west of here is national park, so either pick up a green park buoy and settle with the ranger on the front, which also covers the crew's park entry, or drop stern-to at the village pontoon. From the water it's half an hour on foot over to Veliko Jezero, the bigger of the island's two salt lakes, where a little park boat ferries out to a Benedictine monastery marooned on its own islet. Block out the whole afternoon for the lakes and don't rush it.
thingsToDo: Walk the fourth-century Roman ruin on the shore
Ride the park boat out to St Mary's monastery
Swim the salt lakes, colder than the open sea
Loop the trail around both lakes on foot
Settle up with the ranger for a green park buoy
7.mooringTip: Polače works on paid national-park buoys, and the fee folds in park entry for the crew; settle with the ranger on arrival. It's among the best-sheltered overnights on the south coast, covered from north round to southeast. A handful of boats can also go stern-to on their own anchor at the village pontoon.

8.shortDescription: Down to Dubrovnik, twenty-four miles, tying up at ACI Marina in Komolac, eight kilometres up the river from the walls. You visit the Old Port; you never sleep in it.
8.description: Wait for the Maestral, which on a quiet summer morning is up by ten to carry you the twenty-four miles down the coast to Dubrovnik. Forget the old harbour under the walls; it's a UNESCO postcard and useless to a crew, open to the south, thick with tour tenders and daytime only. Every charter boat lies instead at ACI Marina Dubrovnik in Komolac, eight kilometres inland up the Rijeka Dubrovačka, where you get lazy lines, fuel and the full run of services. Bus 1A or a taxi has you at the Pile Gate in a quarter of an hour, set down at the head of the Stradun. Walking the walls isn't optional: start from Pile before the sun drops and give the circuit a good ninety minutes. After that, find dinner up one of the Prijeko alleys, then a nightcap at Buža, the bar clinging to the rocks outside the south wall.
thingsToDo: Walk the full circuit of the walls before sunset
Find dinner up a Prijeko alley, off the Stradun
Have a drink at Buža out on the sea rocks
Take the cable car up Mount Srđ
Look in on the Rector's Palace and Franciscan cloister
8.mooringTip: ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac is the only sensible overnight; book ahead in season for lazy lines and full services. Allow thirty minutes door to door from the pontoon to the Pile Gate on Bus 1A or by taxi. The Old Port is a daytime tender stop and nothing more.

9.shortDescription: Twenty miles back up the south coast to a bay called Okuklje, just beyond the park boundary. It has three konobas, no village and about the calmest night the trip offers.
9.description: Dubrovnik behind you, the return runs twenty miles up Mljet's south side to Okuklje, chosen precisely because it's the anti-Dubrovnik. No village, no day boats, just three family konobas and a slot of a bay with room for maybe twenty keels. It falls outside the park line, so there's no fee, and each konoba strings its own free buoys for anyone dining ashore. Pick one up, run in and put your name down for a table. The safe order is black risotto, inked black with cuttlefish and whatever the boat brought in that morning. Steep hills close the bay on three sides, and the wind simply quits at sundown no matter what's happening out in the channel. Spend the afternoon over the side or out on the headland path, which opens a view south to Korčula. Nothing happens after dark, and that is exactly the reason you came.
thingsToDo: Claim a free konoba buoy and reserve a table
Order black risotto made with the day's catch
Walk the headland path for the view to Korčula
Swim straight off the transom into flat water
Turn in early, with no town to keep you up
9.mooringTip: Okuklje's konoba buoys come free with a dinner booking, so square it away as you tie on. If they're all taken you can anchor, but the seagrass makes for patchy holding. The bay is snug from north, east and south; only a hard northwesterly works any swell as far as the mouth.

10.shortDescription: The Pelješac Channel funnels you into Korčula Town: leave the walls for dusk, when the ferry crowds have gone and the stone turns gold.
10.description: Up the Pelješac Channel for fifteen miles, and Korčula Town rises off the bow, a fist of terracotta roofs on a walled spit with one bell tower over the lot. ACI Marina Korčula on the east side has the full kit, lazy lines and all. The town quay across on the west takes a few boats stern-to on their own anchor and sits nearer the gates, but it's open to the west and turns rolly the moment the Maestral finds the channel. The old town is small enough to cross in ten minutes, though its herringbone lanes, cut on the slant to catch the sea breeze and shut out the wind, reward a slower loop. St Mark's Cathedral and the Marco Polo house, whose claim to the man is thin but the building genuinely fourteenth-century, are the two to see. Walk it at dusk, after the day ferries have carried the crowds off to Split. If you fancy a wine run, Lumbarda and its home-grown Grk white lie three miles to the southeast.
thingsToDo: Walk the old town at dusk, after the ferries clear
Climb the bell tower at St Mark's Cathedral
Look in on the doubtful Marco Polo house
Taste Grk white over at nearby Lumbarda
Catch a Moreška sword dance if it's on
10.mooringTip: In any weather, pick ACI Marina Korčula east-side — laid lines, full services. Its west-side town quay is closer to the gates but opens westward and begins to roll through early summer afternoons. If the channel kicks up, duck three miles southeast to Lumbarda Bay.

11.shortDescription: Twenty-three miles to Jelsa on Hvar's north side, everything Hvar Town isn't. The lavender villages inland sit within an easy walk or a short bike ride.
11.description: Back across to Hvar's quiet side, twenty-three miles northwest to Jelsa, which sits about as far in spirit as it does in miles from the town on the far shore. It's the island's third settlement, smaller and calmer, wrapped round a broad bay whose town quay takes you stern-to on your own anchor over good mud-and-sand holding. There's a small marina on the east side too, for anyone who wants lazy lines and shore power. Jelsa runs the farming heart of the island, and the lavender inland still comes in as a real July harvest. In the back lanes the konobas pour the local Bogdanjuša white and cut the Faros cheese. With the afternoon yours, walk or borrow a bike out to Vrboska, three miles along the shore, where the little canals and footbridges have earned it the tag of a pocket Venice, and be back at a Jelsa table by dinner.
thingsToDo: Pedal over to Vrboska for its canal-laced centre
Order Bogdanjuša white in a back-lane konoba
Climb to the Tor watchtower above town
Walk out into the Stari Grad Plain
Pick up Hvar lavender oil while it's in season
11.mooringTip: Stern-to on the Jelsa town quay on your own anchor, small fee, water and power along the central run. The bay covers you well from south and southwest but stands open to the north, uncommon in summer bar a late Bora. The small marina on the east side is the any-weather fallback.

12.shortDescription: Ten miles over the Brač Channel to Lučice, a line of empty coves next door to Pučišća. Two beach restaurants lay the buoys, and the swimming is the clearest you'll find all trip.
12.description: A short north-bound skip, ten miles over the Brač Channel to the island's south coast and the string of little coves at Lučice, just east of Pučišća. Nobody lives out here. The headland is bare white limestone and pine, and the only roofs belong to a pair of seasonal beach restaurants that lay free buoys for anyone who eats with them. Take one in the deepest cove and go straight over the side. The water is why you bother: Brač's white stone, the same they cut for Diocletian's Palace, bleaches the bottom pale and stretches the visibility to twenty metres on a still day. The two kitchens keep it plain, grilled fish and a jug of Plavac Mali. If you want an hour off the boat, motor the dinghy west into Pučišća to watch the masons work their yards and the old carving school turn out apprentices. Call it the calm before tomorrow points you home.
thingsToDo: Grab a restaurant buoy in the middle cove
Snorkel the pale limestone floor in glass-clear water
Eat grilled fish and Plavac Mali on the beach
Dinghy into Pučišća to see the carving school
Paddle the empty headland at first light
12.mooringTip: Lučice's restaurant buoys are free once you commit to dinner ashore, so check in with them as you arrive. With the buoys gone, anchor in eight to twelve metres over sand and weed. The coves hold off north and east but lie open to the south; in a fresh southerly, Pučišća harbour a mile west is fully closed in.

13.shortDescription: Fifteen miles to Maslinica, out at Šolta's western point and the island's only genuine natural harbour, screened by a knot of islets. A calm final night before Saturday's handover.
13.description: Last night afloat. Fifteen miles along Brač's underside and over the Šolta Channel brings you to Maslinica, a deep S of a harbour at the far western point of Šolta and the only true natural port the island has. Houses ring the inner half of the bay; the outer half is scattered with seven islets, and the biggest of them, Stipanska, sits exactly where it needs to be to knock down the open-sea swell. Take your pick of two berths. Martinis Marchi on the south side gives you lazy lines and full services, built around an eighteenth-century baroque castle that trades now as a hotel and restaurant. Across from it, the village quay takes you stern-to on your own anchor for less money and a good deal more life. Šolta is the closest of these islands to Split and still one of the sleepiest; the konobas on the square work through the local olive oil and the island's own Dobričić red. Tomorrow's run home is a short one, so there's no need to hurry off anywhere tonight.
thingsToDo: Wander into the Martinis Marchi castle courtyard
Dinghy over to Stipanska for the swim path
Taste Šoltansko olive oil at a square konoba
Try Dobričić, the island's own red grape
Take a sundowner out on a western islet
13.mooringTip: Martinis Marchi Marina on the south side is the any-weather pick, with lazy lines and full services; reserve ahead for July and August. The village quay opposite takes you stern-to on your own anchor for a small fee, open only to the rare summer northerly. The islets across the mouth close the bay off from south round to northwest.

14.shortDescription: Twelve miles east to base for the Saturday handover, with time in hand for a last loop of Diocletian's Palace before the airport.
14.description: Handover day, and the shortest one of the lot, twelve miles east into the Split base cluster. The Saturday routine is fixed up and down the Croatian coast: boat alongside, tanks pumped and topped, decks hosed, inventory checked, and the skipper standing by for the off-going once-over between eight and nine. Most Split bases are at Marina Kaštela, ACI Marina Split or ACI Trogir, and every one of them is a straightforward approach whatever the visibility. Slip out of Maslinica at first light, before the Maestral wakes and while the channel still lies like glass, and you'll be alongside with time in hand. If the paperwork clears early, walk the length of Diocletian's Palace one last time, then draw out a lunch at a café on the Riva before the car comes for the airport. That slow final meal is the closest thing a Dalmatian charter has to a proper goodbye.
thingsToDo: Top up fuel ahead of the nine o'clock inspection
Walk Diocletian's Palace one final time
Swim at Bačvice, just east of the centre
Grab Šoltansko olive oil at the Pazar market
Draw out a long lunch on the Riva
14.mooringTip: Head back to whichever base your contract names, Marina Kaštela, ACI Marina Split or ACI Trogir; each berths you stern-to or alongside as the base manager calls it. The handover window is usually eight to nine on Saturday, so if your contract wants you in the night before, be alongside by late Friday afternoon.

split-kornati-krka-route.metaDesc: Seven days from Split into Kornati National Park and up the Krka to Skradin, out past Primošten and back by Rogoznica. Distances and mooring notes.

1.shortDescription: A short 8-mile hop west to the sandy Krknjaši shallows between the Drvenik islands, an easy first night while the crew settles in.
1.description: Getting out of Split on a Saturday afternoon takes patience. The fairway off ACI Marina and the city Riva fills with departing boats, so the plan on day one is modest: point west and let the crowd disperse behind you. The anchorage lies in the shallow neck of water dividing Veli Drvenik from Mali Drvenik, a sand-floored basin of three to six metres that goes hard turquoise once the sun is up. Holding is reliable. A trio of tavernas on Veli Drvenik will hand you a free buoy if you book a table, but you can just as easily set the anchor in the sand and run the tender in for grilled fish. There is a footpath around the headland and a little chapel on the far side if anyone wants to stretch their legs. We usually turn in early here. Tomorrow's leg north is longer.
thingsToDo: Anchor on the sand shallows in 4–6 m
Dinghy ashore for grilled fish on Veli Drvenik
Walk the headland path to the chapel
Swim off the stern before the day-boats arrive
Watch the sun drop over the open Adriatic
1.mooringTip: Drop the hook in four to six metres over sand and it will bite hard; the restaurant buoys cost nothing if you eat ashore. Only the west and northwest find their way in, so with a fresh westerly on the card, run the two miles into ACI Marina Trogir for the night.

2.shortDescription: Fourteen miles up the coast to Primošten, a terracotta old town on its own tied-off peninsula, with the Babić vineyards climbing the slopes behind.
2.description: You spot Primošten a long way off: a knot of terracotta roofs on a near-island, joined to the mainland by the thinnest neck of land, with the church of St. George crowning the top. The old town is still lived in, with no resort gloss over it, and the konobas tucked into the back lanes cook honest Dalmatian food. Berthing is the one thing to plan. Daytime boats can lie on the town quay, but nobody stays the night there; the overnight berths are at Marina Kremik a couple of miles south, and a shuttle brings you back into the centre for dinner. Behind the town the hillsides carry the dry-stone Bucavac terraces, walled by hand over generations to shelter the Babić vine. That grape makes a dark, structured red worth ordering over the easy coastal whites. Find a spot on the harbour wall, glass in hand, and watch the light go off the water.
thingsToDo: Climb to St. George's church on the summit
Order a glass of Babić on the quay
Walk the hand-built Bucavac wine terraces
Eat brudet in a back-lane konoba
Ride the Kremik shuttle in for dinner
2.mooringTip: Kremik is the marina you can count on overnight, two miles down the coast, with lazy lines, full services and a shuttle running into Primošten. Treat the town quay as a daytime perch only; it faces due west, and you want to be off it before the fishing fleet comes back at dusk.

3.shortDescription: Twenty miles offshore into Kornati National Park and its maze of bare, empty islands, tying up at Piškera, the sole marina the park allows inside it.
3.description: Kornati is unlike anywhere else you sail in Croatia. Eighty-nine islands, not a single permanent resident, low stone ridges combed with dry walls and dropping straight into deep water. Fishing is banned, so the sea underneath stays busy. The night's berth is ACI Marina Piškera, set on the islet of Panitula Vela and the only marina within the park lines. It gives you lazy lines, water, power and one restaurant on the rock above the pontoons. Sort the park ticket before you arrive, since booking online costs less than paying at the kiosk. The afternoon is yours: run the tender across to the clear water off Lavsa for a snorkel, or head south to where the cliffs of Mana and Kornat drop straight into the sea. It is a stark, quiet place, and the silence after sundown is the point.
thingsToDo: Book the Kornati park ticket online first
Snorkel the channel toward Lavsa islet
Sail beneath the southern cliffs of Mana
Dine at the marina's cliff-top restaurant
Scramble the karst ridge on Panitula
3.mooringTip: Every berth at Piškera has a lazy line; in July and August book ahead, because it fills. Anchoring inside the park is limited to five bays for the night (Lavsa, Lojena, Vrulje, Statival and Stiniva), and the konobas there will usually leave you a free buoy if you eat in. Ranger boats come round, so don't skip the fee.

4.shortDescription: Fifteen miles east out of Kornati to Zlarin, a green island with no cars, a single harbour village, and seven centuries of red-coral trade.
4.description: Leave Kornati astern and steer east into the Šibenik islands, where Zlarin makes a gentle contrast to the raw karst behind you. One village, no cars, lanes that run inland under cypress and pine. Coral runs through the whole of the island's past: divers here worked the red beds offshore for the best part of seven hundred years, and the village keeps a coral museum where the weighted lines and iron frames show how the beds were fished. A few workshops still turn out coral jewellery by hand. Berthing is stern-to off your own anchor at the quay, with water and power laid on at the inner berths for a small fee. In the afternoon walk one of the paths up to the little chapels on the ridge, or swim off the flat rocks along the south shore. After dinner, the harbour wall is as lively as Zlarin gets, and that suits most crews fine.
thingsToDo: See the red-coral museum on the square
Walk the ridge paths under cypress and pine
Swim off the rocks on the south shore
Buy hand-worked coral from a village workshop
Take dinner and the harbour wall at dusk
4.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth on the Zlarin village quay using your own anchor; water and power reach the middle of the quay for a small charge. You are tucked in from anything out of the west, northwest or north, but a sirocco from the southeast blows straight in. If the forecast shows southeast over 15 knots, it's a four-mile run north into the sheltered water off Šibenik, either ACI Marina Mandalina or the town quay.

5.shortDescription: Twelve miles up the Krka estuary past Šibenik and through the St. Anthony narrows to Skradin, gateway to the waterfalls of Krka National Park.
5.description: This is one of the strangest, best legs on the whole coast. Twelve of those miles run inland. You slip past the old walls of Šibenik, thread the rock cut of the St. Anthony channel under its road bridge, cross a belt of brackish water, and come out on the deep, hill-shadowed freshwater pool where ACI Marina Skradin sits. Any charter rig clears the bridge with room to spare. Skradin is the doorstep to Krka National Park, and a park shuttle boat runs upstream from the marina to Skradinski Buk, the terraced cascades that made the river famous. You pay at the kiosk on the seawall and the boat fare is folded into it; through the season one leaves about every half hour. The 2021 rules put a stop to swimming right under the falls, but the boardwalk rings both banks and the haul up to the top lookout earns its keep. Back in town, order the Skradin risotto, slow-cooked with saffron for hours, and a bottle of local red.
thingsToDo: Ride the park boat to Skradinski Buk
Loop the boardwalk on both riverbanks
Climb to the lookout above the falls
Order slow-cooked Skradin saffron risotto
Wander the medieval lanes behind the marina
5.mooringTip: Every berth at ACI Marina Skradin takes a lazy line, the yard runs full services, and there's fuel on the way in by the entrance. The marked river channel carries enough depth for any charter draft; reckon about an hour and a half at hull speed from where the river mouths at Šibenik. The park lets you anchor nowhere along its stretch of the river, so it comes down to the marina or nothing.

6.shortDescription: Twenty miles retrace the river, then hit the open coast bound for Rogoznica, with its strange Dragon's Eye salt lake alongside the sizeable Marina Frapa.
6.description: Retrace the river, then bear southeast down the open coast to Rogoznica, a low peninsula town roughly halfway between Šibenik and Split. What makes the place odd is the Dragon's Eye: a pool of salt water sunk deep into the karst at the very tip of the peninsula, with no outlet you can see. A passage hidden below the surface lets the tide fill and drain it twice a day. Its surface lies under the level of the open sea, you can walk the limestone rim in about twenty minutes from town, and the colour of the water keeps changing as the light moves. On the south side, Marina Frapa counts as one of the bigger yards in this stretch of Dalmatia, with lazy lines, a fuel dock, a hotel and a line of restaurants along the water. There is space on the north quay for smaller boats to lie stern-to, but it catches the northwesterly, so in season most skippers just take the marina and leave it at that. The town itself is small: a fishing fleet, a clutch of konobas, an old quarter on the point.
thingsToDo: Walk out to the Dragon's Eye lake
Look down from the karst rim cliffs
Eat brudet at a peninsula konoba
Wander the old quarter on the point
Tender across to Smokvica islet for a swim
6.mooringTip: In any weather the answer is Marina Frapa on the south side: lazy lines on the berths, full services, fuel taken on right at the mouth. The north-quay alternative means dropping your own anchor and lying stern-to for a small charge, but nothing shields it from the northwest, so cross to the marina the minute a fresh one is on the way.

7.shortDescription: The run home is twenty miles east-southeast to base, ACI Marina Split or the Riva, aiming to be alongside for the 09:00 Saturday handover.
7.description: Twenty miles east-southeast puts you back in Split, and it's the Saturday handover that sets the clock. You're normally wanted alongside by nine: tanks topped up, the heads pumped out, decks washed down and every line on the inventory accounted for. Most Split-based charters return to ACI Marina Split beside Diocletian's Palace, or to one of the bases out toward Kaštela and Trogir, and the run in is simple whatever the light. Heading east you run under the south side of Čiovo, and its little bays are good for a last swim; Slatine or Saldun are the ones most crews pick. Once she's squared away and the paperwork signed, the palace is a few minutes on foot and the airport about half an hour out. Most crews finish a week like this over a slow lunch on the Riva.
thingsToDo: Swim a last time off south Čiovo
Top up fuel and pump out before check-out
Walk Diocletian's Palace before the transfer
Cool off at Bačvice beach in Split
Take a long lunch on the Riva
7.mooringTip: Bring her back to whichever base your contract names, be it Split's ACI marina, Kaštela or Trogir, and take the berth the base manager gives you. Handover normally runs eight to nine on Saturday morning; where the contract calls for a Friday-night return, be tied up by about five the evening before.

split-vis-korcula-hvar-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Split, Vis & Hvar Island Route | Boat4You
split-vis-korcula-hvar-route.metaDesc: Seven days from Split through Šolta, Vis, the Blue Cave, Korčula, the Pakleni islands, Stari Grad and Brač, with distances and mooring notes for each stop.

1.shortDescription: A short first hop of 15 miles southwest lands you in Maslinica, the quiet cove at Šolta's western end, tucked behind seven small islets.
1.description: Fifteen miles out of Split and you are already on the hook in Maslinica, which makes it a forgiving way to start the week. Šolta sits closer to the mainland than any other island in this group, yet it stays off the day-tripper circuit, so the pace is slower than you would expect this near a city. The bay curls back into the hills and a scatter of small islets across its mouth takes the sting out of any westerly swell. You have two choices for the night. Martinis Marchi on the south side runs proper lazy lines and full services beside its restored baroque castle; the village quay opposite takes you stern-to on your own anchor for a small fee. We usually walk out to the western islets before dinner, swim off the rocks, and let the crew settle in early. Tomorrow is the long one.
thingsToDo: Swim off the rocks at Polebrnjak islet
Order the Dobričić red at a village konoba
Tour the Martinis Marchi castle courtyard
Taste Šoltansko olive oil in the village
Dinghy over to Stipanska for the swim path
1.mooringTip: Martinis Marchi has lazy lines and full services on the south side; book it for July and August. The northern village quay is cheaper, stern-to on your own hook, and the bay covers you from everything but a rare northerly.

2.shortDescription: Twenty-four miles of open water south to Komiža, a working fishing town on the far side of Vis with the finest sunset on the coast.
2.description: This is the longest stretch of open water all week, so we tend to slip the lines early. The track runs south under the bottom of Brač with the Hvar Channel widening away to port, then out to Vis, the furthest offshore of the inhabited Croatian islands. It stayed shut to visitors as a military zone until 1989, which is why you will not find resort sprawl here. Komiža wraps itself around a small bay on the western shore, its fishing fleet still tied along the eastern side. Berth stern-to in the inner basin on your own anchor; holding in the sand and weed is generally sound, though the outer wall is no place to spend a night once the southwesterly gets up. Dinner is grilled bogue and a bottle of Vugava, the local white. Then the sunset, dropping into open sea with nothing west of you until Italy.
thingsToDo: Walk the fishing fleet on the eastern quay
Try grilled bogue with a glass of Vugava
See the Fishermen's Museum in the Venetian tower
Hike Mount Hum for the 360-degree view
Watch the sunset from the harbour wall
2.mooringTip: Go stern-to on your own anchor inside the inner basin; the ground is sand and weed and mostly holds well. If the forecast shows southwesterly over 15 knots, run the six miles up to Vis Town in St George's Bay, which is properly closed in.

3.shortDescription: Cave light at dawn, a swim at Budihovac, then an easy easterly afternoon that finishes deep inside Vela Luka's bay.
3.description: Timing rules this day. The cave on Biševo only glows that improbable blue when the sun sits right, roughly between nine and eleven in the morning, so plan to be waiting off the entrance early. Tie up at the little concession quay outside, pay the fee at the kiosk, and take one of the official tenders in; your own dinghy stays put. Forty minutes covers it. From there we drop down to Budihovac off the south coast of Vis, a pair of islets with water so clear it looks staged, and anchor in six to eight metres of pale sand for a swim and lunch aboard. The afternoon is an eighteen-mile run east into Vela Luka, a long protected inlet at the western end of Korčula with fuel, water and everything you need on the quay. Book a table for žrnovski makaruni, the pasta they roll by hand here.
thingsToDo: Take the tender into the Blue Cave
Anchor and swim off Budihovac's twin islets
Order žrnovski makaruni at a Vela Luka konoba
Stroll the Vela Luka waterfront at dusk
3.mooringTip: Vela Luka gives you a long town quay, stern-to on your own anchor, with water, power and a fuel berth at the western end and holding that rarely lets go. It is well shut from the north and east; only a strong southerly nudges any swell inside.

4.shortDescription: Thirty miles east to the Pakleni islands and Palmižana, the archipelago's one marina and the back door to Hvar Town.
4.description: The longest passage of the week, thirty miles east-northeast, and it pays to be off the quay at first light. The aim is to get across the Korčula Channel before the afternoon Maestral fills in around two, though when it does arrive it sits nicely on the beam and carries you the last leg into the Pakleni group at pace. Palmižana lies on the east side of Sv Klement, the biggest of these islets and the only one with a marina, so the lazy-line berths book out fast in high summer. The main bay holds in five to ten metres but rolls once the wind funnels through in the afternoon; if you would rather not, the coves on the south side such as Vinogradišće sit quieter with a line run ashore. Ashore, the Meneghello family have kept a botanical garden here since 1906. Dinner is at one of the konobas round the bay, and there are Roman amphorae off the western point shallow enough to snorkel.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Roman amphorae off the western shore
Walk Meneghello's 1906 botanical garden
Anchor with a line ashore in Vinogradišće cove
Water-taxi across to Hvar Town for dinner
4.mooringTip: Book the ACI lazy-line berths at Palmižana online well ahead for July and August. If it is full, or the afternoon southwesterly is building, tuck into Vinogradišće on the south side and run a line ashore rather than roll in the main bay.

5.shortDescription: Round Hvar's northern cape and slide up the long flat inlet to Stari Grad, where fields planted by Greek settlers still line the shore.
5.description: A gentle fourteen miles today, bending clockwise round the western cape of Hvar and into the deep inlet that shelters Stari Grad. The Greeks founded Pharos here in 384 BC, and their street grid still shapes the old quarter. Behind the town spreads the Stari Grad Plain, a UNESCO site where the field boundaries laid out by those same colonists are farmed to this day, more than two thousand years on. Mooring is easy. The town quay along the western shore takes you stern-to on your anchor for a modest fee, and there is a small ACI marina across on the east side if you want power and lines in any weather. Walk out to the poet Hektorović's Tvrdalj, a fortified stone house wrapped round its own fishpond. In the afternoon we hire bikes and ride out through the old Greek fields; a peka lunch at a plain konoba is worth the three-hour notice they ask for.
thingsToDo: Bike the Greek field grid of Stari Grad Plain
Visit Tvrdalj Castle and its fishpond
Wander the Greek street grid of the old town
Book a peka lunch at a plain konoba
5.mooringTip: The western town quay is stern-to on your own anchor with water and power on the central section; the ACI marina opposite is the all-weather fallback. The inlet is deep and fjord-like, so it stays calm whichever way the wind blows.

6.shortDescription: A dozen miles north over the Brač Channel to Milna, the best-protected harbour on the island and a fine spot for peka.
6.description: Twelve miles across the Brač Channel brings you to Milna, a slim inlet cut into the island's western side that has sheltered boats since Venetian times. You will spot the old parish church on the inner quay long before you are alongside. Tie stern-to on the town quay inside, on your own anchor, with water and power along the middle stretch; the outer half of the bay is open anchoring in six to ten metres of sand, with a line to the rocks if the afternoon breeze comes in. Two things made Brač's name: the white stone, still cut at Pučišća up the coast and hauled off long ago for Diocletian's Palace, and lamb done slow under the peka. If you fancy peka tonight, order it the minute you tie up; three hours is the going wait. Later, walk out to the little church on the headland just north of the harbour for the view back over the boats.
thingsToDo: Order lamb under the peka on arrival
Walk to the headland church north of the harbour
See the 17th-century parish church on the quay
Dinghy to Bobovišća for a sunset swim
6.mooringTip: Berth stern-to on the inner town quay with your own anchor for a small fee, water and power midway along. The outer bay is free anchoring with a line to the rocks; the quay stays comfortable when the Maestral pipes up, and Bobovišća three miles north is the bad-weather bolt-hole.

7.shortDescription: A last fifteen miles across the channel to Split for the Saturday handover, with time to spare for Diocletian's Palace.
7.description: The run home is fifteen miles east-northeast over the open Split Channel. Most charters wrap on a Saturday morning, and base runs a tight window: alongside by nine, and before the eight-to-nine walk-through you want the fuel filled, the holding tanks pumped, the decks hosed and the inventory squared against the sheet, with someone left on board to hand over. Depending on your contract you will be heading for ACI Marina Split, Marina Kaštela or ACI Trogir; the approach to each is clear in any light. The Palace sits five minutes' walk from the ACI berths; the airport is twenty-five in the car. If time allows, close the week properly. Take an hour down in the Palace cellars and up on the Peristyle, or hold a table on the Riva and watch the ferries come and go. Seven days, three of Dalmatia's best islands, and not a passage all week that felt like a slog.
thingsToDo: Pump out and top up fuel before inspection
Walk Diocletian's Palace one last time
Climb St Domnius bell tower for the panorama
Take a final swim at Bačvice Beach
Linger over lunch on the Split Riva
7.mooringTip: Head back to whichever base your contract names, ACI Marina Split, Marina Kaštela or ACI Trogir, and berth as the base manager directs. The Saturday inspection window is eight to nine; if your paperwork asks for a night-before return, aim to be in by around five on Friday.

sukosan-kornati-hvar-korcula-vis-skradin.metaTitle: 14-Day Kornati to Vis Sailing Loop | Boat4You
sukosan-kornati-hvar-korcula-vis-skradin.metaDesc: Two weeks under sail from Sukošan through the Kornati, Hvar, Korčula, Vis and back up the Krka to Skradin. Anchorages, konobas and honest mooring tips.

1.shortDescription: A long opening run of about 25 miles down to Žut, largest of the outer Kornati islands, with a handful of seasonal konobas.
1.description: There's no gentle warm-up on this route. From Marina Dalmacija the first leg runs a solid 25 miles south, so we usually slip the lines by nine and let the morning stay flat before the afternoon breeze fills in. Žut sits just east of Kornat, all bare grey ridges and dry-stone walls, with not a single year-round resident. The reward is space. Bays like Hiljača and Bizikovica open one after another, and the konobas here run their own buoys, so a table ashore comes with a night on the mooring. Anchor over pale sand and you'll see the bottom at ten metres. Dinner is whatever the boat brought in — grilled fish, a carafe of house white, the smell of wild sage drifting off the hill as the light goes.
thingsToDo: Anchor over sand in Hiljača bay
Walk the ridge for Kornat views
Snorkel the sheltered shallows
Fresh grilled fish at a Žut konoba
1.mooringTip: The konoba buoys off Žut are the easy call: eat ashore and the mooring is thrown in. In a strong bura, tuck into Hiljača on the eastern side rather than the open western bays.

2.shortDescription: Head north to Pantera, a well-protected sandy bay on Dugi Otok's top end, a short walk from the tallest lighthouse in the Adriatic.
2.description: Pantera, sometimes marked Čuna on the charts, is a broad bay of sand and gravel at the northern tip of Dugi Otok. Holding is good in five to eight metres and the bay shrugs off anything from the north and northeast, which makes it a calm night after the exposure of the outer Kornati. Row or walk ashore and it's fifteen minutes to Veli Rat, a 40-metre lighthouse from 1849 that locals swear was rendered with egg whites mixed into the lime. West of the anchorage lies Sakarun, a long shallow beach where the water turns the pale turquoise you get over white sand. Time it for late afternoon, after the day boats leave. Squid off the grill, a cold Pošip, and the mast lights coming on one by one.
thingsToDo: Walk to Veli Rat lighthouse
Swim at Sakarun sandy beach
Anchor on sand in Pantera bay
Sundowners on deck over white sand
2.mooringTip: Free anchoring on sand at five to eight metres, well sheltered from the north and northeast. It fills up by early evening in July, so drop the hook before the charter fleet arrives and back down hard to set.

3.shortDescription: An easy dozen miles reach Vrulja, set deep in Kornati National Park, its three konobas ringing the archipelago's busiest mooring field.
3.description: Short day, so there's no rush. We tend to sail the long way round the outside of Kornat to see the crowns, the sheer cliffs where the island drops straight into open sea off Mana and Klis. Buy your park ticket before you enter; rangers do check. Vrulja itself is a working little bay with three konobas lined up along the shore, each with its own buoys, and it gets full in season. Book ahead if you want the good ones. Ashore, the menu is lamb from under the peka and fish off the coals, the smell of it carrying across the water long before you're seated. Ambitious crews climb Metlina, at 237 metres the high point of Kornat, for a view over the whole grey labyrinth.
thingsToDo: Sail the outer Kornati crowns
Book a konoba buoy in Vrulja
Peka lamb at a Vrulja konoba
Climb Metlina for the archipelago view
3.mooringTip: Have your Kornati park ticket sorted before you cross the boundary. The Vrulja konoba buoys go with dinner, but there are only so many, so call ahead on a summer weekend or arrive by mid-afternoon.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles south past Šibenik to Primošten, a terracotta old town packed onto a headland above the Babić vineyards.
4.description: Primošten reads as an island from the water, a knot of stone houses on a headland tied to the mainland by a low neck of land. The church of St. George crowns the top, its cemetery looking out over the channel. Behind town, the Bucavac slopes are ribbed with dry-stone walls, the pattern famous enough to hang in the UN building in New York, and they grow the Babić that goes into the region's dense red. Most crews berth at Marina Kremik, about a mile and a half south, and taxi or walk in for the evening. The old town's lanes are steep and worth an hour before dinner. Swim off Velika Raduča on the way in if the maestral hasn't kicked up chop.
thingsToDo: Climb to St. George's hilltop church
See the Bucavac dry-stone vineyards
Taste the local Babić red
Swim off Velika Raduča beach
4.mooringTip: Marina Kremik, a mile and a half south, is the standard overnight and needs booking in summer. Primošten's own town quay is small and gets swell in a westerly, so treat it as a lunch stop rather than a berth.

5.shortDescription: A short 14-mile hop to Trogir, a walled UNESCO town on its own islet between the mainland and Čiovo.
5.description: Trogir packs a lot of stone into a small island. Founded by Greeks, layered by Romans and then the Venetians, the whole core is a UNESCO site you can cross on foot in ten minutes and still not tire of. The headline is the Cathedral of St. Lawrence, and specifically Radovan's carved portal from 1240, worth craning your neck at. Climb the Kamerlengo fortress on the western point for the rooftops and the channel beyond. The riva along the Čiovo side is where the evening happens, gelato in hand, superyachts on one side and fishermen's boats on the other. It's a busy waterway, so keep a proper lookout coming through the narrows. Book the ACI marina; the town quay is short-stay and you rarely find a gap in August.
thingsToDo: See Radovan's portal on the cathedral
Climb the Kamerlengo fortress
Wander the walled old town on foot
Evening gelato on the Čiovo riva
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Trogir on the Čiovo side is the reliable overnight and worth pre-booking in high season. The town quay is short-stay only and the channel through town is tight, so post someone on the bow coming in.

6.shortDescription: South past Šolta and Brač to Hvar Town, the busiest, best-dressed harbour in Dalmatia, facing the Pakleni Islands.
6.description: Hvar Town is where the route turns glossy. The main square is the largest in Dalmatia, closed on one side by the 16th-century cathedral and watched over by the Fortica, the Spanish fort on the hill. Walk up before sunset; the climb takes twenty minutes and the view back over the Pakleni Islands is the reason people put up with the crowds. Those islands, a couple of miles off the harbour, are where you actually want to be at anchor. Palmižana on Sveti Klement has a proper marina and a string of konobas hidden in the pines. In town it's cocktails at Hula-Hula and late music in old courtyards, if that's your crew. If it isn't, cross to the Pakleni for a quiet cove and come in only for dinner.
thingsToDo: Climb the Fortica for Pakleni views
Anchor off Palmižana in the Pakleni
Drinks at the Hula-Hula bar
See Hvar's main square and cathedral
6.mooringTip: The Hvar town quay is expensive, exposed and usually full; ACI Marina Palmižana on Sveti Klement, two miles across, is the calmer and safer overnight. Book Palmižana ahead and tender or water-taxi into town for the evening.

7.shortDescription: A long 35-mile leg southeast to Korčula, a fortified old town with a herringbone street plan built to break the wind.
7.description: This is the longest single day on the route, around 35 miles, so it pays to be off Hvar early and use whatever southerly there is. Korčula's old town juts out on a little peninsula, its streets laid out in a fishbone so the lanes on the windward side stay sheltered while the leeward ones let the summer breeze through. Clever medieval planning. The town leans hard on its claim to Marco Polo, and you can climb the bell tower of St. Mark's for the roofscape and the channel toward Pelješac. In summer they still perform the moreška, the old sword dance, on a stage by the walls. For a drink, find a bottle of Grk from the Lumbarda sands, a white you won't easily meet outside this island.
thingsToDo: Walk Korčula's herringbone old-town lanes
Climb St. Mark's bell tower
Catch the moreška sword dance
Try local Grk wine from Lumbarda
7.mooringTip: ACI Marina Korčula is small, so book it. The old-town quay is exposed to the sirocco; if a strong jugo is forecast overnight, shift to Lumbarda bay or a leeward anchorage rather than lying alongside in swell.

8.shortDescription: South to Lastovo, the most far-flung inhabited island in Croatia, and Skrivena Luka, one of the best-sheltered natural harbours on the coast.
8.description: Lastovo sits far enough out that it stayed a military zone for decades, and it still feels a step removed from the rest of the coast. The whole island is a nature park, thick with pine and dotted with the tall ornamented chimneys the locals are known for. Skrivena Luka, the Hidden Harbour on the south side, does exactly what the name says: a near-landlocked bay that holds calm in almost any weather. Above it stands the Struga lighthouse, an easy walk for a view down the cliffs to the open sea. The konobas here run moorings, and dinner tends toward slow food, smoked ham and local goat, rakija poured whether you asked or not. It's a quiet night, and after a week of harbours that's the point of coming this far.
thingsToDo: Overnight in sheltered Skrivena Luka
Walk up to Struga lighthouse
Spot Lastovo's ornate stone chimneys
Slow konoba dinner with local rakija
8.mooringTip: Buy the Lastovo Nature Park ticket, and take a konoba buoy in Skrivena Luka rather than anchoring across the mooring field. The bay is famously calm, but the inner corners hold best if any swell wraps in.

9.shortDescription: An open 25-mile passage north to Vis, closed to visitors until 1989, and its western fishing town of Komiža.
9.description: The hop up to Vis is proper open water, around 25 miles with little to duck behind, so pick a settled forecast and reef early if the maestral is up. Vis was off-limits to foreigners through the Yugoslav years, which spared it the overdevelopment you see elsewhere. Komiža, on the western side under Hum hill, is still a fishing town first: stone houses around a tight harbour, nets drying, a Venetian tower on the waterfront that now holds the fishing museum. Eat the pogača, the island's flat anchovy-and-onion pie, and drink the local Vugava white. The Blue Cave on nearby Biševo is the big excursion; go early with a licensed boat before the light drops off inside. Climb Hum at the end of the day if your legs are willing.
thingsToDo: Day trip to the Blue Cave, Biševo
Komiška pogača anchovy pie
Visit the Komiža fishing museum
Climb Hum hill for sunset
9.mooringTip: Komiža's harbour is short-stay and can get gusty when the bura funnels down the bay. For a settled night, book ACI Marina Vis on the opposite side of the island and day-trip back over to Komiža.

10.shortDescription: A 30-mile passage back northeast to Zlarin, the traffic-free coral island off Šibenik, its single village strung along the harbour.
10.description: Long day back across open water, so an early start again, and by afternoon you're closing the Šibenik islands. Zlarin is small and quiet in a way the busier ports aren't; no cars, one village wrapped around the harbour, footpaths through olive and pine. The island made its name on coral, hand-harvested from these waters since the fifteenth century, and there's a little Coral Centre in the village that tells the story better than I can.
