Buy a piece straight from a workshop if you want a proper souvenir. The harbour quay is short and shallow, so most of us anchor just outside on sand and tender in. Sit on the waterfront at dusk with a loza and watch Šibenik's fortresses turn gold across the channel.
thingsToDo: Visit the Zlarin Coral Centre
Buy coral from a village workshop
Walk the car-free island footpaths
Loza rakija on the harbour front
10.mooringTip: The Zlarin quay is short-stay and shallow, so anchor outside the harbour on sand in five to seven metres and tender ashore. Holding is decent, but check your swing if the fleet packs in.

11.shortDescription: A gentle motor up the Krka river reaches Skradin, a little medieval town set within the national park just below the Skradinski Buk falls.
11.description: After days of salt and open sea, the run up the Krka is a change of scene: flat green water between wooded banks, the smell of pine and river instead of brine. Skradin sits where the river widens, a compact medieval town with a marina right at its foot. You can't take the yacht up to the falls; leave her in ACI Skradin and hop the park boat up to Skradinski Buk, a wide staircase of cascades you can walk around on boardwalks. The town itself is worth a slow evening. Order the Skradinski rižot, cooked down for hours until it's almost a paste, and finish with the local honey cake. If you've a spare morning, rent a bike and ride one of the park trails along the water.
thingsToDo: Park-boat up to Skradinski Buk falls
Wander Skradin's medieval lanes
Try the slow-cooked Skradinski rižot
Cycle a Krka park riverside trail
11.mooringTip: ACI Marina Skradin at the river mouth is the base for the park; book it for July and August. Take the park's own boat up to the falls rather than trying to move the yacht upriver.

12.shortDescription: A short 8-mile drop back down the river and out to Tijat, a tiny uninhabited pine-covered island in the Šibenik archipelago.
12.description: An easy day, and after Skradin it's meant to be. Drop back down the Krka, out past Šibenik's old channel under the St. Nicholas fortress, and it's a short reach to Tijat. Nobody lives here. The island is low and green, ringed by smaller islets you can nose a kayak between, with good anchoring on sand and weed off its southern side. There are no services at all, so top up water and provisions in Skradin or Šibenik before you leave, and bring what you need for the night. This is a swimming-and-nothing day: snorkel the shallows, string a hammock in the olives ashore, grill whatever's left in the fridge as the sun drops. On a still evening it's about as quiet as the Adriatic gets.
thingsToDo: Kayak the islets off Tijat's south side
Snorkel Tijat's sheltered shallows
Hammock in the shoreline olives
Grill supper on deck at anchor
12.mooringTip: Anchor on sand and seagrass in five to eight metres off Tijat's southern shore. There are no services on the island, so fill water and stock the galley in Skradin or Šibenik first.

13.shortDescription: A gentle 8 miles to Murter, the gateway island for the Kornati, with Slanica's sandy beach and two marinas by the town.
13.description: Murter is the launch pad for most Kornati charters, joined to the mainland by a small drawbridge at Tisno that swings open twice a day. It's a low-key working island, and after the outer stops it feels almost bustling. Slanica, on the western side, is the rare proper sandy beach in these parts, shallow and good for a last long swim. In Betina, the next village round, they've built wooden gajeta boats for generations, and the little maritime museum there is worth half an hour. For the final dinner, find pašticada, beef braised slowly in wine and prošek until it falls apart, usually served over gnocchi. Berth in Marina Hramina or Marina Betina, both within a mile of town, and settle up for fuel before the morning rush.
thingsToDo: Last swim at sandy Slanica beach
See the wooden-boat museum in Betina
Watch the Tisno drawbridge open
Pašticada beef stew at a konoba
13.mooringTip: Marina Hramina or Marina Betina, both within a mile of Murter town, take the overnight; book ahead in summer. Sort your fuel slot the evening before so you're not queueing at the pump on handover day.

14.shortDescription: Handover day: 22 miles home along the Pašman channel to close the loop at Sukošan, where Marina Dalmacija ranks as Croatia's largest.
14.description: Last leg, and no reason to hurry it. The route runs up the Pašman channel, sheltered water between the island and the mainland, with time for one more swim in a quiet cove before you close the coast. Marina Dalmacija at Sukošan is the biggest in the country, a small town of pontoons, so know your berth number before you arrive and give the office a call on approach. Leave time to clean up and pack; charter handovers go smoother when the boat comes back sorted. If the galley still has provisions, cook a proper last dinner aboard, black cuttlefish risotto if you can get the ink, and raise a glass to two weeks that covered most of what Dalmatia does well. Fuel up on the way in so the morning is just paperwork.
thingsToDo: Last swim in a Pašman channel cove
Cook black cuttlefish risotto aboard
Fuel up before the Marina Dalmacija berth
Pack and clean for the handover
14.mooringTip: Marina Dalmacija at Sukošan is huge, so confirm your berth assignment and a fuel slot with the office a day ahead. Come in slow and have fenders and lines ready on both sides; the fairways get busy on Saturday turnaround.

sukosan-kornati-krka-route.metaDesc: A 7-day yacht charter from Sukošan through Telašćica, the Kornati islands and Krka's waterfalls, with honest anchorage and mooring tips.

1.shortDescription: A long opening leg out of Marina Dalmacija, up the inside of Ugljan and around Dugi Otok to the quiet harbour at Božava.
1.description: Twenty-five miles is a full day, so we usually slip the lines at Marina Dalmacija by nine and take the flat water inside Ugljan while it lasts. The olive terraces give way to open channel, and by early afternoon the maestral fills in from the northwest, which pushes you nicely toward the top of Dugi Otok. Božava itself is small and green, a handful of stone houses around a pine-shaded inlet. The real draw sits a mile west: Sakarun, a shallow white-sand bay that turns the water pale turquoise even under grey skies. Drop the hook there for a long swim before dinner. Ashore, the harbour taverna does grilled squid and a cold Pošip that tastes better after a hard sail. Turn in early; tomorrow is shorter, but the scenery gets serious.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Sakarun beach
Climb Veli Rat lighthouse for the view
Grilled squid at the Božava harbour taverna
Walk the pine path above the inlet
1.mooringTip: Božava's town quay only holds a few boats, so call ahead in July and August or plan to anchor. Sakarun holds well on sand in 4-6 m, but it empties out in a northwesterly, so treat it as a daytime stop rather than an overnight.

2.shortDescription: An easy fourteen miles down Dugi Otok's outer coast into Telašćica, the deep sheltered inlet at the island's southern end.
2.description: The run south hugs the seaward side of Dugi Otok, so keep an eye on the swell if there's any west in the wind. Telašćica opens up like a long protected corridor, nearly nine miles of it, walled on the ocean side by the Stene cliffs that stand well over a hundred metres. Anchor deep in the bay near Uvala Mir and walk the fifteen minutes across the ridge to the saltwater lake. It sits warmer and saltier than the sea, and the mud along its edge is the local remedy for tired joints. Climb the marked path to the cliff top for the drop straight down to the open Adriatic. Back in the bay, the konobas do lamb and fresh fish; the wild donkeys will happily inspect your lunch if you let them.
thingsToDo: Float in the warm Mir saltwater lake
Walk to the top of the Stene cliffs
Lunch at a Uvala Mir konoba
Meet the park's wild donkeys
2.mooringTip: There's a park entry fee collected at the buoys inside the inlet. Uvala Mir has restaurant moorings and room to anchor in 8-12 m; in high summer the buoys fill by mid-afternoon, so aim to arrive before three.

3.shortDescription: A short eight-mile hop into the bare karst maze of the Kornati islands, aiming for the restaurant moorings at Levrnaka or Piškera.
3.description: The hop to the Kornati is only eight miles, barely a morning's work, which leaves the whole afternoon for the islands proper. This is stripped-back country: eighty-odd islands of white stone, dry-stone walls climbing to nowhere, and almost no shade. Levrnaka is the pick for a swim. The sandy beach at Lojena on its western side shelves gently, and the snorkelling along the rocks is good in clear light. If you want a proper harbour instead, ACI Piškera has laid moorings and a small shop. Either way, dinner ashore is the move here, since a table at one of the island konobas usually comes with a free buoy for the night. Bring cash. Once the day boats leave, the silence is total and the stars are the best on the route.
thingsToDo: Swim the sandy Lojena beach on Levrnaka
Hike to the Metlina viewpoint
Snorkel the clear rocks off Levrnaka
Dinner ashore for a free mooring buoy
3.mooringTip: Buy your Kornati park ticket before you enter, either online or from an agent. At Levrnaka, Piškera and Vrulje the konobas hold buoys for dining guests, so book a table by VHF or phone in the morning and the overnight mooring comes with it.

4.shortDescription: A longer leg southeast out of the park to Zlarin, the traffic-free coral island that guards the approach to Šibenik.
4.description: Leaving the Kornati means a couple of hours of open water before the coast firms up again around Šibenik. Zlarin sits just off that entrance, and stepping ashore it feels a generation slower than the mainland. No cars, one village wrapped around the harbour, cats asleep on warm stone. The island made its name on red coral, dived by hand here since the fifteenth century, and the small coral collection in the village tells that story without much fuss. Walk the shaded lanes up toward the church for the view back over the channel. In the evening the harbour benches fill with older men slapping down cards over a game of briškula, and a glass of homemade loza costs next to nothing. It's a gentle day, and after two nights in the wild you'll want one.
thingsToDo: Tour the village coral collection
Walk the car-free lanes to the church
Watch a harbour game of briškula
Try a glass of homemade loza
4.mooringTip: Zlarin's harbour quay is short and fills fast in season, so aim to be alongside by early afternoon or book ahead. If it's full, anchor off the bay on sand in 5-7 m and take the tender in.

5.shortDescription: A calm inland leg past Šibenik and up the Krka estuary to Skradin, the walled town at the gate of the waterfalls.
5.description: This is the most unusual sailing of the week. You thread the narrow St Anthony channel below the Šibenik fortresses, cross the wide Prokljan lake, then motor up a green river gorge with barely a breath of wind. Skradin appears at the head of it, a small stone town that has been a harbour since Roman times. Tie up in the ACI marina or on the town quay and leave the boat there; from the marina, a free park boat runs the last stretch up to Skradinski Buk, the big terraced waterfall system. Walk the boardwalks through the falls, then find a bench in town for a plate of Dalmatian pršut and a wedge of Pag cheese. Skradin also makes a serious risotto, cooked here for the better part of a day, if you're staying for dinner.
thingsToDo: Ride the free park boat to Skradinski Buk
Walk the boardwalks through the falls
Wander Skradin's Roman-era lanes
Try the slow-cooked Skradin risotto
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Skradin sits right at the river mouth and books out in July and August, so reserve a berth in advance. The park water above Prokljan is a no-anchoring zone, so plan to be tied up in Skradin for the night.

6.shortDescription: Eighteen miles back down the river and up the coast to Vrgada, a tiny sandy island off Pakoštane with no cars and one bay.
6.description: Retrace the river in the morning cool, then turn north up the coast as the maestral gets going. Vrgada is low and green, ringed by shallows, and the anchorage off Lučica bay is one of the softer sand bottoms in this part of the Adriatic. The holding is excellent, which matters if the wind swings overnight. About 250 people live here through the winter, more than double that in August, so it never gets loud. Wade ashore, walk the sandy paths under the pines, and comb the far beaches for smoothed sea glass. The family konobas above the bay run on whatever came in that morning; ask whether they're doing brudet, the slow Dalmatian fish stew, and settle in. There's nothing to rush back for.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim in sandy Lučica bay
Walk the car-free paths under the pines
Beachcomb the quiet outer beaches
Brudet fish stew at a family konoba
6.mooringTip: Vrgada's konobas keep restaurant buoys off Lučica bay that come free with dinner. Otherwise the anchoring is some of the best around, sand holding well in 3-5 m, but stay clear of the marked shallows on the approach.

7.shortDescription: A relaxed final sixteen miles up the Pašman channel back to Marina Dalmacija at Sukošan for the handover.
7.description: The last leg is a straightforward run up the sheltered Pašman channel, flat water with the islands to port and the mainland to starboard. There's no need to hurry. Duck into one of the coves along Pašman or Ugljan for a final swim while the boat is still yours, and time your arrival at Marina Dalmacija for the early afternoon. It's the biggest marina in Croatia and the check-out runs smoothly, but the fuel berths get busy at changeover, so book your slot the day before. Give the deck a rinse, sort the fenders and lines, and hand back a tidy boat. Then a last plate of crni rižot, the black cuttlefish risotto, on the marina front while you total up the miles. A week that starts and ends in the same berth still covers a lot of Adriatic.
thingsToDo: Final swim in a Pašman channel cove
Book the fuel berth a day ahead
Black cuttlefish risotto on the marina front
Rinse and prep the boat for handover
7.mooringTip: Marina Dalmacija is huge, so confirm your assigned berth and a fuel slot with the office 24 hours before you arrive. Come in slow; the fairways get congested on Friday and Saturday changeover.

sukosan-pag-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Sukošan to Pag Island Hop | Boat4You
sukosan-pag-route.metaDesc: Sail this 7-day yacht charter from Sukošan out to Molat, Silba, Ilovik and Pag, then home via Olib and Iž, with quiet anchorages, sandy bays and konobas.

1.shortDescription: A short 4 nm shake-down north from Marina Dalmacija into the calm Pašman channel, time to find the winches before dark.
1.description: Most crews are still stowing fenders when the lines come off in Sukošan, so keep this first leg gentle. Marina Dalmacija is one of the largest charter bases in Croatia, and the exit can feel busy, but once you clear the breakwater the Pašman channel opens flat and green ahead. We usually motor the four miles north and get the sails up halfway across, just to shake the system out. Ždrelac sits at the top of the channel, near the low road bridge linking Ugljan and Pašman. Drop the hook over sand, swim before the light goes, then row ashore for brudet at one of the stone konobas. The water settles glassy after sunset. A quiet first night, with the rigging still smelling of the chandlery.
thingsToDo: Swim off the sand at Ždrelac
Brudet fish stew at a stone konoba
Watch boats pass under the Ždrelac bridge
Sundowner on the Sukošan riva before leaving
1.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 5–7 m, well sheltered from the N and NW; the town quay is short-stay only, so plan to swing on your own hook overnight.

2.shortDescription: Fourteen miles west to Molat, one of the emptiest islands in the Zadar chain, three tiny villages, dark skies, almost no one about.
2.description: Molat rewards an early start. Leave Ždrelac by nine and you carry the morning calm most of the way, the afternoon maestral filling in behind you as you close the coast. Three small villages share the island, Molat, Zapuntel and Brgulje, and their combined population would barely fill a café. Brgulje makes the natural anchorage: a wide southern bay with sand and patchy seagrass, decent holding once you find a clear patch. Ashore, a rough lane links the hamlets past dry-stone walls and a shuttered school. Molat is a designated Important Bird Area with almost no light pollution, so on a clear night, stay up on deck. I have rarely seen the Milky Way this crisp from a cockpit. Bring provisions; the shops here amount to a counter.
thingsToDo: Walk the lane between all three hamlets
Anchor and swim in Brgulje Bay
Stargaze from the cockpit after dark
Beach afternoon below Zapuntel
2.mooringTip: Anchor over sand and seagrass in 5–8 m in Brgulje Bay; hold off the village if you want a restaurant mooring. Good shelter from the N, exposed if the sirocco gets up.

3.shortDescription: The long leg west: lunch and a swim at car-free Silba, then on to Ilovik to spend the night in its sheltered channel.
3.description: This is the longest day of the week, roughly eighteen miles, so top up water and leave in good time. Silba comes first. No cars run here, only footpaths and the odd wheelbarrow, and the village keeps its old sandstone houses and the hexagonal Toreta tower above the main street. Anchor off Sotorišće or tuck into Sv. Ante on the south side for lunch and a swim over pale sand. By mid-afternoon we push on to Ilovik, an hour or so south. The channel between Ilovik and the Sveti Petar islet is one of the best natural harbours out here, protected from almost every direction. Oleander lines the village path, the shop sells the basics, and the local grill does a good lamb. Settle in early; the anchorage fills fast in July.
thingsToDo: Swim at Sotorišće beach on Silba
Climb to the Toreta tower viewpoint
Walk Silba's car-free stone lanes
Overnight in the Ilovik–Sveti Petar channel
Lamb at the Ilovik village grill
3.mooringTip: At Ilovik, pick up a restaurant buoy in the channel and book ahead in season; it packs out by early evening. If you prefer to anchor, the sand off Sveti Petar holds well in 5–8 m.

4.shortDescription: An easy 14 nm reach east into the Pag channel to Šimuni, a working fishing village with the wild Lun olive groves up the coast.
4.description: Pag looks like nowhere else on the coast, a long spine of bare grey stone scoured by the bura. The crossing east is usually straightforward, but keep an eye on the forecast, because when the bura runs off the Velebit it funnels hard down this channel. Šimuni sits about midway along the island, a small ACI marina and a handful of fishing boats. The wild olive grove up at Lun is why many people stop here, thousands of gnarled trees, some reckoned well past a thousand years old, though it is a proper ride or a taxi to reach the northern tip. Closer to hand, ask for Paški sir, the hard sheep cheese from the Gligora dairy at Kolan, and grill some Pag lamb. Salt has been worked on this island for centuries.
thingsToDo: Day-trip to the ancient Lun olive grove
Taste Paški sir from Gligora dairy
Grill Pag lamb by the water
Wander Šimuni's small fishing harbour
4.mooringTip: ACI Marina Šimuni is small, so book ahead in July and August. For a quieter night, anchor in a cove just north or south of the marina over sand in 5–7 m, watching for bura gusts.

5.shortDescription: A short 10 nm hop back to Olib, a sandy, car-free island of barely a hundred residents and some of the quietest bays around.
5.description: Olib is the sort of place people mean when they say they want to get away from it all. Ten miles is a lazy morning, so there is no rush off the Pag channel. The island runs flat and low, ringed by sand rather than rock, and cars stay parked by the harbour. Slatina and Banve are the swim bays worth rowing to, shallow and soft underfoot. Behind the shore, footpaths thread through old stone hamlets and olive terraces that have half gone back to scrub. Barely a hundred people live here year round, and the harbour has room for only a few visiting boats. Ask around for the island's hand-rolled makaruni pasta and a glass of the local wine. It stays dark and silent after nine.
thingsToDo: Row to sandy Slatina Bay for a swim
Walk the footpaths past old stone hamlets
Try Olib's hand-rolled makaruni pasta
Beach afternoon at Banve
5.mooringTip: The fishing port has only a handful of slots, so most crews anchor. Slatina Bay holds well over sand in 4–6 m and shelters you from the N and NE; give the shallows a wide berth on the way in.

6.shortDescription: Sixteen miles recross the channel to Iž, a slim island of potters where the traditional Croatian peka bells are still thrown by hand.
6.description: The run back to Iž is a proper sail if the maestral cooperates, sixteen miles of open water with the Kornati laid out to the south. Iž is long and thin, greener than the outer islands, and it has kept a craft most places lost. Veli Iž potters still throw the heavy clay bells used for cooking peka, and the village ethnographic collection is worth an hour if the pottery interests you. There is a small marina, well tucked in and protected. If you would rather swing at anchor, Knež Bay on the south side is calm and has a konoba right on the water. We tend to arrive by late afternoon, walk up to the old church, and let the evening go slowly. Watch for a village fjera if your dates line up.
thingsToDo: See the peka bell potters at Veli Iž
Visit the village ethnographic collection
Anchor and swim in Knež Bay
Walk up to the old village church
Catch a fjera village fair if timed right
6.mooringTip: ACI Marina Iž at Veli Iž is small but well protected, a safe bet if the wind is up. For a quieter night, anchor in Knež Bay over sand in 5–8 m, with a konoba ashore for dinner.

7.shortDescription: The last leg home, twelve miles through the Pašman channel with a final swim off Ugljan before the Marina Dalmacija handover.
7.description: Twelve miles stand between you and the handover, so there is time for one more swim. We usually cut in along Ugljan and drop the hook off Veli Školj for an hour, a last plunge before the marina swallows you again. Then it is a straight run back down the Pašman channel to Sukošan. Marina Dalmacija turns over more charter boats than anywhere in the country, and Saturday mornings are chaos, so sort your fuel and your paperwork the evening before. Call ahead for a fuel slot and confirm your berth assignment. Ashore, book a table for crni rižot, the black cuttlefish risotto, and toast a week of empty anchorages. Rinse the salt off, pack slowly, and leave the boat the way you would want to find her.
thingsToDo: Final swim off Ugljan at Veli Školj
Fuel and paperwork the night before
Crni rižot dinner at the marina
Browse the Marina Dalmacija waterfront
7.mooringTip: At Marina Dalmacija, confirm your berth assignment and reserve a fuel slot 24 hours ahead; the Saturday turnover is heavy and the fuel dock backs up early. Clean and pack the night before to make the morning handover painless.

sukosan-vis-hvar-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Sukošan to Hvar Islands Loop | Boat4You
sukosan-vis-hvar-route.metaDesc: Sail a seven-day loop from Sukošan out to Vis and Hvar, taking in Primošten, Komiža, Šolta and Šibenik's stone cathedral along the way.

1.shortDescription: A relaxed four-mile shake-down from Croatia's biggest charter base up to the Ždrelac strait, with a swim and an early night.
1.description: A gentle first leg while you learn the boat. We usually motor out of Marina Dalmacija mid-morning, once the paperwork and provisioning are done, and point north up the Pašman channel. It is barely four miles to the Ždrelac strait, the shallow gap where the road bridge links Ugljan to Pašman. Drop the hook on sand behind the village and the afternoon is yours: a swim in warm, waist-deep water, a paddle to shore, an early check that every winch and reef line works before you commit to longer passages. Evenings here are quiet. Fishing boats come and go, the light turns amber over the pines, and dinner is whatever the local grill has landed that day. A shake-down leg with no pressure, which is exactly what the first night should be.
thingsToDo: Cross the Ždrelac bridge on foot
Anchor swim in the Pašman shallows
Provision at Marina Dalmacija
Grilled fish at a Ždrelac grill
1.mooringTip: The strait is shallow and narrow, so hold the marked channel and mind the bridge clearance of roughly 16.5 m if you carry a tall rig. Anchor on sand in 4-6 m with good holding and shelter from the bura, but keep the fairway clear.

2.shortDescription: The week's longest sail, thirty-two miles south past Šibenik to Primošten's headland old town, Babić vineyards and a berth at Kremik.
2.description: The longest haul of the week, so we like to slip the anchor by nine. Once clear of the strait you can lay a course south past the Šibenik approaches, and with the summer maestral filling in from the northwest most afternoons, it often turns into a proper sail rather than a motor. Primošten earns the miles. The old town sits on a rounded headland, lanes climbing to the church of St. George at the crown, and the drystone Bucavac vineyards fan out across the slopes behind it. This is Babić country, a dense red worth an hour in a cellar. Berth down at Marina Kremik, walk back for dinner, and if anyone still has energy the open-air Aurora club runs late through July and August. A long day that pays off.
thingsToDo: Walk the Bucavac drystone vineyards
Climb to St. George's hilltop church
Taste Babić red in a cellar
Berth and dine at Marina Kremik
Late night at Aurora club
2.mooringTip: The town mole is exposed and short-stay only. Marina Kremik, about 1.5 nm south, is the dependable berth and fills fast in high summer, so reserve ahead.

3.shortDescription: Head offshore to remote Vis, dropping into Komiža's fishing harbour for anchovy flatbread, a hilltop monastery and the Blue Cave nearby.
3.description: Now you go offshore. Vis lies farther out than any other inhabited island in this part of Dalmatia, closed to visitors until 1989 and still refreshingly unhurried. Expect open water and a real swell if the maestral is up, so reef early and keep the crew fed. Komiža appears as a tight bowl of stone houses around a working fishing harbour, the old Venetian tower squat on the quay. Climb to the Muster monastery above the town for the light going down over Biševo, then eat komiška pogača, the local anchovy and tomato flatbread, straight from the oven. If the morning is calm, run the five miles southwest to the Blue Cave on Biševo before the tour boats arrive. Pour a glass of Vugava, the island's honeyed white, and let the passage settle.
thingsToDo: Day-trip to the Blue Cave, Biševo
Climb to the Muster monastery
Komiška pogača on the quay
Visit the Fishing Museum tower
Taste Vugava white wine
3.mooringTip: Komiža's outer mole takes swell whenever the afternoon maestral blows in from the west. For a settled night, book ACI Marina Vis across the island at Kut, or tuck into a lee cove instead.

4.shortDescription: An easy reach to glamorous Hvar Town: Venetian square, hilltop fortress, the Pakleni Islands opposite and sundowners at Hula-Hula.
4.description: An easier reach northeast brings you to the busiest, most polished port on the coast. Hvar Town wears its Venetian past well: the wide St. Stephen's Square, the cathedral at its head, and the Fortica fortress standing guard on the hill above. Leave time to walk up before sunset, when the Pakleni Islands lay out in silhouette across the channel. The town itself runs at two speeds. By day it is cafés and market stalls; after dark the beach clubs take over, and a sundowner at Hula-Hula on the western shore is close to compulsory. Berthing in town is a scrum in season, so we usually cross to Palmižana for the night and dinghy back for dinner. Truffle pasta, cold Pošip, and the hum of the old stone streets carrying down to the water.
thingsToDo: Climb to Fortica fortress at sunset
Cross to Palmižana, Pakleni Islands
Sundowners at Hula-Hula beach bar
Wander St. Stephen's Square
4.mooringTip: Hvar Town's quay is stern-to, pricey and open to the sirocco. ACI Marina Palmižana on Sveti Klement, about 2 nm across, is calmer and worth reserving; the Pakleni buoy fields fill by early afternoon.

5.shortDescription: A gentle hop to sleepy Šolta, anchoring off Maslinica's seven islets below a restored castle, with olive oil and slow evenings.
5.description: A short, unhurried hop north to Šolta, the island Split sailors keep for themselves. Maslinica sits on the western tip, looking out at a scatter of seven small islets that make for a lazy afternoon of dinghy exploring and quiet swimming. The Martinis-Marchi castle anchors the village, restored now as a hotel and marina. If the basin is full, Šešula bay just around the corner is deeper and better protected, with laid moorings off a couple of konobas. This is olive-oil country, so pick up a bottle of the local Levantinka pressing and some Šolta honey while you can. Evenings are slow. A plate of lamb, a glass of the native Dobričić red, and not much to do but watch the light drain out of the bay. After Hvar, the calm is welcome.
thingsToDo: Dinghy the seven Maslinica islets
Tour the Martinis-Marchi castle
Anchor lunch in Šešula bay
Buy Šolta olive oil and honey
5.mooringTip: The Martinis-Marchi basin is small and books out in season. Šešula bay just south has laid moorings off the restaurants and better all-round shelter if you arrive to a full marina.

6.shortDescription: Sail back to the mainland through St. Anthony's Channel to Šibenik, its stone cathedral, hilltop forts and the Krka waterfalls.
6.description: Turn back northwest for the mainland and one of the finest harbour approaches in Croatia. The entrance threads the narrow St. Anthony's Channel, walls of rock close on either side, before Šibenik opens up ahead. The Cathedral of St. James is the reason to come: built entirely of stone, no timber, no mortar, and ringed with a frieze of local faces around the apse. Climb St. Michael's Fortress for the view over the red roofs and the delta. With a spare afternoon, the boat trip up to the Krka waterfalls at Skradinski buk is well worth the detour. Berth at Mandalina and eat along the river, finishing with fritule, the little sugared doughnuts sold warm from a stall on the Riva. A working city with real history, not a resort dressed up as one.
thingsToDo: Enter via St. Anthony's Channel
Tour the Cathedral of St. James
Climb St. Michael's Fortress ramparts
Krka waterfalls trip from Skradin
Warm fritule on the Riva
6.mooringTip: Approach through the narrow St. Anthony's Channel under power. D-Marin Mandalina is the standard charter berth; ACI Skradin upriver puts you at the Krka gateway but needs booking in season.

7.shortDescription: Thirty final miles home down the Pašman channel into Sukošan, a farewell swim and cuttlefish risotto to close before the handover.
7.description: The run home. Ease back up the coast and into the Pašman channel, with time for one last anchor stop in a quiet cove for a swim before the marina swallows you again. We aim to be alongside at Marina Dalmacija by late afternoon, which leaves the evening free and the handover unhurried. Fuel up on the way in, rinse the salt off, and stow the boat while there is still light. Dinner ashore is an easy call in Sukošan: a black cuttlefish risotto, a bottle of something Dalmatian, and the slow accounting of the week's best moments. The marina strip is busy on a Saturday changeover, so confirm your berth and fuel slot the day before. A gentle last leg, more about winding down than pushing on.
thingsToDo: Last swim in a Pašman cove
Cuttlefish black risotto ashore
Stroll the Marina Dalmacija promenade
Fuel and rinse for handover
7.mooringTip: Confirm your berth assignment and a fuel-dock slot at Marina Dalmacija a day ahead, since the base is large and Saturday turnaround is hectic. Come in with a near-full tank to speed the handover.

trogir-dubrovnik-trogir-14day-route.metaDesc: Two-week yacht route from Trogir south to Dubrovnik and back, calling at Šolta, Hvar, Vis, Lastovo, Mljet and Korčula, with distances and mooring notes.

1.shortDescription: A gentle twelve-mile shakedown south from the Trogir base into Maslinica, the one truly enclosed harbour on Šolta's western tip.
1.description: The first day is short on purpose. We usually clear the Trogir bridge channel by mid-morning, run down the south side of Čiovo, and are tied up in Maslinica before lunch, twelve miles logged and the crew settled in. Maslinica sits at the far western end of Šolta, an S-shaped cut in the coast with a row of seven small islets standing guard across its mouth. Those islets do the real work, killing almost any swell before it reaches the boats inside. You have two choices for the night. Martinis Marchi on the south shore is a proper marina with lazy lines and a baroque castle behind it, or the village quay opposite where you go stern-to on your own anchor for a few euros. Šolta stays quiet while its neighbours fill up. Dinner runs on local olive oil and Dobričić red.
thingsToDo: Step into the Martinis Marchi castle courtyard
Try the Dobričić red at a village konoba
Dinghy to Stipanska islet for a swim
Watch sunset from the outer islet chain
1.mooringTip: South-side Martinis Marchi provides full services and laid lines, so reserve for July and August; across from it, the village quay is stern-to on your own hook for a modest charge. That ring of islets blocks NW, W, SW and S, so only a rare northerly finds you exposed.

2.shortDescription: Twelve gentle miles east over the Brač Channel land you at Milna, the best-protected inlet along the island's western shore.
2.description: Milna has been a harbour since Venetian galleys needed shelter here, and it still feels like a working village rather than a resort. The run over from Šolta is another short one, twelve miles across the channel, so there is no rush to leave. Line up the 17th-century church on the inner quay and follow it in; the bay narrows as you go, with stern-to berthing on your own anchor along the inner half and open anchoring in the outer part. Brač built half of Diocletian's Palace out of its white stone, and the quarries at Pučišća still cut it by hand. The village's other claim is lamb under the peka. If you want it for dinner, order by two in the afternoon, then fill the wait with a swim and a walk out to the little chapel on the northern headland.
thingsToDo: Order the lamb peka by 14:00
Walk to the parish church on the quay
Buy Brač olive oil at the co-op
Dinghy north to Bobovišća for a sunset swim
2.mooringTip: Go stern-to on the inner town quay with your own anchor for a modest fee, with water and power along the central section. The outer bay is free to anchor with a line to the rocks, the standard trick when the afternoon Maestral builds past 15 knots.

3.shortDescription: Fifteen miles southeast into the pine-covered Pakleni Islands, where Palmižana offers the only marina and a string of calm south-side coves.
3.description: The Pakleni chain screens the whole southern approach to Hvar Town, a long line of low, wooded islets with clear water between them. Palmižana sits on the eastern flank of Sv. Klement, the biggest of the group and the only one with a marina. That ACI berth fills fast in high season, so the online booking is worth doing days ahead. The catch with the main bay is the afternoon funnel; once the Maestral settles in around two, it gets rolly, and the smart overnight shifts to the south-side coves at Vinogradišće or Tarsce with a line ashore. Afternoons here have a rhythm. Wander the Meneghello family's botanical garden, snorkel the Roman amphorae off the western rocks, then take dinner at one of the bay's konobas. Hvar Town is a short water-taxi hop if the crew wants a late night.
thingsToDo: Snorkel the Roman amphorae off the west shore
Walk the Meneghello botanical garden
Anchor in Vinogradišće cove with a line ashore
Water-taxi across to Hvar Town at night
3.mooringTip: Book the ACI Palmižana lazy-line berths online well ahead for July and August. If it is full, or the main bay turns rolly in the afternoon westerly, drop into Vinogradišće on the south side and run a line to the rocks for a calmer night.

4.shortDescription: Komiža lies twenty miles southwest — a working fishing town on Vis that still feels like the Adriatic of thirty years back.
4.description: Vis was a closed military zone until 1989, and the decades behind the wire spared it the resort sprawl you see elsewhere. Komiža shows it. The town is fifteen minutes end to end on foot, the fishing fleet still lands its catch on the eastern side of the harbour, and the konobas grill bogueroni and pour the island's own Vugava white. Tie up stern-to on your own anchor in the inner basin, where the sand-and-weed bottom holds well; leave the outer wall alone overnight, since any southwest swell rolls straight in. There is history above the town too. The British SOE and Tito's Partisans ran wartime operations from here, and the walk up Mount Hum to Tito's cave earns you the widest view in central Dalmatia. Time the harbour wall for the open-Adriatic sunset.
thingsToDo: Order grilled bogueroni with a glass of Vugava
Hike Mount Hum to Tito's wartime cave
See the Fishermen's Museum in the Venetian tower
Watch the open-sea sunset from the harbour wall
4.mooringTip: Drop the hook and back into Komiža's inner basin, floor of sand and weed, holding decent, a harbour fee to settle. Skip the outer wall for the night; with a southwesterly over 15 knots forecast, cut 6 miles round into fully enclosed St George Bay at Vis Town.

5.shortDescription: Catch the Blue Cave's morning light on Biševo, dip into the Green Cave at Ravnik, then push eighteen miles east to Vela Luka.
5.description: Everything today keys off the sun. The Blue Cave on Biševo only glows its full electric blue when the light angles through the underwater opening, roughly nine to eleven in the morning, so this is an early start. You cannot take your own dinghy inside; tie to the concession quay, pay at the kiosk, and wait for one of the official tenders. Forty minutes gets you in and out. From there it is a short hop to Ravnik and its Green Cave, lit through a hole in the roof rather than from below, and open to your own tender with far less of a queue. By early afternoon we point east, eighteen miles to Vela Luka at the far end of Korčula. The bay is deep and sheltered, the town has everything, and dinner means žrnovski makaruni rolled by hand in the village kitchens.
thingsToDo: Queue for the Blue Cave tender by 10:30
Take your own dinghy into Ravnik's Green Cave
Order žrnovski makaruni at a Vela Luka konoba
Stroll the Vela Luka waterfront at dusk
5.mooringTip: Vela Luka gives you stern-to on the long town quay with your own anchor, water, power, and a fuel pump at the western end, all for a modest fee. Holding in mud and sand is excellent, and the bay shrugs off everything but a strong southerly over 18 knots.

6.shortDescription: Fourteen miles south to Lastovo, the most remote inhabited island in the Adriatic and the darkest night sky in Croatia.
6.description: Lastovo is a Nature Park and, by most reckonings, the quietest inhabited island in this part of the Adriatic. Zaklopatica is where you overnight, a U-shaped bite in the north coast with a low islet lying across the entrance to break any swell. Five family konobas run the show here, each keeping a line of free buoys for guests who eat ashore. The deal is simple: book a buoy and a table at the same place. Anchoring on the bottom is frowned on because of the seagrass, but the buoy field rarely fills outside the July peak. Kitchens lean on the local lobster and šporki makaruli, a rough-and-ready beef and pasta stew. There is almost nothing to do off the water, which is the whole appeal. On a clear night the Milky Way stands overhead and you can read by starlight in the cockpit.
thingsToDo: Take a konoba's free buoy in trade for dinner
Order Lastovo lobster or šporki makaruli
Walk up to Lastovo Town's chimney stacks
Stay on deck for the midnight Milky Way
6.mooringTip: Free konoba buoys at Zaklopatica, secured by booking dinner ashore, so confirm with the konoba as you arrive; skip anchoring on the bottom over the seagrass. If a southeasterly above 15 knots blows up, slip 4 miles south to Skrivena Luka, the all-weather bolthole.

7.shortDescription: Twenty-two miles northeast to Mljet, where the walled inlet of Polače hides Roman ruins and a walk to the island's saltwater lakes.
7.description: The run to Mljet is longer, twenty-two miles, and the payoff comes at the end. Polače is a deep, walled inlet on the north coast that stays hidden until you are almost inside, then opens into one of the best-protected anchorages in the south. This is the western gateway to Mljet National Park. The walls and corner tower of a 4th-century Roman complex stand right on the shore, free to wander through. Pick up one of the green Park buoys and pay the ranger on the waterfront, which covers crew entry to the Park, or take a stern-to slot at the village pontoon. From here a half-hour walk brings you to Veliko Jezero, the larger saltwater lake, where a small ferry runs out to the Benedictine monastery on its islet. The full loop of both lakes is about nine kilometres, swimming allowed, cicadas for company.
thingsToDo: Walk through the 4th-century Roman ruins ashore
Ferry to St Mary's monastery on Veliko Jezero
Hike the 9 km loop around both lakes
Swim in the cooler saltwater lakes
7.mooringTip: Polače runs on paid Park buoys, around €40 a night including crew entry to the National Park, paid to the ranger on arrival. It is among the most sheltered overnights on the whole south coast, fully protected from N through SE, and the village pontoon also takes a few stern-to boats.

8.shortDescription: Twenty-four miles southeast to Dubrovnik, berthing up the Rijeka Dubrovačka at Komolac, with the Old City a short bus ride away.
8.description: Dubrovnik is the turning point of the trip, though you sleep well inland of the famous walls. Every charter boat overnights at ACI Marina Dubrovnik in Komolac, eight kilometres up the fjord-like Rijeka Dubrovačka. The marina has lazy lines, fuel, power and water on every berth, and a chandler if you need one. The Old Port on the seafront looks the part but takes daytime visitors only; it sits open to the south and stays jammed with tour tenders all season. From the marina, Bus 1A or a taxi drops you at the Pile Gate in about fifteen minutes. Climbing the walls is non-negotiable: the full circuit runs just under two kilometres and takes about ninety minutes with stops. Come down into the Stradun for dinner in the lanes off Prijeko, or ride the cable car up Mount Srđ for the rooftops at dusk.
thingsToDo: Walk the full 1.94 km city wall circuit
Ride the cable car up Mount Srđ
Eat down a Prijeko side lane off the Stradun
Drink at Buža bar on the outer rocks
8.mooringTip: ACI Marina Dubrovnik at Komolac is the only sensible overnight, so book ahead in season; it has lazy lines and full services. Treat the Old Port as a daytime tender stop only, and allow half an hour door to door to the Pile Gate by Bus 1A or taxi.

9.shortDescription: Twenty miles back to Mljet's quiet southeast coast and Okuklje, a tiny buoy-field bay with three konobas and no town at all.
9.description: After the crowds of Dubrovnik, Okuklje is the antidote, and that is exactly why we point back this way. Twenty miles northwest brings you to a tight U-shaped bay on Mljet's southeast coast that holds maybe twenty boats. There is no town, no day-tripper traffic, and no Park fee, since Okuklje falls outside the National Park line. Three family konobas hang their own free buoys for guests. Pick one up, dinghy in, choose a kitchen, and the dinner booking locks the buoy. Black risotto with cuttlefish ink and grilled bream are the reliable orders. Hills of a hundred metres wall the bay on three sides, so the wind dies at sunset whatever it is doing outside, and the night goes silent bar the sea on the entrance rocks. Swim before lunch, then walk the headland path for the view down to Korčula.
thingsToDo: Take a free konoba buoy and book dinner
Order black risotto with cuttlefish ink
Walk the headland path toward Korčula
Swim off the boat in flat water
9.mooringTip: Free konoba buoys at Okuklje, locked in by booking dinner ashore, so confirm on arrival. Bottom anchoring is possible but holding is patchy in the seagrass; the bay is well protected N, E and S, exposed only to a strong northwesterly over 18 knots.

10.shortDescription: Fifteen miles along the Pelješac Channel bring you to Korčula Town, a medieval wall-ringed port laid out on the fishbone street plan the island is known for.
10.description: Korčula Town shows itself from miles out — a small headland of red rooftops under a single bell tower, limestone walls falling sheer to the sea on three faces. Coming up the Pelješac Channel makes a fine approach. On the peninsula's east flank sits ACI Marina Korčula, full services and laid lines, while a few stern-to boats squeeze onto the western town quay under their own anchor, nearer the gates but exposed to the afternoon Maestral, rolly once it funnels through near two o'clock. You cross the old town in ten minutes, its herringbone lanes deliberately angled to let the summer breeze through while breaking the winter bora. Climb St Mark's bell tower for the view down the channel, look in on the contested Marco Polo house, and time a Moreška sword dance for Monday or Thursday.
thingsToDo: Climb St Mark's bell tower for the channel view
Wander the old town after the day ferries leave
See a Moreška sword dance (Mon or Thu)
Order fish brodet in a back-lane courtyard
10.mooringTip: The eastern-side ACI Marina Korčula takes any weather, with full services and laid lines. Its western town quay sits nearer the gates but catches the Maestral and rolls once past 14:00; when the channel gets lumpy, Lumbarda Bay waits 3 miles southeast.

11.shortDescription: Twenty-three miles back across the Hvar Channel to Jelsa, the calm north-coast alternative to Hvar Town, ringed by lavender and old vineyards.
11.description: Jelsa is the third town of Hvar, set on the north coast, and it carries maybe a quarter of the noise of Hvar Town over the ridge. The bay is wide and well shut off from southerly weather. Go stern-to on the town quay with your own anchor over good mud and sand, or take a lazy-line berth in the small marina on the east side if you want shore power. This is the agricultural heart of the island. The lavender belt still yields commercially each July along the road to Vrboska and Stari Grad, and the back-lane konobas pour the local Bogdanjuša white. Two good afternoons present themselves: bike the three miles of coast road to Vrboska with its bridged canals, or walk inland across the Stari Grad Plain, a UNESCO field system the Greeks laid out and nobody has stopped farming since.
thingsToDo: Bike the coast road to Vrboska's canals
Walk the UNESCO Stari Grad Plain
Order Bogdanjuša white at a back-lane konoba
Climb to the Tor watchtower above town
11.mooringTip: Back onto the Jelsa town quay on your own hook for a small fee; water and power run the central section and the mud-and-sand holding is solid. The bay screens you across S, SE and SW, while the little east-side marina and its laid lines make the all-weather backstop.

12.shortDescription: Ten miles north to Lučice, a chain of uninhabited coves under Brač's white cliffs with the clearest swimming water of the trip.
12.description: Today belongs to the water, pure and simple. A ten-mile hop north over the Brač Channel sets you on the island's southern shore at Lučice, a chain of joined-up coves a little east of Pučišća. Nobody lives out on the headland — just white limestone, cliff and pine, and two seasonal beach restaurants as the only buildings, each dropping free buoys for guests who dine ashore. Grab a buoy in the deepest inlet, roll over the side, and the day takes care of itself. The pale Brač stone floors the seabed, so a calm summer's day gives you twenty metres of clarity. Kitchens stay uncomplicated: grilled fish, a glass of the local Plavac Mali. No shop, no bar, no late-night anything — and that is the whole appeal. If every buoy is gone, sand and weed hold well in eight to twelve metres.
thingsToDo: Take a free restaurant buoy in the central cove
Snorkel the pale limestone seabed at 20 m
Order grilled fish and Plavac Mali ashore
Dinghy west to Pučišća's stone-mason yards
12.mooringTip: Free restaurant buoys at Lučice, held for you by eating ashore, so confirm on arrival; otherwise anchor in 8 to 12 metres of sand and weed. The coves shelter you from N, NE and E but open to the south; if a southerly over 15 knots blows, duck 1 mile west into the enclosed Pučišća harbour.

13.shortDescription: Fifteen miles northwest to Krknjaši, a glassy sand-bottomed swim anchorage between the Drvenik islands, a short hop from the Trogir base.
13.description: The last night at anchor is a soft landing. Fifteen miles northwest from Lučice drops you into Krknjaši, the channel between Veli and Mali Drvenik, two uninhabited islands four miles short of the Trogir base. Krknjaši is a swim anchorage pure and simple. The water is shallow, the floor is pale sand at three to six metres, and it turns proper turquoise when the day is calm. Holding is excellent, so anchoring is the usual choice and it costs nothing, though three restaurants on Veli Drvenik lay free buoys for diners. There is a small chapel on the eastern shore, a half-hour footpath around the headland, and no other infrastructure to speak of. Stopping here on the penultimate day is deliberate: the Saturday return is only four miles east, so the morning stays unhurried and the boat rolls into base with the right fuel and no stress.
thingsToDo: Anchor on the sand in 4-6 m and swim
Walk the 30-minute headland loop on Veli Drvenik
Order grilled fish at a Veli Drvenik restaurant
Take in the open-sea sunset from the deck
13.mooringTip: Anchor in 4 to 6 metres on excellent sand holding, the standard choice in this shallow, forgiving bay; free restaurant buoys are there too if you dine ashore. It shelters you from most directions and opens only to the W and NW, so a strong westerly means running the last 4 miles into ACI Trogir early.

14.shortDescription: The short four-mile run back into ACI Trogir for the Saturday handover, with time for a final swim and a walk through the old town.
14.description: Shortest hop of the two weeks closes the loop — the four-mile skip east from Krknjaši to ACI Marina Trogir. The Saturday hand-back keeps to a schedule, so be alongside by nine: fuel topped, tanks pumped, decks hosed, inventory checked, and the skipper aboard for the eight-to-nine inspection. The marina stands on Čiovo, a channel's width from Trogir's old town. Once signed off, five minutes over the footbridge drop you in a UNESCO core good for an hour — St Lawrence's Cathedral, with its 13th-century Radovan portal, tops the list, and the Kamerlengo fortress anchors the western tip. Split's airport is a ten-minute drive north. Close the trip properly with a leisurely Riva lunch, capping a fortnight as rounded as any you can sail from an Adriatic base.
thingsToDo: Top up fuel and pump out before 09:00
See the Cathedral's 13th-century Radovan portal
Climb the Kamerlengo fortress on Čiovo
Take a long lunch on Trogir's Riva
14.mooringTip: Return into ACI Marina Trogir as your charter contract sets out; the base manager assigns the slot and the handover window runs 08:00 to 09:00. Arrive by 17:00 on Friday if your contract calls for a night-before return, and note the fuel pontoon is on the western entrance side.

trogir-kornati-krka-route.metaDesc: A week's sail out of Trogir: north into Kornati's islands, upriver to the Krka falls at Skradin, home past Primošten, Zlarin and Rogoznica. Legs in NM.

1.shortDescription: A short four-mile hop west into the shallow lagoon between the two Drvenik islands, chosen so the crew settles in before the real miles begin.
1.description: Nobody books a week in Dalmatia to spend the first afternoon at the marina, so we clear ACI Trogir early and point west. Four miles later the boat slides into Krknjaši, the pale-sand lagoon locals call the Blue Lagoon, lying in the channel between Veli Drvenik and little Mali Drvenik. The bottom here is sand and weed at three to six metres, so the hook bites first time and the water goes that improbable milky turquoise by mid-morning. No road reaches the three tavernas along the Veli Drvenik shore; you arrive by tender, and each one drops a few free buoys for anyone booking a table. There is little to do beyond swim, check the rig in light air, and eat grilled fish with your feet nearly in the water. That is the whole point. Day one is about shaking down the boat, not chasing distance.
thingsToDo: Drop the hook onto sand in four to six metres
Swim the shallow Blue Lagoon at Krknjaši
Dinner ashore at a Veli Drvenik taverna
Walk out to the point on Veli Drvenik
Practise reefs and sail trim while it's calm
1.mooringTip: Sand holding is excellent at three to six metres, so anchoring is the usual call; the taverna buoys are free if you eat ashore. The lagoon covers everything but west and northwest, so if a fresh westerly is forecast above 18 knots, drop back the four miles to ACI Trogir for the night.

2.shortDescription: Fourteen miles up the open coast to Primošten, a terracotta town crowded onto its own peninsula, with the Babić vineyards climbing the hills behind.
2.description: You spot Primošten a good while before you reach it. The old town crams onto a knob of rock that only a low sandbar keeps joined to the coast, houses stacked right down to the waterline and the church of St. George pinning the top. Fourteen miles from the Drveniks, past Rogoznica which you will visit later in the week, and it earns its reputation up close: no resort gloss, just tight lanes and a handful of konobas doing brudet and grilled fish. The town quay is daytime only, so we take a berth at ACI Kremik two miles south and ride the shuttle in for the evening. Behind the town, the Bukovac terraces wall in the native Babić grape, dry stone stacked by hand over generations. Sit on the sea wall with a glass of the Babić as the light goes flat. It is worth the stop.
thingsToDo: Climb to the St. George church up top
Nurse a glass of Babić on the sea wall
Walk the hand-stacked Bukovac wine terraces
Find brudet down a Primošten side lane
Ride the Kremik shuttle in for the evening
2.mooringTip: ACI Kremik, two miles south of the centre, is the dependable overnight berth with lazy lines and a shuttle into town; the town quay itself takes daytime visitors only and sits open to the west. If you do lie on the quay in settled weather, clear it by five so the fishing boats have their space back at dusk.

3.shortDescription: Twenty miles northwest into Kornati National Park: eighty-nine bare islands and one solitary marina, Piškera, out on the islet of Panitula.
3.description: This is the leg crews remember. Twenty miles northwest and the coast just falls away into Kornati: eighty-nine islands of bare grey karst, dry-stone walls climbing their backs, not a soul living out here, and a no-take zone that has the shallows crawling with life again. We aim for ACI Piškera, perched on a scrap of an islet called Panitula Vela at the park's western edge, the only pontoon berth anywhere within the park lines. There are a hundred and fifty berths here, all on lazy lines with water and power, and a single konoba sat on the ledge above the boats. Buy your park ticket online before you arrive; it costs less than at the kiosk, and in high summer the day rate for a charter yacht runs near a hundred euros. Come afternoon, drop the tender and go: snorkel the reef in the gap between Piškera and Lavsa, or push south to the outer crowns where Mana and Kornat drop a sheer eighty metres under the keel.
thingsToDo: Sort the park ticket online before you get there
Snorkel the shallows off Lavsa in the tender
Sail below the sheer south face of Mana
Eat at the lone konoba above the pontoons
Walk the stony spine of Panitula Vela
3.mooringTip: Every berth at ACI Piškera has lazy lines; book online for July and August because it fills. Overnight anchoring inside the park is limited to a few licensed bays, Lavsa, Lojena, Vrulje, Stiniva and Statival, where the konobas usually lay free buoys for their dinner guests.

4.shortDescription: Fifteen miles east into the Šibenik islands to Zlarin, a no-car island whose divers have worked the red-coral beds for seven hundred years.
4.description: Kornati drops off the stern and it's fifteen miles east to the Šibenik islands, where Zlarin sits with its one village strung along the east coast and not a car on the whole rock. Coral made this place. For the best part of seven centuries the men here dove the red coral off the beds offshore, and the little museum on the square still keeps the tools of it — the foot-stones, the weighted lines, the timber cage-frames they lowered before anyone owned a tank. You lie stern-to off the village quay for pocket change, water and power on the inner berths. No cars means the inland lanes are yours on foot; half an hour uphill through pine and cypress puts you on the west slope, looking back down the channel. The konobas cook whatever the boats landed that morning, and once the plates are cleared the sea wall is where the village ends up, which by the fourth night suits most crews fine.
thingsToDo: Duck into the red-coral museum off the square
Climb through the pine and cypress to the west slope
Swim off the flat rocks below the village
Eat the morning's catch at a quay konoba
Watch coral still cut by hand in a workshop
4.mooringTip: Take a stern-to berth on the village quay with your own anchor; the fee is modest and the central berths carry water and power. Zlarin shelters you from the west and north but stands open to a southeasterly, so if the sirocco is forecast over 15 knots, run the four miles up into Šibenik and lie in the estuary instead.

5.shortDescription: A rare twelve miles run inland: up the Krka past Šibenik, through the tight St. Anthony cut, to tie up at Skradin under Krka's falls.
5.description: Not much else on this coast sails like the fifth day, because you spend it going the wrong way, away from the open sea and straight up a river. You clear Šibenik's old town, then the channel pinches down to the St. Anthony narrows: six hundred metres of blasted rock with the bridge forty-nine metres up, so no charter mast comes close to fouling. Past the cut the salt thins out, hills crowd both banks, and the river runs deep and green to the pontoons at ACI Skradin. A park boat leaves the marina every half hour in season, its fare already inside your entry ticket, and runs you up to Skradinski Buk, where the water steps down forty-six metres across seventeen limestone lips and a boardwalk threads along both banks. Ashore, the thing to order is Skradin risotto, beef and saffron cooked right down over the better part of a day, with a jug of the local Babić. Catch an early boat up; by noon the walkways are shoulder to shoulder.
thingsToDo: Boat upriver to the Skradinski Buk cascades
Follow the boardwalk around both sides of the falls
Eat Skradin risotto, saffron beef cooked all day
Poke through the old stone lanes above the quay
Hike to the viewpoint over the top of the falls
5.mooringTip: ACI Skradin runs lazy lines on every berth with full services and a fuel pontoon at the entrance. The river channel is well-buoyed and deep for any draft; give yourself about ninety minutes at displacement speed from the Šibenik river mouth, and note there is no anchoring in the park stretch, so it is the marina or nothing.

6.shortDescription: Twenty miles back down to the sea and southeast to Rogoznica, a headland town sitting over the strange Dragon's Eye salt lake, with the huge Marina Frapa for the night.
6.description: Off the river and back onto salt water, you make twenty miles southeast to Rogoznica, a town spread over a stubby headland about midway from Šibenik down to Split. What people come to see is Zmajevo Oko, the Dragon's Eye: a saltwater pool sunk into the limestone at the point's southern end. The sea feeds it through a channel nobody can see, wringing the water in and out on the tide, so at low water the surface lies a good six metres under the sea just beyond the rim. Twenty minutes on foot from town puts you up on the cliffs looking down into it. Frapa, on the peninsula's south side, is about as big as marinas get in this part of Dalmatia, and it has the lot: lazy lines, fuel, a hotel, restaurants along the quay. A small boat can take the north village quay instead, but it sits wide open to the maestral, so most skippers just pay for the marina and sleep easy.
thingsToDo: Find the path out to Zmajevo Oko
Stand on the rim and watch the tide work the pool
Order brudet in a konoba on the point
Wander the handful of old lanes by the harbour
Run the tender over to Smokvica for a swim
6.mooringTip: Marina Frapa on the south side is the all-weather choice, with lazy lines, full services, a fuel dock at the entrance and restaurants on site. The north village quay takes stern-to with your own anchor for a small fee but lies open to the northwest, so move round to the marina if the maestral is forecast over 15 knots.

7.shortDescription: Eighteen miles southeast to bring the boat home to the Trogir and Split bases for the nine o'clock Saturday handover, with one more swim off Čiovo before you dock.
7.description: Last morning, and the clock matters. It's eighteen miles southeast to run the boat home into the Trogir and Split bases, and Saturday means a hard deadline: alongside by nine, tanks topped and pumped out, decks hosed, gear checked back in, and someone aboard for the walk-round between eight and nine. Whichever of the three your paperwork names, ACI Trogir, Marina Kaštela or ACI Split in town, the way in is easy to read in any weather. You'll track the south shore of Čiovo the whole run, and it's dotted with small bays, so drop the hook off Slatine or Saldun for a last swim before you commit to the marina. Once the inspection's signed off and the bags are ashore, ACI Trogir leaves you five minutes from the old town, where St. Lawrence's cathedral still holds the carved portal Radovan cut in the thirteenth century. It's worth the half hour you have before the airport car comes.
thingsToDo: Anchor off Čiovo for one final swim
Fuel up and empty the holding tank early
Stand under Radovan's carved portal at the cathedral
Go up the Kamerlengo fortress at the town's edge
Take a slow lunch on the Trogir waterfront
7.mooringTip: Return to the base named on your charter contract, whether that is ACI Trogir, Marina Kaštela or ACI Split, and berth stern-to or alongside as the base manager directs. The handover window is eight to nine on Saturday; if your contract asks for a night-before return, be in by five on Friday.

trogir-vis-korcula-hvar-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Trogir to Vis, Hvar & Korčula | Boat4You
trogir-vis-korcula-hvar-route.metaDesc: Round trip from ACI Trogir out to Vis and the Blue Cave, then Korčula, Hvar and Stari Grad. Seven days of distances, marinas and honest mooring notes.

1.shortDescription: A short 12-mile hop from ACI Trogir to the one natural harbour on Šolta, walled in by seven small islets.
1.description: Clear the Trogir bridge channel, run down the south side of Čiovo, and Šolta opens up ahead within a couple of hours. Maslinica sits on the island's western tip, the only harbour here that holds you snug in almost any weather, thanks to the little chain of islets guarding the mouth. We usually split the crew's vote between two options: Martinis Marchi on the south shore, where lazy lines and a converted baroque castle wait, or the village quay opposite, stern-to on your own hook. Šolta stays quiet while Split fills up an hour away. No day-boat crowds, no resort strip, just konobas pouring the local Dobričić red and pressing their own olive oil. Late afternoon, dinghy over to Stipanska for a swim, then find a table ashore before the light goes soft over the water.
thingsToDo: Swim off the rocks at Stipanska islet
Try Šolta's indigenous Dobričić red
Tour the Martinis Marchi castle grounds
Buy cold-pressed Šoltansko olive oil ashore
Catch the sunset from Polebrnjak islet
1.mooringTip: Two choices here: Martinis Marchi on the south side has lazy lines and full services but books out fast in July and August, while the village quay opposite takes you stern-to on your own anchor for a small fee. The islet chain shuts out swell from every direction bar a northerly, which almost never blows in high summer.

2.shortDescription: The week's longest open leg, 24 miles south past Brač into Komiža, a proper working fishing port on the far side of Vis.
2.description: Twenty-four miles is a decent day's sail, and the Hvar Channel swings open on your port quarter as you leave Brač astern. Vis was off-limits to visitors until 1989, a Yugoslav garrison island, and it wears that isolation well: fewer hotels, more vineyards, a slower pulse than anything closer to the mainland. Komiža curls around a small bay on the western coast, its fishing fleet drawn up along the eastern arm. Tie inside the basin, stern-to on your own anchor over sand and weed, where the holding is generally sound. Skip the outer wall overnight; any southwesterly rolls straight in. Ashore, the old Venetian tower holds a small fishing museum, the konobas grill the day's catch, and the Vugava white pours cold. Come sunset the western horizon runs unbroken toward Italy, and it earns its reputation.
thingsToDo: Grill-fresh sardines and a glass of Vugava
Browse the fishing museum in the Venetian tower
Climb Mount Hum for the island-wide panorama
Wander the fishing fleet on the east quay
Watch the sun drop toward Italy from the wall
2.mooringTip: Go stern-to with your own anchor in the inner basin, over sand and weed that mostly holds well; expect a harbour fee. If a southwesterly above 15 knots is forecast, the outer wall is untenable overnight, so run the six miles up to Vis Town in St George's Bay for full shelter.

3.shortDescription: Blue Cave as it opens, a midday swim off Budihovac, then eighteen miles east to reach Vela Luka at Korčula's far tip.
3.description: Get to Biševo early. The cave only glows that electric blue when the low sun angles through the underwater opening, roughly nine to eleven in the morning, so beat the tour crowd off Komiža and you queue less. Private dinghies stay out; you tie at the little concession quay and take one of the official tenders in for a twenty-minute look. From there it is a short hop to Budihovac, a pair of low islets off the south coast of Vis with water so clear the anchor chain hangs like glass in eight metres of sand. Swim, eat aboard, then point the bow east. Eighteen miles on lies Vela Luka, a long west-facing inlet at the end of Korčula with fuel, water and real shelter. Dinner is žrnovski makaruni, the hand-rolled pasta they form on knitting needles.
thingsToDo: See the Blue Cave glow around 10am
Anchor for a swim off Budihovac islets
Eat žrnovski makaruni, Korčula's hand-rolled pasta
Top up fuel at the Vela Luka pump
Stroll the Vela Luka waterfront after dark
3.mooringTip: The Blue Cave concession quay is lunchtime only, fee paid at the kiosk, no overnight stops. Vela Luka takes you stern-to on the long town quay over hard-holding mud and sand, fuel at the western end, and only a strong southerly ever pushes swell into the basin.

4.shortDescription: The big passage: 32 miles east-northeast into Hvar Town, the noisiest, most photographed harbour on the coast, with ACI Marina the safe bet.
4.description: Leave early. Thirty-two miles across two channels is the longest single run of the week, and the Maestral typically fills from the west around two in the afternoon. Clear the Korčula Channel before it builds and you sail the rest on a comfortable beam reach into Hvar. The town announces itself from a distance: the Fortica fortress crowning the hill, the long stone arcade of the Arsenal on the waterfront, the bell tower of St Stephen's above the square. Take a berth at ACI Marina Hvar on the west side of the bay; for anything larger than a light-air night it is the only sensible option, with lazy lines and services laid on. The riva will tempt you, but it packs out with tour boats and tenders by noon and offers no cover from the afternoon wind. Berth, walk in, climb the fort before the heat, then lose an evening in the back-lane konobas.
thingsToDo: Climb the Fortica for the Pakleni view
Walk the Arsenal and St Stephen's square
Find a quiet konoba off the main square
Dinghy to the Pakleni islands for a swim
People-watch on the riva after dark
4.mooringTip: Book ACI Marina Hvar ahead in season; in any real wind it is the only safe overnight spot for a mid-size yacht, and it comes with lazy lines, water and power. The town riva charges by the hour, sits exposed to west gusts and fills with tenders, so treat it as a daytime stop only.

5.shortDescription: A gentle 14 miles round Hvar's western cape into Stari Grad, Croatia's oldest town and gateway to the UNESCO Greek field plain.
5.description: An easy morning. Fourteen miles takes you clockwise around the western cape and deep into the long bay that shelters Stari Grad on Hvar's north side. The Greeks landed here in 384 BC and called it Pharos; their street grid still runs through the old quarter, and the plain behind town, the Stari Grad Plain, has been farmed on the same divisions ever since, which is why UNESCO listed it. Mooring is easy either way: the town quay along the western shore takes you stern-to on your own anchor for a fee, or the small ACI marina on the eastern side gives you lazy lines and shore power. The bay runs like a fjord and stays flat in any wind, so this is a rare night you can relax about the forecast. Walk to Tvrdalj, the poet Hektorović's fortified house with its stone fishpond, and eat peka out on the plain if you book ahead.
thingsToDo: Walk the Greek grid in the old quarter
See Tvrdalj Castle and its stone fishpond
Cycle out across the Stari Grad Plain
Book peka at a plain-side konoba
Ride the lavender road east of town
5.mooringTip: Either mooring works: stern-to on the town quay along the western shore for a modest fee with water and power on the central stretch, or lazy lines at ACI Marina Stari Grad opposite. The bay is fjord-shaped and holds calm in every wind direction, making it one of the surest overnight anchorages on Hvar.

6.shortDescription: 12 miles north over the Brač Channel to Milna, the best-protected harbour on the island, and a short final morning back to Trogir.
6.description: Milna lies a dozen miles north, a narrow inlet cutting into the western side of Brač that has sheltered boats since Venetian days. The mouth is easy to read: the tall parish church on the inner quay, grey stone houses stacked up the slope, the basin narrowing to a pocket of still water at the head. Tie stern-to on the inner town quay for a small fee, water and power laid on, or drop the hook out in the wider bay in six to ten metres of sand and run a line to the rocks if the Maestral pipes up. Brač built half of Dalmatia from its white limestone, the same stone cut for Diocletian's Palace and still quarried up at Pučišća. Order lamb under the peka the moment you're tied off; the coals need three hours.
thingsToDo: Order lamb peka the moment you moor
Anchor out with a stern line ashore
See the 17th-century parish church
Sample Brač olive oil at the co-op
Dinghy north to Bobovišća for a sunset swim
6.mooringTip: Stern-to on the inner town quay gives you a small fee plus water and power on the central section; the outer bay is free anchoring with a stern line to the rocks. Milna opens to the west and southwest, so if the afternoon Maestral gets up above 15 knots the inner quay stays calmer, and Bobovišća three miles north is the all-weather fallback.

7.shortDescription: A final 15 miles northwest across two channels, one last swim off Čiovo, then into ACI Trogir for the Saturday handover by nine.
7.description: One last run northwest closes the loop, fifteen miles back across the Brač and Split Channels to ACI Marina Trogir. Saturday handovers run tight, so aim to be alongside by nine with the tanks pumped, fuel topped and decks rinsed, and the skipper on hand for the checkout between eight and nine. There is still time for a last swim: the southern shore of Čiovo is dotted with small bays, and Slatine or Saldun make an obvious final dip before you turn into the marina. Once the inspection clears, Trogir's old town is a five-minute walk over the bridge. Head for the Cathedral of St Lawrence and stand under the Radovan portal, the finest piece of medieval carving in the country, or climb the Kamerlengo tower for the view. Split airport is ten minutes up the road, and a long lunch on the Riva is the right way to end the week.
thingsToDo: Swim a last bay off Slatine or Saldun
Stand under the Cathedral's Radovan portal
Climb the Kamerlengo fortress for the view
Pump out and fuel before the checkout
Take a long lunch on Trogir's Riva
7.mooringTip: Bring her back into ACI Marina Trogir as your contract sets out; the base manager assigns the berth on arrival. The Saturday checkout window is eight to nine, so if your paperwork calls for a night-before return, be alongside by five on Friday, and the fuel pontoon sits on the western entrance side.

zadar-kornati-hvar-korcula-vis-skradin-1.metaTitle: 14-Day Kornati, Hvar & Vis from Zadar | Boat4You
zadar-kornati-hvar-korcula-vis-skradin-1.metaDesc: Sail 14 days round-trip from Zadar through the Kornati islands, Hvar, Korčula, Vis and Krka at Skradin, with anchorage and konoba tips per leg.

1.shortDescription: A 25-mile run south from Zadar drops you among the empty outer Kornati, with Žut's quiet coves and a handful of seasonal konobe.
1.description: Provision well in Zadar before you slip the lines, because Žut has no shop and nobody lives here through the winter. The passage south is straightforward in the usual summer maestral, roughly four hours of easy reaching. Žut is the biggest of the outer Kornati, a long spine of bare limestone cut by sheltered inlets on its eastern side. We usually pick a cove with a restaurant buoy and settle in early; by mid-afternoon the popular ones fill up. Swim off the stern into water that stays clear over the sand, then take the goat track up the ridge for the view back toward Dugi Otok. Dinner ashore is the reward. Order the brudet, a slow Dalmatian fish stew mopped up with polenta, and let the evening go quiet around you.
thingsToDo: Anchor in a sheltered Žut inlet
Walk the ridge track for Kornati views
Snorkel the sandy shallows off the stern
Brudet fish stew at a seasonal konoba
1.mooringTip: Take a restaurant buoy in one of the occupied coves and dinner ashore covers the mooring; otherwise anchor further out on sand in 7-10 m. Arrive by early afternoon in July and August, as the buoy fields off the konobe fill fast.

2.shortDescription: A short hop north to Dugi Otok's wooded top end, anchoring under the Veli Rat lighthouse with the Sakarun sands a walk away.
2.description: This is a gentle day, mostly motoring or a light reach up the outside of Dugi Otok. Pantera is a broad, pine-fringed bay near the island's northwest tip, well protected from the north and northeast, which makes it a calm overnight when the bura is not blowing hard. The headline ashore is the Veli Rat lighthouse, a 40-metre limestone tower from 1849 and one of the tallest on the Adriatic; local lore says egg whites went into the render to keep it white. Land the tender and stroll through the pines to Sakarun, a rare shallow bay with a genuine sand bottom that turns the water pale green. Back aboard, grill whatever you bought in Žut and watch the light drop behind the lighthouse.
thingsToDo: Climb the 1849 Veli Rat lighthouse
Walk through pines to Sakarun beach
Snorkel the caves along the rocky shore
Grilled squid on the aft deck
2.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 5-8 m; Pantera holds well and shelters you from the north and northeast. There are no laid buoys here, so give yourself swinging room and check the set before you leave the helm.

3.shortDescription: An easy 12 miles into the core of Kornati National Park, where Vrulja Bay lines up the archipelago's biggest cluster of konobe.
3.description: Buy your park ticket before you enter if you can; the wardens board inside the boundary and it is cheaper bought ahead. The sail down through the inner islands is the reason people come to Kornati, a maze of bare grey humps stitched with dry-stone walls that climb straight out of the sea. Vrulja sits on the sheltered flank of Kornat, the main island, and it holds three konobe side by side, each with its own buoys. Take one, then walk out to the seaward cliffs where the walls run right to the drop. Strong walkers can push up Metlina, at 237 metres the high point of the ridge, for a view over the whole park. Fish grilled on the buoy that night tastes better for the effort.
thingsToDo: Hike up Metlina for park views
Walk the dry-stone walls of Kornat
Snorkel the clear water off the buoys
Fresh grilled fish at a Vrulja konoba
3.mooringTip: Vrulja has three restaurant konobe with their own buoy fields; take a buoy and dinner ashore covers it. Book the buoy by VHF or phone on a busy August evening, as this is the most popular stop in the park.

4.shortDescription: Twenty miles south past Šibenik to Primošten, a stone town on its own headland ringed by the Bucavac vineyards and their Babić red.
4.description: Leaving the park, the water opens up and you get a proper sail, twenty miles down the coast with the maestral usually on the beam by midday. Primošten stands on a rounded headland that was once an island, its old town packed tight around the hilltop church of Sveti Juraj. Above and behind the town spread the Bucavac vineyards, a lattice of dry-stone plots that has drawn photographers for decades; this is where the dense Babić red is grown. Berth at Marina Kremik a mile and a half south, then taxi or walk in for the evening. Find a table in the old lanes, order the Babić by the carafe, and pair it with black risotto or fresh-caught fish.
thingsToDo: Walk up to Sveti Juraj church
See the Bucavac dry-stone vineyards
Taste the local Babić red
Black risotto in the old-town lanes
4.mooringTip: Marina Kremik, about 1.5 miles south of town, is the standard overnight berth and worth pre-booking in July and August. The town quay is exposed and best kept for a lunch stop only.

5.shortDescription: A calm 14 miles southeast to Trogir, a walled island town of Venetian stone with the Radovan portal on its cathedral front.
5.description: Short and simple today, so there is no rush to leave. Trogir sits on a small island wedged between the mainland and Čiovo, joined to both by bridges, and the whole medieval core is on the UNESCO list.
