The set-piece is the Cathedral of Saint Lawrence, and above its main door is the Radovan portal, carved in 1240 and covered in figures worth a slow look. Climb the Kamerlengo, the squat Venetian fort on the point, for a view over the channel and your own masthead. When the heat drops, the riva along the water fills with people; join them for an ice cream and a slow lap of the stone lanes. It is an easy town to like after days on anchor.
thingsToDo: See the 1240 Radovan portal
Climb Kamerlengo fortress for channel views
Wander the medieval stone lanes
Ice cream on the Trogir riva
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Trogir on the Čiovo side is the reliable pre-booked berth. The town quay along the riva is short-stay and gets swell from passing traffic, so treat it as a daytime visit.

6.shortDescription: South past Brač and Šolta to Hvar town, with its Spanish fortress above the square and the Pakleni Islands just across the water.
6.description: Set off by mid-morning to make the crossing before the afternoon breeze pipes up in the Hvar channel. Hvar town is the busiest, most dressed-up port on the coast, built around the largest open square in Dalmatia with the sixteenth-century Fortica fortress standing over it. The walk up to the fort is steep but quick, and the view over the harbour and the Pakleni Islands at sunset earns its reputation. Down in town, Saint Stephen's cathedral closes the square and the lanes behind it hide small wine bars. If the marina is full, and in summer it often is, take a buoy or berth across at Palmižana on the Pakleni. Cross back for a drink at the Hula-Hula bar and let the night run late.
thingsToDo: Climb to the Fortica for sunset
See Saint Stephen's cathedral and square
Day-anchor off the Pakleni Islands
Sundowners at the Hula-Hula bar
6.mooringTip: The Hvar town quay is first-come and chaotic in season, so plan for ACI Marina Palmižana on Sveti Klement, about 2 miles across in the Pakleni, as your safe pre-booked fallback.

7.shortDescription: A longer 35-mile leg southeast to walled Korčula, all fishbone lanes, a Marco Polo story and glasses of local Grk white.
7.description: This is the longest passage of the trip, so get an early start and top up fuel and water before you go. With luck the maestral fills in from the northwest and carries you most of the way on a reach. Korčula town sits on a small oval peninsula, its streets laid out in a herringbone so the summer sirocco is funnelled through without turning the alleys into wind tunnels. The town trades hard on being Marco Polo's birthplace, and you can visit the house that claims him. Climb the bell tower of Saint Mark's for the rooftops and the channel beyond. In summer the moreška, a clashing sword dance with real blades, is still performed by locals. Finish with a glass of Grk, the dry white grown on the sandy flats at nearby Lumbarda.
thingsToDo: Climb Saint Mark's cathedral bell tower
Visit the Marco Polo house
Watch the moreška sword dance
Taste Grk white from Lumbarda
7.mooringTip: ACI Marina Korčula is small and books out in high summer, so reserve ahead. The moorings along the old-town wall are an atmospheric backup but exposed to passing wash, so set fenders and a stern line carefully.

8.shortDescription: South to Lastovo, Croatia's most far-flung island, dropping the hook in Skrivena Luka, one of the best-sheltered natural harbours on the coast.
8.description: Lastovo takes effort to reach, which is exactly why it stays quiet. The island is a protected nature park, thickly wooded, with a scatter of stone villages and far fewer boats than Hvar or Korčula. Skrivena Luka, the Hidden Harbour, bites deep into the south coast and wraps around almost completely, so it holds you calm even when it blows outside. Above it stands the old Struga lighthouse, a good walk with a long view toward the open sea and the islet of Sušac. There is not much to do here beyond swim, walk and eat, and that is the point. A couple of konobe run buoys in the bay; take one, order whatever came off the boats that day, and turn in early under a sky with no light pollution at all.
thingsToDo: Walk up to the Struga lighthouse
Swim the sheltered Skrivena Luka inlet
Fresh fish at a bay konoba
Stargaze over the dark nature park
8.mooringTip: Buy the Lastovo Nature Park ticket, then take one of the restaurant buoys deep inside Skrivena Luka where dinner ashore covers the mooring. The holding is good on sand if you prefer to anchor further out.

9.shortDescription: An open 25-mile passage north to Vis, closed under the military for decades and reopened only in 1989, landing at the fishing port of Komiža.
9.description: This is real open water, the most exposed leg of the itinerary, so check the forecast the night before and pick your weather. Vis lies further out than any other inhabited island in central Dalmatia, and that isolation was enforced for years when it served as a Yugoslav military base, closed to outsiders until 1989. Komiža is the western port, a huddle of stone houses around a working fishing harbour where nets still dry on the quay. Walk up Hum in the late afternoon for the best sunset on the island. From Komiža it is a short run out to the islet of Biševo and its Blue Cave, best entered late morning when the light turns the water electric. Eat komiška pogača, a flat anchovy-and-tomato pie, and you have tasted the island properly.
thingsToDo: Day trip to the Biševo Blue Cave
Climb Hum hill for sunset
Try komiška pogača anchovy pie
See the Komiža fishing museum
9.mooringTip: The Komiža town quay is fine by day but rolls at night and offers little shelter, so book ACI Marina Vis on the far side of the island for a settled overnight. Time any Blue Cave visit for calm weather, as swell shuts the entrance.

10.shortDescription: A 30-mile crossing back northeast to Zlarin, the car-free coral island off Šibenik where divers have gathered red coral since the 1400s.
10.description: Another decent passage, so leave in good time and enjoy the miles while the wind lasts. Zlarin is a low, green island close to Šibenik with one village gathered around its harbour and not a single car on it. The island made its living from coral, dived by hand off these shores since the fifteenth century, and you can still find small workshops turning the deep red into jewellery. Wander up through the alleys, look in at the coral centre that tells the story, and buy a piece straight from a maker if it takes you. Everything moves at walking pace. Settle on a bench by the water with a glass of loza, the local grape rakija, and let the pine and salt do the rest before dinner aboard.
thingsToDo: Visit the Zlarin coral centre
Browse a working coral workshop
Walk the car-free village paths
Loza rakija on the harbour front
10.mooringTip: The Zlarin harbour quay is short-stay and can be busy, so anchor just outside the bay on sand in 5-7 m for the night. It is open to the west, so pick a settled evening.

11.shortDescription: A quick run along the Krka river to Skradin, a walled town within Krka National Park, the way in to the Skradinski Buk falls.
11.description: Today the sea gives way to a river. From the Šibenik channel you turn inland and motor up the Krka through a green, steep-sided gorge to Skradin, a small medieval town that sits at the head of the navigable water. This is the doorstep of Krka National Park. Your park ticket includes the electric shuttle boat that runs up to Skradinski Buk, the tiered waterfall complex where the river drops over travertine steps into wide pools. Come early, before the day-tripper buses arrive from Split. Back in town, Skradin itself is worth an evening, its lanes tight and quiet once the crowds thin, and the local reputation for pršut and slow-cooked lamb is well earned. Tie up at the marina and stay the night on the river.
thingsToDo: Ride the shuttle to Skradinski Buk
Walk the Skradin medieval lanes
Cycle a Krka park trail
Dalmatian pršut and lamb for dinner
11.mooringTip: ACI Marina Skradin sits right at the river mouth in town and is the obvious overnight; pre-book for July and August. The river approach is well marked and calm, an easy end to the day.

12.shortDescription: Back down the river and a short coastal hop to Tijat, a tiny empty island in the Šibenik channel with nothing ashore but pines.
12.description: After the falls and the crowds, this is a deliberate day of nothing. Drop back down the Krka, out through the Šibenik channel, and pick your way among the small pine-clad islands to Tijat, a low uninhabited hump with no village, no konoba and no water tap. That is the whole appeal. Anchor off the sheltered side, launch the kayak or the paddleboard, and poke around the neighbouring islets at your own speed. The swimming is quiet and the snorkelling over seagrass turns up the odd fish and plenty of sea urchins, so keep your shoes on. Rig a hammock in the shade if you have one. Cook simply on board that evening, sardines over coals if you stocked up, and let the anchor light be the only one in the bay.
thingsToDo: Kayak among the pine-clad islets
Snorkel the seagrass shallows
Swing a hammock in the pines
Grill sardines at anchor
12.mooringTip: There are no buoys or services at Tijat, so anchor on sand and seagrass in 5-8 m and set the hook well. Stock food, water and fuel at Skradin or Šibenik first, because you will find nothing here.

13.shortDescription: A gentle 8 miles to Murter, the working gateway to the Kornati and home to many of the archipelago's konoba families.
13.description: An easy last full day, a short reach across to Murter to close the loop near where the Kornati charters begin. Murter is a proper island community rather than a resort, and many of the families who run the konobe out in the national park keep their homes here. Slanica is the island's headline beach, a shallow sandy bay on the edge of Murter town that is good for a long swim and easy to reach on foot. Look in at the small ethno museum, then walk out to the Tisno swing bridge that links the island to the mainland and opens on a schedule for masts to pass. For dinner, hunt down pašticada, beef marinated for days and stewed slow with prunes and red wine over homemade gnocchi. It is the right meal for a last night out.
thingsToDo: Swim the sandy Slanica beach
Visit the Murter ethno museum
Walk out to the Tisno bridge
Pašticada beef stew for dinner
13.mooringTip: Marina Hramina and Marina Betina both sit within a mile of Murter town and either works; pre-book in summer. Hramina is the larger and closer to the beach and old town.

14.shortDescription: Twenty-two closing miles thread the Pašman channel back to Zadar, arriving at dusk to the Sea Organ's notes and a glowing Greeting to the Sun.
14.description: Last leg home, back up the sheltered Pašman channel toward Zadar. There is time for a final swim, so drop into one of the quiet coves along Pašman on the way and make the most of the water while you have it. The channel is protected and usually calm, an undemanding sail to end on. Aim to reach Zadar late in the day. On the tip of the old-town riva, Nikola Bašić's Sea Organ plays as the swell pushes air through pipes under the steps, and beside it the Greeting to the Sun disc lights up at dusk. Walk in through the Roman Forum first, then finish the trip with a small glass of Maraschino, the cherry liqueur Zadar has made for centuries. Two weeks and most of central Dalmatia behind you.
thingsToDo: Last swim in a Pašman cove
Hear the Sea Organ on the riva
See the Greeting to the Sun at dusk
Maraschino cherry liqueur in the old town
14.mooringTip: Marina Zadar at Borik is the usual charter base for the drop-off. Book your fuel-dock slot about 24 hours ahead, as the queue backs up on Friday and Saturday turnaround.

zadar-kornati-krka-route.metaDesc: Sail seven days from Zadar through Telaščica, the Kornati islands and Krka's waterfalls, mooring in sandy bays like Sakarun and Vrgada.

1.shortDescription: About 22 miles out of Zadar past the olive slopes of Ugljan to Božava, with a swim stop at sandy Sakarun on the way.
1.description: The Sea Organ groans and sighs under the Riva as you cast off, which is as good a send-off as any harbour gives. From Zadar it's a steady reach of roughly 22 miles, first past Ugljan's terraced olive groves, then around the northwest shoulder of Dugi Otok. We usually break the passage at Sakarun, a rare stretch of white sand where the bottom shelves so gently the whole bay glows pale green. Drop the hook there for lunch and a long swim before carrying on the last mile into Božava. The village is tiny: a stone church, a couple of konobas, boats nodding at the quay. Order the grilled sea bream and eat late, the way the locals do. Above the mast the swifts wheel until dark.
thingsToDo: Anchor and swim at Sakarun beach
Climb the Veli Rat lighthouse tower
Grilled sea bream at a Božava konoba
Hear the Zadar Sea Organ at departure
1.mooringTip: Božava's little marina and quay fill fast in July and August, so radio ahead or plan to lie at anchor. Sakarun holds well on sand in 4-6 m but empties out if the northwesterly gets up, so treat it as a daytime stop rather than an overnight one.

2.shortDescription: A brief 14-mile run down Dugi Otok's seaward edge to Telaščica, among the Adriatic's best-sheltered bays and the doorway onto the Kornati.
2.description: Leave Božava after breakfast; it's an easy 14 miles south along the seaward side of Dugi Otok, though keep an eye on the swell where the cliffs meet the open water. Telaščica opens up like a long protected finger of the sea, ringed by more than twenty coves. Tie up and walk across the neck of land to Mir, a saltwater lake that runs a few degrees warmer than the Adriatic and saltier too; the mud along its edge has a local reputation for good skin. From there the path climbs to the top of the Stene cliffs, which drop close to 160 metres straight into the water. Sit up there a while. On the way down you'll likely meet the park's half-wild donkeys working the scrub for shade.
thingsToDo: Swim the warm saltwater Mir lake
Hike to the top of the Stene cliffs
Watch for the park's wild donkeys
Lunch at a Telaščica bay konoba
2.mooringTip: There's a park entry fee collected at the buoy. The bay is one of the safest overnight spots in the region, but the buoy fields fill by late afternoon in high summer, so reserve through one of the konobas or arrive early.

3.shortDescription: Just 8 miles into the bare-stone maze of Kornati National Park, 89 islands of karst with restaurant buoys at Levrnaka and Piškera.
3.description: Only about 8 miles today, but it's the heart of the trip. Kornati is 89 islands of pale, treeless rock, no villages, no roads, nothing much but stone and sea. The seaward cliffs, the ones the locals call the crowns, face the open Adriatic and take the full weight of the swell. Moor at Levrnaka and swim off Lojena, one of the few sandy beaches in the whole park. If you've the legs for it, the climb up Metlina on Kornat gives you the entire archipelago laid out below. By evening we generally settle near Piškera, where an old fishing chapel still stands among the ruins. Book a table ashore, grill whatever the konoba landed that morning, and watch the sky do its thing once the generators go quiet.
thingsToDo: Swim sandy Lojena beach on Levrnaka
Climb Metlina viewpoint on Kornat island
See the Piškera fishermen's chapel
Dinner at a Kornati restaurant buoy
3.mooringTip: You'll pay a daily park ticket on the way in; buy it online ahead of time, as it costs less than at the boat. Restaurant buoys at Levrnaka, Piškera and Vrulje are free if you eat ashore, so book the table and the mooring comes with it.

4.shortDescription: A relaxed coastal run to Zlarin, Croatia's car-free coral island, where families have harvested red coral since the fifteenth century.
4.description: After the raw stone of Kornati, Zlarin feels almost lush. It's a gentle sail southeast toward the Šibenik archipelago, the maestral usually filling in by midday to give you a proper beam reach for the last stretch. No cars run on the island, so the pace drops the moment you step off the boat. Zlarin has traded in red coral for five centuries, and the small Centre for Coral Tradition lays out the tools and the story. Wander the lanes behind the harbour, past workshops where a few craftsmen still polish the stuff by hand. Come evening, grab a bench by the water with a glass of local rakija and watch the old men slap down cards at briškula. Šibenik's forts glow gold across the channel as the light goes.
thingsToDo: Visit the Centre for Coral Tradition
Walk the car-free harbour lanes
Local rakija on the waterfront bench
Watch a game of briškula
4.mooringTip: The town quay at Zlarin is short and best treated as a day berth; get in before mid-afternoon in season if you want a spot. For a calm night, anchor just outside the harbour on sand in 5-7 m and go ashore by tender.

5.shortDescription: Head inland on the Krka to Skradin, a walled town of the Middle Ages and the waterborne route to Krka's celebrated falls.
5.description: Today you leave the sea behind. The route runs into the Šibenik channel, under the bridge, and up the green throat of the Krka river, with a strict slow-speed limit once you're in the gorge, so take your time and enjoy the reflections. Skradin sits where the river widens, a compact town of stone arches and shaded courtyards. From the quay, the national park's own boats run up to Skradinski Buk, the tiered waterfall complex that made Krka famous; wooden boardwalks thread between the cascades and the pools. Save room for the food ashore. Skradin risotto simmers for the better part of a day, and the local pršut and cheese make an easy lunch on deck. Cyclists can pick up a park trail that follows the water upstream.
thingsToDo: Boat up to the Skradinski Buk falls
Walk Skradin's medieval stone lanes
Try slow-cooked Skradin risotto
Cycle a Krka riverside trail
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Skradin sits right at the river mouth and is the practical overnight choice; book well ahead for July and August. The town quay is short-stay only, and anchoring isn't allowed in the river or Prokljan lake, so don't count on dropping the hook here.

6.shortDescription: Back down to salt water and roughly 18 miles to Vrgada, a car-free island near Pirovac with a rare stretch of soft sand.
6.description: Retrace the river down to the coast and point the bow northwest; it's around 18 miles to Vrgada, a low green island off Pirovac that most charter routes skip. That's rather the point. The bay at Luka shelves onto genuine sand, which is scarce along this part of the coast, so the anchorage glows the same pale blue you got at Sakarun. Ashore there's not much: a scatter of houses, pine woods, footpaths and no cars. Walk one of the tracks over to the far beaches and comb the tideline. In summer a couple of family konobas open up, and if brudet is on the board, order it; the fishermen's stew here is unhurried and honest. The island fills a little in August and empties again by September.
thingsToDo: Swim the sandy bay at Luka
Walk the no-car island tracks
Beachcomb the quiet far-side beaches
Brudet fish stew at a family konoba
6.mooringTip: Vrgada has restaurant buoys off Luka bay that come free if you dine ashore. Holding is good on sand in 4-6 m if you'd rather anchor and keep the evening to yourselves; it can turn rolly with any southerly, so tuck well in.

7.shortDescription: The closing 22 miles run north up the Pašman channel into Zadar, arriving after dark for the Greeting to the Sun display on the riva.
7.description: The last day is a straightforward 22 miles up the Pašman channel, and there's no rush if you leave Vrgada mid-morning. Fit in one more swim wherever the water tempts you off Pašman or Ugljan. Aim to be back in Zadar by late afternoon so you can hand over the boat and still have the evening. The old town rewards a slow walk: the round church of St Donatus, the Roman Forum stones scattered underfoot, columns worn smooth by centuries. Stay out past sunset. The Greeting to the Sun, a big disc of solar cells set into the Riva, wakes up as the light fades and throws colour around your feet while the Sea Organ plays the swell. Toast the week with a Maraschino, the cherry liqueur Zadar has made for two hundred years.
thingsToDo: Final swim off Pašman or Ugljan
Walk the Roman Forum and St Donatus
See the Greeting to the Sun at dusk
Taste Zadar's Maraschino cherry liqueur
7.mooringTip: Marina Zadar at Borik is the usual charter base for the drop-off, though some fleets return to Marina Dalmacija at Sukošan, so check your paperwork. Either way, book your fuel slot a day ahead; the pontoons back up on Friday and Saturday turnarounds.

zadar-pag-route.metaDesc: Sail 7 days from Zadar to Pag through Molat, Silba, Ilovik and Olib: quiet anchorages and buoy fields most charter crews sail past.

1.shortDescription: A gentle 8-mile shake-down out of Zadar to Ždrelac, the narrow gap where Ugljan and Pašman almost touch under their low bridge.
1.description: Spend the morning in Zadar before you slip the lines. The Sea Organ breathes under the Riva steps, the Roman Forum sits a few minutes' walk inland, and the town keeps its Maraschino cherry liqueur close to hand. Once the boat is provisioned, it's a short first leg north into the Pašman channel. Ždrelac is where Ugljan and Pašman nearly meet, joined by a road bridge that arches over the strait. The bay tucks in beside it on sand, well protected from the northwest. We usually drop the hook by mid-afternoon, swim off the transom, and let the crew find their sea legs before dinner. There's no pressure to go far on night one. A plate of brudet ashore and an early bunk sets the tone for the week.
thingsToDo: Hear the Sea Organ on Zadar's Riva
Provision at Zadar's central market
Swim off the sandy Ždrelac bay
Brudet fish stew for the first night
1.mooringTip: Anchor on sand in 5–7 m in the bay beside the bridge; it holds well and shelters from the northwest. Note the bridge clearance is low, so keep to the anchorage rather than trying to carry your mast through the strait.

2.shortDescription: Fourteen quiet miles west to Molat, one of the emptiest islands off Zadar, where three tiny villages share a single lane.
2.description: Molat runs on island time. Fewer than two hundred people live here year-round, split between Molat village, Zapuntel and Brgulje, all linked by one rural road that goes nowhere in a hurry. We tend to anchor in Brgulje bay, where the bottom is sand and seagrass and the holding is good once the anchor bites through the weed. Take the tender in and walk. The lane out to Zapuntel passes dry-stone walls and abandoned olive terraces, and the beach at the far end is usually yours alone. There's little in the way of nightlife, which is the point. After dark the sky opens up. With no streetlights to speak of, Molat is one of the better spots in the archipelago to lie on deck and watch the Milky Way come out.
thingsToDo: Walk the lane to Zapuntel's empty beach
Anchor and swim in Brgulje bay
Wander Molat village's stone lanes
Stargaze from the deck after dark
2.mooringTip: Anchor on sand and weed in 5–8 m in Brgulje bay, and check the hook is set before you leave the boat. A few restaurant buoys sit off the village if you'd rather not swing on your own tackle.

3.shortDescription: A longer passage day: lunch and a swim at car-free Silba, then south to the sheltered Ilovik–Sveti Petar channel for the night.
3.description: This is the biggest sailing day of the week, so we like to leave Molat by nine. Silba makes a fine mid-morning stop. Cars are banned on the island, and the walk from the harbour up through the whitewashed lanes takes you past the Toreta, a curious spiral stone tower a sea captain built for a lost love. Swim, grab a coffee, then push on. South of Lošinj lies Ilovik, low and green and thick with oleander and palms. The real prize is the channel between Ilovik and the islet of Sveti Petar, with its old cemetery chapel on the far shore. It's one of the best-protected overnight spots in the Kvarner, ringed with restaurant buoys. Pick one up, and dinner ashore comes with a calm night at anchor thrown in.
thingsToDo: Walk to Silba's Toreta tower
Swim off car-free Silba at midday
Pick up a buoy in Ilovik's channel
See the chapel on Sveti Petar islet
3.mooringTip: The Ilovik–Sveti Petar channel is packed with restaurant buoys and fully sheltered from all sides; take one and settle up over dinner ashore rather than anchoring. In August it fills early, so aim to be tied up by late afternoon.

4.shortDescription: Cross east into the Pag channel to Šimuni, a working fishing village below the island's bare, wind-scoured stone hills.
4.description: Pag looks like nowhere else on the coast. Centuries of bura wind and salt have stripped the north of the island back to bare grey rock, and the crossing gives you the full effect as you close the shore. Šimuni sits midway down the west side, a real fishing village with a tidy ACI marina and a couple of good grills. Hire bikes and ride north to the Lun peninsula, where a grove of wild olives has stood for centuries, some trees said to be over a thousand years old. Back aboard, the eating is the reason to stop here. Pag lamb, grazed on salt-tinged herbs, and the sharp aged sheep cheese from the Gligora dairy at nearby Kolan are worth the detour on their own.
thingsToDo: Bike the ancient Lun olive groves
Taste Paški sir at Gligora dairy
Grill Pag lamb at a beach eatery
Wander Šimuni's fishing harbour at dusk
4.mooringTip: Book a berth at ACI Marina Šimuni ahead in July and August, as it's small and popular. For a quieter night, anchor in one of the sandy coves just south of the village in 5–7 m.

5.shortDescription: A short 10-mile hop northwest to Olib, an under-visited island with genuine sand beaches and its own hand-rolled pasta.
5.description: After Pag's stone, Olib feels soft. This is one of the few islands up here with real sand rather than pebble, and Slatina bay shelves gently into water the colour of weak tea over the shallows. Fewer than two hundred people live on the island, and the old stone hamlets inland are half-swallowed by fig and bramble. We treat this as a slow day. Swim in the morning, walk the tracks between deserted houses in the cool of late afternoon, and eat ashore in the evening. Ask around for olibške makarule, the island's own hand-rolled pasta that the older women still make by hand, usually served with a simple meat sauce. It's the kind of place where the loudest sound after sundown is the cicadas winding down.
thingsToDo: Swim the sandy shallows of Slatina bay
Walk the tracks past deserted stone houses
Try olibške makarule pasta ashore
Cycle inland to Banve beach
5.mooringTip: Olib's little fishing port has only a handful of slots, so don't count on a berth. Anchor in Slatina bay on sand in 4–6 m, well tucked from the north and northeast.

6.shortDescription: Sixteen miles back across the channel to slender Iž, the last place in Croatia where potters still throw clay peka bells by hand.
6.description: Iž is long, thin and green, a low ridge of pine and vine running parallel to the mainland. It has kept a craft most islands lost: Veli Iž is one of the last places on the coast where potters still throw clay by hand, including the round baking bells used for peka. Stop in at a workshop before you settle for the night. The island rewards a bike or a good pair of shoes. Ride over to one of the swim coves on the seaward side, then come back for an early dinner. If your week happens to land on the first Saturday of August, the Fjera village fair takes over Veli Iž with music, food stalls and the mock crowning of the King of Iž. It's as local as it gets.
thingsToDo: Visit a Veli Iž pottery workshop
Cycle to a seaward swim cove
Time your stop for the August Fjera
Try smoked octopus salad ashore
6.mooringTip: The small ACI marina at Veli Iž is well protected but fills fast, so radio ahead. For a quiet night on the hook, anchor in Knež bay on sand in 5–8 m.

7.shortDescription: A short final leg across the Zadar channel with a last swim off Ugljan, timed to reach the Riva for the sunset light show.
7.description: The last day is short by design, which leaves room for one more swim. Ugljan lies right across the channel from Zadar, and its quieter east-coast coves make a good final anchorage before the boat has to be handed back. Have lunch aboard, then motor the short hop into the marina. Try to time your arrival for dusk. As the light goes, the Greeting to the Sun, a big glass disc set into the Riva, soaks up the day's sun and throws it back as a slow wash of colour underfoot, while the Sea Organ plays the swell alongside. Walk up to the Roman Forum, then raise a last glass of Maraschino, the cherry liqueur Zadar has been making for two centuries, to a week in the archipelago's quiet corner.
thingsToDo: Final swim in an Ugljan cove
Watch the Greeting to the Sun at dusk
Walk Zadar's Roman Forum
Toast the week with local Maraschino
7.mooringTip: Marina Zadar at Borik is the usual charter base for the handover. Book your fuel-dock slot a day ahead and give the boat a clean the evening before to keep the morning stress-free.

zadar-vis-hvar-route.metaTitle: 7-Day Vis & Hvar Sail from Zadar | Boat4You
zadar-vis-hvar-route.metaDesc: Sail seven days round-trip from Zadar to Vis and Hvar, taking in Ždrelac, Primošten, Komiža's Blue Cave, Šolta and Šibenik's stone cathedral.

1.shortDescription: A short shake-down hop across the Pašman channel into Ždrelac, where a sandy-bottomed bay lets a new crew settle in before dark.
1.description: Pick up the boat in Zadar and give the systems a proper check before you slip lines; there's no rush on day one. The passage to Ždrelac runs about eight miles, an easy reach across the Pašman channel, with the low bridge that joins Ugljan and Pašman marking the narrows. We usually motor the last stretch and drop the hook on the sand in five to seven metres, well tucked from anything out of the north. The water here sits flat and pale over the shallows, ideal for a first swim while the kettle goes on. Back in town, leave time before departure for the Roman Forum and the Sea Organ, which turns the swell into low chords under the quay. A quiet konoba ashore and an early night set the rhythm for the week.
thingsToDo: Listen to Zadar's Sea Organ
Wander the Roman Forum and St. Donatus
Swim off Ždrelac's sandy shallows
Walk out to the Ždrelac bridge
1.mooringTip: Anchor free on sand in 5-7 m, good holding and sheltered from the N and NW; there's generous swinging room, so lay plenty of scope. The Zadar town quay is short-stay only, so plan to spend the night at anchor or in the marina.

2.shortDescription: A longer coastal run south past Šibenik's channel to Primošten, the stone town stacked on a headland above dry-stone vineyard terraces.
2.description: This is the week's first real distance, roughly thirty miles down the mainland coast, so we like to be off the hook by nine. The morning is usually light; the maestral fills in from the northwest around midday and gives you a proper sail for the back half. Primošten shows itself from a long way out, a knot of red roofs and a bell tower on its own little peninsula. Ashore, climb the lanes to St. George's church for the view back over the moorings, then walk the Bucavac hillside where dry-stone walls hold the Babić vines in place. The red they make from those grapes is worth ordering with dinner. If the crew wants a beach afternoon, the Raduča coves on the north side shelve gently into clear water.
thingsToDo: Climb to St. George's hilltop church
Walk the Bucavac vineyard terraces
Order a glass of Primošten Babić
Swim at the Raduča beaches
2.mooringTip: Marina Kremik sits about 1.5 miles south and is the reliable berth; book ahead through July and August. If you'd rather anchor, the nearby Garma and Raduča coves hold well on sand in 5-8 m, though they stay open to the afternoon maestral.

3.shortDescription: An offshore passage out to Vis, the Adriatic's most remote island, ending in Komiža's palm-lined fishing harbour under Hum hill.
3.description: Vis lies further out than anything else in this part of Dalmatia, which is exactly why it feels different when you arrive. The crossing is around twenty-eight miles of open water, so check the forecast the evening before; a settled day with the afternoon northwesterly makes for a fine beam reach. Komiža opens up slowly, a curve of shuttered houses and fishing boats with Hum hill rising steep behind. Once you're secure, the walk up Hum at dusk pays off with the whole archipelago laid out in the last light. Down at the waterfront, order komiška pogača, the local flatbread packed with anchovy, onion and tomato, and let the evening run long. The Fishing Museum in the old Venetian tower is a good hour if the sun is too strong for the deck.
thingsToDo: Boat trip to the Blue Cave, Biševo
Hike Hum hill for sunset
Komiška pogača on the waterfront
Fishing Museum in the Venetian tower
3.mooringTip: Komiža's harbour quay is exposed and really only for a short stay; for a settled night, book ACI Marina Vis on the island's eastern side and cross over. The Blue Cave is best reached by a small local tour boat from Komiža rather than your own keel.

4.shortDescription: An easy northeast leg into Hvar Town, the flashiest port on the Adriatic, its harbour mouth strewn with the Pakleni Islands.
4.description: After the quiet of Vis, Hvar comes as a jolt of colour and noise, in the best way. It's a shortish leg of about twenty-two miles, with room to slow down and swim in the Pakleni Islands before you cross into the harbour. Hvar Town wraps around the largest public square in Dalmatia, with the cathedral at one end and the Spanish Fortress on the hill above. Climb up to the fort in the late afternoon; the light over the Pakleni Islands from up there is the reason people come back. In the evening the town shifts gear. Book a table early, then let the crew loose on the bars, or take the tender across to Hula-Hula for sundowners and a swim back to the boat.
thingsToDo: Climb the Spanish Fortress for sunset
Cruise the Pakleni Islands by tender
Coffee on St. Stephen's Square
Sundowners at Hula-Hula beach bar
Swim stop in Vinogradišće bay
4.mooringTip: Hvar town quay slots go first-come and fill by early afternoon in season, so aim to arrive before lunch. The calmer overnight is ACI Marina Palmižana on Sveti Klement, two miles across in the Pakleni Islands; book it, and tender in for dinner.

5.shortDescription: A gentle hop north to Šolta's western village of Maslinica, where seven bare islets sit off a harbour built around an old castle.
5.description: Šolta is the antidote to Hvar, low-key and green with olive groves, and the run up is an easy eighteen miles. Maslinica faces west into a scatter of seven small uninhabited islets, which makes for a lazy afternoon of dinghy-hopping and swimming off the rocks. The village itself is tidy and quiet, gathered around the Martinis-Marchi castle that now works as a hotel and marina. If the harbour is full, drop into Šešula bay a mile south, a deep finger of water with good holding and a couple of konobas that will send a tender for you. Šolta is known for its olive oil and its lamb, so this is the night to eat well ashore. Watch the sun go down behind the islets with a glass of the island's Dobričić red.
thingsToDo: Dinghy out to the seven islets
Aperitif at the Martinis-Marchi castle
Anchor and swim in Šešula bay
Šolta olive oil and lamb dinner
5.mooringTip: ACI Marina Maslinica is small and well protected but fills fast; reserve if you can. Šešula bay, one mile south, is the dependable fallback, with sand and weed holding in 5-8 m and restaurant buoys near the head of the inlet.

6.shortDescription: A longer run back north to Šibenik, the old royal city guarding its river channel with a stone cathedral and a ring of hilltop forts.
6.description: Another long day, close to twenty-eight miles, and worth an early start to beat the afternoon chop off the mainland. The approach to Šibenik is one of the best on this coast; you thread the narrow St. Anthony Channel under the St. Nicholas fort and the city unfolds at the top. Leave the boat and walk straight up to the Cathedral of St. James, built entirely of interlocking stone with no mortar and a frieze of seventy-one carved faces around the apse. St. Michael's Fortress above it earns the climb for the harbour view. If you have the afternoon, the Krka waterfalls are a short trip upriver from Skradin. Down on the riva in the evening, the fritule stalls fry little doughnuts that are hard to walk past.
thingsToDo: Tour the mortarless St. James Cathedral
Find the 71 carved stone faces
Climb St. Michael's Fortress
Krka waterfalls trip from Skradin
Fritule doughnuts on the riva
6.mooringTip: Mandalina is the usual charter marina, just inside the channel and easy to enter under engine; book in high season. For a quieter night among the reeds, ACI Marina Skradin sits upriver, a scenic motor up the Krka but a longer haul back out in the morning.

7.shortDescription: One last coastal run threads the Pašman channel to Zadar, timed to reach the riva just as the Greeting to the Sun glows on.
7.description: The last hop is a straightforward thirty miles back up the coast and into the Pašman channel, with plenty of coves to duck into for a final swim if the day is kind. There's no need to push hard; time the run so you're alongside in Zadar with the light still up. As it fades, the Greeting to the Sun begins its slow colour show on the quay, right beside the Sea Organ. Hand the boat back in good order, then take the crew into the old town for a last dinner. Zadar makes Maraschino, the cherry liqueur the city has distilled for the best part of two centuries, and a small glass of it is the right way to close a week that started here. Doviđenja until the next charter.
thingsToDo: Final swim in a Pašman-channel cove
Watch the Greeting to the Sun
Roman Forum and St. Donatus church
Maraschino nightcap in the old town
7.mooringTip: Marina Zadar at Borik is the standard drop-off berth; confirm your fuel-dock slot at least a day ahead, as the queue backs up on Friday and Saturday turnarounds. Leave time to top up and pump out before the handover check.
